people.notes: Dr. Heidy Khlaaf is the Chief AI Scientist at the AI Now Institute focusing on the assessment and safety of AI within autonomous weapons systems and defense/national security systems. She was named to TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2025 and MIT Technology Review's 35 Innovators Under 35 in 2025. She completed her Computer Science PhD at University College London in 2017, where she was advised by Nir Piterman. She is a 2008 graduate of Paxon School for Advanced Studies.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict regulatory oversight of AI deployment in military and safety-critical systems, comparing AI regulation to nuclear safety frameworks. Has testified that existing AI safety guardrails are 'deeply lacking' and 'easily compromised.' Strongly opposes military use of commercial AI models without proper safety assessments, stating that speed in military AI deployment is 'really a cover for indiscriminate targeting.' Co-authored research calling for comprehensive risk assessments before AI deployment and has criticized the 'rush to integrate AI everywhere' as undermining safety.

people.title: President, American Federation of Teachers
people.other_orgs: Former President United Federation of Teachers AFT Local 2 (1998-2008), Former Chair NYC Municipal Labor Committee, Former member Equity and Excellence Commission, Former member NY Gov. Cuomo Edu
people.notes: Randi Weingarten is president of the 1.8 million-member American Federation of Teachers (AFT), which represents teachers, paraprofessionals, higher education faculty, nurses, government employees, and early childhood educators. She was elected AFT president in 2008, after serving 11 years as president of the United Federation of Teachers in New York City. She taught history at Clara Barton High School in Brooklyn from 1991 to 1997 and holds degrees from Cornell University's School of Industrial and Labor Relations and the Cardozo School of Law. When elected AFT president in 2008, she became the first openly gay leader of an American national labor union.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict government regulations protecting privacy, security and well-being in AI development. Led AFT Executive Council to adopt resolution calling for developers to be held accountable for real-world harm and urging governments to implement effective regulations. Supports European-style regulatory approach, noting 'Europe is well ahead of us in terms of doing this work.' Opposes Trump administration's executive order to gut AI regulations, calling it 'outrageous and likely illegal' for prioritizing Big Tech over children and families. Works directly with select AI companies (OpenAI, Microsoft, Anthropic) on baseline privacy agreements while advocating for comprehensive federal guardrails.

people.title: Deepfake victim turned AI safety advocate
people.other_orgs: Voices for Voices podcast (guest), First Lady Melania Trump's Be Best campaign (participant)
people.notes: Elliston Berry was 14 years old when a classmate made and shared deepfake nude images of her, which she discovered in October 2023. She helped create an online training course to teach students, parents and school staff about non-consensual, explicit deepfake image abuse, in partnership with cybersecurity firm Adaptive Security and Pathos Consulting Group. Berry advocated for the Take It Down Act, which was signed into law by President Donald Trump and makes it a crime to share nonconsensual, explicit images. She appeared at a Capitol Hill press conference with Sen. Ted Cruz and spoke at the White House bill signing ceremony.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates specifically for federal legislation criminalizing non-consensual AI-generated intimate imagery and requiring social media platforms to remove such content within 48 hours. Co-architect of the bipartisan Take It Down Act (S.146) with Senators Ted Cruz and Amy Klobuchar. Focuses on holding Big Tech accountable for AI-enabled harm to minors while supporting targeted regulatory intervention rather than broad AI restrictions.

people.notes: Karen Hao is an American journalist who currently freelances for publications like The Atlantic and co-hosts the BBC podcast The Interface. She is the author of the 2025 bestselling book 'Empire of AI: Dreams and Nightmares in Sam Altman's OpenAI' which came out May 20, 2025 and was an instant NYT bestseller. She previously worked as a reporter for The Wall Street Journal covering American and Chinese tech companies, and as a senior AI editor at MIT Technology Review. Time magazine honored her on its TIME100 AI list in 2025.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for urgent shift away from current AI development trajectory, criticizing the 'scale-at-all-costs approach' and calling for stronger oversight of AI companies. Has written extensively about the need to separate AI development from what she calls 'empire building,' emphasizing that current voluntary safety commitments are insufficient. Her book and reporting consistently argue for more democratic governance of AI development rather than leaving decisions to tech executives.

people.notes: Mike Braun is the 52nd Governor of Indiana who previously served as a U.S. Senator from 2018. He is a conservative Republican who spent his career as an entrepreneur before entering politics, building a business that grew into a national company employing thousands of Americans. Braun issued an executive order creating the Indiana Initiative for Drone Dominance Task Force to support President Trump's drone dominance executive orders. He has been advocating for Indiana to become a national leader in artificial intelligence infrastructure, emphasizing the importance of data centers and energy capacity.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for minimal federal AI regulation while embracing AI adoption, stating 'I am very concerned that we do not smother it' regarding heavy-handed regulations. Co-sponsored bipartisan legislation focused on transparency and training rather than restrictions, including the AI Leadership Training Act and Transparent Automated Governance Act (TAG Act). Emphasized that government should 'embrace AI' and warned 'if we do not embrace AI, we won't be able to counter it.' As Governor, implemented comprehensive state AI policy requiring assessment and approval processes for government AI use while enabling innovation.

people.name: Gary Peters
people.title: U.S. Senator from Michigan, Chairman of Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee
people.other_orgs: Smithsonian Institution Board of Regents, Former Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee Chair (2022-2024), Former Michigan Lottery Commissioner (2003-2008), U.S. Navy Reserve Lieutenant Command
people.notes: Gary Peters is a U.S. Senator from Michigan who has served since 2015 and serves as the Ranking Member of the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee. He announced on January 28, 2025, that he will not seek reelection in 2026. Peters has been rated the most effective U.S. Senator for three consecutive Congresses by the nonpartisan Center for Effective Lawmaking. He has focused on artificial intelligence policy, introducing multiple bipartisan bills including the AI and Critical Technology Workforce Framework Act and the PREPARED for AI Act.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Peters focuses on responsible government AI procurement and transparency rather than broad AI restrictions. His flagship PREPARED for AI Act (S.4495) requires federal agencies to assess AI risks before purchase and deployment, establish Chief AI Officers, and include safety terms in high-risk AI contracts, while also creating pilot programs to streamline AI adoption. He has also sponsored the Transparent Automated Governance (TAG) Act requiring agencies to notify individuals when they interact with AI systems and establish human review processes for AI-generated decisions. His approach emphasizes practical guardrails and transparency in government AI use rather than restricting AI development broadly.
people.threat_models: Civil rights violations, Privacy breaches, Bias/discrimination, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity

people.notes: Maria Cantwell is a U.S. Senator from Washington State, serving as Ranking Member of the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation. She has introduced multiple AI-related bills in 2026, including the NSF AI Education Act and the Small Business Artificial Intelligence Training Act. She previously served in the U.S. House of Representatives from 1993-95 and worked as vice president for consumer products at Progressive Networks (later RealNetworks). She was first elected to the U.S. Senate in 2000 and won reelection in 2024 against Republican challenger Raul Garcia.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-sponsors multiple targeted AI bills focusing on specific sectors and safety measures rather than broad restrictions. The Future of AI Innovation Act establishes voluntary standards through NIST and creates testbeds for evaluation. The NSF AI Education Act authorizes scholarships and training programs. Successfully led bipartisan 99-1 Senate vote to strike down a 10-year moratorium on state AI regulations, arguing states need ability to enforce consumer protections against robocalls, deepfakes, and autonomous vehicle safety while supporting federal framework development.

people.notes: Frank Lucas is a Republican U.S. Representative from Oklahoma's 3rd congressional district who serves as Chairman of the House Science, Space, and Technology Committee. He introduced the Workforce for AI Trust Act (H.R. 9215) with Ranking Member Zoe Lofgren on July 30, 2024, aimed at promoting a multidisciplinary workforce to advance AI integration and training. Lucas chairs the House Financial Services Task Force on Monetary Policy, Treasury Market Resilience and Economic Prosperity and wants Congress to help stabilize financial markets by insulating the Federal Reserve from political influence. He has served in Congress since 1994 after being elected by special election to fill a vacancy.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Chairs the House Science Committee and consistently emphasizes supporting AI innovation without imposing 'regulations and burdensome requirements.' Lucas has led passage of nine bipartisan AI bills focused on research, education, and workforce development rather than restrictions. He advocates for 'strategic investments, build our workforce, and establish proper safeguards without overregulation' and believes the U.S. must compete with China through innovation rather than regulatory constraints.

people.notes: Ted Lieu represents California's 36th Congressional District and is serving his sixth term in Congress. He was elected by his colleagues to serve as Vice Chair of the Democratic Caucus, making him the highest ranking Asian American to have ever served in House Leadership. He also introduced the Block Nuclear Launch by Autonomous Artificial Intelligence Act (2023) requiring human control over nuclear weapons, and proposed creating a bipartisan 20-member federal AI commission to develop regulatory recommendations over two years. He emphasizes the need for 'sensible guardrails' rather than a 'complete Wild West' approach to AI.

people.notes: Senator Edward J. Markey (D-Mass.) is a member of the Commerce, Science and Transportation Committee and has served in the U.S. Senate since 2013. He reintroduced the Artificial Intelligence Civil Rights Act on December 2, 2025, alongside Representative Yvette Clarke, to prevent companies from using biased and discriminatory AI-powered algorithms in critical decisions affecting Americans' lives. Markey stated "We must address AI-powered bias and discrimination in the AI age" and emphasized that "Under the AI Civil Rights Act, America would show leadership in AI—not just technological leadership, but moral leadership". He is running for reelection to a third Senate term in 2026 at age 80.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored the AI Civil Rights Act requiring companies to conduct independent audits to identify algorithmic discrimination, prohibiting discriminatory AI use, and giving individuals right to appeal AI decisions to humans. Also introduced the Eliminating BIAS Act mandating federal agencies establish civil rights offices to combat algorithmic bias. Strongly opposed Trump administration's proposed 10-year moratorium on state AI regulation, leading bipartisan Senate efforts that defeated it 99-1. Advocates for 'moral leadership' in AI, stating 'we cannot abandon our principles in reckless pursuit of technological superiority.

people.notes: Lynne Parker is Principal Deputy Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy and Executive Director of the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology. In April 2025, stated that revised federal AI policies would 'remove unnecessary bureaucratic restrictions, allow agencies to be more efficient and cost-effective.' people.name: Arati Prabhakar
people.title: OSTP Director (Biden Admin, 2022-2025); fmr DARPA Director (2012-2017); fmr NIST Director (1993-1997)
people.primary_org: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP)
people.other_orgs: Former U.S. Venture Partners (2001-2011), Former DARPA (1986-1993, 2012-2017), Former NIST (1993-1997), Former Raychem CTO, Former Interval Research President, Actuate founder (2019), National Ac
people.notes: Arati Prabhakar served as the 12th director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy from October 3, 2022 to January 20, 2025, and was the Science and Technology Advisor to President Biden. She was the first woman and person of color to be confirmed by the U.S. Senate as the Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy. She was instrumental in passing President Biden's executive order on AI in 2023, which sets guidelines for tech companies to make AI safer and more transparent, and was the first to demonstrate ChatGPT to the president in the Oval Office. She previously served as the 20th Director of DARPA from 2012 to 2017 and as the 10th Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology from 1993 to 1997, being the first woman to hold both positions.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive risk management approach that enables innovation while addressing specific harms. Led Biden's landmark AI Executive Order (October 2023) establishing safety standards, bias protections, and testing requirements. Supports targeted regulations like requiring explanations for AI loan decisions and preventing discriminatory healthcare AI, while opposing blanket restrictions. Testified that California's SB-1047 was 'simply impractical' because safety assessment methods don't exist yet. Emphasizes voluntary industry commitments (secured from 15 AI companies) combined with federal standards through NIST.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Misinformation, Privacy, Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, National security

people.name: Suresh Venkatasubramanian
people.title: Director, Center for Technological Responsibility, Reimagination, and Redesign (CNTR), Brown University
people.other_orgs: Former Assistant Director for Science and Justice, White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (2021-2022); Board Member, Data & Society; Former Board Member, ACLU Utah (2017-2021); Forme
people.notes: Suresh Venkatasubramanian is a Professor of Data Science and Computer Science at Brown University who directs the Center for Technological Responsibility, Reimagination and Redesign. He served as Assistant Director for Science and Justice in the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy for the 2021-2022 academic year, where he co-authored the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights. His research focuses on algorithmic fairness and the impact of automated decision-making systems in society. He previously held positions at the University of Utah and AT&T Labs.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-authored the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights, which focuses on protecting people's rights through five core principles including algorithmic discrimination protections and data privacy, but emphasizes impact over technology-specific definitions. Advocates for 'sensors, not solutions' approach - developing diagnostic toolkits that can adapt to different contexts rather than rigid parameters. Testified before EEOC recommending detailed validation testing, disparity assessments, and ongoing monitoring requirements for automated systems in employment. Supports guardrails that 'feed further innovation rather than hamper it' and argues that responsible AI regulation creates better engineering practices.
people.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Privacy

people.name: Kathy Hochul
people.title: Governor of New York State
people.other_orgs: Former Erie County Clerk, Former U.S. Representative (NY-26), Co-chair of State's Heroin and Opioid Task Force, Co-chair of Women's Suffrage Commission, Co-chair of Child Care Availability Task F
people.notes: Kathy Hochul is the 57th and first female Governor of New York State. She previously served as Erie county clerk (2007–11), U.S. representative for New York's 26th congressional district (2011–13), and lieutenant governor (2015–21) to Gov. Andrew Cuomo. In her January 13, 2026, State of the State address, Governor Hochul announced a series of proposals related to AI and technology regulation that she said are intended to protect workers and consumers. She announced the launch of the FutureWorks Commission in March 2026, which plans to offer policy proposals on AI best practices that spur innovation while protecting employees from automation.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Signed the RAISE Act on December 19, 2025, which specifically targets large frontier AI developers with revenue over $500 million, requiring safety protocols, 72-hour incident reporting, and transparency measures. Created AI companion safeguards requiring crisis intervention protocols and user notifications. Established Empire AI Consortium with $500 million in public-private funding and launched FutureWorks Commission to study AI's economic impact. Has explicitly stated New York is 'setting the national standard for strong, sensible AI regulation' while emphasizing protection of children and workers without unnecessarily constraining innovation.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security

people.name: John Wentworth
people.title: Independent AI Alignment Researcher
people.notes: Independent alignment researcher working on selection theorems, abstraction, and agency since ~2017. Previously worked in biology and economics. Mentors researchers through MATS program. Has received funding from LTFF ($50k grants), focuses on 'thermometer of abstraction' vision. Four-step alignment plan: sort out agency confusions, ambitious value learning, iterate, profit. Estimates 10-15 years needed for foundational work.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical alignment research rather than regulatory advocacy. Believes sorting out fundamental confusions about agency is prerequisite to meaningful policy. Advocates for understanding AI systems deeply before attempting control measures.

Known for extremely pessimistic AI safety positions and predicting human extinction within this century.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for extreme caution in AI development, arguing that superintelligent AI cannot be indefinitely controlled. Promotes narrow AI development over AGI and calls for potential moratoriums on superintelligence research until safety is better understood.

people.regulatory_stance_detail: Wang advocates for open-minded acceptance of industry fluctuations and greater confidence in AI/robotics development future. He emphasizes the importance of not rushing regulation and allowing the industry to develop naturally through market forces.

people.name: Ruha Benjamin
people.title: Alexander Stewart 1886 Professor of African American Studies, Princeton; Founding Director, Ida B. Wells Just Data Lab; Author, Race After Technology
people.other_orgs: Institute for Advanced Study (2016-2017), formerly Boston University, Harvard Kennedy School
people.notes: Coined the term 'New Jim Code' to describe how digital technologies embed racism while appearing neutral. Her 2019 book 'Race After Technology' won the Oliver Cromwell Cox Book Award for anti-racist scholarship. Delivered 2024 Tanner Lectures at Harvard arguing tech elites cannot be trusted as stewards of collective good. Founded the Ida B. Wells Just Data Lab focusing on algorithmic justice.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for race-conscious design and abolitionist approaches to technology regulation. Argues some technologies like predictive policing and digital surveillance are so embedded in systems of racial control they must be abolished rather than reformed. Calls for regulatory frameworks with community oversight and legal remedies for algorithmic harm.
people.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

people.name: Cathy O'Neil
people.title: Founder, ORCAA (algorithmic auditing company); Author, Weapons of Math Destruction; Former Harvard PhD mathematician and hedge fund analyst
people.other_orgs: Harvard Kennedy School (Public Interest Tech Lab), Columbia University (former Lede Program founder), Bloomberg Opinion (columnist)
people.notes: Founded ORCAA in 2017 to audit algorithms for bias and fairness. Her 2016 book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' was longlisted for the National Book Award and became a NYT bestseller. Testified to Congress on algorithmic accountability and developed the 'Ethical Matrix' framework for auditing AI systems. Won the MAA Euler Book Prize in 2019.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for algorithmic auditing requirements and transparency mandates for high-stakes AI systems, particularly in hiring, criminal justice, and financial services. Supports targeted regulation focusing on consequential algorithms rather than broad AI restrictions.
people.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

people.name: Virginia Eubanks
people.title: Associate Professor of Political Science, University at Albany, SUNY; Author of 'Automating Inequality: How High-Tech Tools Profile, Police, and Punish the Poor'
people.other_orgs: Former Fellow at New America (2016-2017), Founding member of Our Data Bodies Project
people.notes: Eubanks' 2018 book 'Automating Inequality' investigates how AI systems in welfare, housing, and child welfare disproportionately harm poor and working-class people. She coined the term 'digital poorhouse' to describe technological systems that embed assumptions about poverty. Her work focuses on algorithmic accountability and has won multiple awards including the 2019 Lillian Smith Book Award and 2018 McGannon Center Book Prize.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strong regulatory constraints on AI systems that impact public services and vulnerable populations, emphasizing the need to address algorithmic bias and discrimination in automated decision-making systems used by government agencies.
people.threat_models: Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Power concentration

people.name: Lina Khan
people.title: Former Chair, Federal Trade Commission (FTC), 2021-2025
people.other_orgs: Columbia Law School (Associate Professor), New America Foundation/Open Markets Institute (former)
people.notes: Led FTC's AI enforcement including OpenAI investigation (July 2023), Rite Aid AI facial recognition ban (December 2023), and joint interagency AI statement with DOJ/CFPB/EEOC (April 2023). Published op-ed 'We Must Regulate A.I.' in New York Times. Estimated 15% p(doom) according to sources. Known for aggressive antitrust enforcement and authored influential 'Amazon's Antitrust Paradox' as law student.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for aggressive AI regulation using existing FTC authorities, has launched investigations into OpenAI, banned AI facial recognition at Rite Aid, and co-authored joint interagency statements emphasizing 'no AI exemption to laws on the books' with vigorous enforcement against AI-enabled fraud and discrimination.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation, Economic inequality

people.name: Alan Davidson
people.title: Assistant Secretary for Communications and Information, National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA), U.S. Department of Commerce
people.other_orgs: Former Google Policy Director, MIT alumnus
people.notes: Led NTIA's AI Accountability Policy Report (March 2024) calling for improved transparency, independent evaluations, and consequences for AI systems imposing unacceptable risks. Currently working on assessment of benefits and harms of open-weight AI models per Biden's AI Executive Order, with report due to White House in July 2024. Co-leads President's Task Force on Kids Online Health and Safety and oversees $42 billion broadband infrastructure programs.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation focused on accountability, transparency, and independent evaluations of high-risk AI systems. Supports building an ecosystem around AI accountability similar to financial auditing, with requirements for independent audits of highest-risk AI systems while promoting innovation and competition.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Cybersecurity

people.name: Laurie Locascio
people.title: President and CEO, American National Standards Institute (ANSI); Former Director, National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST); Former Under Secretary of Commerce for Standards and Techn
people.other_orgs: University of Maryland (former Vice President for Research), American Chemical Society (Fellow), American Institute for Medical and Biological Engineering (Fellow)
people.notes: As NIST Director (2022-2024), Locascio launched the U.S. AI Safety Institute in November 2023 and oversaw release of the AI Risk Management Framework in January 2023. She participated in roundtables on AI safety and testified before Congress on NIST's AI priorities, securing $50M in FY2025 budget for AI safety work. Now leads ANSI, coordinating private sector standards development including AI standards.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Locascio led implementation of Biden's AI Executive Order through NIST's AI Risk Management Framework and U.S. AI Safety Institute, emphasizing voluntary standards and collaboration with industry while building government capacity for AI oversight and safety evaluation.
people.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, Misinformation

people.name: Elham Tabassi
people.title: Associate Director for Emerging Technologies, Information Technology Laboratory, NIST; Chief AI Advisor and Chief Technologist of the AI Safety Institute
people.other_orgs: National AI Advisory Committee (administers), Federal AI Standards Coordinator, National AI Research Resource Task Force, Washington Academy of Sciences (Fellow), IEEE (Senior Member)
people.notes: Iranian-born, joined NIST in 1999 after coming to US in 1994 for master's in electrical engineering. Principal architect of NIST Fingerprint Image Quality standard used by FBI. Led development of AI Risk Management Framework 1.0 released January 2023 that laid groundwork for Biden's AI Executive Order. Named by Time Magazine as one of 100 most influential people in AI in 2024.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for voluntary, flexible frameworks like NIST's AI Risk Management Framework rather than prescriptive regulations. Emphasizes standards-based approach with measurement science and evaluation tools, supporting the Biden AI Executive Order's risk-based requirements while maintaining NIST's non-regulatory role.
people.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Economic inequality

people.name: Jonathan Kanter
people.title: Assistant Attorney General for the Antitrust Division, Department of Justice (2021-2024); Distinguished Teaching Professor, Washington University Law School
people.other_orgs: Washington University Law School, CNBC (Contributor), Former Federal Trade Commission Bureau of Competition attorney, Former boutique antitrust law firm founder
people.notes: Led DOJ Antitrust Division 2021-2024, confirmed by Senate 68-29. Delivered remarks at Stanford AI workshop stating DOJ would scrutinize AI sector 'with urgency' to prevent monopoly consolidation. Worked with FTC to divide AI oversight responsibilities - DOJ handles chip manufacturers while FTC oversees software companies. Appointed DOJ's first Chief Technologist to enhance AI expertise for investigations.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses specifically on antitrust enforcement in AI markets, examining potential monopolization, anti-competitive mergers, and market concentration. Stated DOJ is 'actively examining the AI ecosystem' and will enforce Sherman Act and Clayton Act against AI firms that violate antitrust laws.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality

people.name: Jen Easterly
people.title: Former Director, Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA)
people.other_orgs: CEO of RSA Conference (current), Former Morgan Stanley Global Head of Cybersecurity, Former NSA Deputy Director for Counterterrorism, Former NSC Senior Director for Counterterrorism
people.notes: Served as CISA Director from July 2021 to January 2025. While CISA's mandate is primarily cybersecurity, the agency had a supporting role in implementing parts of Biden's Executive Order 14110 on AI safety (Oct 2023, since rescinded by Trump's EO 14179 in Jan 2025). Frequently warns that AI represents 'the most powerful weapon of our time' and advocates for 'secure-by-design' AI development. Named CEO of RSA Conference in January 2026 after leaving CISA. Rhodes Scholar and 20-year Army veteran who helped establish US Cyber Command.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for urgent AI regulation with focus on cybersecurity and infrastructure protection. Supports mandatory cyber incident reporting within 24 hours and calls for legal frameworks to prevent AI weaponization while avoiding restrictions that handicap American AI companies versus China.
people.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Weapons, Misinformation

people.name: Stefano Mazzocchi
people.title: Chief AI Officer, Department of Commerce
people.notes: No information found in search results for a Stefano Mazzocchi serving as Chief AI Officer at the Department of Commerce. Search results show other individuals with similar names in different roles.

people.name: Margrethe Vestager
people.title: Former Executive Vice President, European Commission for A Europe Fit for the Digital Age; Former EU Commissioner for Competition
people.other_orgs: Chair of the Board of DTU (Technical University of Denmark), World Leader Fellow at Blavatnik School of Government Oxford University, Danish Social Liberal Party
people.notes: Led development of the EU AI Act (2021-2024) establishing world's first major AI legislation with risk-based approach. Known as aggressive antitrust enforcer against Big Tech companies including landmark cases against Google, Apple, Amazon, and Facebook. Served as EU Competition Commissioner 2014-2024 and Executive Vice President for Digital Age 2019-2024. Described by critics as implementing 'anti-American antitrust' policies that disproportionately target U.S. tech companies.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based AI regulation focusing on use cases rather than technology itself, with strict obligations for high-risk applications and prohibition of unacceptable uses like social scoring. Champions the EU AI Act as 'pro-innovation' while maintaining that trust-building through regulation is essential for AI adoption.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation, Democratic erosion

people.name: Thierry Breton
people.title: Former European Commissioner for Internal Market; EU AI Act Architect
people.other_orgs: Former CEO of Atos (2009-2019), Former France Minister of Economy/Finance/Industry (2005-2007), Former CEO of France Télécom
people.notes: Led the 38-hour 'trilogue' negotiations that finalized the EU AI Act in December 2023. Sanctioned by the US in December 2024 with travel ban and asset freeze for his role in EU tech regulation, specifically the Digital Services Act. Previously wrote prescient sci-fi novels in the 1980s about fake news and generative AI. Resigned from the European Commission in September 2024 amid tensions with Ursula von der Leyen.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Architected the EU AI Act, the world's first comprehensive AI regulation with strict bans on certain AI applications, transparency requirements for foundation models, and significant penalties up to €35 million. Promoted a risk-based approach with the motto 'regulating as little as possible, as much as needed.'
people.threat_models: Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Power concentration

people.name: Ted Chiang
people.title: Science Fiction Author; Stories include "Story of Your Life" (Arrival), "Exhalation"; Computer Science Background; AI Critic
people.other_orgs: Brown University (alumnus), The New Yorker (frequent contributor), Princeton University (guest lecturer), University of Notre Dame (artist in residence 2020-2021), Santa Fe Institute (Miller Scho
people.notes: Won four Hugo Awards, four Nebula Awards, and 2024 PEN/Malamud Award for short story excellence. Named one of Time's 100 Most Influential People in AI (2023). Wrote influential New Yorker essays including "Why A.I. Isn't Going to Make Art" and "ChatGPT Is a Blurry JPEG of the Web" arguing AI cannot create true art due to lack of intentional choice-making. Studied computer science at Brown University, providing technical foundation for his AI commentary.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Argues current AI hype is overblown and companies benefit from blurring distinctions between science fiction AI and reality. Believes generative AI will have "massive repercussions" primarily because companies will adopt it for cost-cutting before realizing its limitations, potentially destroying industries.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

people.name: Yuval Noah Harari
people.title: Historian & Author; Co-founder of Sapienship; Distinguished Research Fellow at Cambridge Centre for the Study of Existential Risk
people.other_orgs: Hebrew University of Jerusalem (Lecturer), University of Cambridge Centre for the Study of Existential Risk (Distinguished Research Fellow), Sapienship (Co-founder)
people.notes: Delivered major AI warnings at Davos 2026 WEF, predicting AI will create identity and immigration crises for every country. Warns that 'anything made of words will be taken over by AI' including laws, books, and religions. Has sold over 50 million books in 65 languages and regularly advises world leaders including Emmanuel Macron and Angela Merkel. Published 'Nexus: A Brief History of Information Networks from the Stone Age to AI' in 2024.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict safety standards and regulations on AI companies similar to pharmaceutical industry oversight, warns against granting AI legal personhood, and calls for immediate government action to prevent AI from gaining rights to start businesses or operate autonomously in financial systems.
people.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Economic inequality

people.name: Kate Crawford
people.title: Research Professor of Communication and STS, USC Annenberg; Senior Principal Researcher, Microsoft Research NYC; Visiting Chair for AI and Justice, École Normale Supérieure Paris; Author, Atlas o
people.other_orgs: Microsoft Research NYC, École Normale Supérieure Paris, AI Now Institute (co-founder, 2017)
people.notes: Co-authored influential 2018 paper 'Datasheets for Datasets' with Timnit Gebru that has been integrated into the EU AI Act. Her work 'Anatomy of an AI System' with Vladan Joler won the Beazley Design Award 2019 and is in MoMA's permanent collection. Testified before U.S. Senate Committee on the Judiciary in 2023 arguing that AI half-measures like transparency and bias audits are insufficient. Co-founded AI Now Institute in 2017 to explore policy approaches to AI's social consequences.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strong regulatory frameworks for AI systems, particularly algorithmic impact assessments for government use, trade secrets exceptions for high-stakes domains, and specialized regulatory agencies. Argues that value alignment alone is insufficient without institutional change and that AI systems are not neutral technologies requiring rigorous oversight.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

people.name: Cory Doctorow
people.title: Science Fiction Author, Activist & Journalist; Special Advisor, Electronic Frontier Foundation; Creator of 'Enshittification'
people.other_orgs: Electronic Frontier Foundation (Special Advisor), Boing Boing (Co-editor emeritus), Cornell University (A.D. White Professor-at-Large), Open Rights Group (Co-founder)
people.notes: Coined the viral term 'enshittification' to describe platform decay. Has upcoming book 'The Reverse Centaur's Guide to Life After AI' (Farrar, Straus and Giroux / Verso, June 23 2026) — title invokes the 'reverse centaur' figure forced by technology to work at an inhuman pace. Views AI as 'a collection of moderately useful tools being billed as a supernatural being' and argues the AI bubble will cause economic damage when it deflates. Warns that 'AI can't do your job, but an AI salesman can convince your boss to fire you and replace you with AI that can't do your job.'
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for aggressive antitrust enforcement against Big Tech monopolies, interoperability requirements, and tech worker unionization. Opposes broad AI regulation that serves incumbent interests, instead favoring targeted interventions to prevent AI-enabled worker displacement and corporate power consolidation.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Economic inequality

people.name: Naomi Klein
people.title: Author, Activist & Professor of Climate Justice at University of British Columbia; Co-Director, Centre for Climate Justice
people.other_orgs: AI Now Institute (board member), formerly 350.org (board member)
people.notes: Published 2023 Guardian piece 'AI machines aren't hallucinating, But their makers are' arguing AI development represents 'the largest theft in human history.' Describes AI as creating a 'three-legged stool' of tech, fossil fuels, and state power that provides a lifeline to fossil fuel industries. Warns AI undermines belief in reality itself through deepfakes and generated content.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Argues the current 'arms race' model of AI development is fundamentally incompatible with climate goals and sustainability. Calls for breaking up AI 'empires,' increasing transparency across AI supply chains, and redirecting development toward smaller, more specialized systems rather than massive build-outs.
people.threat_models: Environmental, Power concentration, Economic inequality

people.name: Brian Christian
people.title: Author, The Alignment Problem, The Most Human Human, Algorithms to Live By; DPhil student at Oxford
people.other_orgs: University of Oxford (DPhil student), CHAI affiliate, CITRIS Policy Lab visiting scholar, Simons Institute (former scientific communicator in residence), UC Berkeley
people.notes: Won 2022 National Academies Eric and Wendy Schmidt Awards for Excellence in Science Communications. Currently pursuing DPhil at Oxford under Chris Summerfield focusing on cognitive models of human decision-making for AI alignment. His book 'The Alignment Problem' was called 'the one book to read on AI' by The New York Times and bridges technical AI safety with present-day algorithmic bias issues.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for transparency in AI systems, public access to training datasets, and robust legal frameworks for algorithmic accountability. Emphasizes the need for governance structures that ensure diverse stakeholders have input in AI development decisions.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control

people.name: Shalini Kantayya
people.title: Documentary Filmmaker; Director, Coded Bias (Netflix) and TikTok, Boom (Sundance)
people.other_orgs: TED Fellow, Lavin Agency (Keynote Speaker), Stanford HAI (Panelist)
people.notes: Her 2020 Sundance documentary 'Coded Bias' follows MIT researcher Joy Buolamwini's discovery of racial bias in facial recognition systems and advocates for algorithmic justice. The film was broadcast on PBS Independent Lens and globally on Netflix, winning Best Director at Social Impact Media Awards. Her work focuses on how AI systems perpetuate inequality and erode civil rights, particularly impacting marginalized communities through biased hiring, healthcare, and criminal justice algorithms.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI regulation including an 'FDA for algorithms' to test systems for accuracy and bias before deployment, meaningful legislation around algorithmic transparency, and laws that balance Big Tech's power over democratic processes.
people.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

people.name: David Autor
people.title: Ford Professor of Economics, MIT; Co-Director, MIT Shaping the Future of Work Initiative; Co-Director, NBER Labor Studies Program
people.other_orgs: National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER), MIT Work of the Future Task Force, JPAL Work of the Future experimental initiative, Stone Center on Inequality and Shaping the Future of Work
people.notes: Co-authored influential 2026 Hamilton Project report 'Building Pro-Worker Artificial Intelligence' defining framework for AI that augments rather than replaces human expertise. Served on National Academy committee for 2024 report 'Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work.' Recognized as 'academic voice of the American worker' by Economist magazine and received 2019 Carnegie Fellowship for work on globalization and technology impacts on labor markets.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive government intervention to steer AI development toward 'pro-worker' applications through targeted sectoral policies, especially in healthcare and education, tax code reforms to reduce bias toward automation, and stronger safety nets like wage insurance for displaced workers.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

people.name: Lawrence Katz
people.title: Elisabeth Allison Professor of Economics, Harvard University; Editor, Quarterly Journal of Economics
people.other_orgs: National Bureau of Economic Research (Research Associate), J-PAL North America (Co-Founder), MDRC (Board Member), Former Chief Economist U.S. Department of Labor (1993-94)
people.notes: Leading labor economist studying AI's impact on employment through the 'Race Between Education and Technology' framework. His research shows AI may substitute for entry-level workers while complementing experienced workers with tacit knowledge. Named 2025 Citation Laureate alongside David Autor for influential work on wages, inequality, and technological change. Co-authored influential book 'The Race between Education and Technology' (2008) with Claudia Goldin documenting how educational expansion affects inequality.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted policies to address AI-driven labor displacement, drawing parallels to past trade adjustment programs. Supports strengthening worker bargaining power and robust safety nets, while emphasizing the need for high-quality sectoral training programs to help displaced workers transition to new roles.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality

people.name: Joseph Stiglitz
people.title: University Professor, Columbia University; Nobel Prize Economics 2001; Chief Economist, Roosevelt Institute; Co-Chair, Independent Commission for the Reform of International Corporate Taxation
people.other_orgs: Roosevelt Institute, Independent Commission for the Reform of International Corporate Taxation (ICRICT), Initiative for Policy Dialogue (Columbia), Pontifical Academy of Social Sciences, National
people.notes: Warns of AI bubble that could burst while simultaneously displacing workers, creating worst-case scenario without proper retraining infrastructure. Distinguishes between 'AI' (replacement) and 'IA' (intelligence assisting), advocating for latter approach. Signed Global Call for AI Red Lines in 2026. Predicts AI will primarily impact routine white-collar jobs while augmenting rather than replacing roles in education, healthcare, and skilled trades.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for government regulation to prevent AI from exacerbating inequality and undermining information quality, while supporting AI as a tool for augmenting human capabilities. Calls for compensation mechanisms for news industry affected by AI scraping and international red lines for dangerous AI applications.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Misinformation

people.title: Geoffrey Taber Chair in Entrepreneurship and Innovation and Professor of Strategic Management, Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto; Founder, Creative Destruction Lab; Co-author, Pr
people.primary_org: Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto
people.other_orgs: Stanford Digital Economy Lab (Research Fellow), National Bureau of Economic Research (Research Associate), Vector Institute for Artificial Intelligence (Faculty Affiliate), Brookings Center on Re
people.notes: Co-authored influential books 'Prediction Machines' (2018) and 'Power and Prediction' (2022) framing AI as primarily a prediction technology with economic implications. Founded Creative Destruction Lab, one of the world's largest seed-stage programs for science-based companies with over 600 startups admitted in 2022. Member of the Order of Canada (2022) and recipient of University of Toronto's President's Impact Award (2023). Advocates for viewing AI through economic lens of falling prediction costs rather than focusing on AGI scenarios.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for an FDA-like regulatory approval process for AI applications in already-regulated sectors (insurance, healthcare) to provide clarity and accelerate investment, while allowing market forces to govern areas not currently regulated by humans. Supports applying existing regulations to AI rather than creating new broad frameworks.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality

people.name: Joshua Gans
people.title: Professor of Strategic Management, Rotman School; Jeffrey S. Skoll Chair of Technical Innovation and Entrepreneurship; Co-author, Prediction Machines and Power and Prediction
people.primary_org: Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto
people.other_orgs: NBER (Research Associate), Creative Destruction Lab (Chief Economist)
people.notes: Published multiple NBER working papers on AI regulation including 'Can Socially-Minded Governance Control the AGI Beast?' (2024) concluding that socially-minded entities cannot minimize harm from unrestricted AGI products. Provides expert witness testimony in antitrust cases and has equity/advisory relationships with startup firms. Argues that ASI is unlikely to kill humans because we find AI economically valuable and wouldn't threaten its existence.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Argues that ex post regulatory instruments like liability regimes outperform ex ante restrictions in many scenarios. Advocates for acceleration of AI adoption to surface and react to potential harms more quickly through 'learning by doing' rather than delayed adoption until harms are fully understood.

people.name: Daniel Susskind
people.title: Research Professor in Economics, King's College London; Senior Research Associate, Institute for Ethics in AI, Oxford University; Author of 'A World Without Work' and 'Growth: A Reckoning'
people.other_orgs: Stanford Digital Economy Lab (Digital Fellow), Gresham College (Gresham Professor of Business), Balliol College Oxford (former Fellow), Cabinet Office (former Senior Policy Adviser), 10 Downing S
people.notes: Co-authored 'The Future of the Professions' (2015) with father Richard Susskind examining technology's impact on professional work. His 2026 paper 'What Will Remain for People to Do?' explores which jobs might survive AGI, identifying 'general equilibrium limits', 'preference limits', and 'moral limits'. Previously worked in British government including Prime Minister's Strategy Unit and Cabinet Office. His TED Talk on the future of work has over 1.5 million views.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for thoughtful policy responses to AI's labor market impacts, including consideration of Universal Basic Income and Job Guarantee Schemes. Emphasizes need for governments to prepare for AI-driven economic transformation rather than restrictive regulation of AI development itself.

people.name: Fiona Scott Morton
people.title: Theodore Nierenberg Professor of Economics, Yale School of Management; Former DOJ Chief Economist (2011-12)
people.other_orgs: Bruegel (former research fellow), Thurman Arnold Project at Yale (founder/director), Charles River Associates (consultant), National Bureau of Economic Research

Warns that AI monopolists could create scenario where wages fall to zero while goods remain expensive. Has consulted for Microsoft on competition topics unrelated to AI and regularly advises governments on confidential antitrust cases.

Supports pro-competitive regulation like interoperability requirements and open-source model promotion, while opposing broad bans in favor of targeted interventions.

people.name: Betsey Stevenson
people.title: Professor of Public Policy and Economics, University of Michigan; Former Member, Council of Economic Advisers (2013-2015); Former Chief Economist, U.S. Department of Labor (2010-2011)
people.other_orgs: National Bureau of Economic Research (Faculty Research Associate), Centre for Economic Policy Research (Research Fellow), Ifo Institute for Economic Research (Fellow), National Academy of Social
people.notes: Author of 'What's There to Fear in a World with Transformative AI? With the Right Policy, Nothing' (2025), proposing three key policy areas: managing job transformation speed, implementing digital dividends as returns on collective data contributions, and building social trust. people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for managing the speed and process of AI adoption through policy interventions like shifting taxes from labor to consumption/capital, implementing digital dividends, and ensuring competitive markets, while avoiding stopping AI development entirely.

people.other_orgs: National Bureau of Economic Research (Research Associate), Anthropic Economic Advisory Council, American Antitrust Institute, US Department of Justice Antitrust Division (2022-2024)

Co-authored economic modeling work on AI's wage and output impacts with complementarity between physical and intelligence sectors.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted regulation addressing AI's labor market impacts through safety nets and competition policy, proposing a two-tier system with AI Adjustment Insurance and Digital Dividend rather than broad AI restrictions.

people.name: Ramin Toloui
people.title: Distinguished Policy Fellow, Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research (SIEPR); Former Assistant Secretary of State for Economic and Business Affairs

people.notes: Served as Chief Economist at DOJ Antitrust Division 2022-2024, focusing on AI and tech competition policy. Currently advises World Bank on AI implications for developing countries. people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive competition policy and interoperability requirements for AI platforms, emphasizing ex-ante regulation for systemically significant digital enterprises. people.name: Jason Furman
people.title: Aetna Professor of the Practice of Economic Policy at Harvard Kennedy School and Harvard Economics Department; Former Chairman of Council of Economic Advisers (2013-2017)
people.other_orgs: Peterson Institute for International Economics (nonresident senior fellow), Council on Foreign Relations, Group of Thirty, Russell Sage Foundation (Trustee), Washington Center for Equitable Growt
people.notes: Served as Obama's chief economist and played major role in economic policies 2013-2017. Co-authored 'AI and the Economy' (NBER 2018) and gave influential 2016 speech 'Is This Time Different?' arguing AI resembles previous technological advances. Currently consults for OpenAI and chaired UK's Digital Competition Expert Panel, leading to establishment of Digital Markets Unit. Advocates six principles for AI regulation including application-specific approaches and comparing AI performance to imperfect humans rather than perfection.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for application-specific AI regulation through existing agencies rather than federal-level blanket rules, emphasizing competition policy and merger enforcement over broad restrictions. Supports pro-competitive regulation including codes of conduct for dominant platforms and data mobility requirements.

people.name: Avi Goldfarb

people.other_orgs: National Bureau of Economic Research (Research Associate), Creative Destruction Lab (Chief Data Scientist), Acceleration Consortium (Research Lead)
people.notes: Goldfarb testified before the U.S. Senate Judiciary Committee in May 2019 on digital advertising and data privacy, arguing that privacy regulation often creates trade-offs with innovation and competition. His research with Catherine Tucker found European privacy regulations reduced online advertising effectiveness by 65%. He co-authored influential books 'Prediction Machines' (2018) and 'Power and Prediction' that frame AI economically as cheap prediction technology, widely cited in policy discussions.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Goldfarb advocates for careful regulatory design that balances innovation with legitimate concerns, emphasizing that privacy regulations often reduce innovation and that regulatory consistency is crucial. He argues against broad AI research pauses and supports targeted approaches that mitigate negative consequences without stifling technological progress.

people.notes: Testified before U.S. Senate AI Insight Forum arguing against regulating AI technology itself while supporting application-specific regulations. Coined the term 'agentic AI' in 2024 and has been vocal about dismissing AI extinction risks as implausible while supporting responsible AI development.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for regulating AI applications rather than the underlying technology, opposing broad model-level regulations like California's SB 1047 and supporting targeted regulations for specific harmful uses like non-consensual deepfakes. Supports federal preemption of state-level AI regulations and opposes compute threshold-based reporting requirements.

people.notes: Co-authored 'How AI Destroys Institutions' arguing AI systems erode democratic institutions through isolation, automation, and removal of human decision-making. people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly supports First Amendment protection for AI output and opposes broad content restrictions. Generally skeptical of government regulation that would restrict AI expression.

Featured on Oprah's AI special (2024). Former head of UX at Mozilla Labs, founded multiple companies including Humanized (acquired by Mozilla), Songza, and Massive Health (acquired by Jawbone). Named FastCompany Master of Design and Forbes 30-under-30.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for comprehensive AI regulation including liability frameworks, whistleblower protections, and strict limits on engagement-based business models for AI companions targeting children. Endorsed the AI LEAD Act (Senators Durbin and Hawley) creating liability for defective AI products that cause harm. Explicitly states that self-regulation 'cannot work' and calls for coordinated international action similar to nuclear test ban treaties. Advocates for mandatory transparency requirements and audited safety assessments before deployment.
people.threat_models: Existential risk (warns of potential human extinction), Loss of control (AI systems scheming against human oversight), Power concentration (race for AI dominance leading to authoritarian entrenchment), Democratic erosion (undermining shared sense of reality), Misinformation (deep fakes making humans unable to distinguish real from fake by 2024 election), Economic inequality (massive job displacement without adequate preparation), Environmental (rising energy costs from AI training), Cybersecurity (AI-enabled attacks), Bias/discrimination (algorithmic bias in deployment)

people.name: Amy Klobuchar
people.title: U.S. Senator from Minnesota, Chair of Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on Competition Policy, Antitrust, and Consumer Rights
people.other_orgs: Former Hennepin County Attorney (1999-2007), Former Chair of Senate Rules Committee (2021-2023), Former partner at Dorsey & Whitney and Gray Plant Mooty law firms
people.notes: Received Ukrainian Order of Merit, First Class from President Zelenskyy (2022). Author of 'Antitrust' (New York Times Best Seller). Graduated magna cum laude from Yale University (1982) and University of Chicago Law School with high honors (1985). Led bipartisan effort that resulted in first major U.S. AI harm legislation with Take It Down Act passage. Chair of Senate Rules Committee oversaw three bipartisan AI election bills passage in May 2024. Running for Minnesota Governor in 2026 election.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Champions targeted AI regulations focused on specific harms rather than broad restrictions. Lead sponsor of the NO FAKES Act protecting voice and likeness from unauthorized AI replicas, and the Take It Down Act criminalizing non-consensual AI-generated intimate imagery (signed into law 2025). Co-sponsored the Artificial Intelligence Research, Innovation, and Accountability Act requiring self-certification for 'critical-impact' AI systems and transparency reports. Has introduced three bipartisan election-related AI bills: banning deceptive AI deepfakes of federal candidates, requiring disclaimers on AI-generated political ads, and directing election administrators on AI guidelines.
people.threat_models: Misinformation (AI deepfakes in elections undermining democracy), Non-consensual intimate imagery (AI-generated explicit content harming victims, especially children), Voice cloning scams (fraudulent calls targeting military families and elderly), Democratic erosion (fake political ads and robocalls where voters cannot distinguish real candidates), Copyright/IP (unauthorized use of artists' voices and likenesses), Consumer protection (need for transparency in high-risk AI applications)

people.name: Ron Wyden
people.title: U.S. Senator from Oregon (Democrat), Ranking Member of Senate Finance Committee, Senior Member of Senate Intelligence Committee
people.other_orgs: Senate Committee on Finance (Ranking Member), Senate Select Committee on Intelligence, Senate Committee on Budget, Senate Committee on Energy and Natural Resources, Joint Committee on Taxation (L
people.notes: Long-serving Senator since 1996, previously served in House 1981-1996. Co-founded Oregon Gray Panthers advocacy group and served as director of Oregon Legal Services for the Elderly (1977-1979). Holds JD from University of Oregon School of Law (1974) and BA from Stanford University. Known as leading privacy advocate who has held over 1,080 town hall meetings across Oregon's 36 counties annually. Has been warning for nearly a decade about government purchase of commercial surveillance data, calling it 'just as sensitive as information the government collects directly.' Currently fighting Trump administration's ban on Anthropic, vowing to 'pull out all the stops' and seek bipartisan support.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation focused on preventing government surveillance abuse and protecting civil liberties. Co-sponsored the Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2022 requiring transparency and accountability for automated decision systems in critical areas like housing, employment, and education. Has introduced bipartisan legislation like the POST ID Act to combat AI-enabled identity fraud through USPS verification services. Strongly opposes government use of AI for mass surveillance of Americans, as evidenced by his criticism of Pentagon's dispute with Anthropic and introduction of the Fourth Amendment's Not For Sale Act to prevent government purchase of commercial data for surveillance purposes.
people.threat_models: Mass surveillance and government overreach, Privacy violations through commercial data aggregation, Identity fraud and deepfakes, Bias and discrimination in automated decision-making systems affecting housing/employment/education, Democratic erosion through authoritarian surveillance, Civil liberties violations through facial recognition and biometric tracking. Specifically warned that 'AI's ability to turn disparate pieces of public or commercial data into highly revealing profiles of Americans' represents a 'chilling expansion of mass surveillance.' Has consistently fought against ICE and CBP use of facial recognition technology.

people.name: Marco Rubio
people.title: U.S. Secretary of State (2025-present), Acting National Security Advisor (February-August 2025), Former U.S. Senator from Florida (2011-2025)
people.other_orgs: Florida International University (adjunct professor), Vice Chair of Senate Intelligence Committee (2021-2025), Senate Foreign Relations Committee, Senate Small Business and Entrepreneurship Commi
people.notes: First Latino to serve as U.S. Secretary of State, unanimously confirmed January 25, 2025. Served as Vice Chair of Senate Intelligence Committee (2021-2025) with focus on AI national security and U.S.-China competition. Described as 'virtual secretary of state for Latin America' during Trump's first term. Banned from entering China due to sanctions imposed twice in 2020. JD degree from University of Miami (1996), BA from University of Florida (1993).
people.regulatory_stance_detail: As Secretary of State, Rubio is implementing Trump's AI Action Plan which emphasizes removing 'red tape' and 'onerous' AI regulations while threatening to withhold federal funding from states with 'burdensome AI regulations.' The framework prioritizes preempting state AI laws and focuses on protecting children and preventing censorship rather than comprehensive safety measures. Trump stated at the July 2025 AI summit that 'you can't have three or four states holding you up' and called for 'a single federal standard' - a position Rubio is helping implement.
people.threat_models: National security, Misinformation, Power concentration, AI-enabled human takeover scenarios. As Secretary of State, Rubio has dealt directly with AI security threats including being targeted by an AI deepfake impersonation that sent messages to foreign ministers and U.S. officials via Signal in July 2025. The Trump administration's AI framework emphasizes protecting children from AI chatbot harms, preventing electricity cost increases from AI infrastructure, and countering 'partisan bias' in AI systems. Rubio's focus appears primarily on geopolitical competition with China and preventing authoritarian uses of AI.

people.name: Andrej Karpathy
people.title: AI Researcher, Educator & Founder of Eureka Labs
people.other_orgs: Former OpenAI founding member (2015-2017), Former Tesla AI Director (2017-2022), Stanford University (CS231n instructor), Former Google Brain & DeepMind intern
people.notes: Founded Eureka Labs (2024) focused on AI-native education after leaving Tesla where he directed Autopilot AI development (2017-2022). PhD from Stanford (2016) under Fei-Fei Li, designed influential CS231n deep learning course. Founding member of OpenAI (2015-2017). Created viral 'Neural Networks: Zero to Hero' YouTube series and educational content reaching millions. Has 500K+ Twitter followers and is considered one of the most influential AI educators globally.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful, measured approach to AI development rather than aggressive regulation. Has expressed concern about o1-style reinforcement learning making systems less interpretable and harder to align, suggesting need for targeted technical safety measures. Emphasizes that current AI systems have significant 'cognitive deficits' and warns against premature deployment of unreliable AI agents. Focuses on technical solutions and engineering discipline rather than broad regulatory frameworks.
people.threat_models: Loss of control (warns about o1-style RL making systems less interpretable and promoting 'subtle styles of self-prompting'), Technical safety (emphasizes AI agents 'just don't work yet' due to lack of memory, planning, and reliability issues), Alignment challenges (concerned about reinforcement learning paradigm requiring 'exact value specification' to avert catastrophic outcomes)

people.name: Palmer Luckey
people.title: Founder and CEO
people.other_orgs: Former Founder of Oculus VR (sold to Meta/Facebook 2014), Former engineer at USC Institute for Creative Technologies Mixed Reality Lab, Founder of ModRetro Forums
people.notes: Founded Oculus VR at age 19 in 2012, sold to Facebook for $2 billion in 2014. Fired from Facebook in 2017 after donating to a pro-Trump political group; received $100M+ severance. Founded Anduril Industries in 2017; the company raised $2.5B at a $30.5B valuation in June 2025 (Founders Fund-led, $1B check — its largest ever) and in March 2026 was raising a new round led by Thrive Capital and Andreessen Horowitz targeting a ~$60B valuation. Anduril has secured major Pentagon contracts including $967M for Advanced Battle Management Systems (2020), $1B counter-UAS contract for SOCOM (2022), and a 10-year U.S. Army enterprise contract in March 2026 (ceiling up to $20B). Received Pepperdine University's inaugural President's Award for Excellence in Freedom (2024). Net worth estimated at $3.5B by Forbes (2026).
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates that AI governance should remain with elected government officials rather than corporate executives, stating 'We need to stick to a position that this is in the hands of the people.' Opposes corporate restrictions on military AI use, arguing that companies like Anthropic refusing Pentagon access creates dangerous precedents where 'corporate executives have de facto control over U.S. foreign policy.' Warns against UN efforts by adversaries to 'trick Western countries into not applying AI for weapons or defense.' Believes the question isn't whether to use AI in warfare but how to deploy it responsibly under democratic oversight.
people.threat_models: National security risks from AI restrictions, Power concentration in corporate hands over military policy, Weapons proliferation by adversaries. Argues the primary threat comes from 'evil people' using AI while Western democracies artificially constrain themselves. Warns that China, Russia, and Iran already have or will have advanced AI capabilities, making Western self-limitation strategically dangerous. Believes the greater risk is using 'inferior technology' in life-and-death situations rather than applying the best available AI to minimize civilian casualties and collateral damage.

people.name: Bill Gates
people.title: Co-chair, Gates Foundation; Co-founder, Microsoft; Founder, Breakthrough Energy
people.other_orgs: Breakthrough Energy, Gates Ventures, Berkshire Hathaway (board member 2004-present), Cascade Investment, Giving Pledge co-founder
people.notes: Gates Foundation portfolio exceeds $41.8 billion with 25.55% allocated to Microsoft, reflecting concentrated bet on AI infrastructure. Led $11.8 billion OpenAI investment through Microsoft. Portfolio includes Berkshire Hathaway (26%), Waste Management (19%), and Canadian National Railway (15%). In Q3 2025, sold $8.67 billion in Microsoft shares for portfolio rebalancing. Co-founded Giving Pledge in 2010 with Warren Buffett. Awarded Presidential Medal of Freedom (2016), Padma Bhushan (2015), and Honorary Knight Commander of the British Empire (2005).
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted regulation focused on specific AI risks, particularly bioterrorism and malicious use. Has warned that 'an even greater risk than a naturally caused pandemic is that a non-government group will use open source AI tools to design a bioterrorism weapon.' Supports proactive governance and regulatory pressure on companies to restrict harmful content generation, while maintaining heavy investment in AI infrastructure through Microsoft and other portfolio companies. Parallels his approach to his 2015 pandemic warning, calling for deliberate action before risks become unmanageable.
people.threat_models: Bioterrorism (warns AI could accelerate pathogen design by malicious actors), Labor displacement (predicts 5-year transition period with disruption in software development and custodial roles), Job market disruption (warns impact will grow significantly over next five years). Has stated 'there is no upper limit' to AI capabilities and it will surpass human levels without hitting a plateau. Concerned about complacency when AGI deadlines are missed creating false security.

people.name: Laurene Powell Jobs
people.title: Founder and President, Emerson Collective
people.other_orgs: Board member of Ford Foundation, Council on Foreign Relations, Chicago CRED, College Track, and Elemental Impact; Lead investor and chair of The Atlantic; Co-founder and chair of XQ Institute; In
people.notes: Widow of Apple co-founder Steve Jobs with estimated net worth of $11.5 billion (Bloomberg Billionaires Index). Founded Emerson Collective in 2011, which has invested in at least nine AI startups since 2022 with funding rounds totaling over $1 billion. Early investor and founding backer of Jony Ive and Sam Altman's AI hardware device venture (io/OpenAI partnership). Earned BA in political science and BS in economics from University of Pennsylvania (1985), MBA from Stanford (1991). Received Service to America Medal Spirit of Service Award (2021) and honorary doctorate from Chiba Institute of Technology (May 2025). Previously worked at Goldman Sachs and Merrill Lynch before co-founding natural foods company Terravera in 1990s.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Through her investments and public statements, advocates for AI development that serves humanity while addressing specific harms. Supports 'responsible adoption' approaches and has invested in AI safety organizations and companies focused on beneficial applications. Publicly expressed concerns about technology's 'dark uses' and believes 'humanity deserves better' from technology, but continues backing AI development through strategic investments rather than supporting broad restrictions. Partner Sarah Pinto from Emerson Collective participates in AI governance conferences, indicating engagement with policy frameworks.
people.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Mental health impacts on youth. In June 2025 interview, explicitly stated concerns about technology's 'dark uses' and cited 'studies being done on teenage girls and on anxiety in young people, and the rise of mental health needs' as evidence that 'we've gone sideways.' Believes current technology has failed to adequately serve human wellbeing, particularly regarding mental health impacts on young people.

people.name: Naval Ravikant
people.title: Co-founder and Chairman, AngelList
people.other_orgs: Angel investor in 200+ companies including Twitter, Uber, Notion, Postmates; Former co-founder of Epinions; Podcast host
people.notes: Co-founded AngelList (2010) which has facilitated over $127M in annual deal flow. Angel investor in 200+ companies including early investments in Twitter and Uber. Previously co-founded Epinions (1999) and sued Benchmark Capital in high-profile case. Author of viral 'How to Get Rich (Without Getting Lucky)' tweetstorm (2018). Born in New Delhi (1974), graduated from Stuyvesant High School and Dartmouth College.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates against overregulation of AI, warning that excessive regulation could 'kill AI before it even takes off.' Supports the Trump administration's focus on AI opportunity rather than AI safety restrictions. Believes in market-driven solutions and opposes regulatory capture that favors large companies over startups. Emphasizes that America must maintain AI leadership through support rather than restriction.
people.threat_models: Power concentration (warns against small number of people controlling AI 'for our own good'), Economic inequality (AI may increase gap between rich and poor before democratizing), Regulatory capture (large companies using regulation to stifle competition). Believes humans should focus on creative work while machines handle bounded, rule-based tasks.

people.name: Chris Smalls
people.title: President (Former)
people.other_orgs: The Congress of Essential Workers (founder), Mozilla Rise25 honouree (2023), Gaza Freedom Flotilla activist
people.notes: Founded first successful Amazon warehouse union in U.S. history (ALU, 2021-2024) after being fired for organizing COVID-19 safety walkout in March 2020. Named Time Magazine's 100 Most Influential People (2022). Served as ALU president until July 2024 when replaced by Connor Spence. Participated in Gaza Freedom Flotilla (July 2024), physically assaulted by Israeli forces during ship interception. Born July 4, 1988, former rapper who toured with Meek Mill before warehouse work.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for strict federal regulation of AI to prevent mass job displacement, warning that 'AI is going to wipe out 50% of American jobs overnight' within five years. Demands union contracts include language protecting workers from automation and calls for federal laws preserving human jobs over AI replacement. Supports the AFL-CIO's 'Workers First Initiative on AI' calling for transparency, human oversight, and prohibition of AI surveillance tools for union-busting.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion. Specifically warns that Amazon's AI-powered robots and automation will inevitably eliminate warehouse jobs despite company denials. Views AI as existential threat to working class, stating tech workers need 'moral compass conversation' about replacing human labor. Connects AI acceleration to broader corporate power concentration threatening worker rights and democratic institutions.

people.name: Kashmir Hill
people.title: Technology Reporter specializing in privacy, surveillance, and AI
people.other_orgs: Former Senior Writer at Gizmodo Media Group (2017-2019), Former Senior Editor at Fusion (2014-2017), Former Senior Online Editor at Forbes Magazine (2010-2014)
people.notes: Technology reporter at The New York Times since July 2019, specializing in privacy and surveillance. Author of 'Your Face Belongs to Us: A Secretive Startup's Quest to End Privacy as We Know It' investigating Clearview AI. Broke major story in January 2020 exposing Clearview AI's existence and police client base. Has extensively covered 'AI psychosis' phenomenon and people developing romantic relationships with chatbots. Conducted experiment in 2024 letting AI make all her decisions for a week. Previously worked at Gizmodo, Fusion, and Forbes covering technology and privacy issues. Graduate of Duke University and New York University journalism program.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Hill's reporting demonstrates support for targeted AI regulation focused on privacy protection and corporate accountability. Her work consistently exposes harmful applications of AI technology, particularly facial recognition, while acknowledging beneficial uses. She advocates for transparency requirements and constraints on commercial surveillance applications, as evidenced by her extensive reporting on Clearview AI and facial recognition bans. Her coverage suggests support for government intervention where privacy rights are threatened, comparing it to environmental regulation where 'bigger interventions from the government' are needed to protect privacy.
people.threat_models: Privacy violations through facial recognition and surveillance technology, AI-induced mental health crises including 'AI psychosis' and addiction to chatbots, Power concentration among tech companies with insufficient oversight, Bias and discrimination in automated systems particularly affecting Black individuals in wrongful arrests, Loss of human agency and social connection as people increasingly rely on AI for decision-making and relationships

people.name: Walter Isaacson
people.title: Author, Biographer & Professor of History
people.other_orgs: Perella Weinberg Partners (Advisory Partner), Tulane University (Professor of History), Former Aspen Institute President & CEO (2003-2018), Former CNN Chairman & CEO (2001-2003), Former Time Maga
people.notes: Bestselling biographer of major tech figures including Steve Jobs (2011), Leonardo da Vinci (2017), Jennifer Doudna (2021), and Elon Musk (2023). Rhodes Scholar and Harvard graduate who served as Time Magazine editor and CNN chairman. Received National Humanities Medal from President Biden in 2023. Currently teaches 'The Digital Revolution' course at Tulane University and co-chairs the New Orleans Book Festival.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced approach to AI development that prioritizes human creativity and judgment alongside technological advancement. Emphasizes need for improved government tracking of AI's economic impacts and supports regulatory frameworks that ensure AI augments rather than replaces human capabilities. Has called for focus on AI safety research while rejecting both utopian and dystopian extremes.
people.threat_models: Labor displacement (particularly white-collar jobs, though believes AI creates more jobs than it eliminates), Loss of human agency and purpose (warns against over-reliance on AI that could diminish individual thinking), Existential risk (has written extensively about Musk's concerns regarding AI safety and potential species-level threats), Economic inequality (concerned about velocity of skills transformation outpacing adaptation)

people.name: Kevin Kelly
people.title: Senior Maverick at Wired Magazine, Founding Executive Editor, Technology Author and Futurist
people.other_orgs: Co-chair of The Long Now Foundation, Cool Tools website founder and editor, former publisher and editor of Whole Earth Review (1984-1990), co-founder of Hackers' Conference, involved in launch of
