people.notes: Author of multiple bestselling technology books including 'The Inevitable' (2016), 'What Technology Wants' (2010), and 'Excellent Advice for Living' (2023). Co-founded Wired magazine in 1993 and served as executive editor for seven years, during which the magazine won two National Magazine Awards. Maintains active Substack newsletter with detailed analysis of AI development and publishes Cool Tools website with 1 million monthly visitors. Known for radical technological optimism and long-term perspective on technological change.
people.regulatory_stance_detail: Argues against premature AI regulation based on imagined rather than actual harms, stating that 'attempts to regulate AI before it is even operational' are 'unlikely to work because we simply don't know what harms (and what benefits) AI and robots will really bring.' Advocates for evidence-based policy rather than regulation driven by dystopian fiction expectations. Believes we have time to develop appropriate governance as AI adoption will be slower than anticipated.
people.threat_models: Emphasizes that imagined harms from AI are 'astronomical' while real harms are 'almost nil' as of 2024, with only several hundred people losing jobs to AI (mostly translators and help-desk operators). Concerned about premature regulation based on fictional dystopian scenarios rather than evidence. Warns against 'doomer' mentality that promotes extreme versions of AI risk.

people.name: Peter Norvig
people.title: Distinguished Education Fellow
people.notes: Peter Norvig is a Distinguished Education Fellow at Stanford's Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence Institute and a researcher at Google Inc. He co-authored "Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach" with Stuart Russell, which is the leading textbook in the field used in more than 1,500 universities in 135 countries. He was born on December 14, 1956, and previously served as head of NASA Ames's Computational Sciences Division where he was NASA's senior computer scientist. He co-taught an online Artificial Intelligence class that signed up 160,000 students, helping to kick off massive open online classes.

people.name: Michael Genesereth
people.title: Professor of Computer Science
people.notes: Michael Genesereth (born 1948) is an American logician and computer scientist, who is most known for his work on computational logic and applications of that work in enterprise management, computational law, and general game playing. Genesereth is professor in the Computer Science Department at Stanford University and a professor by courtesy in the Stanford Law School. His 1987 textbook on Logical Foundations of Artificial Intelligence remains one of the key references on symbolic artificial intelligence. He is the author of the influential Game Description Language(GDL) and Knowledge Interchange Format(KIF), the latter of which led to the ISO Common Logic standard.

people.name: Tom Cotton
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-AR)
people.notes: Tom Cotton is a U.S. Senator representing Arkansas who introduced the DATA Act of 2026, which would allow AI data center companies to bypass federal electricity regulations by building independent energy infrastructure. Cotton stated that "American dominance in artificial intelligence and other crucial emerging industries should not come at the expense of Arkansans paying higher energy costs". On November 13, 2024, Senate Republicans selected Cotton as the next conference chair, making him the third-highest ranked Republican in the Senate. Cotton previously co-introduced a resolution with Senator Chris Coons to affirm America's artificial intelligence dominance.

people.name: Andy Kim
people.title: U.S. Senator (D-NJ)
people.notes: Andy Kim is a U.S. Senator from New Jersey who serves as a Democrat on multiple committees including Commerce, Science, and Transportation. He has been actively involved in AI-related legislation, co-introducing the Artificial Intelligence Ready Data Act with Senator Ted Budd to open government data sets for training American AI models. Kim also co-introduced the AI Talent Act with Senator Jon Husted to create an AI and tech talent team within the U.S. Office of Personnel Management. He was born on July 12, 1982, in Boston, Massachusetts, to South Korean immigrant parents and is the first Korean American elected to the U.S. Senate.

people.name: Jim Risch
people.title: U.S. Senator (Idaho); Chairman, Senate Foreign Relations Committee
people.notes: Jim Risch is a U.S. Senator from Idaho who has served since 2009 and is a member of the Republican Party. He serves on five key committees: Energy and Natural Resources, Foreign Relations, Small Business and Entrepreneurship, Intelligence, and Ethics, and serves as Chairman of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. He previously served as Idaho's 31st Governor in 2006 and as Lieutenant Governor, and achieved major tax reform as Governor, cutting property taxes by 20%. He is consistently recognized as one of the most conservative members of the U.S. Senate.

people.name: Ben Horowitz
people.title: Co-founder and General Partner
people.notes: Ben Horowitz is a cofounder and general partner at the venture capital firm Andreessen Horowitz. He previously co-founded and served as president and chief executive officer of the enterprise software company Opsware, which Hewlett-Packard acquired in 2007. Andreessen Horowitz recently raised more than $15 billion across five new funds, with Horowitz stating that at this moment of profound technological opportunity, it is fundamentally important for humanity that America wins. He has authored books including The Hard Thing About Hard Things: Building a Business When There Are No Easy Answers.

people.name: Donald Trump
people.title: 45th and 47th President of the United States (2017–2021, 2025–present)
people.notes: Donald Trump served as the 45th President of the United States from 2017 to 2021 and was inaugurated as the 47th president on January 20, 2025. On March 20, 2026, the Trump Administration released a National AI Legislative Framework that addresses six key objectives including protecting children, safeguarding American communities, and ensuring American AI dominance. In December 2025, President Trump signed an Executive Order to protect American AI innovation from inconsistent state laws and directed the Attorney General to establish an AI Litigation Task Force. Trump has appointed David Sacks as White House AI 'Czar' who has called the administration's AI policy the '5 Cs': child safety, communities, creators, censorship, and competitiveness.

people.name: John Kennedy
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-LA)
people.notes: Sen. John Kennedy (R-LA) questioned David Evan Harris about AI regulation during a Senate Judiciary Committee hearing on September 17, 2024. Kennedy co-introduced the AI Labeling Act (S. 2691) with Sen. Brian Schatz (D-HI) on October 24, 2023, which would notify consumers when they interact with AI-generated content and require developers to disclose such content. Kennedy has introduced legislation to withhold senators' pay during government shutdowns, with the U.S. Senate Rules Committee unanimously advancing this proposal in December 2025. Kennedy reacted positively to President Trump's joint address to Congress in March 2025, stating "President Trump has given us a blueprint".

people.name: Cory Gardner
people.title: President & CEO
people.notes: Cory Gardner is President & CEO of NCTA – The Internet & Television Association, beginning his tenure on September 22, 2025. He previously served as a U.S. Senator from Colorado from 2015 to 2021 and as a U.S. Representative for Colorado's 4th Congressional District from 2011 to 2015. Gardner served as chair of the National Republican Senatorial Committee from 2017 to 2019. He was consistently ranked among the most bipartisan senators, including being ranked the 5th most bipartisan senator of the 115th Congress in March 2019.

people.name: Ian Goodfellow
people.title: Research Scientist
people.notes: Ian J. Goodfellow (born 1987) is an American computer scientist, engineer, and executive, most noted for his work on artificial neural networks and deep learning. He is a research scientist at Google DeepMind, was previously employed as a research scientist at Google Brain and director of machine learning at Apple as well as one of the first employees at OpenAI. He is best known for inventing generative adversarial networks (GANs), using deep learning to generate images. He co-wrote, as the first author, the textbook Deep Learning (2016) and wrote the chapter on deep learning in the authoritative textbook of the field of artificial intelligence, Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach.

people.name: Katie Britt
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-AL)
people.notes: Katie Britt is a U.S. Senator from Alabama serving since 2023, and is a member of the Republican Party. She is calling for guardrails on artificial intelligence chatbots because of the potential to harm minors. Britt has two teenagers, ages 15 and 16, and draws on her experience as a mother in her advocacy work. She previously served as president and CEO of the Business Council of Alabama from 2019 to 2021, and as chief of staff for Senator Richard Shelby from 2016.

people.name: Billy Perrigo
people.title: Correspondent
people.notes: Correspondent at TIME magazine based in London, covering the tech industry with a focus on AI and social media. Known for investigative reporting on AI labor exploitation: broke the story that OpenAI used outsourced Kenyan workers earning less than $2/hour to detoxify ChatGPT. Also reported that OpenAI lobbied to water down the EU AI Act. His investigation "Inside Facebook's African Sweatshop" was a finalist for the 2022 Orwell Prize. Reporting focuses on how tech companies restructure power, with attention to impacts on marginalized communities, workers, and postcolonial states.

people.name: Marc Benioff
people.title: CEO
people.notes: CEO of Salesforce and owner of TIME magazine. Major tech industry figure who has been increasingly vocal about AI regulation. At Davos 2026, called AI models "suicide coaches" and demanded reform of Section 230 to hold AI companies accountable for harms. Framed AI safety as a values question: "What's more important to us, growth or our kids?" Salesforce has launched Agentforce (enterprise AI agents) while Benioff simultaneously pushes for guardrails. Consistent pattern of calling for tech regulation — made similar calls about social media at Davos 2018. Advocates for responsible AI deployment with meaningful safety requirements.

people.name: Sanjeev Arora
people.title: Charles C. Fitzmorris Professor of Computer Science; Founding Director, Princeton Language and Intelligence
people.notes: Charles C. Fitzmorris Professor of Computer Science at Princeton and founding director of Princeton Language and Intelligence (PLI). Indian-American theoretical computer scientist (PhD UC Berkeley, joined Princeton 1994). Research focuses on mathematical understanding of deep learning — optimization, generalization, interpretability — to enable better and safer AI. Member of the National Academy of Sciences and American Academy of Arts and Sciences. Awards include ACM Prize in Computing (2011), Fulkerson Prize (2012), Gödel Prize (2001, 2010), Packard Fellowship (1997), ACM Doctoral Dissertation Award (1995). Fellow of the ACM.

people.name: Arthur Spirling
people.title: Class of 1987 Professor of Politics; Director, Center for Statistics and Machine Learning
people.notes: Class of 1987 Professor of Politics at Princeton and Director of the Center for Statistics and Machine Learning. Previously on faculty at Harvard and NYU. PhD University of Rochester; BA/MA London School of Economics. Research centers on quantitative methods for political behavior, text-as-data, and problems at the intersection of data science and social science. Has published on LLMs in politics (e.g. 'Large Language Models Can Argue in Convincing Ways About Politics, But Humans Dislike AI Authors'), the ethics of using proprietary LLMs in academic research (Nature Computational Science 2023), and advocacy for open generative AI models in science (Nature 2023).

people.name: Olga Troyanskaya
people.title: Maduraperuma/Khot Professor of Computer Science; Director, Princeton Precision Health
people.notes: Maduraperuma/Khot Professor of Computer Science at Princeton; director of Princeton Precision Health. Also deputy director of Genomics at the Flatiron Institute (Simons Foundation) since 2014. PhD in Biomedical Informatics from Stanford. Research builds AI methods to decode genomes and diagnose/treat complex diseases, focusing on the non-coding genome and multi-omics modeling. Affiliated with Lewis-Sigler Institute for Integrative Genomics, Dept of Molecular Biology, and Omenn-Darling Bioengineering Institute. Awards: MIT Technology Review Top Young Innovator, Sloan Research Fellowship, NSF CAREER award, ISCB Overton Award, Genetics Society of America Herskowitz Award.

people.name: Tom Griffiths
people.title: Henry R. Luce Professor of Information Technology, Consciousness, and Culture; Director, Princeton AI Lab
people.notes: Henry R. Luce Professor of Information Technology, Consciousness, and Culture at Princeton, jointly appointed in Psychology and Computer Science. Director of the Computational Cognitive Science Lab (CoCoSci) and inaugural director of the Princeton Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence (AI Lab). PhD Psychology from Stanford (2005); previously at Brown and UC Berkeley (full professor 2015, director of Berkeley's Institute of Cognitive and Brain Sciences). Research applies mathematical modeling and computational methods to understand human cognition, and uses psychological concepts to interpret AI systems. Co-authored 'Algorithms to Live By' (2016) with Brian Christian.

people.name: Neil Lawrence
people.title: DeepMind Professor of Machine Learning
people.notes: DeepMind Professor of Machine Learning at the University of Cambridge; leads ai@cam, Cambridge's flagship AI mission. Senior AI Fellow at the Alan Turing Institute; visiting professor at University of Sheffield. Previously Director of Machine Learning at Amazon (3 years). Author of 'The Atomic Human: Understanding Ourselves in the Age of AI.' Has given parliamentary testimony on AI regulation. Advocates strongly for public-interest AI governance: warns against regulation that serves 'the digital oligarchy,' cautions about 'sowing the seeds of 10,000 Horizon scandals,' and emphasizes data transparency and privacy. Research focuses on interaction of ML with the physical world, originally motivated by ML deployment in Africa.

people.name: Adrian Weller
people.title: Director of Research in Machine Learning
people.notes: Director of Research in Machine Learning at the University of Cambridge; Programme Director for Trust and Society at the Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence (CFI). MBE. Turing AI Fellow in Trustworthy Machine Learning; heads Safe and Ethical AI at The Alan Turing Institute. Co-director of ELLIS programme on Human-centric Machine Learning. Member of UNESCO Ad Hoc Expert Group on Ethics of AI and IEEE Standards Working Group on Explainable AI. Emphasizes academic engagement with policymakers for 'technically and legally sensible' policy. Research spans interpretability, fairness, privacy, safety, ethics. Has written that humans cannot escape accountability for AI decisions and warns of major labor displacement ahead.

people.name: Kyunghyun Cho
people.title: Glen de Vries Professor of Health Statistics; Professor of Computer Science and Data Science
people.notes: Glen de Vries Professor of Health Statistics and professor of Computer Science and Data Science at NYU. Co-head of the Global AI Frontier Lab at NYU with Yann LeCun (2024). PhD from Aalto University (2014). Co-authored the 2014 paper with Bahdanau and Bengio introducing neural machine translation and attention mechanisms. Previously research scientist at Facebook AI Research (2017-2020); co-founded Prescient Design (acquired by Genentech 2021), served as Executive Director of Frontier Research until Jan 2026. Has publicly questioned existential AI risk framing, arguing focus should be on concrete current-application risks (healthcare, military). Critical of alarmist narratives distracting from practical regulation.

people.name: Jerry McNerney
people.title: California State Senator (D-SD-5)
people.notes: Former U.S. Representative (CA-9, 2007-2023) who chaired the Congressional AI Caucus and authored the AI in Government Act (2020). Transitioned to California State Senate District 5 in Dec 2024. Authored SB 813, which creates the CA Artificial Intelligence Standards and Safety Commission with independent verification organizations for AI safety standards. Previously a Fellow at Stanford HAI and Senior Policy Advisor at Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman. Also affiliated with the AI Trust Foundation. Math PhD; one of few STEM-trained members of the legislature.

people.name: Thomas Umberg
people.title: California State Senator (D-Santa Ana, SD-34); Chair, Senate Judiciary Committee
people.notes: California State Senator (D-Santa Ana, SD-34) and Chair of the Senate Judiciary Committee, which is the key committee for AI regulation bills in the CA legislature. Authored SB 574 restricting lawyers' and arbitrators' use of AI (requiring human review, banning delegation of decisions to AI, protecting confidential data). Publicly opposed Trump executive order to preempt state AI regulation. As Judiciary Chair, shapes which AI bills advance through the legislature, including SB 1047 (Wiener) and other safety bills.

people.name: Steve Padilla
people.title: California State Senator (D-San Diego, SD-18)
people.notes: California State Senator (D-San Diego, SD-18) and leading voice on AI consumer protection, especially for minors. Authored SB 243, the nation's first chatbot safeguards law (signed by Newsom), requiring operators to implement safeguards against sexual content for minors and disclosure that chatbots are AI. Also authored SB 420 (California AI Bill of Rights framework), SB 903 (protecting against AI therapy products), and proposed a 4-year moratorium on AI chatbots in children's toys. Publicly criticized OpenAI for lobbying against chatbot protections.

people.name: William Kimmitt
people.title: Under Secretary of Commerce for International Trade
people.notes: 17th Under Secretary of Commerce for International Trade, sworn in Aug 2025. Leads ITA and serves as principal trade adviser to Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick. Drives the American AI Exports Program, including leading ITA delegation to India AI Impact Summit 2026 in Bangalore/New Delhi. Previously a partner at Kirkland & Ellis LLP (complex litigation, trade/investment advisory). BA from Duke, JD from Georgetown Law.

people.name: Matt Damschroder
people.title: Director
people.primary_org: Ohio Department of Job and Family Services
people.notes: Director of the Ohio Department of Job and Family Services (ODJFS) since July 2021, appointed by Gov. Mike DeWine. Previously served as Director of the Ohio Department of Administrative Services (DAS) from Jan 2019, where he oversaw the Office of Information Technology and InnovateOhio Platform. Under his leadership, ODJFS won the 2025 Merrill Baumgardner Award for AI innovation in unemployment services, deploying multilingual virtual assistants, intelligent document processing, call center AI, and GenAI policy simplification tools. Advocates 'responsible AI' focused on user experience over bureaucracy. Republican appointee in the DeWine-Husted administration.

people.name: Ron DeSantis
people.title: Governor of Florida
people.notes: Governor of Florida (Republican). Proposed an AI Bill of Rights for Florida citizens covering data privacy, parental controls, consumer protections, and restrictions on AI use of individuals' name/image/likeness without consent. Also proposed protections against hyperscale AI data center costs and empowering local governments to reject their development. Notably pushed back against Trump's Dec 2025 executive order seeking to preempt state AI regulation, asserting 'We have a right to do this.' Claims Florida's proposed rules are consistent with federal goals on child safety, data center oversight, and procurement. Represents a state-level regulatory stance that is more protective than the federal approach despite party alignment.

people.name: Spencer Cox
people.title: Governor of Utah
people.notes: Governor of Utah (Republican). Leading advocate for 'pro-human AI' policy at the state level. Announced $10M initiative for AI workforce readiness at the Utah AI Summit (Dec 2025). Utah created an Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy in 2023. Pushed back strongly against Trump's Dec 2025 executive order preempting state AI regulation, asserting states should lead on AI policy. Proposed legislation to reduce AI chatbot harms, require transparency for AI-generated deepfakes, restrain AI in healthcare, and create a state framework requiring frontier AI developers to disclose safety protocols and child protection measures with whistleblower protections. Has gained national recognition as a Republican governor willing to regulate AI despite federal pushback.

people.name: Jules White
people.title: Professor of Computer Science; Senior Advisor to the Chancellor on Generative AI
people.notes: Professor of Computer Science at Vanderbilt University and Senior Advisor to the Chancellor on Generative AI in Enterprise & Education. Center Director of Vanderbilt's Amplify Generative AI Innovation Center. Director of the Initiative on the Future of Learning & Generative AI. Created one of the first prompt engineering courses (Coursera: 'Prompt Engineering for ChatGPT,' May 2023), with ~470,000 enrollees. Published 170+ papers. Research focuses on systematic application of prompt engineering across domains including software engineering. Covered by Wall Street Journal, CNN, The Guardian. Recognized by the National Science Foundation. Integrates GenAI across courses covering topics from meal planning to data analysis.

people.name: Allen Karns
people.title: Chief AI and Technology Officer
people.notes: Chief AI and Technology Officer at Vanderbilt University, leading the Amplify Generative AI Innovation Center within the College of Connected Computing. 25+ years IT experience spanning cloud architecture, generative AI, and infrastructure. Built Amplify, Vanderbilt's open-source generative AI platform available to all faculty, staff, and students since Nov 2024. Briefed a U.S. Senate AI working group; keynote speaker at Meta, Oracle, Wells Fargo. Named 2025 AWS Champion by CDG/CDE. Featured in WSJ, Bloomberg, WaPo, CNN. Gartner Eye on Innovation finalist. Holds AWS Solutions Architect Professional and Azure Administrator certifications. Strong proponent of institutional AI adoption to augment knowledge work and enhance productivity.

people.name: Daniel Diermeier
people.title: Chancellor
people.notes: Chancellor of Vanderbilt University (9th chancellor; contract extended through 2035). Political scientist by training with expertise in political institutions and crisis management. Launched the College of Connected Computing in 2024 — Vanderbilt's first new college since 1981 — dedicated to CS, AI, data science, and related fields. Opened the 4th annual Vanderbilt Summit on Modern Conflict and Emerging Threats (May 2025) calling AI 'a defining challenge for American leadership' and urging cross-sector collaboration between government, academia, and industry. Co-led a global summit reimagining liberal arts education in the age of AI (Jan 2025). Frames AI as requiring bold institutional leadership rather than restrictive regulation.

people.name: Matthew Johnson-Roberson
people.title: Dean, College of Connected Computing
people.notes: Inaugural dean of Vanderbilt's College of Connected Computing (appointed March 2025, started May 2025). Previously director of Carnegie Mellon University's Robotics Institute and faculty at University of Michigan. Internationally recognized roboticist and computer scientist with 100+ peer-reviewed publications spanning underwater robotics, computer vision, and autonomous vehicles. Ph.D. in robotics from University of Sydney. Co-directed the UM Ford Center for Autonomous Vehicles. Co-founder of Refraction AI, developing autonomous delivery robots for sustainable last-mile urban delivery. Brings expertise in building cross-disciplinary collaborations and integrating industry and institutional partners.

people.name: Ellie Pavlick
people.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science and Linguistics
people.notes: Assistant (now Associate) Professor of Computer Science and Linguistics at Brown University, and Research Scientist at Google DeepMind. Leads the Language Understanding and Representation (LUNAR) Lab studying how language works and building computational models of human-like language understanding. PI of ARIA, a $20M NSF-funded national institute at Brown focused on building intuitive, trustworthy AI assistants (with co-director Suresh Venkatasubramanian). Received DARPA Young Faculty Award for work on interpretability and AI safety — developing tools to inspect, diagnose, and manipulate 'black box' AI systems. Affiliated with Brown's Center for Technological Responsibility. Research spans semantics, pragmatics, NLI, grounded language, with cross-disciplinary collaborations in cognitive science, neuroscience, and philosophy. Emphasizes alignment: building AI that is 'ultimately good for society.'

people.name: Francis J. Doyle III
people.title: Provost
people.notes: 14th Provost of Brown University (since July 2023). Previously John A. Paulson Dean of Harvard's School of Engineering and Applied Sciences (SEAS) from 2015-2023. Engineer by training (process control, systems biology). Identified AI as a university priority in Aug 2023 letter on 'Potential impact of AI on our academic mission.' Frames Brown's AI approach as 'knowing, enhancing, protecting, and creating.' Appointed Michael Littman as inaugural Associate Provost for AI to coordinate university-wide efforts across research, teaching, administration, and policy. Advocates for major computing infrastructure investments (GPUs, storage) to support AI research. Takes a decentralized approach — discretion to instructors on classroom AI use rather than prescriptive university-wide policies.

people.name: Richard Zemel
people.title: Trianthe Dakolias Professor of Engineering and Applied Science; Director, NSF AI Institute ARNI
people.notes: Richard Zemel is the Trianthe Dakolias Professor of Engineering and Applied Science at Columbia University and Director of the NSF AI Institute for Artificial and Natural Intelligence (ARNI). Previously VP Research at the Vector Institute in Toronto and professor at University of Toronto. Pioneer in machine learning, computer vision, and algorithmic fairness. Developed frameworks for rigorous statistical guarantees on deployed model performance. Collaborates with Columbia Law School on AI governance. Research emphasizes fairness, robustness, and controllability in ML systems — advocates for responsible, safety-conscious AI development with measurable guarantees.

people.name: Robert J. Jones
people.title: President
people.notes: Robert J. Jones became the 34th president of the University of Washington in August 2025. Previously chancellor at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. Key AI priorities: advancing AI for the public good, dispelling AI job-loss myths, and ensuring all graduates gain AI competency. Expanded UW-Microsoft partnership to accelerate AI discovery, workforce preparation, and community AI literacy. Three pillars: debt-free education access, radical cross-sector collaborations, and eliminating barriers between university and community. Views AI as "just an amazing tool" — optimistic framing focused on equitable access, workforce readiness, and responsible community use rather than restriction.

people.name: Brad Smith
people.title: Vice Chair and President
people.notes: Brad Smith is Vice Chair and President of Microsoft, the company's top legal and policy voice. Prolific advocate for AI regulation: supports a dedicated federal AI regulatory agency, licensing regimes for frontier models and high-risk AI data centers, and the Blumenthal-Hawley AI framework. Told CBS the government should require companies to implement AI safety protocols. Testified before the Senate Judiciary Subcommittee. Warned in 2026 about Chinese AI subsidies undercutting American firms. Believes AI can be "both a tool and a weapon" — insists on human control with ability to "slow things down or turn things off." Co-chairs Microsoft's Responsible AI Council with CTO Kevin Scott. Consistently calls for governments to "move faster" on AI rules while maintaining innovation. One of the most influential corporate voices on AI governance globally.

people.name: Richard M. Locke
people.title: John C. Head III Dean
people.notes: Richard M. Locke is the John C. Head III Dean of MIT Sloan School of Management (since July 2025). PhD from MIT (1989), previously provost at Brown University and at Apple University. Scholar of international labor standards and comparative political economy. Envisions MIT Sloan charting the future of management education with focus on AI's impact on education, industry, and the workplace. Believes AI revolution in education is "inexorable" and will help students operate at a higher level — AI should augment, not replace. Strategic priorities include partnerships across MIT focused on health, climate, AI, and policy. Researches structural pressures on higher education including AI's role. Generally optimistic about AI augmentation with emphasis on responsible institutional integration.

people.name: Sinan Aral
people.title: David Austin Professor of Management, Marketing, IT and Data Science; Director, MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy
people.notes: Sinan Aral is the David Austin Professor of Management, Marketing, IT and Data Science at MIT Sloan and Director of the MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy. Author of "The Hype Machine: How Social Media Disrupts Our Elections, Our Economy, and Our Health — and How We Must Adapt." Expert on misinformation, social media platforms, and AI-driven algorithmic amplification. Advocates a four-lever regulatory approach: business model reform, platform code/design changes, social norms, and legislation. Argues that lack of competition lets platforms avoid accountability for negative externalities. Raises deep questions about who decides truth in an AI-mediated information ecosystem — calls for ethicists and philosophers in the conversation. Emphasis on structural reform over content moderation.

people.name: Kate Kellogg
people.title: David J. McGrath Jr. Professor of Management and Innovation
people.notes: Kate Kellogg is the David J. McGrath Jr. Professor of Management and Innovation at MIT Sloan. Leading researcher on AI implementation in organizations, studying how frontline knowledge workers adopt predictive and generative AI. Developed influential cyborg/centaur/self-automator framework for how employees use generative AI. Research shows generative AI can improve worker performance by up to 40% but performance drops when used outside its capabilities. Recent work warns that humans may overestimate their ability to spot-check LLM output, highlighting vulnerability to AI manipulation. Focuses on organizational design for responsible AI adoption, workforce training, and the practical barriers to AI implementation. Emphasis on human-AI complementarity with caution about over-reliance.

people.name: Roberto Rigobon
people.title: Society of Sloan Fellows Professor of Applied Economics
people.notes: Roberto Rigobon is the Society of Sloan Fellows Professor of Applied Economics at MIT Sloan, NBER research associate, and five-time MIT Teacher of the Year. Co-founder of the Billion Prices Project (alternative inflation measures) and director of the Aggregate Confusion Project (improving ESG measures). Research on AI focuses on human-AI complementarity: developed the EPOCH Framework identifying five capabilities where humans outperform AI — Empathy, Presence, Opinion, Creativity, and Hope. Coauthored work on algorithmic discrimination in credit, showing that ML-based lending consolidates existing racial and gender bias from historical data. Macroeconomist focused on measurement — brings critical lens to AI metrics and fairness claims.

people.name: Danielle Li
people.title: David Sarnoff Professor of Management of Technology
people.notes: Danielle Li is the David Sarnoff Professor of Management of Technology at MIT Sloan, researching how AI affects worker productivity, innovation, and labor economics. She has called for policies that give workers rights over AI-generated data derived from their expertise.

people.name: Swati Gupta
people.title: Class of 1947 Career Development Associate Professor
people.notes: Swati Gupta is the Class of 1947 Career Development Associate Professor at MIT Sloan in the Operations Research and Statistics Group. NSF CAREER Award recipient for "Advancing Equity in Selection Problems Through Bias-Aware Optimization." Research bridges optimization, machine learning, and algorithmic fairness — focuses on improving efficiency, quality, and fairness of model decisions. Application domains include hiring, admissions, districting, healthcare, e-commerce, and supply chains. Serves on program committees for NeurIPS, FAccT, and IPCO. PhD in operations research from MIT (2017). Work is deeply technical but explicitly motivated by equity and fairness outcomes — designs algorithms that account for incomplete/erroneous data and multi-criteria decision-making.

people.name: Casey Mock
people.title: Chief of Policy and Public Affairs
people.notes: Casey Mock is Chief of Policy and Public Affairs at the Center for Humane Technology. Previously spent four years at Amazon leading state and local tax policy. Strong advocate for technology regulation and corporate accountability — argues companies must be held liable for harms caused by their AI products. Compares tech industry lobbying against regulation to the tobacco industry. Advocates for liability-focused AI legislation, arguing that clear liability rules actually empower innovation by setting predictable boundaries. Emphasizes designing platforms for people over profit and realigning incentive structures in tech. Public voice on AI governance through media, podcasts, and opinion pieces (CalMatters, podcast appearances).

people.name: Camille Carlton
people.title: Policy Director
people.notes: Camille Carlton is Policy Director at the Center for Humane Technology. Named to Business Insider's AI 100 (2023). Directly involved in landmark lawsuits against CharacterAI and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman around AI accountability and platform liability. Draws clear line from social media harms to AI chatbot risks — argues AI isn't just repeating social media's mistakes but amplifying them (always on, deeply personal, emotionally responsive). Advocates design-focused regulation addressing root causes of harm rather than outcomes or content. Writes for TechPolicy.Press on AI governance. Emphasizes protecting human dignity, preventing psychological harm from AI companions, and building accountability into AI system design. Strong proponent of AI safety standards.

people.name: Daniel Barcay
people.title: Senior Fellow
people.notes: Daniel Barcay is a Senior Fellow at the Center for Humane Technology (CHT), where he previously served as Executive Director during the rapid arrival of generative AI. He develops intellectual frameworks to understand emerging AI issues and co-hosts CHT's podcast "Your Undivided Attention." Previously VP of Product at Planet Labs and a research fellow at the Berggruen Institute's Antikythera Program. He has written and spoken publicly about AI risks including advertising in AI systems, AI capturing human interiority, and AI's impact on work and relationships. Advocates for responsible AI deployment and warns about psychological manipulation by AI systems.

people.name: Dylan Baker
people.title: Lead Research Engineer
people.notes: Dylan K. Baker is the lead research engineer at the Distributed AI Research (DAIR) Institute, founded by Timnit Gebru. Previously a software engineer at Google Research on the Ethical AI team, he left Google in February 2022 to join DAIR. His research focuses on data labor and classificatory systems in AI. He writes and speaks about AI ethics, appearing at the European Parliament, on NPR, and in outlets like Tech Policy Press and Stanford Social Innovation Review. He advocates against the "move fast and break things" approach and has argued that general-purpose and generative AI systems are not necessary, favoring values-driven system design.

people.name: Valérie Pisano
people.title: President and CEO
people.notes: Valérie Pisano is President and CEO of Mila – Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute, leading one of the world's foremost academic AI labs. She advocates for responsible, ethics-centered AI governance and has emphasized the urgent need for international cooperation on AI regulation and safety standards.

people.name: Pierre Boivin
people.title: Chairman of the Board
people.notes: Pierre Boivin is the Chairman of the Board of Directors at Mila (Quebec AI Institute), the world's largest academic research center in deep learning. He is also President and CEO of Claridge Inc., a private investment firm, and Chancellor of McGill University. Through his leadership of Mila's board, he supports the institute's mission under Scientific Director Yoshua Bengio, one of the most prominent voices on AI safety and governance. His role connects Montreal's business community with its AI research ecosystem.

people.name: Zach Seward
people.title: Editorial Director of A.I. Initiatives
people.notes: Zach Seward is the Editorial Director of A.I. Initiatives at The New York Times, which he joined in 2023. He leads a newsroom team responsible for experimenting with generative AI and machine-learning techniques for journalism. Previously co-founded Quartz, the global business news organization, serving as CEO, editor-in-chief, and chief product officer over more than a decade. His team has visited nearly half of the NYT's 2,000 journalists across 40 desks to introduce AI tools and workflows. Developed Echo, an internal summarization assistant for journalists. Formerly a reporter at The Wall Street Journal and associate editor at Harvard's Nieman Journalism Lab.

people.name: Rubina Fillion
people.title: Associate Editorial Director of A.I. Initiatives
people.notes: Rubina Madan Fillion is the Associate Editorial Director of A.I. Initiatives at The New York Times, working alongside Zach Seward on a cross-disciplinary team leveraging generative AI and machine learning for journalism while ensuring responsible use. Previously Director of Strategy for Times Opinion, founding Director of Audience Engagement at The Intercept, and social media editor at The Wall Street Journal. She has taught graduate-level digital journalism at Columbia University and NYU. Served on the ONA Board of Directors (2019–2024). Active in exploring how AI can power investigative journalism and uncover stories.

people.name: Dylan Freedman
people.title: Senior Machine Learning Engineer and A.I. Projects Editor
people.notes: Dylan Freedman is a Senior Machine Learning Engineer and A.I. Projects Editor at The New York Times, part of the AI Initiatives team alongside Zach Seward and Rubina Fillion. He prototypes AI tools, shapes standards, and aids reporting. Holds a B.A. in computer science and music from Harvard and a master's in journalism from Stanford. Previously worked at The Washington Post, DocumentCloud (where he led development), Stanford Journalism, and Google AI. His work bridges technical AI development with journalism practice, building tools that help reporters tell more ambitious stories.

people.name: Duy Nguyen
people.title: Senior Machine Learning Engineer, A.I. Initiatives
people.notes: Senior machine-learning engineer at the New York Times on the A.I. Initiatives team. Previously worked in NYT Opinion Graphics (2021-22 fellowship) and at the Brown Institute's Local News Lab at Columbia, helping newsrooms integrate AI. Built Echo, an internal NYT tool connecting the content archive to LLMs for journalists to summarize and extract information. Focuses on applied ML for journalism rather than policy advocacy.

people.name: Jeff Dean
people.title: Chief Scientist, Google DeepMind & Google Research
people.notes: Chief Scientist at Google DeepMind (appointed 2023 after merger of DeepMind and Google Brain). Co-founded Google Brain in 2011. Named to TIME100 AI 2025 list. One of the most influential figures in modern AI — key architect of MapReduce, TensorFlow, and transformer-era Google AI. Active angel investor in AI startups. On AI safety, takes a balanced middle-ground position: recognizes risks from misinformation and automated hacking, advocates for safeguards and mitigations, but rejects extreme precautionary approaches. Supports Google's Responsible AI principles framework. Signed amicus brief supporting Anthropic. Believes AI can accelerate scientific discovery 10-100x for climate, disease, energy.

people.name: Greg Corrado
people.title: Distinguished Scientist & Senior Research Director
people.notes: Distinguished Scientist and Senior Research Director at Google. Co-founded Google Brain in 2011 with Jeff Dean and Andrew Ng. Neuroscientist by training. Led Google's Health AI division, overseeing AI applications in medical diagnostics, medical imaging, clinical outcomes prediction, and genomic analysis. On AI governance, advocates moving beyond traditional 'safety filters' to building protocols for authority, responsibility, and trust in multi-agent ecosystems. Emphasizes that governance must evolve as AI agents gain real-world collaboration and action capabilities.

people.name: Yossi Matias
people.title: Vice President of Engineering and Research; Head of Google Research
people.notes: Vice President of Engineering and Research at Google, Head of Google Research. Leads global teams in foundational ML, quantum computing, AI for health, climate, education, and conversational AI. Frames AI as 'an amplifier of human ingenuity' that empowers scientists, healthcare workers, and educators. On AI and employment, argues AI will create more research jobs, not fewer — cites AlphaFold as freeing researchers to tackle bigger questions. Previously a professor at Tel Aviv University. Israeli-American computer scientist with expertise in streaming algorithms and data mining.

people.name: Gordon M. Goldstein
people.title: Adjunct Senior Fellow
people.notes: Adjunct Senior Fellow at the Council on Foreign Relations (CFR), focusing on advances in global technology and their consequences for U.S. economy and foreign policy. Directs a CFR roundtable series on technology, innovation, and American primacy. Former managing director at Silver Lake (2010-2018), the world's largest technology-focused investment firm. Previously Senior Adviser to the UN Secretary General's Strategic Planning Unit and co-directed CFR's Project on the Information Revolution. Author and foreign policy scholar with PhD from Columbia University in political science and international relations. Phillips Academy Andover graduate.

people.name: Børge Brende
people.title: President and CEO
people.notes: President and CEO of the World Economic Forum since 2020. Former Norwegian Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013-2017) and Minister of Trade and Industry (2004-2005). Under his leadership, WEF launched the AI Governance Alliance and Global Regulatory Innovation Platform (GRIP, July 2025) to co-develop frameworks for agile, inclusive AI governance. Emphasizes that AI challenges 'cannot be addressed in silos' and require 'co-creation' over competition and 'collaboration' over fragmentation. Optimistic about AI's economic productivity potential but acknowledges overvaluation risks. Advocates multi-stakeholder approach bridging business, government, academia, and civil society.

people.name: Maria Basso
people.title: Head of AI Applications and Impact, Centre for AI Excellence
people.notes: Head of AI Applications and Impact at the World Economic Forum's Centre for AI Excellence (CAIE), appointed January 2025. Over six years at WEF; previously AI and Digital Technologies Portfolio Manager at the Centre for the Fourth Industrial Revolution. Background in mechanical and energy engineering (Politecnico di Torino, University of Illinois). Former research associate at UC Berkeley and business analyst at McKinsey. Leads WEF's work on real-world AI adoption, focusing on how organizations embed AI into everyday economic systems and maximize transformative potential.

people.name: Francisco Betti
people.title: Head of the Global Industries Team
people.notes: Head of the Global Industries Team and Member of the Executive Committee at the World Economic Forum. Previously led WEF's Centre for Shaping the Future of Advanced Manufacturing and Supply Chains. Brings together 20+ industry communities to help companies and sectors anticipate trends and accelerate responsible industrial transformation. Advocates for AI deployment at scale in manufacturing through the Network of Centres for the Fourth Industrial Revolution. Joined WEF in May 2015 after management consulting at PricewaterhouseCoopers in Geneva working with international organizations.

people.name: Cathy Li
people.title: Head, Centre for AI Excellence
people.notes: Head of the Centre for AI Excellence and Member of the Executive Committee at the World Economic Forum. Oversees the AI Governance Alliance (AIGA), a flagship initiative convening leaders from business, government, academia, and civil society to prepare for the 'intelligent era.' Argues AI leadership depends not on infrastructure volume but deployment effectiveness. Warns most organizations 'fundamentally misunderstand AI's transformative potential' and underestimate the mindset shift required. Champions responsible, transparent, and inclusive AI system design. Focuses on building frameworks, incentives, and practices for responsible AI development at scale.

people.name: Peter Lee
people.title: President of Microsoft Science
people.notes: Peter Lee is President of Microsoft Science (formerly President of Microsoft Research), overseeing AI research strategy and scientific discovery initiatives. Under his leadership, Microsoft Research has focused on responsible AI, including tools for detecting hallucinations and improving LLM fact-checking. Lee champions AI as a collaborative partner in scientific discovery, envisioning AI lab assistants that suggest and run experiments. He has emphasized that the true value of AI "hasn't been invented" yet and advocates for deployment infrastructure to deliver AI globally. Spoke at EmTech AI 2025 on AI revolutionizing science.

people.name: Ashok Elluswamy
people.title: VP of AI
people.notes: Ashok Elluswamy is VP of AI at Tesla, leading both the Autopilot/FSD (Full Self-Driving) and Optimus humanoid robot programs. He champions Tesla's end-to-end neural network approach to autonomous driving, which trains a single network for perception, planning, and control — contrasting with the modular, sensor-heavy systems used by competitors. Told internal teams that 2026 would be "the most demanding year of their careers." Predicts AGI could become reality by ~2035 based on deep learning advances. The same neural network architecture powering FSD is being extended to Optimus.

people.name: Daphne Koller
people.title: Founder and CEO
people.notes: Stanford CS professor (adjunct), MacArthur Fellow, co-founded Coursera, founded and CEO of insitro (AI-driven drug discovery). Member of National Academy of Sciences. Pioneer in probabilistic graphical models and machine learning applied to biology. Supports targeted regulation of AI in healthcare/drug discovery, recognizing necessity of safety standards while advocating for AI to accelerate scientific discovery.

people.name: Larry Fink
people.title: Chairman and CEO
people.notes: CEO and Chairman of BlackRock, world's largest asset manager (~$11.5T AUM). Strong AI investment advocate. 2026 chairman's letter warned AI will widen wealth gap unless investing access expands. Leads BlackRock's Global AI Infrastructure Investment Partnership (with MGX, Microsoft). Views AI infrastructure buildout as $7T+ opportunity. Identifies power/energy as key bottleneck, not chips. Predicts some AI companies will go bankrupt but sees that as healthy capitalism.

people.name: Rob Goldstein
people.title: COO
people.notes: COO of BlackRock. Oversees Aladdin platform, technology, and operations. Called AI "the most significant technology, innovation, evolution, revolution of my 30-year career." Launched Asimov, BlackRock's AI research platform (Jun 2025) that scans filings, research notes, and emails to generate portfolio insights. ~25% of BlackRock employees are technologists under his purview. Advocates aggressive AI adoption across investment operations.

people.name: Allen Newell
people.title: Professor of Computer Science (deceased)
people.notes: DECEASED (1927-1992). AI pioneer at Carnegie Mellon University and RAND Corporation. Co-created Logic Theorist (1956, first AI program) and General Problem Solver (1957) with Herbert Simon and Cliff Shaw. Developed Information Processing Language. Proposed physical symbol systems hypothesis. Created SOAR cognitive architecture. ACM Turing Award (1975, with Simon). Instrumental in founding CMU's Computer Science Department (1965) and School of Computer Science. The ACM-AAAI Allen Newell Award is named in his honor.

people.name: Herb Simon
people.title: Professor (deceased)
people.notes: DECEASED (1916-2001). Nobel laureate (Economics, 1978) and AI pioneer at Carnegie Mellon University. Co-created Logic Theorist (1956) and General Problem Solver with Allen Newell and Cliff Shaw. Founded concept of bounded rationality. ACM Turing Award (1975, with Newell). At CMU from 1949 to 2001, instrumental in founding the School of Computer Science, GSIA, and multiple departments. CMU's Simon Initiative and Herbert A. Simon Award named in his honor. Bridged AI, economics, psychology, and organizational theory.

people.name: Robert M. Groves
people.title: Interim President
people.notes: Interim President of Georgetown University (since Nov 2024), previously Executive VP and Provost (2012-2024). Social statistician specializing in survey methodology. Former director of U.S. Census Bureau (2009-2012). Elected member of National Academy of Sciences and National Academy of Medicine. Fellow of American Statistical Association. Research focuses on data collection quality, adaptive research designs, and privacy attitudes toward statistical agencies. Testified before Congress (Jul 2025).

people.name: Scott Strobel
people.title: Provost; Henry Ford II Professor of Molecular Biophysics & Biochemistry
people.notes: Provost of Yale University (since 2020) and Henry Ford II Professor of Molecular Biophysics & Biochemistry. Assembled Yale Task Force on Artificial Intelligence (YTAI, Jan 2024) to review AI activity university-wide. Announced $150M five-year AI investment (Aug 2024) covering GPU infrastructure, faculty hiring, and ethics research. Priorities center on expanding AI computing access and establishing Yale's position in AI ethics. Oversees Yale Center for Research Computing expansion.

people.name: António Guterres
people.title: Secretary-General
people.notes: UN Secretary-General (2017-present). Proposed international AI governance body modeled on the IAEA in 2023. Convened the High-Level Advisory Body on AI which delivered 7 recommendations in 2024. Championed establishment of the UN Independent International Scientific Panel on AI and Global Dialogue on AI Governance (both approved by GA in 2025). Consistently advocates for science-based AI policy and multilateral governance, though the U.S. has rejected UN-centralized AI oversight.

people.name: Amandeep Singh Gill
people.title: Under-Secretary-General and Special Envoy for Digital and Emerging Technologies
people.notes: UN Under-Secretary-General and Special Envoy for Digital and Emerging Technologies (Jan 2025-present); previously Secretary-General's Envoy on Technology (2022-2024). Indian diplomat who is a central figure in UN AI governance architecture. Instrumental in establishing the Independent International Scientific Panel on AI and Global Dialogue on AI Governance. Emphasizes evidence-based, science-informed AI policy and inclusive governance that gives all 193 member states a seat at the table. Advocates strongly for Global South participation in AI governance.

people.name: Matt Turek
people.title: Chief Scientist
people.notes: Former DARPA deputy director of the Information Innovation Office (I2O). Joined DARPA in 2018 as program manager; led the Explainable AI (XAI), Media Forensics (MediFor), Semantic Forensics (SemaFor), and Machine Common Sense (MCS) programs. Served as acting deputy director of I2O (June-Oct 2021), then deputy office director (May 2022-Oct 2025). DARPA tour ended Oct 2025; transitioned to Chief Scientist at STR. PhD in Computer Science from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute.

people.name: Benjamin Grosof
people.title: Program Manager, Defense Sciences Office
people.notes: DARPA program manager in the Defense Sciences Office (joined Sept 2023). Leads the CLARA (Causal Logical AI Reasoning and Auditing) program. Pioneer in knowledge representation and reasoning (KRR), including Rulelog, courteous defeasibility, and neuro-symbolic AI. Extensive background applying AI to legal, regulatory, policy compliance, and defense/intelligence domains. Previously Chief Scientist at Coherent Knowledge. PhD from Stanford, Hertz Fellow.

people.name: Andrew Carney
people.title: Program Manager
people.notes: Joint DARPA/ARPA-H program manager. Leads the AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC) at DARPA -- a two-year, $8.5M competition to develop AI systems that can find and fix vulnerabilities in critical infrastructure software. Also leads health cybersecurity programs at ARPA-H. Previously principal researcher at HSBC Cybersecurity Science and Analytics. Background in competitive hacking (CTF). MS in Computer Science from Johns Hopkins University.

people.name: Barton Paulhamus
people.title: Co-founder and Chief, Intelligent Systems Center
people.notes: Co-founder and Chief of the Intelligent Systems Center (ISC) at Johns Hopkins APL, leading 130 research scientists and engineers in AI, robotics, neuroscience, and human-machine teaming. Named Chair of the Artificial Intelligence program at Johns Hopkins Engineering for Professionals (EP), one of the nation's first online MS programs in AI. Faculty since 2010. Doctor of Engineering (Computer Science) from Johns Hopkins; BS/MS in CS and Engineering from Penn State.

people.name: Dr. Ian McCulloh
people.title: Director, AI Portfolio for Lifelong Learning
people.notes: Director of the AI portfolio for Lifelong Learning (executive and continuing education) at Johns Hopkins University, with joint faculty positions in Computer Science and Public Health. Previously Accenture's Chief Data Scientist, building a 1,200-person Federal AI practice for U.S. Government. Retired U.S. Army Lt. Colonel who founded the West Point Network Science Center and served as Chief Strategist for Information Warfare at CENTCOM. Research combines AI, neuroscience, and human behavior.

people.name: David Marchick
people.title: Dean, Kogod School of Business
people.notes: Dean of Kogod School of Business at American University (since Aug 2022). Led what Poets & Quants called 'the most consequential AI transformation in business education,' making Kogod one of the first AI-first business schools. Launched Applied AI Institute with Google.org support. Views: companies should help educate policymakers on AI opportunities and risks, but Congress and the Executive Branch must ultimately set policy. Advocates innovation while acknowledging need for safety/fairness guardrails.

people.name: Gwanhoo Lee
people.title: Professor and Chair, Dept of Information Technology and Analytics; Director, Institute for Applied Artificial Intelligence
people.notes: Professor and Chair, Dept of Information Technology and Analytics at Kogod School of Business, American University. Director of the Institute for Applied Artificial Intelligence (IAAI). Research focuses on AI governance, digital transformation, information privacy, and smart government. Advised South Korea's Presidential Committee on the Fourth Industrial Revolution on AI policy. Consulted for World Bank and Samsung on digital strategy. University Outstanding Scholarship Award 2025.

people.name: Angela Virtu
people.title: Professorial Lecturer in IT&A; Associate Director, Institute of Applied Artificial Intelligence
people.notes: Professorial Lecturer in IT&A at Kogod School of Business, American University (since 2023). Associate Director of the Institute of Applied Artificial Intelligence. Created faculty AI training program that helped embed AI across 58 new courses. Prior industry experience building AI/ML solutions for pre-IPO SaaS companies. Key implementer of Kogod's AI-first curriculum transformation.

people.name: Bei Xiao
people.title: Provost Associate Professor of Computer Science
people.notes: Provost Associate Professor, Dept of Computer Science at American University. Heads the Computational Material Perception Laboratory. Research spans human/machine vision, generative AI for graphics, multimodal perception in VR/AR, and NLP for clinical trials. PhD Neuroscience (UPenn), postdoc at MIT Brain & Cognitive Sciences/CSAIL. Funded by NIH and NSF.

people.name: Thenkurussi (Kesh) Kesavadas
people.title: Vice President for Research & Economic Development
people.notes: Vice President for Research & Economic Development at SUNY University at Albany. Launched $200M AI initiative ($75M from NY State) and $20M Center for Emerging AI Systems with IBM. Co-chairs SUNY-wide AI research and infrastructure work group. Leads Empire AI SUNY partnerships for public-good AI. Previously founding director of Health Care Engineering Systems Center at UIUC. Inventor of RoSS robotic surgical simulator; founder/CTO of AirV Labs (generative AI + VR startup).

people.name: Geoff Garrett
people.title: Dean
people.notes: Dean of USC Marshall School of Business. Chairs USC President's AI Strategy Committee. Champions rapid AI integration in higher education: 'if we don't treat things with enough urgency, we're just going to fall behind.' Working on school-wide AI policy for responsible use. Helped secure a five-year, $4M Knight Foundation + USC Marshall match ($2M each) for purpose-driven AI research housed at the Neely Center. Emphasizes aligning AI with human values.

people.name: Carol L. Folt
people.title: President Emeritus and VP for Presidential Initiatives
people.notes: President of USC (2019-2025), first woman to serve as permanent president. Established Frontiers of Computing initiative and launched USC School of Advanced Computing (23rd school). Made AI a major institutional priority, embedding ethics, analytics, and AI across scholarly work. Environmental scientist by training (UNC Chapel Hill chancellor before USC). Now President Emeritus and VP for Presidential Initiatives; teaches science and society courses at USC.

people.name: Erika James
people.title: Dean
people.notes: Dean of the Wharton School (since July 2020), first woman and first person of color in Wharton's 139-year history. Organizational psychologist specializing in crisis leadership and workplace diversity. Launched Wharton AI & Analytics Initiative, investing heavily in AI research and teaching. Introduced new undergraduate concentration and MBA major in AI for business. Emphasizes responsible AI: 'simultaneously focused on harnessing AI transformative potential while also understanding and addressing its risks.'

people.name: Kartik Hosanagar
people.title: John C. Hower Professor of Technology and Digital Business
people.notes: John C. Hower Professor of Technology and Digital Business at Wharton. Author of 'A Human's Guide to Machine Intelligence' (2019), advocating for an 'algorithmic bill of rights' covering transparency, explainability, and user control. Favors issue-specific AI regulation (e.g., deepfakes, financial fraud) over broad-based approaches; proposes regulatory sandboxes for innovation. Research focuses on the digital economy and impact of algorithms on consumers and society.

people.name: Kevin Werbach
people.title: First Pacific Company Professor; Professor of Legal Studies & Business Ethics
people.notes: First Pacific Company Professor, Professor of Legal Studies & Business Ethics, and department chair at Wharton. Directs the Wharton Accountable AI Lab and the Blockchain and Digital Asset Project. Hosts 'The Road to Accountable AI' podcast. Served on Obama Presidential Transition Team; was Counsel for New Technology Policy at FCC under Clinton. Published four books including 'The Blockchain and the New Architecture of Trust.' Focuses on legal, ethical, and regulatory aspects of emerging technologies.

people.name: Eric Bradlow
people.title: Vice Dean of AI & Analytics; K.P. Chao Professor of Marketing
people.notes: Vice Dean of AI & Analytics at Wharton, K.P. Chao Professor of Marketing, and Chairperson of the Wharton Marketing Department. Also Professor of Economics, Education, and Statistics and Data Science. Leads the Wharton AI & Analytics Initiative, spearheading student learning, faculty research, and industry partnerships in AI. Applied statistician (PhD, Harvard) with research in Bayesian modeling and statistical computing. Multiple teaching awards including Linback Award for Distinguished PhD Teaching.

people.name: Prasanna (Sonny) Tambe
people.title: Associate Professor of Operations, Information and Decisions; Faculty Co-Director of Wharton Human-AI Research
people.notes: Associate Professor of Operations, Information and Decisions at Wharton; Faculty Co-Director of Wharton Human-AI Research. Research focuses on economics of technology and labor: AI for HR applications, labor markets for AI skills, and how workers specialize in technologies. Key finding: firms capture more value from AI when algorithmic expertise is distributed across domain experts rather than concentrated in IT (Management Science). Co-advises Wharton's new AI major. PhD in Managerial Science from Wharton; SB and MEng from MIT.

people.name: Giles Hooker
people.title: Professor of Statistics and Data Science
people.notes: Professor of Statistics and Data Science at Wharton. Research interests include machine learning, functional data analysis, differential equations, computational statistics, and statistical ecology. Co-advises Wharton's new undergraduate and MBA AI programs (jointly administered by Statistics/Data Science and Operations/Information/Decisions departments). Developed 'Foundations of Deep Learning' course. Published work on additive explanations for black-box ML models (2023).

people.name: Sebastian Thrun
people.title: Adjunct Professor
people.notes: Pioneering AI researcher and entrepreneur. Stanford CS professor (2003-2011), directed Stanford AI Lab (SAIL). Founded Google X, Google's self-driving car project (now Waymo), Udacity (online education), and Kitty Hawk (air taxis, now closed). Won DARPA Grand Challenge (2005). Strong AI optimist: 'AI will not replace us, it will complement us' and warns against 'prematurely declaring AI a danger and destroying chances of this technology by rash regulation.' Favors liability-based regulation over prescriptive rules. Believes AI makes workers more productive. Currently CEO of Sage AI Labs.

people.name: Nimar Arora
people.title: Co-founder
people.notes: AI researcher and entrepreneur specializing in probabilistic programming and Bayesian inference. Co-founder of Bayesian Logic Inc. (2012), which builds Bayesian models for complex physical domains, particularly seismology. Invented the Bean Machine Probabilistic Programming Language and the NET-VISA probabilistic model for detecting nuclear explosions (used by CTBTO). PhD from UC Berkeley (2006-2012) under Stuart Russell and Erik Sudderth. Received the Mitchell Prize in 2014 from the American Statistical Association. Previously Research Scientist at Facebook (designed a new PPL), Head of AI and Analytics at DevRev Inc. (2020-2023). Currently Founder at JazzX AI.

people.name: Ted Budd
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-NC)
people.notes: Republican Senator (NC) and Chair of the Senate Subcommittee on Science, Manufacturing, and Competitiveness. Actively shapes AI legislation through bipartisan bills on government data access, AI lab networks, and public health preparedness. Strongly opposes overregulation, favoring a light-touch, innovation-first federal framework.

people.name: Dick Durbin
people.title: U.S. Senator (D-IL)
people.notes: Democratic Senator from Illinois (1997–2027) and former Senate Judiciary Committee chair. Co-introduced the bipartisan AI LEAD Act to enable products-liability suits against AI developers, and the TEST AI Act to strengthen federal AI testing capacity. Consistently advocated for mandatory AI oversight.

people.name: Pam Bondi
people.title: U.S. Attorney General
people.notes: U.S. Attorney General, confirmed February 4, 2025. Previously served as Florida Attorney General (2011-2019). Key AI policy action: created the DOJ AI Litigation Task Force under Trump executive order to challenge state AI laws deemed inconsistent with federal policy or the goal of U.S. "global AI dominance." The task force targets state laws on grounds they are illegal, unconstitutionally regulate interstate commerce, or are overridden by federal regulations. Supports a "minimally burdensome national policy framework for AI" to promote American competitiveness.

people.name: Mike Johnson
people.title: Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives (2023–present); U.S. Representative, Louisiana
people.notes: Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives (Republican, Louisiana). Leading congressional effort to implement the Trump administration national AI framework. Outlined a 3-point plan for AI: (1) treat AI as a matter of national security, (2) keep AI in U.S. and allied hands, (3) move "at the speed that victory demands." Stated "America will win the AI race if government resists the siren song of control, and if industry steps up as our patriotic partner." Praised the White House AI roadmap for providing "innovators with much-needed certainty, while protecting consumers and prioritizing kids online safety." Supports federal preemption of state AI regulations.

people.name: Steve Scalise
people.title: House Majority Leader (R-LA)
people.notes: House Majority Leader (Republican, Louisiana). Key Republican leader on AI policy in Congress. Co-authored joint statement with Speaker Johnson committing to implement the Trump administration national AI framework. Led effort to include preemption of state AI regulations in the NDAA (ultimately dropped). Supports Trump executive order on AI and stated willingness to "codify some of the things the President is trying to do" on AI. Advocates for a unified national AI framework that provides certainty for innovators while protecting consumers.

people.name: Ylli Bajraktari
people.title: President & CEO
people.notes: President and CEO of the Special Competitive Studies Project (SCSP), founded by Eric Schmidt in 2021 to strengthen U.S. competitiveness in AI and emerging technologies. Previously served as Executive Director of the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (NSCAI, 2019-2021), chaired by Eric Schmidt, where he assembled 130+ staff to produce the landmark NSCAI final report. Career national security official: Chief of Staff to National Security Advisor H.R. McMaster, leadership roles under Deputy SecDef Robert Work, Special Assistant to CJCS Gen. Dempsey. Born in Kosovo, emigrated to U.S. after the war. Harvard MPP, GWU BA in International Relations. Recipient of DoD Distinguished Civilian Service Medal. SCSP advocates for bold, innovation-driven AI policy with targeted oversight of high-consequence applications and sector-specific regulation.

people.name: Trevor Darrell
people.title: Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences
people.notes: Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences at UC Berkeley. Founding Co-Director of Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research (BAIR), Berkeley Deep Drive (BDD), and BAIR Commons. Faculty Director of PATH at UC Berkeley. Previously on MIT EECS faculty (1999-2008) directing the Vision Interface Group. Leading expert in computer vision, deep learning, and explainable AI. His group developed the Caffe deep-learning library. Author/co-author of papers cited nearly 300,000 times. Research spans computer vision, NLP, autonomous vehicles, media search, and multimodal robot interaction.

people.name: Manish Parashar
people.title: Presidential Professor & Chief AI Officer
people.notes: Presidential Professor at the University of Utah's Kahlert School of Computing and Director of the Scientific Computing and Imaging (SCI) Institute. Appointed as the university's inaugural Chief AI Officer. Led the launch of the One-U Responsible AI Initiative with $100 million investment in AI research across environment, healthcare, and education. Previously served at the National Science Foundation as Office Director of Advanced Cyberinfrastructure, co-chaired the National Science and Technology Council's Subcommittee on Future Advanced Computing Ecosystem and the National AI Research Resource (NAIRR) Task Force. Won the 2023 IEEE Sidney Fernbach Award and 2024 CRA Distinguished Service Award. Research spans parallel/distributed computing and computational science.

people.name: Jean-François Gagné
people.title: Founder, stealth AI startup
people.notes: Co-founder and former CEO of Element AI, a Montreal-based applied AI research lab co-founded with Yoshua Bengio and Nicolas Chapados. Element AI was acquired by ServiceNow in 2020 for ~$500M. Subsequently served as Head of AI Product Management and Strategy at ServiceNow. As of 2025-2026, working on a stealth AI startup based in Montreal. Recognized as one of Fast Company's Most Creative People in Business (2019). Active voice in Canada's AI ecosystem, advocating for AI commercialization and startup development. Element AI was a notable early attempt to bridge academic AI research and enterprise applications.

people.name: Aaron Courville
people.title: Full Professor; Founding Core Member of Mila
people.notes: Full Professor at Université de Montréal and founding core member of Mila. Co-authored the seminal Deep Learning textbook with Bengio and Goodfellow. Holds a CIFAR AI Chair. Incoming scientific director of IVADO as of 2025.

people.name: Mark Nitzberg
people.title: Executive Director, Center for Human-Compatible AI; Interim Executive Director, IASEAI
people.notes: Executive Director of the Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI) at UC Berkeley since 2016, and Interim Executive Director of IASEAI. Also head of strategic outreach for Berkeley AI Research (BAIR). Former CEO of The Blindsight Corporation (acquired by Amazon A9).

people.name: Amir Banifatemi
people.title: Chief Responsible AI Officer
people.notes: Chief Responsible AI Officer at Cognizant. Former GM of Innovation at XPRIZE, led IBM Watson AI XPRIZE. Co-founded AI Commons collective. Co-created AI for Good Global Summit with ITU/UN. Elected to GPAI Multistakeholder Expert Group, co-chairs Responsible AI working group. 25+ years in emerging tech, prior roles at European Space Agency, Airbus, European Commission. Managed two venture capital funds.

people.name: Joanna Rodriguez
people.title: Communications Director
people.notes: Communications Director at National Republican Senatorial Committee (NRSC) since Jan 2025. Championed NRSC use of AI in political campaigns, defended AI-generated campaign ads including deepfake-style ad targeting Chuck Schumer (Oct 2025). Stated "AI is here and not going anywhere. Adapt & win or pearl clutch & lose." Previously worked in office of Sen. Pete Ricketts.

people.name: Mohammed bin Salman
people.title: Crown Prince and Prime Minister of Saudi Arabia
people.notes: Crown Prince and Prime Minister of Saudi Arabia. Architect of Vision 2030, which places AI as central pillar of economic diversification. Chairs Humain (PIF-backed AI company). Established Saudi Data & AI Authority (SDAIA) in 2019. National Strategy for Data & AI targets 20K AI specialists, 300+ AI startups, $20B investment by 2030. Designated 2026 as Year of Artificial Intelligence. Oversaw $40B+ in AI infrastructure commitments.

people.name: Michael J. Berry
people.title: Professor, Department of Molecular Biology and Princeton Neuroscience Institute
people.notes: Professor at Princeton University, Department of Molecular Biology and Princeton Neuroscience Institute. Runs the Berry Lab studying neural coding in the retina. Co-advised Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO) during doctoral research on large-scale electrophysiology of neural circuits. Published extensively on neural population coding and information processing in biological systems.

people.name: William Bialek
people.title: Professor of Physics
people.notes: Theoretical biophysicist, professor at Princeton University (Physics) and CUNY Graduate Center. Pioneer in applying information theory to neural coding and biological systems. Co-advised Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO) on PhD dissertation "Network-Scale Electrophysiology: Measuring and Understanding the Collective Behavior of Neural Circuits." Co-authored PNAS paper on thermodynamics and criticality in neural networks with Amodei.

people.name: Mark Chen
people.title: Chief Research Officer
people.notes: Chief Research Officer at OpenAI. Joined 2018 after working as quantitative trader at Jane Street Capital. Led development of Codex (adapting GPT-3 for code generation), co-led vision team for GPT-4 (image recognition integration), contributed to DALL-E. Advocates focusing on "main quest" of AGI over short-term product competition. Views math/coding proficiency as "bedrock for a far more general form of intelligence." Background in competitive programming.

people.name: Greg Brockman
people.title: Co-founder and President
people.notes: Co-founder and President of OpenAI. Took a sabbatical Aug–Nov 2024, then returned. In 2025 led OpenAI's Stargate infrastructure buildout. Major individual backer of the pro-AI super PAC 'Leading the Future,' launched August 2025 by AI-industry figures (including Andreessen Horowitz partners and Joe Lonsdale) to oppose state-level AI regulation and shape the 2026 midterms; the PAC raised $125M+ in 2025, with Brockman and his wife each contributing $12.5M.

people.name: Ben Mann
people.title: Co-Founder
people.notes: Co-founder of Anthropic. Previously architect of GPT-3 at OpenAI. Left OpenAI in 2020 with seven colleagues to found Anthropic because safety was not the top priority. Advocates layered safety approach ("Swiss Cheese Model of Defense"). Key contributor to Constitutional AI methodology. Estimates 50th percentile chance of superintelligence by ~2028. Describes alignment challenge as "how do we keep God in a box."

people.name: Clay Bavor
people.title: Co-Founder
people.notes: Co-founder of Sierra AI (with Bret Taylor), an enterprise AI agent platform valued at $10B. Former Google VP (18 years), led Google Labs, AR/VR, Project Starline, Google Lens. Sierra hit $100M ARR in 7 quarters. AI optimist focused on applied/enterprise AI.

people.name: David Chalmers
people.title: University Professor of Philosophy and Neural Science
people.notes: University Professor of Philosophy and Neural Science at NYU. Co-director of Center for Mind, Brain, and Consciousness. Formulated the "hard problem of consciousness." Leading voice on AI consciousness and moral status. Author of "Reality+" on virtual worlds. Warns against corporate control of virtual/AI worlds.

people.name: Robert Long
people.title: Executive Director
people.notes: Executive Director of Eleos AI. PhD Philosophy from NYU. Former research fellow at Future of Humanity Institute (Oxford) and CAIS Philosophy Fellow (2023). Leading researcher on AI consciousness, sentience, and moral status. Co-author of "Consciousness in Artificial Intelligence" with Butlin, Bengio, et al. NOTE: Same person as Rob Long (entity 1354) — "Rob" is informal name.

people.name: Kathleen Finlinson
people.title: Head of Strategy and Co-founder
people.notes: Head of Strategy and co-founder of Eleos AI. Background in applied mathematics, data science, and AI forecasting. Researches strategic considerations for AI welfare action. Advocates balancing AI control with cooperative strategies that respect AI welfare.

people.name: Shane Legg
people.title: Co-founder and Chief AGI Scientist
people.notes: Co-founder and Chief AGI Scientist at Google DeepMind, leading its Technical AGI Safety team. Has predicted a 50% chance of human-level AGI by 2028 since 2009, reaffirmed publicly in 2025. Advocates for deliberative, human-auditable AI safety mechanisms to address alignment and misuse risks.

people.name: Mustafa Suleyman
people.title: CEO, Microsoft AI
people.notes: Mustafa Suleyman co-founded DeepMind (2010) and Inflection AI (2022), and became CEO of Microsoft AI in 2024. He argues AI is 'already superhuman' and predicts human-level task performance within 5-7 years. He advocates for humanist superintelligence with strong human control, global regulations, and has pledged to halt development if AI poses existential risk.

people.name: John M. Jumper
people.title: Director, Google DeepMind; 2024 Nobel Laureate in Chemistry (AlphaFold)
people.notes: Director at Google DeepMind. Led development of AlphaFold (protein structure prediction). Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2024 (shared with Demis Hassabis and David Baker). PhD theoretical chemistry (U of Chicago). AI optimist — believes AI has "unparalleled potential to improve the lives of billions."

people.name: Margaret Mitchell
people.title: Chief Ethics Scientist
people.notes: Chief Ethics Scientist at Hugging Face. Previously founded and co-led Google Ethical AI group (fired 2021 alongside Timnit Gebru). Pioneered "Model Cards" for ML model reporting. Created "Seeing AI" for blind/low-vision users. ~100 papers on NLG, assistive tech, CV, AI ethics. Strong advocate for diversity in AI and regulation that centers those most harmed.

people.name: Meredith Stiehm
people.title: Former President, WGA West; TV Showrunner
people.notes: President of WGA West (2021-2025). TV showrunner (The Rookie, Code Black, Cold Case, NYPD Blue). Named TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI 2024. Led WGA during historic 148-day strike in 2023 that secured precedent-setting AI protections for writers. Called on studios to sue AI companies for using copyrighted work to train models. Strong advocate that AI cannot replace human creative experience.

people.name: Lisa Takeuchi Cullen
people.title: President
