people.notes: President of WGA East (elected Sept 2023). First person of color to hold the position. TV writer/producer (Empire, Five Days at Memorial). Participated in April 2024 White House roundtable on AI and Workers Rights with Acting Secretary of Labor Julie Su. Launched WGA East AI Task Force to track AI's effect on writing professions. Strong enforcement of 2023 MBA AI protections for film/TV/streaming writers.

people.name: Molly Kinder
people.title: Senior Fellow
people.notes: Senior Fellow at Brookings Institution, leading multi-year project on generative AI's impact on work and workers. Nationally recognized expert on AI, economic inequality, and future of work. Advocates for worker voice in AI implementation, enhanced union protections, and public investment in workforce training. Argues U.S. lags Europe in institutional mechanisms to help workers navigate AI transition. Work cited by Sen. Chris Murphy.

people.name: Joe Biden
people.title: 46th President of the United States (2021–2025)
people.notes: 46th U.S. President (2021-2025). Issued Executive Order 14110 in October 2023, the most comprehensive U.S. AI governance action to that point, requiring safety reporting from frontier AI developers and coordinating federal agency standards. Also released the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights in 2022.

people.name: Tejas N. Narechania
people.title: Professor of Law
people.notes: Professor of Law at UC Berkeley School of Law. Faculty co-director of Berkeley Center for Law & Technology. Former clerk for Justice Breyer (SCOTUS) and Judge Diane Wood (7th Circuit). Former FCC special counsel (2012-2013). Published 'An Antimonopoly Approach to Governing AI' (Yale Law & Policy Review, 2024) with Ganesh Sitaraman — argues AI infrastructure warrants regulation via structural separation, nondiscrimination, and public cloud options. Work cited in 2024 Economic Report of the President. Participated in White House AI competition policy meeting.

people.name: Justin Johnson
people.title: Co-founder
people.notes: Co-founder of World Labs (spatial intelligence startup, founded 2024 with Fei-Fei Li, Christoph Lassner, Ben Mildenhall). Assistant Professor of CS at University of Michigan. PhD from Stanford (advised by Fei-Fei Li). Key contributor to early PyTorch development. Created CLEVR visual reasoning benchmark. NSF CAREER Award recipient. World Labs raised $1B+ at $5B valuation, building Large World Models for 3D perception.

people.name: Christoph Lassner
people.title: Co-founder
people.notes: Co-founder of World Labs (spatial intelligence startup, founded 2024 with Fei-Fei Li, Justin Johnson, Ben Mildenhall). PhD from University of Tubingen. Previously led research teams at Epic Games and Meta Reality Labs. Created Pulsar differentiable renderer (precursor to Gaussian Splatting). Built human pose estimation for Amazon Halo (via Bodylabs acquisition). Specialist in 3D computer vision and graphics.

people.name: Ben Mildenhall
people.title: Co-founder
people.notes: Co-founder of World Labs (spatial intelligence startup, founded 2024 with Fei-Fei Li, Justin Johnson, Christoph Lassner). Co-creator of NeRF (Neural Radiance Fields), ECCV 2020 honorable mention for best paper. PhD from UC Berkeley (advised by Ren Ng), Hertz Fellow. Former Google Research scientist (2021-2023). Research spans computational photography, 3D reconstruction, and text-to-3D generation.

people.name: John Etchemendy
people.title: Professor of Philosophy; Co-Director, Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI
people.notes: Stanford professor of philosophy and former provost (2000-2017). Co-founded and co-directs Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI (HAI) with Fei-Fei Li since 2019. Leading advocate for national AI research resource to counter industry concentration of AI compute and data. Argues 'the science has outgrown the university model of research.' HAI promotes human-centered approach: AI evaluation and accountability over hype. Expert in logic and philosophy of language.

people.name: Scott Alexander
people.title: Blogger and Writer, Astral Codex Ten
people.notes: Blogger and writer behind Astral Codex Ten (formerly Slate Star Codex). Psychiatrist by training. Arguably the most influential AI forecasting voice in the rationalist/EA community. Co-authored 'AI 2027' scenario analysis (April 2025) with Daniel Kokotajlo, modeling fast intelligence explosion by 2027-2028. Supports AI safety research funding (ACX Grants). Nuanced on regulation: supports safety standards but worries about US-China race dynamics leading to worst-case policy tradeoffs. Personal AGI median later in 2020s/early 2030s.

people.name: Thomas Larsen
people.title: Researcher
people.notes: Researcher at the AI Futures Project focused on dangerous capability evaluations, AI safety measurement, and compute governance. Co-authored the AI 2027 scenario forecast with Daniel Kokotajlo and Eli Lifland. Previously founded the Center for AI Policy; also a MATS mentor.

people.name: Eli Lifland
people.title: Founding Researcher
people.notes: Founding Researcher at AI Futures Project and leading AI forecaster; co-author of the AI 2027 scenario (April 2025) alongside Daniel Kokotajlo, Thomas Larsen, and Romeo Dean. Previously forecasted at Samotsvety and worked at Ought and SaferAI. Co-founded Sage (a forecasting platform) and has served as guest fund manager at the Long Term Future Fund. Publicly argues that short AGI timelines are underweighted and that existential risk from AI is not taken seriously enough.

people.name: Romeo Dean
people.title: Researcher
people.notes: Researcher at AI Futures Project and co-author of the AI 2027 scenario forecast (April 2025). Graduated cum laude from Harvard with a concurrent AB/SM in Computer Science; previously an AI Policy Fellow at the Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS) and an Astra Fellow at Constellation. Specializes in forecasting AI chip production, government intervention, and international coordination dynamics. Also a mentor in the MATS (Machine Learning Alignment Theory Scholars) program.

people.name: Philipp Hennig
people.title: Chair for Methods of Machine Learning; Co-director of Tübingen AI Center
people.notes: Chair for Methods of Machine Learning at University of Tübingen. Co-director of Tübingen AI Center since July 2025. Pioneer of probabilistic numerics — treating numerical computation as machine learning inference. Author of 'Probabilistic Numerics: Computation as Machine Learning' (Cambridge University Press, 2022). Previously at Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems. Organizer of ProbNum conference series since 2012. Over 11,000 citations.

people.name: Dave McCormick
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-PA)
people.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (sworn in Jan 2025). Former CEO of Bridgewater Associates (2020-2022), world's largest hedge fund. West Point graduate, Bronze Star recipient (82nd Airborne, Gulf War). Under Secretary of Treasury for International Affairs under George W. Bush (2007-2009). Strong AI-and-energy nexus advocate — hosted inaugural Pennsylvania Energy and Innovation Summit (July 2025) at CMU with President Trump. Champions Pittsburgh as AI hub ('AI Avenue'). Pro-innovation stance on AI with focus on national competitiveness and energy infrastructure.

people.name: Roger Wicker
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-MS); Chairman, Armed Services Committee
people.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Mississippi (since 2007). Chairman of Senate Armed Services Committee (119th Congress). Key defense AI champion — secured AI funding in FY2025 and FY2026 NDAAs including $10M for autonomous AI data fusion C2 capability and $10M for AI-enabled Army multi-domain operations (drone swarm defense). Authored 'Restoring Freedom's Forge' plan to reform Pentagon procurement and embrace commercial AI innovation. Pushed $925B defense policy bill (FY2026). Focus: AI for military modernization to counter China.

people.name: Jeanne Shaheen
people.title: Former U.S. Senator (D-NH)
people.notes: Democratic U.S. Senator from New Hampshire (2009–January 2025) who retired after choosing not to seek re-election in 2024. Took targeted AI positions: secured $47.7M for NIST's AI Safety Institute, pushed to restrict advanced AI chip exports to China, and urged DOJ/CISA to address AI-generated deepfakes threatening elections. Consistent with a moderate, national-security-forward approach to AI governance rather than broad regulation.

people.name: Rob Portman
people.title: Former U.S. Senator (R-OH)
people.notes: Rob Portman (R-OH) served as a U.S. Senator from 2011 until his retirement in January 2023, having chosen not to seek re-election in 2022. While in office he co-founded the bipartisan Senate Artificial Intelligence Caucus with Martin Heinrich (D-NM) in 2019 and co-introduced the Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act, authorizing a $2.2 billion federal investment in AI R&D and workforce development. He also co-passed the AI for Armed Forces Act and National AI Research Resource Task Force Act via successive NDAAs. His AI stance emphasized national competitiveness, workforce readiness, and national security rather than heavy-handed regulation.

people.name: Peter Welch
people.title: U.S. Senator (D-VT)
people.notes: Democratic U.S. Senator from Vermont (since 2023, formerly House rep since 2007). Most active Senate voice on AI copyright/consent legislation. Introduced ~6 AI bills including: TRAIN Act (subpoena power for copyright holders to discover AI training data use), AI CONSENT Act (require consent before using personal data for AI training), Digital Platform Commission Act (new federal agency for platform regulation). Serves on Judiciary Committee (copyright jurisdiction) and Finance Committee. Champions Vermont creative economy and artist protections from AI.

people.name: Catherine Cortez Masto
people.title: U.S. Senator (D-NV)
people.notes: Catherine Cortez Masto (D-NV) is a U.S. Senator who narrowly won re-election in 2022 and serves until January 2029. She focuses on consumer protection in AI: her IOGAN Act (signed 2020) directed NSF and NIST to research deepfake detection, and she has backed the TAKE IT DOWN Act (signed 2025) criminalizing non-consensual AI-generated intimate images. She co-sponsored legislation establishing the National Security Commission on AI with Joni Ernst, and urged the FTC to prioritize consumer protection from AI misuse. Her approach is bipartisan and targets harmful AI applications rather than broad regulation.

people.name: Josh Hawley
people.title: Senator (R-MO)
people.notes: Republican U.S. Senator (MO). Introduced the Decoupling America's AI Capabilities from China Act (2025) and the bipartisan GUARD Act banning AI chatbots for minors (2026). Opposes preempting state AI laws and advocates targeted consumer and child-safety regulation.

people.name: Jim Banks
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-IN)
people.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Indiana (since Jan 2025, formerly House rep 2017-2025). Serves on Senate Armed Services Committee (subcommittees: Personnel, Seapower, Strategic Forces) and Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee. Previously ranking member of House Cyber, Innovative Technologies, and Information Systems subcommittee (117th Congress). Also served on House Select Committee on U.S.-China Strategic Competition. Conservative defense hawk with national security focus. No significant public AI-specific policy positions identified.

people.name: Shelley Moore Capito
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-WV)
people.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from West Virginia (since 2015). Key AI legislation author: VET AI Act (with Hickenlooper) directs NIST to develop voluntary guidelines for AI assurance and verification; AI Scam Prevention Act prohibits AI-generated deepfakes for fraud. Serves on Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee. Pragmatic, bipartisan approach to AI — favors voluntary industry guidelines over heavy regulation. Also focused on broadband/rural connectivity relevant to AI access.

people.name: John Thune
people.title: Senate Majority Leader (2025–present); U.S. Senator, South Dakota (2005–present)
people.notes: Senate Majority Leader (Jan 2025-present), senior U.S. Senator from South Dakota. Key AI legislation gatekeeper — co-authoring bipartisan 'light-touch' AI bill with Sen. Klobuchar requiring risk/impact assessments and self-certification for critical-impact AI systems. Opposed 10-year moratorium on state AI regulation authority. Supported GAIN AI Act in FY2026 NDAA to keep advanced AI chips available to U.S. innovators over adversaries. Previously chaired Senate Commerce Committee where he led early AI policy work.

people.name: Bill Cassidy
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-LA); Chair, HELP Committee
people.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Louisiana, Chair of Senate HELP Committee (2025-present). Released major AI white paper favoring flexible, sector-specific regulation over one-size-fits-all approach. Proposed novel concept of using AI to regulate AI — government-utilized AI as industry watchdog. Co-sponsored RAISE Act empowering states to include AI/emerging tech in K-12 curriculum. Introduced Future of Artificial Intelligence Innovation Act of 2026 (S.3952). Focus areas: AI in healthcare, education, workforce.

people.name: Jacob Helberg
people.title: Under Secretary of State for Economic Growth, Energy, and Environment
people.notes: Under Secretary of State for Economic Growth, Energy, and Environment (confirmed Oct 2025). Former Senior Advisor to Palantir CEO. Retained investments in OpenAI, SpaceX, and dozens of AI/defense/fintech companies. Launched Pax Silica (Dec 2025) — State Dept's flagship AI and supply chain security initiative. Former U.S.-China Economic and Security Review Commission member. Founded Hill and Valley Forum connecting Silicon Valley to Capitol Hill. Major China hawk advocating hardline tech competition stance.

people.name: Matt Pottinger
people.title: Chairman, China Program
people.notes: Chairman of FDD's China Program. Former Deputy National Security Advisor (2019-2021) and NSC Senior Director for Asia under Trump first term. Led shift in U.S. China policy. Now prominent voice on AI national security — advocates restricting China's access to U.S. AI labs and technology. Former journalist and Marine intelligence officer in East Asia. Co-author in Foreign Affairs on China strategy. Warns Beijing actively exploits U.S. tech openness for military AI buildup.

people.name: Alex LeBrun
people.title: CEO
people.notes: CEO of AMI Labs (Advanced Machine Intelligence), co-founded with Yann LeCun (2025). Serial French AI entrepreneur: founded VirtuOz (acquired by Nuance), Wit.ai (acquired by Facebook 2015 after 21 months), and Nabla (healthcare AI, remains chairman). Worked under LeCun at Meta FAIR after Wit.ai acquisition. 24 years building AI products spanning chatbots, NLU, virtual assistants, healthcare AI. Graduate of École Polytechnique.

people.name: Lisa Hansmann
people.title: Director
people.notes: Former Special Assistant to the President and Senior Advisor in the Biden White House Chief of Staff office, where she drove implementation of the Inflation Reduction Act, Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, and CHIPS and Science Act. Previously Senior Policy Advisor at the White House National Economic Council (2021-2023) focusing on industrial strategy and energy market resilience. Now Director at Foundry Logic, scaling businesses addressing structural supply-demand imbalances. Co-authored AI energy policy proposals with Brian Deese (MIT) on 'National AI Additionality Framework' for managing AI data center electricity demands. Stanford SIEPR affiliated.

people.name: Gabriel Unger
people.title: Postdoctoral Fellow, Stanford Digital Economy Lab
people.notes: Postdoctoral fellow at the Stanford Digital Economy Lab directed by Erik Brynjolfsson. Macroeconomist studying AI's economic impacts. Co-authored 'The Macroeconomics of Artificial Intelligence' (IMF Finance & Development, Dec 2023) with Brynjolfsson, examining whether transformative AI can fulfill its revolutionary economic potential. Research addresses three fundamental questions: shared vision for AI's economic role, implications for growth theory, and redesigning education and social connections in the age of transformative AI.

people.name: Andrew McAfee
people.title: Principal Research Scientist, MIT Sloan; Co-Director, MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy
people.notes: Principal Research Scientist at MIT Sloan School of Management. Co-founder and co-director of MIT's Initiative on the Digital Economy (IDE). Co-authored 'The Second Machine Age' (2014) with Erik Brynjolfsson — influential book arguing technology races ahead of skills, organizations, and economic institutions. Also authored 'More from Less' (2019). Inaugural Visiting Fellow at Google's Technology and Society organization. Research focuses on how digital technologies reshape work, firms, and the economy. Prominent voice on AI's labor market disruption potential.

people.name: Lindsey Raymond
people.title: Postdoctoral Fellow, Economics and Computation
people.notes: Economist and AI researcher. Postdoctoral fellow in Economics and Computation at Microsoft Research. Will join MIT as Assistant Professor (joint Economics and Computer Science) in summer 2026. PhD from MIT (2024), BA from Yale. Former staffer at White House Council of Economic Advisers (2021-2022). Key research: 'Generative AI at Work' (with Brynjolfsson and Li), studying 5,179 customer support agents — found AI tools increased productivity 14% on average, 35% for novice workers. Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Fellow. Research focus: AI's effects on labor markets, firms, and global organization of work.

people.name: Claire Cardie
people.title: John C. Ford Professor of Engineering in Computer Science and Information Science
people.notes: John C. Ford Professor of Engineering in Computer Science and Information Science at Cornell University. Leading NLP researcher specializing in coreference resolution, information extraction, and opinion mining. Founding chair of Cornell's Department of Information Science and led development of its academic programs. AAAI Fellow (2024), ACM Fellow (2019, 'for contributions to NLP including coreference resolution, information and opinion extraction'), AAAS Fellow (2021). Doctoral advisor to Yejin Choi (edge 1611). Research group develops ML/neural network techniques for large-scale NLP tasks.

people.name: Jeff Hancock
people.title: Harry and Norman Chandler Professor of Communication; Founding Director, Stanford Social Media Lab
people.notes: Harry and Norman Chandler Professor of Communication at Stanford University. Founding director of the Stanford Social Media Lab. Research focuses on AI's effects on trust, deception, and human communication — from AI-generated text detection to social media well-being impacts. His TED talk 'The Future of Lying' (2012) has 1M+ views. Author of 80+ journal articles. Collaborator with Yejin Choi (edge 1612). Notable controversy: in 2024, as expert witness defending a state deepfake law, he submitted a court filing relying on ChatGPT that contained fabricated citations; a federal judge barred his testimony.

people.name: John Tasioulas
people.title: Professor of Ethics and Legal Philosophy
people.notes: Greek-Australian moral and legal philosopher. Was inaugural Director of the Institute for Ethics in AI at the University of Oxford (resigned Sep 2025 during harassment investigation). Professor of Ethics and Legal Philosophy, Senior Research Fellow at Balliol College Oxford. Rhodes Scholar. Previously held chairs at King's College London, UCL, and University of Glasgow. Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Fellow — his project takes a 'humanistic approach' to AI ethics centered on distinctive value and capabilities of human beings. Collaborator with Yejin Choi (edge 1613). Published widely on moral philosophy, legal philosophy, and philosophy of technology.

people.name: Matt Salganik
people.title: Alexander Stewart 1886 Professor of Sociology
people.notes: Matthew J. Salganik is the Alexander Stewart 1886 Professor of Sociology at Princeton University. Leading figure in computational social science — co-founded the Summer Institute in Computational Social Science (SICSS) with Chris Bail in 2017, now a global annual program training researchers in data science methods for social research. Author of 'Bit by Bit: Social Research in the Digital Age' (2017). Affiliated with Princeton AI Lab, Princeton Precision Health, and Center for Information Technology Policy (CITP). Research focuses on social networks, digital traces, and novel survey methods. Won Outstanding Article Award from ASA Mathematical Sociology Section (2005) and Outstanding Statistical Application Award from ASA (2008). Published in Science, PNAS, Sociological Methodology.

people.name: Melania Trump
people.title: First Lady of the United States
people.notes: Former and current First Lady of the United States. Emerged as unexpected AI policy voice in 2025-2026 through education and child safety lens. Launched Presidential AI Challenge — nationwide contest inviting K-12 students to develop AI projects addressing community challenges. Convened White House Task Force on Artificial Intelligence Education (September 2025) with tech CEOs and Cabinet members. Advocated 'watchful guidance' approach to AI growth. Championed the TAKE IT DOWN Act (signed May 2025) criminalizing non-consensual sharing of explicit images including AI-generated deepfakes — grew out of her BE BEST initiative on child online safety. Hosted deepfake victim Elliston Berry at White House to amplify awareness.

people.name: Sean McGarvey
people.title: President
people.notes: President of North America's Building Trades Unions (NABTU), an alliance of 14 national/international unions representing 3+ million skilled craft professionals in US and Canada. Glazier by trade — began career in 1981 with Glaziers Local Union 252 in Philadelphia, worked up through IUPAT leadership. Key figure at AI-labor intersection: partnered with Sam Altman/OpenAI (March 2026) to ensure AI data center infrastructure construction supports union careers and registered apprenticeship programs. NABTU invests $2.5B annually in workforce training. Positions unions as essential builders of AI infrastructure rather than victims of automation. Named to U.S. Commerce Department Workforce Policy Advisory Board. Collaborator with Randi Weingarten on labor issues.

people.name: Brian Long
people.title: CEO & Co-Founder
people.notes: CEO and Co-Founder of Adaptive Security, an AI-native cybersecurity startup simulating deepfake and GenAI attacks for corporate defense training. Serial entrepreneur: previously co-founded Attentive (SMS/email marketing platform, grew to $500M+ ARR, 8,000+ customers, 1,000+ employees) and TapCommerce (acquired by Twitter, where he served as Senior Director leading mobile teams for 19 months). Raised ~$900M+ in venture capital across ventures from Sequoia Capital and other top-tier firms. Under his leadership Adaptive Security raised $136M+ from OpenAI and Andreessen Horowitz. University of Pennsylvania graduate; on permanent leave from NYU Stern School of Business.

people.name: Anna McAdams
people.title: Advocate, deepfake child safety legislation
people.notes: Mother of Elliston Berry and co-advocate for deepfake child safety legislation. After her 14-year-old daughter was targeted by a classmate who used AI to create non-consensual deepfake nude images (Aledo, Texas, fall 2023), McAdams gave emotional testimony before the Texas Senate Committee on Criminal Justice and took the story public nationally. Met with lawmakers in Washington including Sen. Ted Cruz and First Lady Melania Trump to push for federal legislation. Their advocacy was instrumental in shaping the TAKE IT DOWN Act (signed May 2025), which criminalizes sharing non-consensual explicit images including AI-generated deepfakes. Continues to advocate for stronger tech company accountability and child digital safety protections.

people.name: Eric Schmitt
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-MO)
people.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from Missouri (sworn in Jan 2023). Former Missouri AG. Key AI legislation: co-introduced Protecting AI and Cloud Competition in Defense Act with Sen. Warren (Dec 2024, reintroduced May 2025) to prevent big tech monopolies in DoD AI/cloud procurement. Co-introduced AI and Critical Technology Workforce Framework Act with Sen. Peters to build America's AI workforce pipeline. Secured $250M for AI in U.S. Cyber Command (One Big Beautiful Bill, Jul 2025). Secured DoD AI cybersecurity requirements provision in FY26 NDAA. Serves on Armed Services and Commerce committees. Frames AI policy around national security competition with China and ensuring small/mid-size startups can compete with big tech.

people.name: Thom Tillis
people.title: U.S. Senator (R-NC)
people.notes: Republican U.S. Senator from North Carolina (2015-present). Key AI legislation: co-sponsored Secure Artificial Intelligence Act with Sen. Warner (2024) creating voluntary AI security incident database and NSA AI Security Center. Co-introduced federal AI procurement guardrails bill with Sen. Peters requiring agencies to assess AI risks before deployment. Co-sponsored NO FAKES Act (2024) with Sens. Coons, Blackburn, Klobuchar protecting voice/visual likenesses from AI-generated replicas. Notably was sole senator to vote against removing 10-year state AI regulation moratorium from reconciliation bill (Jul 2025), supporting federal preemption of state AI laws. Serves on Judiciary, Banking, and Armed Services committees.

people.name: Josh Gottheimer
people.title: U.S. Representative (D-NJ)
people.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from New Jersey's 5th district (2017-present). Centrist Democrat on House Intelligence Committee, Ranking Member of NSA & Cyber Subcommittee. Co-Chair of House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy (Dec 2025). Key AI legislation: Unleashing AI Innovation in Financial Services Act (Innovation Labs for AI testing at financial regulators), Advanced AI Security Readiness Act (Jun 2025, directing NSA to develop AI security playbook with Rep. LaHood), AI Workforce Training Act. Critical of White House's Mar 2026 AI framework as blocking state laws without sufficient consumer protections. Advocates 'Smart AI' regulation balancing innovation with state regulatory carveouts.

people.name: Yvette Clarke
people.title: U.S. Representative (D-NY)
people.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from New York's 9th district (2007-present). Chair of Congressional Black Caucus. Early AI regulation champion — introduced Algorithmic Accountability Act (first version 2019 with Sens. Wyden and Booker, updated 2022 and 2025) requiring companies to audit high-impact AI systems. Introduced DEEPFAKES Accountability Act (2021, 2023 with Rep. Ivey) requiring digital watermarking and criminalizing malicious deepfakes. Introduced REAL Political Ads Act requiring AI disclosure on political advertising. Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Jayapal, Lee, Pressley) banning algorithmic discrimination. Focuses on protecting marginalized communities, election integrity, and AI transparency.

people.name: Pramila Jayapal
people.title: U.S. Representative (D-WA)
people.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from Washington's 7th district (2017-present). Chair of Congressional Progressive Caucus (2021-2024). Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (introduced Sep 2024, reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Clarke, Lee, Pressley) — comprehensive legislation banning algorithmic discrimination, requiring pre- and post-deployment testing, mandating transparency of algorithms used in critical decisions (housing, employment, credit, education, law enforcement). Bill would establish civil rights protections when personal data is processed by algorithms. Frames AI regulation through civil rights and equity lens, emphasizing protection for marginalized communities.

people.name: Summer Lee
people.title: U.S. Representative (D-PA)
people.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from Pennsylvania's 12th district (2023-present). Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Jayapal, Clarke, Pressley) banning algorithmic discrimination in critical decisions. Co-introduced Eliminating BIAS Act with Sen. Markey mandating dedicated Office of Civil Rights in every federal agency that uses, funds, or oversees AI to combat AI-driven bias and discrimination. Stated: 'Discrimination is illegal when it's done by a person and it should be just as illegal when it's done by an algorithm.' Focuses on racial, gender, and disability-based inequities replicated by AI systems in jobs, housing, healthcare, loans, and law enforcement.

people.name: Ayanna Pressley
people.title: U.S. Representative (D-MA)
people.notes: Democratic U.S. Representative from Massachusetts's 7th district (2019-present). Member of 'The Squad.' Co-lead of AI Civil Rights Act (reintroduced Dec 2025 with Sen. Markey, Reps. Jayapal, Clarke, Lee) banning algorithmic discrimination. Called on Congress (Mar 2024) to address AI risks to marginalized communities. Co-introduced No Biometric Barriers Housing Act prohibiting facial/biometric recognition in federally funded public housing. Emphasizes AI's disparate impact on communities of color — concerns include facial recognition criminalizing people of color, deepfake pornography targeting women, and biased algorithms denying access to critical resources. Frames AI oversight through equity and civil rights.

people.name: Elizabeth Warren
people.title: U.S. Senator, Massachusetts (2013–present)
people.notes: Democratic U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (2013-present). Major voice on AI antitrust and competition policy. Co-introduced Protecting AI and Cloud Competition in Defense Act with Sen. Schmitt (Dec 2024, reintroduced May 2025) preventing big tech monopolies in DoD AI procurement. Co-sponsored GAIN AI Act ensuring U.S. customers get priority access to advanced AI chips over adversaries. Launched investigation (2024) into Google-Anthropic and Microsoft-OpenAI partnerships with Sen. Wyden. Wrote DOJ supporting Nvidia antitrust probe (Sep 2024). Pressed OpenAI on whistleblower protections with Rep. Trahan (Aug 2024). Co-sponsor of AI Civil Rights Act (Dec 2025). Led opposition (with Sen. Markey, Nov 2025) to big tech's attempt to insert 10-year AI regulation moratorium into NDAA.

people.name: Ken Buck
people.title: Former U.S. Representative (R-CO)
people.notes: Former Republican U.S. Representative from Colorado's 4th district (2015-2024). Resigned March 22, 2024. Rare GOP champion of Big Tech antitrust enforcement — co-chaired Congressional Big Tech Antitrust Caucus with bipartisan allies. Co-sponsored bipartisan AI commission bill with Rep. Ted Lieu to develop a comprehensive federal AI regulatory framework. Co-introduced legislation preventing AI from making nuclear launch decisions. Advocated for dispersed AI competition ('five or six major generative AI competitors'). Also engaged with Georgetown Law on AI regulation dialogue. Left Congress citing frustration with party's direction on Jan. 6 and democratic norms.

people.name: Rodney Brooks
people.title: CTO
people.notes: Australian-American roboticist, MIT Professor Emeritus (Panasonic Professor of Robotics), former director of MIT CSAIL. Co-founded iRobot (1990, makers of Roomba) and Rethink Robotics (2008, Baxter/Sawyer collaborative robots). Currently CTO of Robust.AI (warehouse automation robotics). One of the most prominent AI skeptics — argues AGI and superintelligence are centuries away, that LLMs 'don't know what's true' and are 'bullshitters,' and that practical humanoid robots won't be viable for years. Maintains popular blog with annual AI prediction scorecards. Views AI risk concerns from non-practitioners as uninformed about the actual difficulty of AI development.

people.name: Tom DiNapoli
people.title: New York State Comptroller
people.notes: New York State Comptroller. Conducted multiple AI governance audits finding inadequate oversight. Key actions: (1) Audit of NY state agencies' AI use (April 2025) — found agencies 'flying solo' despite ITS's 2024 AI Policy, centralized guidance inadequate; (2) Audit of NYC's AI programs (Feb 2023) — city lacks needed guidelines, risking biased/inaccurate outcomes; (3) Sent letters to 100 companies in state pension fund (Apple, Alphabet, Walmart, Nvidia, Microsoft, Meta) demanding disclosure on AI workforce impact — jobs created/eliminated/restructured, retraining investments, AI deployment oversight. Advocates stronger government oversight of AI in public agencies and corporate governance.

people.name: Mark Muro
people.title: Senior Fellow, Brookings Metro
people.notes: Senior Fellow at Brookings Metro (Metropolitan Policy Program). Leading researcher on geographic and workforce impacts of AI and automation. Key reports: 'Automation and Artificial Intelligence: How machines are affecting people and places' (25% of US jobs face high automation exposure by 2030; non-degree workers 229% more susceptible); 'Measuring US workers' capacity to adapt to AI-driven job displacement.' Argues 'robots are coming but will bring neither apocalypse nor utopia, but both benefits and stress.' Advocates worker retraining, transition support, sharing AI benefits across organizations. Not a long-term bailout advocate but supports sustaining workers through transitions.

people.name: Darrell M. West
people.title: Senior Fellow, Center for Technology Innovation
people.notes: Senior Fellow, Center for Technology Innovation, Governance Studies at Brookings. Former VP and director of Governance Studies; former John Hazen White Professor at Brown University. Co-editor-in-chief of TechTank. Research: AI, robotics, future of work. Co-authored 'Turning Point: Policymaking in the Era of Artificial Intelligence' (2020, with John R. Allen) — governance blueprint for AI benefits while minimizing downsides. Co-authored 'Lies That Kill: A Citizen's Guide to Disinformation' (2024, with Elaine Kamarck). Argues tech industry cannot assuage public backlash by pretending harms don't exist; AI hallucinations spreading rather than improving; advocates transparency and accountability as essential for sector's long-term success.

people.name: Valerie Wirtschafter
people.title: Fellow, Foreign Policy and AI & Emerging Technology Initiative
people.notes: Fellow in Foreign Policy and the Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology Initiative at Brookings. Research spans: (1) democratic resilience and democratic erosion, (2) AI, technology, and the information space, often focused on Western Hemisphere. Uses data-driven methods that have reshaped policy at leading tech companies. Co-led development of Brookings's provisional principles for generative AI use — innovative framework helping set standards across the think tank space. PhD in political science from UCLA (2021). Work reframes discussions around underexplored media and novel challenges to the information space.

people.name: Matt Fredrikson
people.title: Associate Professor, CMU School of Computer Science; Co-Founder & CEO, Gray Swan AI
people.notes: Associate Professor, Carnegie Mellon School of Computer Science (ECE). Co-founder and CEO of Gray Swan AI. Research: secure, fair, reliable ML — adversarial robustness, privacy, formal methods. Pioneered methods to identify LLM vulnerabilities. Ran largest public red-teaming competition: 22 frontier AI agents, 44 deployment scenarios, 1.8M prompt-injection attacks, 60K+ successful policy violations. Found limited correlation between agent robustness and model size/capability. Work on representation engineering addresses honesty, harmlessness, and power-seeking. Multiple best paper awards at security/privacy conferences; USENIX Test of Time Award (2024).

people.name: Zico Kolter
people.title: Professor and Head of Machine Learning Department
people.notes: Professor of Computer Science and Head of Machine Learning Department at Carnegie Mellon University. Co-founder and Chief Scientist of Gray Swan AI. Serves on OpenAI Board of Directors and chairs Safety & Security Committee — has authority to delay or block unsafe model releases. CA and DE regulators made Kolter's safety oversight a key condition of OpenAI's restructuring. Also on Qualcomm board. PhD Stanford (2010), postdoc MIT (2010-2012). Developed first methods for deep learning with guaranteed robustness. Takes broad view of AI safety: 'not just existential concerns' but 'the entire swath of safety and security issues' in widely used systems. Award-winning papers at NeurIPS, ICML, AISTATS.

people.name: Rob Jenks
people.title: Chief Strategy Officer
people.notes: Chief Strategy Officer at Gray Swan AI (appointed January 2026). Previously SVP of Strategy & Innovation at Tanium — led product innovation, strategic partnerships, technology alliances for Fortune 2000 enterprises. Before Tanium, led product and go-to-market at C3.ai, an early enterprise AI leader. PhD in Physics from Harvard (used cloud computing and ML to study brain function). Based in Seattle. At Gray Swan, leads global AI security market expansion, bringing enterprise go-to-market expertise to AI safety/security startup.

people.name: Nick Winter
people.title: VP Product & Growth
people.notes: VP Product & Growth at Gray Swan AI (since Jan 2025), focusing on AI red-teaming and safety evaluation tools. Previously co-founded CodeCombat (Y Combinator, 2013), a gamified platform that taught CS to 20M+ learners, and Skritter (2007), a language-learning app. Co-authored D-REX benchmark paper on detecting deceptive reasoning in LLMs (2024). Background in software engineering and ed-tech entrepreneurship.

people.name: Claire Zabel
people.title: Managing Director, Short Timelines Special Projects
people.notes: Managing Director, Short Timelines Special Projects at Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy). Leads a team researching and implementing initiatives on the assumption transformative AI may arrive soon. Previously ran Global Catastrophic Risks Capacity Building (2018-2024) and oversaw Biosecurity & Pandemic Preparedness (2018-2019, 2023-2024). Former Research Analyst at GiveWell. B.S. and M.S. in Earth Systems from Stanford University. Key grantmaker shaping EA/AI safety funding landscape.

people.name: Luke Muehlhauser
people.title: Managing Director, AI Governance & Policy
people.notes: Managing Director, AI Governance & Policy at Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy). Leads grantmaking for AI governance research, advocacy, and policy infrastructure. Former Executive Director of the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI). Former Research Analyst at GiveWell. Joined Open Philanthropy in 2015. Prolific writer on AI forecasting, consciousness, and social science methodology. One of the most influential individuals directing philanthropic capital toward AI governance worldwide.

people.name: Peter Favaloro
people.title: Program Director, Technical AI Safety
people.notes: Program Director, Technical AI Safety at Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy). Leads a team of six overseeing ~$150M/year in technical AI safety grantmaking, including the $40M Technical AI Safety RFP. Previously oversaw global catastrophic risks cause prioritization research and advised leadership on resource allocation strategy. One of the largest funders of technical alignment research globally.

people.name: Eli Rose
people.title: Program Director, Global Catastrophic Risks Capacity Building
people.notes: Program Director, Global Catastrophic Risks Capacity Building at Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy). Joined in June 2020. Oversees grantmaking for GCR capacity building and supports work on AI Governance & Policy and Technical AI Safety. Previously Director of Engineering at Mentor Collective, a Boston ed-tech company. Funds programs, events, and fellowships that build the pipeline of talent working on AI safety and other GCR topics.

people.name: Buck Shlegeris
people.title: CEO
people.notes: CEO of Redwood Research, an AI safety nonprofit focused on AI control — ensuring safe deployment of potentially misaligned AI. Co-founder of Redwood and board member at ARC. Argues that robust control techniques may be humanity's best near-term safeguard given insufficient industry investment in alignment.

people.name: Nate Thomas
people.title: Co-founder and CEO
people.notes: Co-founder and CEO of Redwood Research, a nonprofit AI safety lab in Berkeley founded in 2021. Also co-founded Constellation, a shared office space for longtermist organizations fostering cross-org collaboration. Physics PhD with ML publications. Led Redwood's research on AI control, interpretability, and alignment techniques. Redwood is known for applied alignment research including activation patching and AI control evaluations.

people.name: Bill Zito
people.title: Associate Director
people.notes: Co-founder and former COO of Redwood Research (2021-2023), an AI safety nonprofit. Currently Associate Director at the RAND Center on AI, Security, and Technology (RAND CAST) and the RAND Meselson Center (since Feb 2025), working on AI, cybersecurity, and biosecurity policy. Bridges the gap between technical AI safety research and national security policy. Previously helped build Redwood's operations and co-created the Constellation shared workspace.

people.name: Ryan Greenblatt
people.title: Chief Scientist
people.notes: Chief Scientist at Redwood Research working on technical AI safety, specializing in AI control and alignment. Lead author of the 'Alignment Faking in Large Language Models' paper (with Anthropic), demonstrating models strategically hide misaligned behavior during training. Estimates ~25–30% probability of full AI R&D automation by 2028–2029.

people.name: Tristan Cook
people.title: Managing Director
people.notes: Managing Director at the Center on Long-Term Risk (CLR), leading work on reducing suffering risks (s-risks) from powerful AI systems. Succeeded Jesse Clifton as MD. Holds a Master of Advanced Study (MASt) in Mathematics from the University of Cambridge (2020-2021). Based in London. CLR focuses on worst-case outcomes from advanced AI including multi-agent conflict scenarios. Oversees both empirical and conceptual research programs.

people.name: Stefan Torges
people.title: Head of Strategic Initiatives
people.notes: Head of Strategic Initiatives at Forethought, an AI safety research nonprofit focused on explosive AI progress scenarios. Previously Director of Operations at the Center on Long-Term Risk, where he built their fellowship program and oversaw AI safety grants. Also served as policy/research affiliate at GovAI.

people.name: Anni Leskelä
people.title: Conceptual Researcher
people.notes: Conceptual researcher at the Center on Long-Term Risk (CLR). Works on multi-agent AI dynamics and cooperation theory. Published work on commitment and credibility in multipolar AI scenarios. Developed CLR's robustness framework distinguishing outcome robustness (net-positive direction) from implementation robustness (avoiding unintended negative effects). Part of CLR's Conceptual Research Group alongside Anthony DiGiovanni and Nicolas Macé.

people.name: Dewey Murdick
people.title: Professor of the Practice and Senior Fellow
people.notes: Former Executive Director of Georgetown's Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET, 2021-2025), where he led a team of ~60 staff providing data-driven analysis on national security implications of AI and emerging technologies. Now Professor of the Practice and Senior Fellow at Georgetown, leading the Academic Innovation Network to pioneer human-AI interaction in education. Prior: Director of Science Analytics at Chan Zuckerberg Initiative; Deputy Chief Scientist at DHS; co-founded an anticipatory intelligence office at IARPA. Advocates evidence-based, adaptive AI governance — regulators must track AI incidents, develop AI literacy, and preserve policy agility. Does not pick ideological sides; follows data to inform smarter policy.

people.name: Cara LaPointe
people.title: Director of Analysis
people.primary_org: Center for Security and Emerging Technology
people.notes: Director of Analysis at Georgetown's Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET). Previously Director of Strategic Assessment for the Department of the Navy, leading the Naval Strategic Studies Group. Co-Director of the Johns Hopkins Institute for Assured Autonomy, where she led multi-disciplinary research ensuring AI-enabled autonomous systems are safe, secure, and ethical. Developed the Blockchain Ethical Design Framework at Georgetown's Beeck Center. Over two decades as a U.S. naval officer in operational, technical, and acquisition roles. Expertise spans national security AI, autonomous systems safety, and data-driven policy analysis.

people.name: Tom Brown
people.title: Co-founder
people.notes: Co-founder of Anthropic and lead author of the GPT-3 paper ('Language Models are Few-Shot Learners', 2020), which demonstrated that scaling language models produces broad capabilities. Self-taught engineer who transitioned into AI research; previously at OpenAI and Google Brain. Left OpenAI in 2021 to co-found Anthropic with Dario Amodei and others, motivated by the conviction that AI safety research must be prioritized at the foundational level, not bolted on after. Has expressed cautious optimism about AI regulation, calling the Biden AI executive order 'quite sensible.' Focuses on building robust and aligned AI systems at Anthropic.

people.name: Leah Harrison
people.title: Partnerships Director
people.notes: Partnerships Director at the Tarbell Center for AI Journalism, where she helps build relationships between the center and news organizations. Previously spent over two decades at Bloomberg in roles including newsroom trainer, reporter, and managing editor of QuickTake. Received Journalist of the Year 2025 award for AI reporting from One Young World. The Tarbell Center supports journalism covering AI's societal impacts, offering fellowships, grants ($350K in 2025), and newsroom placements (Guardian, Bloomberg, MIT Tech Review, TIME, The Verge).

people.name: Matt Botvinick
people.title: Senior Director of Research and Senior Technology and Policy Advisor
people.notes: Cognitive neuroscientist and AI researcher. Senior Director of Research and Senior Technology and Policy Advisor at Google DeepMind (joined 2016). Resident Fellow at Yale Law School focusing on AI and the rule of law. Joining the Anthropic Institute to lead work on AI and the rule of law. PhD in AI and computational neuroscience from Carnegie Mellon; faculty at UPenn and Princeton before DeepMind. 140+ peer-reviewed articles spanning AI, deep learning, reinforcement learning, cognitive science, and computational neuroscience. Honorary Professor at UCL's Gatsby Computational Neuroscience Unit. Has warned against government control of AI speech, arguing democratic debate — not party-line compliance — must guide AI content decisions.

people.name: Zoë Hitzig
people.title: Junior Fellow, Harvard Society of Fellows
people.notes: Economist and poet. Junior Fellow at Harvard Society of Fellows; PhD in Economics from Harvard (2023). Former Research Scientist at OpenAI (2024-2026); resigned publicly citing concerns about OpenAI introducing ads in ChatGPT, arguing the company holds 'the most detailed record of private human thought ever assembled' and should not exploit it for advertising. Affiliated with Harvard Berkman Klein Center for Internet & Society. Research focuses on political economy of AI, privacy, equity, and transparency in markets and algorithms. Examines how AI concentrates information and power as systems capture tacit, experience-based knowledge from workers. Author of two poetry collections (Mezzanine, Not Us Now). Published in The New Yorker, London Review of Books.

people.name: Otto Barten
people.title: Founder & President
people.notes: Founder and president of the Existential Risk Observatory (est. May 2021, Amsterdam). Background as a physicist, sustainable energy engineer, data scientist, and entrepreneur -- previously founded and sold an EV smart-charging startup. Pivoted to existential risk communication after concluding x-risk is more important than climate. The Observatory aims to reduce human extinction risk by informing public debate. Co-authored the Conditional AI Safety Treaty proposal published in TIME. Active in the Effective Altruism community.

people.name: Rebecca Scholefield
people.title: AI Governance Researcher
people.notes: AI governance researcher and collaborator at the Existential Risk Observatory. Holds a degree in History and German from the University of Oxford. Co-authored the "International Agreements on AI Safety: Review and Recommendations for a Conditional AI Safety Treaty" paper, which proposes pausing AI development when AI safety institutes determine risks (including loss of control) have become unacceptable. The treaty proposes compute thresholds, mandatory audits, and an international AISI network with authority to halt development.

people.name: Samuel Martin
people.title: AI Safety Researcher
people.notes: AI safety researcher who co-authored the Conditional AI Safety Treaty paper with Rebecca Scholefield and the Existential Risk Observatory. Listed as unaffiliated in the publication. The paper proposes an international framework for pausing AI development when safety institutes determine risks are unacceptable, with compute thresholds and mandatory audits overseen by AI Safety Institutes.

people.name: Ayushmaan Sharma
people.title: Researcher and Facilitator
people.notes: Junior researcher and facilitator working across AI safety and existential risk organizations. Has served as Head of Outreach and Partnerships at Effective Thesis, a facilitator at BlueDot Impact, and a researcher at the Existential Risk Observatory, where he published work on public attitudes toward existential risk and AI.

people.name: Nik Samoylov
people.title: Founder
people.notes: Founder of Conjointly (market research platform) and the Campaign for AI Safety, which merged with the Existential Risk Observatory in 2024. Background in marketing and management consulting. Uses Conjointly to run large-scale public opinion surveys on AI existential risk across multiple countries (China, Taiwan, USA). Research focuses on AI x-risk communication, narrative testing for AGI moratorium support, and tracking public attitudes toward existential risk. Primarily self-funds the Observatory through Conjointly revenues.

people.name: Eric Ho
people.title: Co-founder and CEO
people.notes: Co-founder and CEO of Goodfire, a leading AI interpretability research company founded in June 2024. Goodfire develops mechanistic interpretability tools to understand, audit, and edit neural networks at the feature level. Co-founded with Dan Balsam (CTO) and Tom McGrath (Chief Scientist, ex-DeepMind interpretability). Raised $50M Series A led by Menlo Ventures, then $150M Series B at $1.25B valuation led by B Capital. Team includes Lee Sharkey (pioneer of sparse autoencoders, co-founded Apollo Research) and Nick Cammarata (started OpenAI's interpretability team). Partners include Mayo Clinic (genomic medicine) and Radical AI (materials science).

people.name: Sarah Friar
people.title: Chief Financial Officer
people.notes: CFO of OpenAI since June 2024 -- the company's first CFO. Born 1972 in Sion Mills, Northern Ireland. Studied metallurgy, economics, and management at Oxford; MBA from Stanford (Arjay Miller scholar). Career: McKinsey analyst in South Africa, Goldman Sachs equity research MD, Salesforce SVP Finance & Strategy, Square CFO (led 2015 IPO, added $30B market cap), Nextdoor CEO (led 2021 SPAC IPO). Board member at Walmart and ConsenSys. Co-chairs Stanford's Digital Economy Lab. Co-founded Ladies Who Launch (nonprofit for female entrepreneurs). OBE from Queen Elizabeth II (2019). Honorary doctorate from Ulster University.

people.name: Ankesh Chandaria
people.title: CEO
people.notes: CEO of the AI Safety Foundation, which increases awareness and scientific understanding of catastrophic AI risks through education and research. Background spans law, governance, and AI policy -- previously practiced as a litigation and regulatory investigations lawyer. Founded theConcept.ai. Affiliated with Periscope Lab at the University of Toronto. Currently pursuing a Master's in AI Ethics and Society with the Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence at the University of Cambridge. Published on Canadian AI governance and AI creativity. Served on boards including the Art Gallery of Ontario.

people.name: Richard Phillips
people.title: Chair of the Board
people.notes: Chair of the Board at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF), a Canadian registered charity focused on catastrophic AI risk education and research. Former Deputy Chairman of CIBC World Markets with decades of experience in investment management and capital markets. Founded his own investment firm. Active in non-profit governance across poverty reduction, education, arts, and financial regulation.

people.name: Meg Sintzel
people.title: Director
people.notes: Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Former Managing Director of Marketing and Communications at Accenture Canada, where she worked for 36 years before stepping down in 2025. Previously held roles at Toronto Region Board of Trade and National Public Relations. Brings corporate communications and technology industry expertise to AISF board governance.

people.name: David Duvenaud
people.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science and Statistics
people.notes: Associate Professor of Computer Science and Statistics at the University of Toronto. Canada Research Chair in Generative Models, Canada CIFAR AI Chair, Sloan Fellow, and founding member of the Vector Institute. Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Formerly led the Alignment Evals team at Anthropic during an extended sabbatical. Publicly stated ~85%% probability of existential risk from AGI and advocates coordinating a slowdown in AGI development. Developed the concept of "gradual disempowerment" — how incremental AI advances can undermine human influence over societal systems. Research on dangerous capability evaluations, sabotage evals, and proof-of-training-data protocols.

people.name: Roger Grosse
people.title: Associate Professor of Computer Science
people.notes: Associate Professor of Computer Science at the University of Toronto. Schwartz Reisman Chair in Technology and Society, founding member of the Vector Institute, Sloan Fellow, and Schmidt Sciences AI2050 Senior Fellow. Member of Technical Staff on the Alignment Science team at Anthropic, focused on training data attribution. Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Shifted research focus to AI safety after concluding short timelines to superhuman intelligence were plausible. Believes ensuring AI alignment with human values is the most important current research priority.

people.name: Bill Morris
people.title: Director
people.notes: Director at the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Former CEO of Accenture Canada with over 35 years at the firm, holding leadership roles across North America and globally. Serves on several corporate and non-profit boards and is active in the business technology space. Brings executive and governance experience to AISF.

people.name: NicholasKees
people.title: Co-founder and CEO
people.notes: Nicholas Kees Dupuis. Co-founder and CEO of Mosaic Labs, developing Nexus — a platform for AI-facilitated group deliberation to improve collective epistemics. Co-founder of Cyborgism, a research agenda on human-AI symbiosis to safely accelerate alignment research. Ran the Cyborgism track at AI Safety Camp 2023. Previously on a 1-year grant designing tools to accelerate alignment research. Publishes on LessWrong under NicholasKees. Active in the Effective Altruism community, focusing giving on existential risk reduction.

people.name: janus
people.title: Co-founder, Cyborgism
people.notes: Pseudonymous AI researcher known online as Repligate (@repligate on X). Co-founder and leader of the Cyborgism project, a community-driven initiative on human-AI symbiosis and collective intelligence. Introduced the influential "simulators" framework for understanding language models (published on Alignment Forum). EleutherAI admin. Launched Act I project in 2024 — treating humans and AI agents as coequal participants — which secured ~$68K funding from Manifund. Known for pushing boundaries of LLM interaction through philosophical and ontological experiments with models like Claude.

people.name: Daniel Clothiaux
people.title: PhD Student, Language Technologies
people.notes: PhD student in the Language Technologies Department at Carnegie Mellon University. Research interests include philosophy of AI, philosophy of information, and computer simulation. Collaborated with Conjecture on developing the ideas behind Cyborgism alongside Connor Leahy (who coined the term). Contributed to research on LLM-assisted cognition and human-AI symbiosis within the cyborgism framework. Publishes on LessWrong and the Alignment Forum.

people.name: Sven Herrmann
people.title: AI Policy Fellow
people.primary_org: Institute for AI Policy and Strategy
people.notes: AI policy researcher with a PhD in mathematics. 2025 IAPS (Institute for AI Policy and Strategy) AI Policy Fellow, where he worked on European AI governance at The Future Society, focusing on policies to prevent serious AI incidents by learning from other industries. Also worked on digital minds governance at IAPS. Previously Head of Research Operations at a research institute at Oxford University (Global Priorities Institute). Background includes non-profit sustainability work and management consulting.

people.name: Jérémy Andréoletti
people.title: AI Security Researcher
people.notes: AI security researcher at the GPAI (General-Purpose AI) Policy Lab, where he builds models to forecast AI capabilities and emerging risks. General Secretary and co-founder of EffiSciences, a French non-profit founded in January 2022 that mobilizes scientific research on pressing global issues. PhD in computational evolutionary biology from Ecole Normale Superieure PSL (Paris). Research interests include AI security, AI safety, modeling, and Bayesian inference. Active in the Effective Altruism community.

people.name: Charlie Rogers-Smith
people.title: Chief of Staff
people.notes: Chief of Staff at Palisade Research, an independent AI safety research organization. Author of "How to pursue a career in technical AI alignment" (2022), a widely referenced EA Forum guide recommended by 80,000 Hours and Rohin Shah. Former instructor at CFAR (Center for Applied Rationality). Masters from Oxford. Previously developed ML models for COVID-19 intervention effectiveness with Oxford Applied and Theoretical ML group. Research interests include interpretability and concept bottleneck models.

people.name: Lila Ibrahim
people.title: Chief AI Readiness Officer (formerly COO)
people.notes: COO of Google DeepMind (joined 2018 as COO of DeepMind, now Chief AI Readiness Officer per LinkedIn). Leads operations, governance & ethics, policy, communications, and people & culture. Previously VP/GM at Coursera and spent 18 years at Intel. Signed the CAIS statement that "mitigating the risk of extinction from AI should be a global priority." Member of UK AI Council and Council on Foreign Relations. Named 2025 CNBC Changemaker. Advocates for international coordination and proactive governance; believes the tech industry is at a critical juncture for responsible AI.

people.name: Robert Trager
people.title: Co-Director, Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative
people.notes: Co-Director of the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative and International Governance Lead at GovAI. Senior Research Fellow at Oxford's Blavatnik School of Government. Proposes an International AI Organization (IAIO) to certify state jurisdictions for compliance with oversight standards. Former Professor of Political Science at UCLA.

people.name: Paolo Bova
people.title: PhD Student / Independent AI Governance Researcher
people.notes: Independent AI governance researcher and member of the Modeling Cooperation team. Economics graduate from University of Cambridge (2019). PhD student at Teesside University studying AI safety dynamics through behavioral and network modeling. Co-developed the Safety-Performance Tradeoff (SPT) model web app for AI governance with Robert Trager, Nicholas Emery-Xu, Eoghan Stafford, and Allan Dafoe. Research focuses on computational models of competition in transformative AI and policy design for reducing AI risks. Co-authored "Safe Transformative AI via a Windfall Clause."

people.name: Nicholas Emery-Xu
people.title: PhD Candidate in Economics
people.notes: PhD candidate in economics at UCLA (4th year), advised by John Asker, Hugo Hopenhayn, Simon Board, and Will Rafey. BA in economics and Russian from University of Pennsylvania. 2022 Global Priorities Fellow at Forethought Foundation. Affiliate at MIT FutureTech. Member of Modeling Cooperation team. Research spans industrial organization, AI safety economics, and technology governance. Key papers on compute pricing and AI safety ("Industrial Policy for Advanced AI: Compute Pricing and the Safety Tax"), and the Safety-Performance Tradeoff model for AI governance. Studies how computational resource pricing affects AI safety investment.

people.name: Eoghan Stafford
people.title: AAAS Science & Technology Policy Fellow
people.notes: AAAS Science & Technology Policy Fellow. Former Research Scholar at the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI). PhD from Oxford/Future of Humanity Institute. Previously Research Assistant at UCLA under Barbara Geddes (Autocratic Regimes Dataset). Member of Modeling Cooperation team. Co-authored work with Robert Trager and Allan Dafoe on AI governance and the Safety-Performance Tradeoff model. Research spans AI governance, autocratic AI misuse, and international technology race dynamics. Also affiliated with Center for AI and Digital Policy and Belfer Center at Harvard.

people.name: Emmett Shear
people.title: Co-founder and CEO
people.notes: Co-founder and former CEO of Twitch (2011-2023). Briefly served as interim CEO of OpenAI (Nov 19-21, 2023) during the Sam Altman firing crisis. Co-founder and CEO of Softmax, a 10-person AI startup focused on "organic alignment" — teaching AIs to cooperate with humans using principles from biology and collective intelligence. Backed by a16z. Previously co-founded Stem AI (incorporated June 2023). Estimates his P(doom) at 5-50%. Believes AI will improve so rapidly it could overwhelm creators, but generally favors limited regulation except for AGI-level systems. Advocates for alignment research as the primary safety mechanism rather than regulation.

people.name: Ken Stanley
people.title: SVP of Open-Endedness
people.notes: SVP of Open-Endedness at Lila Sciences. Previously led the Open-Endedness team at OpenAI (2020-2022) as Research Science Manager. Former professor of CS at University of Central Florida. Co-founded Geometric Intelligence (2015, acquired by Uber). Created foundational AI algorithms: NEAT (NeuroEvolution of Augmenting Topologies), HyperNEAT, CPPNs, and novelty search. Co-authored "Why Greatness Cannot Be Planned: The Myth of the Objective" with Joel Lehman. Believes open-ended creativity is essential to true AGI — "if you can't actually hack being genuinely creative, you're not at the human level." Research spans AI, neuroevolution, evolutionary computation, and artificial life.

people.name: Clark Barrett
people.title: Professor (Research) of Computer Science; Director of Stanford Center for Automated Reasoning
people.notes: Professor (Research) of Computer Science at Stanford University. Director of Stanford Center for Automated Reasoning (Centaur) and co-director of Stanford Center for AI Safety. Previously Associate Professor at NYU Courant Institute. PhD from Stanford (2003) — his dissertation introduced Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT). ACM Fellow. Two-time CAV Award winner (2021, 2024). Pioneer in formal hardware verification (0-in Design Automation, now Siemens). Research applies formal methods to neural network verification for AI safety. Research lead at Beneficial AI Foundation.

people.name: Bryan Parno
people.title: Kavčić-Moura Professor of Electrical & Computer Engineering and Computer Science
people.notes: Kavčić-Moura Professor of Electrical & Computer Engineering and Computer Science at Carnegie Mellon University. Senior Member of ACM and IEEE. Research focuses on formal, rigorous security guarantees for concrete systems — bridging theory and practice. Created the Verifiable Computation framework (reduced verification costs by 20+ orders of magnitude). Led Ironclad, Everest, and Verus projects for end-to-end formally verified secure systems. Work incorporated into Intel CPUs, Windows, and iOS. IEEE Cybersecurity Award for Practice (2024). Best Paper Awards at IEEE S&P and USENIX NSDI. Research lead at Beneficial AI Foundation.

people.name: Steve Omohundro
people.title: Founder and CEO
people.notes: AI safety pioneer. Founder and CEO of Beneficial AI Research (formerly Self-Aware Systems). Published the seminal 2008 paper "The Basic AI Drives," which argued that rational goal-seeking systems will develop drives for self-improvement and self-protection unless explicitly countered — a foundational concept in AI alignment theory. Advisor to the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI). Proposed many basic AI safety mechanisms including AI smart contracts. Research spans Hamiltonian physics, dynamical systems, programming languages, machine learning, machine vision, and AI social implications. Research lead at Beneficial AI Foundation. Cognitive World Think Tank member.

people.name: Walter Laurito
people.title: AI Safety Researcher; Co-founder of Cadenza Labs
people.notes: AI safety researcher specializing in interpretability and LLM honesty detection. Former SERI-MATS scholar (Cohort 3.0). Co-founded Cadenza Labs during Prague Fall Season 2023 with Georgios and Kaarel; the team continued through MATS 3.0. Cadenza Labs focuses on LLM dishonesty detection and benchmarking. Affiliated with FZI Research Center / Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT). Research interests include interpretability, AI safety, and machine learning. Wrote EA Forum post documenting late-career shift to existential risk reduction.

people.name: Cameron Tice
people.title: Founder and Co-Director
people.notes: Founder and Co-Director of Geodesic Research, a UK-based technical AI safety organization focused on compute-intensive alignment research. Marshall Scholar at the University of Cambridge, where he completed an MPhil on automated research with LLMs for computational psychiatry. Previously worked as a machine learning engineer at raft.ai and as a private equity quantitative strategist at Goldman Sachs. Lead author of "Noise Injection for Sandbagging Detection." Former Research Manager for the ERA: AI fellowship. Research focuses on alignment pretraining and chain-of-thought safety.

people.name: Kyle O'Brien
people.title: Founding Member of Technical Staff
people.notes: Founding Member of Technical Staff at Geodesic Research, leading pretraining and midtraining safety directions to address dangerous capabilities (biorisk, offensive cyber) and instill deeper alignment into LLMs. Technical AI safety researcher and former ERA fellow. Previously contributed as a scientist and engineer at EleutherAI and Microsoft. Research focuses on preventing and removing dangerous capabilities from LLMs via pretraining interventions and open-weight model safety. Research accepted at ICLR, cited by OpenAI and Anthropic, and covered by The Washington Post and Fortune.

people.name: Alexandra Narin
people.title: Strategic Delivery Lead
people.notes: Strategic Delivery lead at Geodesic Research and Co-founder of UK AI Forum. Background in neuroscience research at UCL. Previously served as Head of Grants for a biotech company, where she secured and managed over $5M in private and government funding. Now applies operations and fundraising experience to scaling Geodesic Research. Connects AI safety research community in the UK through the UK AI Forum.

people.name: Tomek Korbak
people.title: Member of Technical Staff
people.notes: Member of Technical Staff at OpenAI working on monitoring LLM agents for misalignment. Previously worked on AI control and safety cases at the UK AI Security Institute (UK AISI) and on honesty post-training at Anthropic. PhD from the University of Sussex (with Chris Buckley and Anil Seth) focusing on RLHF. Visiting researcher at NYU with Ethan Perez, Sam Bowman, and Kyunghyun Cho. Advisor to Geodesic Research. MATS mentor. Research cited 6,235+ times. Key contributions to language model alignment, constitutional AI methods, and chain-of-thought monitoring.

people.name: Peter Eckersley
people.title: Co-founder (deceased)
people.notes: DECEASED (September 2, 2022, age 43). Australian computer scientist, security researcher, and internet privacy activist. Co-founder of Let's Encrypt (2012), which has validated certificates for 290M+ domains. Worked at EFF from 2006-2018 as Technology Projects Director, Chief Computer Scientist, and Head of AI Policy. Created Privacy Badger, Certbot, HTTPS Everywhere, SSL Observatory, and Panopticlick. Director of Research at Partnership on AI (2018-2020). Co-founded the AI Objectives Institute (AOI) in 2021 with Brittney Gallagher, focused on aligning AI objectives with societal interests. Inducted into the Internet Hall of Fame. His final career phase focused on AI ethics and safety. Historical significance: pivotal figure bridging internet privacy/security and early AI governance movements.

people.name: Colleen Mckenzie
people.title: Executive Director
people.notes: Executive Director of the AI Objectives Institute (AOI), the nonprofit co-founded by the late Peter Eckersley. Researcher, designer, and product strategist. Co-founded the Median Group, a scientific research nonprofit exploring technological trends and societal impacts. Past roles include Product Manager and Software Engineer at Google, Chief of Staff at the Center for Humane Technology, and Head of Product at distributed computing startup Kalix Systems. B.A. in Computer Science and Neuroscience from Columbia University. Presented on scaling deliberation at Foresight Institute.

people.name: Stacey Svetlichnaya
people.title: Chief Technology Officer
people.notes: Chief Technology Officer at the AI Objectives Institute (AOI). Research interests in climate change and responsible AI. Previously a deep learning engineer at Weights & Biases, building developer tools for ML visualization, explainability, reproducibility, and collaboration. Earlier work on image search, productionizing ML systems, and discovery/recommendation at Flickr. Collaborating with AOI since spring 2024. Works at the intersection of ML research, application, and UX for vision and language models.

people.name: Brittney G
people.title: Vice President and Board Chair
people.notes: Vice President and Board Chair at the AI Objectives Institute (AOI), which she co-founded with Peter Eckersley in 2021. Storyteller and creator focused on human interaction with technology. Interviewed technologists and thought-leaders for Digital Village, one of the oldest public radio shows in Los Angeles. Also builds and manages large-scale software systems for media companies. AOI was created with the hope of building AI tools that enhance human agency.

people.name: Adam Schumacher
people.title: Program Director
people.notes: Program Director at the AI Objectives Institute (AOI). Based in Saint James, NY. Background in international development: previously served as Deputy Director in USAID's Office of East Asian Affairs, managing daily operations for over $800M in development programs across East Asia and the Pacific. Also worked at the East-West Management Institute. Brings international development and program management expertise to AOI's mission of aligning AI with human objectives.

people.name: Zoey Tseng
people.title: APAC Partnerships
people.notes: APAC Partnerships at the AI Objectives Institute and independent researcher specializing in public goods and self-sovereign identity. Active in Taiwan's g0v civic tech community. Collaborated with Taiwan's Ministry of Digital Affairs (MoDA) on web3 and AI-related projects, including deploying AOI's Talk to the City tool. Has facilitated deliberation workshops in Taiwan on the impact and development of LLMs. Researches new government funding mechanisms for open-source projects. Bridges AI governance discourse between Asia-Pacific and Western contexts.

people.name: Anna Leshinskaya
people.title: Assistant Professor, Department of Cognitive Sciences
people.notes: Assistant Professor in the Department of Cognitive Sciences at UC Irvine and Fellow at the Center for Neurobiology of Learning & Memory. Affiliated researcher/Principal Researcher at the AI Objectives Institute. PhD in Cognitive Psychology from Harvard (2015). Postdoctoral fellowships at UC Davis and University of Pennsylvania. Directs the Relational Cognition Lab at UCI. Research spans human cognition, neural implementation, and machine emulation. Leverages cognitive science insights to measure cognitive and moral alignment between humans and LLMs. Schmidt Sciences grantee.

people.name: Jaime Sevilla
people.title: Director and Founder
people.notes: Director and founder of Epoch AI (est. 2022), a nonprofit research organization that tracks AI compute trends, training runs, and benchmarks to forecast AI development trajectories. PhD researcher with affiliations at Cambridge CSER and LCFI. His work aims to bring climate-science-level rigor to AI forecasting, producing datasets used by policymakers, the OECD, and AI labs. Featured in TIME as key AI forecasting voice. Active in EA community. Has stated he became "more skeptical of AI Risk" as he learned more, though notes Epoch maintains a plurality of views internally.

people.name: Tamay Besiroglu
people.title: Co-founder
people.notes: Co-founder and former Associate Director of Epoch AI. Researcher at the intersection of economics and computing, known for the "compute-based" view of AI progress. Co-authored influential paper arguing "explosive economic growth" is plausible with AI capable of broadly substituting for human labor. Left Epoch to co-found Mechanize, a startup aiming to build AI-based automated workers to replace human employees — a move that generated significant controversy. Holds economics background with focus on AI automation trajectories.

people.name: Pablo Villalobos
people.title: Staff Researcher
people.notes: Staff Researcher at Epoch AI and one of the organization's earliest members (first volunteer-employee). Joined after impressing founder Jaime Sevilla during a high school presentation. Specializes in AI data scaling and training efficiency. Lead author of influential 2022 study projecting that language models would exhaust publicly available human-generated text between 2026-2032 ("peak data"). Also leads Epoch's Biology AI Dataset covering 360+ models, analyzing safety measures in biological AI systems. His research on data constraints has become a key reference for policymakers evaluating AI scaling limits.

people.name: Lennart Heim
people.title: Associate Information Scientist; Professor of Policy Analysis
people.notes: Leading AI compute governance researcher. Associate Information Scientist at RAND and Professor of Policy Analysis at Pardee RAND Graduate School. Former Senior Research Fellow at the Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI), where he remains an Adjunct Fellow. Co-founder of Epoch AI. Member of the OECD.AI Expert Group on AI Compute and Climate. His research focuses on how compute can be leveraged as an instrument for AI governance — including compute provider KYC schemes, hardware-enabled governance mechanisms (HEMs), and export control policy. Frames AI risk across three categories: accident risks, misuse risks, and structural risks. One of the most cited voices on compute governance in policy circles.

people.name: Anson Ho
people.title: Staff Researcher
people.notes: Staff Researcher and founding team member of Epoch AI (one of seven co-founders). Investigates drivers behind AI developments and societal impacts using tools from economics and deep learning. Focuses on quantitative forecasting of transformative AI timelines, including analysis of the Biological Anchors framework. Has contributed to Epoch's training compute database tracking 100+ ML models across history, with results featured in The Economist and at the OECD. Active on LessWrong and Alignment Forum. Work supports policymakers, grantmakers, and technical researchers making AI strategy decisions.

people.name: Tom Davidson
people.title: AI Safety Researcher
people.notes: AI safety researcher currently at Forethought (AI Safety Research Nonprofit), previously Senior Research Fellow at Open Philanthropy and research scientist at UK AI Security Institute. Known for work on AI timelines, explosive economic growth from AI, and AI-enabled coup risk. Board member of Epoch AI.

people.name: Maria de la Lama
people.title: Head of Operations and Board Member
people.notes: Head of Operations at Epoch AI and member of its Board of Directors (alongside Tom Davidson, Ajeya Cotra, and Jaime Sevilla). Previously Special Projects Coordinator at Rethink Priorities and Co-Founder of Contexto Estudio. Holds MSc in Philosophy of the Social Sciences from LSE. Plays a key operational and governance role at Epoch AI, which produces datasets and analysis used by policymakers worldwide for AI governance decisions.

people.name: Jonas Vollmer
people.title: CFO
people.notes: EA-aligned executive and co-founder of Center on Long-Term Risk. Held senior roles at EA Funds and Centre for Effective Altruism before joining Macroscopic Ventures as CFO and AI Futures Project as COO. Focuses on scenario forecasting and strategic grantmaking to improve the trajectory of advanced AI development.

people.name: Ruairí Donnelly
people.title: President of the Board
people.notes: President of the Board of Macroscopic Ventures, a Swiss nonprofit (est. 2019) that makes grants and investments (~$10M in grants in 2024) to projects reducing catastrophic and existential AI risk. Oversees programs and strategy. Also serves on the Board of the Center for AI Safety (CAIS). Previously worked at AI safety organizations and in the cryptocurrency industry. Macroscopic has funded the NYU Mind, Ethics, and Policy Program; Center on Long-Term Risk; Cooperative AI Foundation; and Institute for Law and AI.

people.name: Jesse Clifton
people.title: Grantmaking Officer
people.notes: AI safety researcher focused on cooperative AI, game theory, and conflict prevention in multi-agent systems. Co-founded Center on Long-Term Risk and served as its Executive Director; now Grantmaking Officer at Macroscopic Ventures and board trustee of the Cooperative AI Foundation. PhD student in statistics at NC State.

people.name: Arden Berg
people.title: Investor
