Research focuses on how AI weapons systems will impact military strategy and global political stability. Strong advocate for mandatory AI regulation over industry self-regulation; has stated "we cannot rely on private industry to govern AI; we need aggressive public policy interventions now." Connected to EA/longtermist community.

people.name: Brad Carson
people.title: Co-Founder and President
people.notes: Co-Founder and President of Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI), a bipartisan nonprofit focused on AI policy advocacy. Former U.S. Representative (D-OK, 2001-2005) and 21st President of the University of Tulsa (2021-2025). Left academia in mid-2025 to lead ARI full-time. Advocates for preventing near-term AI harms (children, cognitive off-loading, insufficient human oversight) and addressing long-term risks of misaligned AI. Has testified before the Senate Commerce Committee on AI legislation. Opposed Trump executive order directing agencies to sue states with AI regulations. ARI does not accept money from for-profit corporations or non-U.S. entities.

people.name: Eric Gastfriend
people.title: Co-Founder and Executive Director
people.notes: Co-Founder and Executive Director of Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI), a bipartisan nonprofit focused on AI policy advocacy founded in late 2023. Entrepreneur who also runs a telehealth startup for addiction treatment. Co-founded an effective altruism student group at Harvard. ARI aims to help U.S. policymakers develop governance frameworks for AI that protect the public from harm while fostering innovation. The organization does not accept money from for-profit corporations or non-U.S. entities to maintain independence.

people.name: Viktoriya Krakovna
people.title: Research Scientist
people.notes: Research scientist at Google DeepMind focusing on AI alignment — ensuring advanced AI systems act in accordance with human interests. Co-founder of the Future of Life Institute (2014). PhD in statistics and machine learning from Harvard, focused on building interpretable models. Research spans deceptive alignment, dangerous capability evaluations, specification gaming, goal misgeneralization, and avoiding side effects. Active contributor to AI safety discourse on LessWrong and the EA Forum. Has discussed AGI ruin scenarios and the "sharp left turn" alignment challenge. One of the most prominent technical AI safety researchers working inside a frontier lab.

people.name: Anthony Aguirre
people.title: Executive Director
people.notes: Executive Director and Secretary of the Board at the Future of Life Institute (co-founded 2014 with Max Tegmark and others). Faggin Presidential Professor for the Physics of Information at UC Santa Cruz. Theoretical cosmologist (PhD Astronomy, Harvard 2000) who transitioned into existential risk and technology governance. Co-founder of the Foundational Questions Institute (FQXi) and President of Metaculus, a science and technology forecasting platform. Advocates for mandatory licensing of frontier AI systems with requirements scaling to capability level, independent third-party evaluations with "actual teeth," and warns that AGI is being developed in an unsafe race dynamic by un-overseen companies. Has written that uncontained AGI would "replace humanity."

people.name: Meia Chita-Tegmark
people.title: Co-founder; Postdoctoral Researcher
people.notes: Co-founder of the Future of Life Institute (FLI), alongside Max Tegmark, Jaan Tallinn, Viktoriya Krakovna, and Anthony Aguirre. Cognitive scientist with a PhD in Psychology from Boston University. Postdoctoral researcher in the Human Robot Interaction Lab at Tufts University, studying AI's impact on human social interactions and emotional well-being. Teaches courses on AI and society at Tufts Experimental College. FLI advocates for steering transformative technology toward benefiting life and away from existential risks, including through the EU AI Act (foundation model provisions) and UN-level governance. Chita-Tegmark emphasizes critical engagement with AI's psychological impacts, questioning which activities should be delegated to AI and warning about disengagement from moral responsibility.

people.name: Hamish Hobbs
people.title: Director of AI Policy
people.notes: Director of AI Policy at the Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR). Previously a Research Scholar at Oxford University's Future of Humanity Institute researching governance of emerging technologies and long-term risks. Former policy advisor to the Prime Minister of Australia and adviser to the UK Government's Office for AI. Also served as Policy Advisor at Longview Philanthropy supporting the OECD Strategic Foresight Unit on AI policy, biotechnology governance, and anticipatory innovation. Recognized as an OECD.AI Network of Experts member and GovAI affiliate. CLTR works on reducing extreme risks from AI through policy engagement with UK government.

people.name: Dr Jess Whittlestone
people.title: Head of AI Policy (Senior Adviser)
people.notes: Head of AI Policy (Senior Adviser) at the Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR), leading work on policy recommendations to reduce extreme risks from AI. Named in TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2023. PhD in Behavioural Science from University of Warwick; first-class degree in Mathematics and Philosophy from Oxford. Previously Senior Research Fellow and Deputy Director of the AI: Futures and Responsibility Programme at the University of Cambridge (CSER). Has published widely on AI policy and provided expert advice to UK government bodies including the Centre for Data Ethics and Innovation and Office for AI. Also affiliated with the Centre for Emerging Technology and Security (CETaS) at the Alan Turing Institute, GovAI, and CNAS.

people.name: Tommy Shaffer Shane
people.title: Interim Director of AI Policy
people.notes: Interim Director of AI Policy at the Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR), leading the AI policy unit's work on mitigating extreme risks from AI. Over 10 years of experience in technological risks policy. Previously at the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT), where he led a team delivering a £10m programme to develop AI techniques to analyse disinformation impacting UK national security and conducted risk assessments of frontier AI. PhD candidate at King's College London researching AI incidents monitoring. Authored key reports on AI security incident preparedness (2025) recommending DSIT implement a preparedness framework, and the Loss of Control Observatory tracking real-world AI scheming behavior.

people.name: Nicole Alvarez
people.title: Senior Policy Analyst, Technology Policy
people.notes: Senior Policy Analyst for Technology Policy at the Center for American Progress (CAP). Previously at the Future of Privacy Forum. Develops forward-looking policies on challenges from emerging technologies. Has authored and co-authored multiple CAP reports on AI policy, including work opposing federal moratoriums and preemption of state AI laws. Argues that moratoriums offer no federal protections in return and that preemption undermines state regulatory authority while entrenching industry power. Covers AI regulation, generative AI safeguards, federal privacy, and the intersection of AI with employment, healthcare, housing, and elections.

people.name: Megan Shahi
people.title: Director of Technology Policy
people.notes: Director of Technology Policy at the Center for American Progress (CAP). Focuses on AI governance, digital policy, and societal impacts of emerging technologies. Previously worked at Meta, Instagram, and X (formerly Twitter) on privacy, platform policy, and product strategies to protect users from harm. Earlier career in government: U.S. Department of Treasury (Office of International Affairs, Office of Domestic Finance) and the White House Domestic Policy Council. Siegel Research Fellow. Has contributed to CAP work on responsible AI deployment, NIST generative AI risk frameworks, and protecting democracy online. Also a Georgetown TPP visiting fellow (spring 2026).

people.name: Adam Conner
people.title: Vice President of Technology Policy
people.notes: Vice President of Technology Policy at the Center for American Progress (CAP), leading the inaugural Technology Policy team. Builds progressive technology policy platform covering AI regulation, competition, disinformation, and content policy. Previously spent seven years on the Facebook Privacy and Public Policy team as their first D.C. employee. Vocal advocate for AI safety regulation; has argued against federal preemption of state AI laws, criticized the Trump administration's rollback of AI safety restrictions, and warned about AI-enabled disinformation. Co-authored CAP pieces including "4 Reasons the Senate's AI Pause Should Be Opposed" and work on regulating high-risk AI under existing U.S. law.

people.name: Daniel Filan
people.title: Risk Assessor at METR; Host of AXRP - the AI X-risk Research Podcast
people.notes: Researcher at METR focused on loss-of-control risk assessment. Host of AXRP (AI X-risk Research Podcast), interviewing safety researchers since 2020. Previously a senior researcher at MATS. Active contributor to the AI safety research community.

people.name: Frances Lorenz
people.title: Project Manager, Events Team at FAR.AI
people.notes: Project Manager on the events team at FAR.AI, delivering events that bring together AI safety researchers, policymakers, and industry leaders. Honours Integrated Sciences degree (concentration in Mathematics and Statistics) from McMaster University. Three years of experience running large-scale conferences on pandemic preparedness, global health, animal welfare, and AI safety. Previously worked at AI Safety Support. Involved in organizing FAR.AI's London Alignment Workshop featuring researchers from leading labs, academia, and government.

people.name: Cari Tuna
people.title: Co-Founder and Chair, Good Ventures and Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)
people.notes: Cari Tuna is an American philanthropist and former Wall Street Journal reporter who co-founded Good Ventures and Open Philanthropy (now Coefficient Giving) with her husband Dustin Moskovitz. Open Philanthropy was an early backer of AI safety, giving more than $580 million toward building a field of researchers studying the potential catastrophic risks of AI. She was included in TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI 2024.

organizations.name: Brookings Institution
organizations.notes: The Brookings Institution is an American think tank in Washington, D.C. that conducts research and education in the social sciences, primarily in economics and tax policy, metropolitan policy, governance, foreign policy, global economy, and economic development. The organization was founded in 1916 as the Institute for Government Research (IGR), with the mission of becoming "the first private organization devoted to analyzing public policy issues at the national level". Brookings states that its staff "represent diverse points of view" and describes itself as nonpartisan. The institution is currently led by President Cecilia Rouse.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted AI regulation with sector-specific approaches rather than blanket restrictions. Supports responsible AI frameworks emphasizing transparency, accountability, and consumer protection while maintaining innovation competitiveness. Calls for robust federal oversight through existing agencies like FTC and CFPB, plus new governance structures for high-risk AI applications in healthcare, finance, and education.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Democracy, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration

organizations.name: Gray Swan
organizations.notes: Gray Swan is an AI security startup founded by Carnegie Mellon professors, based in Pittsburgh, PA. The company creates AI security solutions including Cygnal (an AI firewall) and Shade (an automated AI red teamer) to protect AI systems from attacks. Gray Swan conducted a groundbreaking study with Stanford comparing AI agents to human cybersecurity professionals in real-world penetration testing. The company runs competitive red-teaming challenges through their Arena platform, including the Ultimate Jailbreaking Championship and Agent Red-Teaming Challenge with significant prize pools.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI deployment through technical safety measures and security standards. Focuses on developing tools for automated risk assessment and red-teaming rather than prescriptive regulations. Supports disclosure requirements for AI-generated content in professional settings.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy

organizations.name: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (HQ at 182 Howard Street)
organizations.notes: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) is an American philanthropic advising and funding organization with CEO Alexander Berger. As of June 2025, Coefficient Giving has directed more than $4 billion in grants across various focus areas including global health, scientific research, pandemic preparedness, potential risks from advanced AI, and farm animal welfare. In November 2025, Open Philanthropy was renamed Coefficient Giving, reflecting its evolution from working primarily with one philanthropic partner to supporting multiple major donors pursuing cost-effective, high-impact giving. The organization's founding and most significant ongoing partnership is with Good Ventures, the foundation of Cari Tuna and Dustin Moskovitz.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports both AI progress and safety measures through targeted regulation. Advocates for proportionate government intervention focused on catastrophic risks while opposing overly restrictive regulations on smaller-scale harms. Emphasizes bipartisan approaches and evidence-based policy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Power concentration

organizations.name: Redwood Research
organizations.notes: Redwood Research is a nonprofit organization founded in 2021 and based in Berkeley, California, dedicated to AI safety and security research aimed at mitigating catastrophic risks from misaligned advanced AI systems. The organization focuses on developing techniques to ensure AI systems align with developers' intentions, even under adversarial conditions, with key research areas including AI control protocols and the detection of strategic deception in models. Buck Shlegeris currently serves as CEO, having previously been CTO, and Ryan Greenblatt appears to be a key researcher at the organization. The organization reported total revenues of $10,036,347 for the fiscal year ending December 2023.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI control techniques where safety measures prevent unacceptable outcomes even if AIs intentionally subvert them. Argues labs should ensure powerful AIs are controlled and proposes transparency requirements including detailed risk reports and third-party audits.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Center on Long-Term Risk
organizations.notes: The Center on Long-Term Risk's goal is to address worst-case risks from the development and deployment of advanced AI systems, currently focused on conflict scenarios as well as technical and philosophical aspects of cooperation. The organization does interdisciplinary research, makes and recommends grants, and builds a community of professionals and other researchers around their priorities. CLR works on addressing the worst-case risks from the development and deployment of advanced AI systems in order to reduce s-risks. The organization was founded in 2013 according to external sources.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful development of AI systems with emphasis on preventing worst-case scenarios involving deliberate creation of suffering (s-risks). Focuses on technical interventions through AI safety community and frontier labs rather than direct regulatory advocacy.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
organizations.location: Washington, DC (Georgetown University Walsh School of Foreign Service)
organizations.notes: The Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET) is a policy research organization within Georgetown University's Walsh School of Foreign Service that was founded in 2019. When founded, it was Washington's first think tank focused entirely on security and emerging technology, and it quickly grew to be the largest research organization in the world working on AI policy. CSET provides decision-makers with data-driven analysis on the security implications of emerging technologies, currently focusing on the effects of progress in artificial intelligence (AI), advanced computing and biotechnology. The organization has received more than $100 million in funding through 2025, with Open Philanthropy as its main funder.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance with flexible, adaptable regulatory frameworks. Supports incident tracking, transparency requirements for frontier AI companies, and federal coordination while preserving innovation. Emphasizes need for regulatory agility that can evolve with technology rather than rigid one-size-fits-all approaches.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Loss of control, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Partnership on AI
organizations.notes: Partnership on AI (PAI) is a nonprofit global community of academic, civil society, and industry organizations who come together to address the most important questions in AI. PAI is an independent, nonprofit 501(c)(3) organization and funded by charitable contributions from philanthropy and corporate entities. The organization has 141 partners from 19 countries and focuses on advancing the safety and responsibility of AI systems. PAI was originally established by a coalition of representatives from technology companies, civil society organizations, and academic institutions, and supported originally by multi-year grants from Apple, Amazon, Meta, Google/DeepMind, IBM and Microsoft.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for worker inclusion in AI transparency efforts, responsible data enrichment practices protecting data workers, and multi-stakeholder governance approaches. Supports moderate regulation through documentation requirements, human rights due diligence in AI supply chains, and collective bargaining rights for workers affected by AI systems.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination, Privacy

organizations.name: Anthropic
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (with Sydney, Bengaluru offices)
organizations.notes: Anthropic is an artificial intelligence company founded in 2021 by former OpenAI leaders including Dario Amodei and Daniela Amodei. The company specializes in Constitutional AI and has developed Claude, a family of large language models designed for safety and enterprise use. Anthropic has experienced rapid growth, scaling from $1 billion ARR in 2024 to $14 billion in February 2026. The company is structured as a Public Benefit Corporation focused on AI safety research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'responsible scaling' with voluntary safety commitments, AI Safety Levels framework, and capability-based thresholds. Supports proportional regulation that scales with AI capabilities but opposes overly restrictive measures. Publicly supports targeted disclosure requirements and has influenced policy in California (SB 53), New York (RAISE Act), and EU AI Act.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, National security

organizations.name: Founders Fund
organizations.notes: Founders Fund is an American venture capital fund formed in 2005 and based in San Francisco, with roughly $17 billion in total assets under management as of 2025. The firm is closing in on a $6 billion fundraise for its fourth growth vehicle, with this rapid-fire pace happening less than a year after it wrapped up its previous $4.6 billion fund. Founders Fund was the first institutional investor in Space Exploration Technologies (SpaceX) and Palantir Technologies, and an early investor in Facebook. The firm's investments include Airbnb, Anduril, DeepMind, Rippling, Facebook, Ramp, Palantir Technologies, Pudgy Penguins, SpaceX, Spotify, Stripe, Neuralink, and Nubank.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Portfolio heavily weighted toward AI capabilities and defense tech companies (Anthropic, OpenAI, Anduril, Palantir). Investment thesis emphasizes 'competing with nation-states' and frontier technology development. Partners like Trae Stephens have criticized restrictive government approaches to China tech policy, favoring market-driven solutions.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Power concentration

organizations.name: Writers Guild of America
organizations.location: Los Angeles, CA (WGAW) and New York, NY (WGAE)
organizations.notes: The Writers Guild of America reached a tentative four-year agreement with the Alliance of Motion Picture and Television Producers in 2026, finalized after roughly three weeks of negotiations. The deal addresses AI protections, compensation increases, and health plan strengthening, coming nearly three years after a 148-day strike in 2023. The Writers Guild of America is comprised of two different American labor unions representing writers in film, television, radio, and online media: the Writers Guild of America, East (WGAE) headquartered in New York City and affiliated with the AFL-CIO, and the Writers Guild of America West (WGAW) headquartered in Los Angeles. The WGAE was officially founded in 1954 and represents thousands of members who write content for motion pictures, television, news and online media.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict AI protections including: writers cannot be replaced by AI-generated content, AI cannot be used as source material for guild-covered work, writers must be compensated when their work is used for AI training, and AI-generated text cannot determine writing credits.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Copyright/IP

organizations.name: Tarbell Center for AI Journalism
organizations.location: Claymont, DE (with global team in US and UK)
organizations.notes: The Tarbell Center for AI Journalism supports journalism that helps society navigate the emergence of increasingly advanced AI. The organization offers grants of $1,000–$20,000 to support original reporting published in established outlets, whether from freelancers or staff. The Tarbell Fellowship is a one-year program for journalists interested in covering artificial intelligence, with fellows securing a nine-month placement at a major newsroom and participating in a 10-week course covering AI and journalism fundamentals. Editorial independence is a core value for Tarbell and their grantees maintain complete autonomy over their reporting.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports journalism that emphasizes AI accountability, corporate transparency, and risk awareness. Mission statement explicitly focuses on holding AI companies accountable and fostering informed public discourse about AI risks ranging from bias to existential threats.
organizations.threat_models: Mission statement specifically mentions deepfake harassment, algorithmic bias, AI-enabled scams, bioterrorism, cyberattacks, authoritarian surveillance, mass unemployment, and human extinction/disempowerment as key concerns driving their journalism support.

organizations.name: Anthropic Institute
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (expanding to Washington, DC)
organizations.notes: The Anthropic Institute was launched on March 11, 2026, as a new effort to confront the most significant challenges that powerful AI will pose to societies. The Institute is led by Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark, who assumed a new role as Anthropic's Head of Public Benefit. The Institute has an interdisciplinary staff of machine learning engineers, economists, and social scientists, bringing together and expanding three of Anthropic's research teams: the Frontier Red Team, Societal Impacts, and Economic Research. The Institute launches with about 30 people and Clark expects the think tank's number of staff will double every year.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible scaling policies with safety thresholds, transparent governance frameworks, and evidence-based regulation that scales with AI capabilities. Supports mandatory safety evaluations and public reporting while maintaining ability to refuse certain use cases like mass surveillance.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Cybersecurity, National security, Power concentration

organizations.name: Existential Risk Observatory
organizations.notes: The Existential Risk Observatory is a nonprofit foundation founded in May 2021 in Amsterdam, The Netherlands, committed to reducing human extinction risks by informing the public debate. The organization employs 5 people and focuses on areas including unaligned AI, man-made pandemics, climate change, nuclear war, and other existential risks. The Observatory conducts research, media work, lobbying, and organizes events such as conferences on existential risks. Their work includes tracking public awareness of AI existential risk, finding that 24% of the US public is now aware of AI xrisk as of their most recent survey.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a Conditional AI Safety Treaty that would halt unsafe AI training if systems approach loss of control and alignment remains unsolved. Proposes compute thresholds requiring rigorous oversight, mandatory model evaluations, and international coordination through AI Safety Institutes with authority to pause development.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation

organizations.name: Goodfire
organizations.notes: Goodfire is an AI research company using interpretability to understand, learn from, and design AI systems, with a mission to build the next generation of safe and powerful AI. The company raised $150 million in Series B funding at a $1.25 billion valuation in February 2026, led by B Capital. Goodfire has developed a platform called Ember that decodes neurons inside AI models and lets users precisely shape their behaviors. The company has partnered with organizations like Arc Institute, Mayo Clinic, and Microsoft to deploy their interpretability technology.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI interpretability as essential for safety and control of advanced AI systems. Emphasizes the need for understanding and intentionally designing AI models rather than relying on black-box approaches. Supports collaborative safety research and responsible development practices.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation

organizations.name: OpenAI
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (HQ) with offices in London, Dublin, Tokyo, Singapore
organizations.notes: OpenAI is an American artificial intelligence research organization comprising both a nonprofit foundation and a controlled for-profit public benefit corporation, headquartered in San Francisco. The company was founded in December 2015 as a nonprofit, with a for-profit subsidiary created in 2019, and restructured in 2025 to operate more independently from the nonprofit. OpenAI's mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence benefits all of humanity. The company has 4,500 employees as of 2026 and reported revenue of US$13.1 billion in 2025.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'sensible regulation that does not slow us down' and opposes pre-deployment approval processes as 'disastrous.' Previously supported AI safety regulation (2023 Senate testimony) but shifted to opposing heavy-handed regulation by 2025. Supports voluntary safety frameworks like their Preparedness Framework over government mandates.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Existential risk, National security

organizations.name: AI Safety Foundation
organizations.notes: The AI Safety Foundation is a registered Canadian charity based in Canada that increases awareness and scientific understanding of the catastrophic risks of AI, by advancing education and research, in order to ensure a safer future for humanity. The AISF is proud to present an annual lecture series, the Hinton Lectures™, delivered by world-leading AI safety experts. The organization is supported by generous individuals, corporations and partners. Geoffrey Hinton serves as a Director and is described as Nobel Laureate and "Godfather of AI" who hosts The Hinton Lectures.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI safety measures and democratic oversight of AI development. CEO Ankesh Chandaria has written about the need for transparent processes, parliamentary review of high-impact AI deployments, and clear societal boundaries while avoiding innovation-stifling overreach.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Power concentration

organizations.name: Cyborgism
organizations.notes: Cyborgism is a strategy for safely accelerating alignment research by setting up human-in-the-loop systems which empower human agency rather than outsource it. The approach focuses on training and empowering 'cyborgs', a specific kind of human-in-the-loop system which enhances and extends a human operator's cognitive abilities without relying on outsourcing work to autonomous agents. Connor Leahy coined the term 'cyborgism'. The core of cyborgism is to reduce bandwidth constraints between humans and GPT in order to make deep integration possible.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for human-in-the-loop AI systems that enhance rather than replace human agency. Proposes cyborgism as an alternative to autonomous AI agents, emphasizing human-AI collaboration where humans remain the source of agency and decision-making while AI provides cognitive augmentation.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Effective Thesis
organizations.notes: Effective Thesis is an organization that aims to empower students and early-career individuals to focus their research and careers towards solving the world's biggest challenges. The organization was founded in July 2017 by David Janku, with initial support from Daniel Hnyk and members of the Czech EA Association. Since then, they have directly supported over 2,000 students across all services and coached more than 500 students one-on-one. The organization is based on the principles of Effective Altruism and is informed by the career advice organisation, 80,000 Hours.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on AI governance research through student thesis work and expert network. Director Joël Christoph and experts like Aditya Gilra work on AI safety and policy research. Expert network includes researchers advocating for responsible AI development, governance frameworks, and safety measures.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Bias/discrimination

organizations.name: How to pursue a career in technical AI alignment
organizations.notes: How to pursue a career in technical AI alignment is a comprehensive guide written by Charlie Rogers-Smith. The guide is written for people who are considering direct work on technical AI alignment and is expected to be most useful for people who are not yet working on alignment. The guide was published on June 4th, 2022 and provides information on types of alignment work, how to pursue different paths, and resources for learning AI alignment. It has been referenced and acknowledged by other major AI safety career resources from organizations like 80,000 Hours.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: This is a career guide rather than an organization with policy positions. The guide acknowledges different perspectives exist on AI alignment approaches and encourages readers to form their own views on AI safety priorities and strategies.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Aether
organizations.notes: Founded 2024/2025 as independent research group. 3 full-time researchers: Rohan Subramani (starting PhD at University of Toronto), Rauno Arike, and Shubhorup Biswas. $200k funding through December 2025 from anonymous donor. Advised by experts from Astera Institute, Apollo Research, Google DeepMind. Research agenda focuses on enhancing monitorability of LLM agents, with published work on chain-of-thought monitoring and hidden reasoning taxonomy. Currently hiring 1-2 additional researchers.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical research to influence frontier AI companies and governments through chain-of-thought monitorability and safe deployment of LLM agents. Advocates for preserving externalized reasoning traces to maintain interpretability as AI systems become more capable.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security

organizations.name: Google DeepMind
organizations.location: London, UK (HQ) + Mountain View, CA, New York, Montreal, Paris offices
organizations.notes: Google DeepMind brings together two of the world's leading AI labs — Google Brain and DeepMind — into a single, focused team led by CEO Demis Hassabis. The company's mission is to build AI responsibly to benefit humanity, working to advance artificial general intelligence (AGI). Google DeepMind has achieved notable breakthroughs including AlphaGo defeating world champion Go players, AlphaFold solving protein folding problems, and developing the Transformer architecture that underlies most large language models. The organization employs over 1,000 researchers and engineers from diverse disciplines and maintains partnerships with academic institutions, healthcare organizations, and government agencies.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports 'smart, adaptable regulation' over rigid rules, emphasizing international cooperation on AI governance. Advocates for evidence-based safety frameworks and voluntary industry standards while warning against overregulation that could stifle innovation. Promotes targeted safety measures that scale with AI capabilities.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, National security, Bias/discrimination, Existential risk

organizations.name: Modeling Cooperation
organizations.notes: Modeling Cooperation conducts long-term future research on improving cooperation in competition for the development of transformative artificial intelligence (AI). They build upon AI competition models to analyze ways to shift competitors' incentives toward more safety. The organization received a $77,116 grant from the Foresight Institute for their ongoing collaboration with Technology Strategy Roleplay (TSR). Modeling Cooperation is fiscally sponsored by Convergence Analysis.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on improving cooperation in AI competition dynamics through research tools and policy analysis. Develops software for decision-makers to understand AI competition risks and mitigate dangerous competition scenarios between AI developers.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration

organizations.name: Softmax
organizations.notes: Softmax is an AI alignment research company co-founded by Emmett Shear (cofounder and ex-CEO of Twitch, ex-interim-CEO of OpenAI) that is building a mathematical theory of 'organic alignment' - developing AI systems that can align with each other and with humans organically, through emergent cooperation, rather than via hard-coded, hierarchical control. The company is focused on AI agents discovering their 'values' even as those agents meet and aim to surpass human intelligence. Softmax has Ken Stanley as an advisor and collaborates with research partners.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for federal AI regulation that creates transparency requirements, safety documentation, and compliance barriers that favor companies with existing safety infrastructure. Through $20M political donation, supports regulatory frameworks that align US standards with EU AI Act principles.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration

organizations.name: Equilibria Network
organizations.notes: Equilibria Network is a Technology, Information and Internet company that describes itself as 'Designing New Forms Of Collective Intelligence'. The organization employs 2 people and has presence in United States and Sweden. They are running a workshop and research program on phylogeny of agents during autumn 2025. The company focuses on exploring the vast design space of possible coordination mechanisms and helping evaluate emergent risks and safety of multi-agent AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for spectral analysis-based governance approaches to monitor AI coordination systems, proposing specific metrics like human betweenness centrality across mechanism boundaries and spectral gap ratios to prevent AI disempowerment through gradual erosion of human agency in collective decision-making.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: Beneficial AI Foundation
organizations.notes: Beneficial AI Foundation (BAIF) is a nonprofit organization founded in 2023 with the mission "TO ENSURE THAT AI TECHNOLOGY DOESNT CAUSE HARM". The organization supports AI safety research through university partnerships and its Buterin Fellowship program, with research areas including mechanistic interpretability, audit standards, and red-teaming. BAIF manages the Quantitative AI Safety Initiative (QAISI), a multi-university partnership pursuing quantitative guarantees that AI will not cause harm. The organization is led by Max Tegmark as President and Treasurer and operates the Buterin AI Existential Safety Fellowship program in partnership with Future of Life Institute.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for quantitative safety standards similar to FDA drug approval and FAA aircraft certification requirements. Supports mandatory AI safety audits and standards requiring quantitative guarantees that AI systems will do what we want before deployment.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Cadenza Labs
organizations.notes: Cadenza Labs is an AI Safety research group consisting of four SERI-MATS alumni who work on interpretability research. The organization focuses on doing research which contributes to AI Safety, with their main goal being to research, benchmark and develop robust lie detectors for LLMs. They received funding from LTFF until the end of September 2023 and have been seeking additional funding to continue their work and expand their team. The group has produced research including work on LIARS' BENCH, a testbed for evaluating lie detection techniques in large language models.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical AI safety research, particularly developing lie detection and interpretability methods for LLMs. Work contributes to broader AI safety evaluation and monitoring capabilities without explicit policy advocacy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation

organizations.name: RAND Corporation
organizations.location: Santa Monica, CA (with offices in Arlington VA, Pittsburgh PA, Cambridge UK, Brussels, Canberra)
organizations.notes: RAND Corporation is a nonprofit, nonpartisan research organization that provides leaders with the information they need to make evidence-based decisions. The organization studies the potential opportunities and risks of artificial intelligence, including ways to strengthen the AI workforce, countering AI-driven social media manipulation by U.S. adversaries, AI governance and how to make future AI systems safer, potential military applications of AI, how AI is being used in the classroom, and identifying occupations at risk of being replaced by AI. RAND operates the RAND Center on AI, Security, and Technology (RAND CAST), which is at the forefront of charting the path for a flourishing future in an era of rapid technological change. The organization was founded in 1948 as an independent nonprofit corporation, separating from the Douglas Aircraft Company.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI governance approaches that strengthen security while preserving innovation. Research identifies four governance models ranging from government-enforced standards to industry self-regulation, emphasizing the need for proportionality, stakeholder engagement, and evidence-based policy responses calibrated to risk levels.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Cybersecurity, Loss of control, Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Misinformation, Power concentration

organizations.name: Geodesic Research
organizations.notes: Geodesic Research is a UK-based technical AI Safety organization focused on compute-intensive alignment research. The organization was founded by students at the University of Cambridge and employs 6 people. Geodesic Research published a paper titled "Alignment Pretraining: AI Discourse Causes Self-Fulfilling (Mis)alignment" which found that LLMs pretrained on data about misaligned AIs become less aligned, while pretraining with synthetic data about good AIs helps them become more aligned. The organization is a project of Meridian Cambridge.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on building the science of alignment and control through pretraining interventions, arguing that safety should be an end-to-end strategy throughout model training. Advocates for pretraining approaches to alignment as complement to post-training safety techniques.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: AI Objectives Institute
organizations.notes: The AI Objectives Institute (AOI) is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit R&D lab founded by the late internet security pioneer Dr. Peter Eckersley. The organization focuses on harnessing the potential of large-scale systems, including AI, markets, and bureaucracies, to advance humanity's interests. AOI was founded in 2021 and employs 13 people. The institute collaborates with and employs working groups of academics, scientists, economists, entrepreneurs, and government officials.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for market-based governance mechanisms alongside traditional regulation, including AI insurance, auditing, procurement standards, and due diligence. Supports standardized AI disclosures and participated in EU AI Act Codes of Practice development. Emphasizes sociotechnical alignment over purely technical approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Ergo Impact
organizations.location: Location not specified in search results
organizations.notes: Ergo Impact appears to be referenced in multiple contexts across different sources. According to source, Ergo Impact is listed as a funding supporter for RAND's Geopolitics of AGI Initiative, alongside other donors like Good Ventures, Open Philanthropy, and Jaan Tallinn. Source indicates that Effective Giving rebranded to Ergo Impact. Source shows Ergo Impact operates as ergoimpact.org and describes itself as helping UHNW philanthropists find and fund solutions to pressing problems, laying the foundation for what became Ergo Impact.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Philanthropic organization helping major donors find and fund solutions to pressing problems including AI safety. Listed as funder of AI safety research through RAND's Center for the Geopolitics of AGI. No explicit regulatory positions identified.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk

organizations.name: Forecasting Research Institute
organizations.location: Philadelphia, PA with distributed team across US, UK, and other countries
organizations.notes: The Forecasting Research Institute develops forecasting methods to improve decision-making on high-stakes issues. The organization employs 41 people and operates as a 501(c)(3) research nonprofit. FRI was founded in 2021 with initial support from Coefficient Giving. The institute's flagship project is the Existential Risk Persuasion Tournament (XPT), which brought together 169 participants who made forecasts about existential threats including AI, biosecurity, climate change, and nuclear war.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance through forecasting research. Conducts expert surveys showing median predictions that AI progress will be substantial but slower than AI lab leaders claim, suggesting measured regulatory approaches based on empirical forecasting rather than extreme positions.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Misinformation

organizations.name: Epoch AI
organizations.notes: Epoch AI is a nonprofit research institute founded to investigate the trajectory of artificial intelligence through empirical analysis of historical trends in machine learning. The organization was co-founded in June 2022 by Jaime Sevilla and Tamay Besiroglu, among others, with Sevilla serving as the organization's Executive Director. Epoch AI's mission is to improve society's understanding of the drivers, progress, and impact of artificial intelligence. The organization focuses on building a shared scientific foundation for thinking about AI, neutral to any specific agenda, so decisions about this technology are informed by the best available data.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for compute-based governance approaches while highlighting challenges with current metrics. Publishes research on AI governance frameworks and provides evidence-based analysis to inform policy decisions without pushing specific agendas.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Economic inequality, National security

organizations.name: Macroscopic Ventures
organizations.notes: Macroscopic Ventures is a Swiss nonprofit founded in 2019 that makes grants and investments to projects and people helping to build a future guided by reason and compassion. The organization employs 6 people and is headquartered in Basel, Switzerland, operating in 5 countries including United States, Japan, United Kingdom, and Canada. Their focus areas include artificial intelligence governance and policy, AI welfare, cooperative AI, societal long-term risks, and animal welfare. The organization was previously known as Center for Emerging Risk Research (CERR) and adopted its current name in February 2025.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on preventing catastrophic risks from AI misuse and conflict through governance, policy, and compute governance investments. Portfolio emphasizes AI safety, alignment, and risk mitigation rather than capabilities advancement. Partners with other safety-focused funders on initiatives like the Consortium for Digital Sentience Research.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration

organizations.name: Effective Institutions Project
organizations.location: San Francisco Bay Area (operates globally with staff in US, UK, Taiwan, South Africa, Norway, Belgium, Canada)
organizations.notes: The Effective Institutions Project is a philanthropic advisory and research organization that seeks out and incubates high-impact strategies to improve institutional decision-making around the world. AI governance has been a core focus for the organization since its founding in 2021, and the majority of the organization's resources are currently devoted toward making progress on this important and timely issue. The organization has 12 employees according to LinkedIn data. EIP works in partnership with funders and civil society organizations across the globe to coordinate efforts, share knowledge, and drive towards coherent and broadly endorsed policy outcomes that reduce the risks of transformative AI and enable society to flourish.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for slowing down advancement on the most powerful AI models to allow society time to prepare, supports mandatory safety evaluations and transparency requirements for frontier models, and promotes international cooperation on AI governance. Emphasizes 'buying time' for thoughtful oversight while the window for governance remains open.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, National security, Economic inequality, Misinformation

organizations.name: Cooperative AI Foundation
organizations.notes: The Cooperative AI Foundation (CAIF) is a charitable entity backed by a $15 million philanthropic commitment from Macroscopic Ventures. CAIF's mission is to support research that will improve the cooperative intelligence of advanced AI for the benefit of all. The foundation is registered with the Charity Commission for England and Wales under charity number 1201294. CAIF employs 16 people with a 77.8% year-over-year growth.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on differential progress in cooperative AI capabilities relative to dual-use capabilities like deception and manipulation. Emphasizes building AI systems that improve social welfare rather than general acceleration. Supports research on AI governance and international coordination challenges.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, National security

organizations.name: Lionheart Ventures
organizations.location: San Francisco Bay Area, CA (operates in 6 countries including UK, Costa Rica)
organizations.notes: Lionheart Ventures is an early-stage venture capital firm focused on the wise development of transformative technologies. Their main areas of focus are artificial intelligence and frontier mental health. The firm was founded in 2019 and is based in San Francisco. Their AI portfolio includes Softmax, Anthropic, Goodfire, Gray Swan, Piramidal, and Lucid Computing.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Takes AI risks seriously and invests specifically in AI startups that 'advance and defend human agency, resilience, and flourishing.' Aligns with Vitalik's defensive acceleration thesis and contributed to AI Assurance Technology report. Focuses on responsible AI development as investment strategy rather than explicit policy advocacy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Existential risk

organizations.name: Meta Charity Funders
organizations.notes: Meta Charity Funders (MCF) is a funding circle that aims to fund charitable projects working one level removed from direct impact. MCF is a network of donors sharing knowledge, discussing funding opportunities, and running joint open grant rounds in the EA meta space. The organization received approximately 60 applications in Spring 2025 and ultimately funded 8 projects, with members granting approximately $530,000 in that round. Members are expected to give above $100,000 to this cause area each year, which is the expected minimum annual giving to join.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Network of donors funding meta-charitable projects across cause areas including AI safety, global health, and animal welfare. Individual donors may have varying regulatory views, but the collective focuses on funding infrastructure rather than direct policy advocacy.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: Mythos Ventures
organizations.notes: Mythos Ventures is an early-stage venture capital firm based in San Francisco, California, founded in 2023. The firm closed $14 million in capital commitments for its first fund to invest in transformative AI companies. Mythos Ventures announced Fund II as a $30M pre-seed fund backing founders building AGI-proof companies. The firm employs 5 people and is headquartered at 1410 Franklin St, San Francisco.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Investment thesis emphasizes 'prosocial technologies' and 'safety by design' as market opportunities rather than regulatory necessities. Focuses on AI risk reduction through due diligence and enabling industry safety protocols, suggesting market-based solutions over regulatory intervention.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Existential risk

organizations.name: Senate AI Working Group
organizations.notes: The Senate AI Working Group is a bipartisan organization formed in the 118th Congress, consisting of Senate Majority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-NY), Senator Mike Rounds (R-SD), Senator Martin Heinrich (D-NM), and Senator Todd Young (R-IN). The Working Group was formed due to shared recognition of the profound changes artificial intelligence could bring to the world and the need to proactively work to harness opportunities and address risks of this transformational technology. The Working Group's objective has been to complement the traditional congressional committee-driven policy process, considering that AI technology does not neatly fall into the jurisdiction of any single committee. On May 15, 2024, the Working Group released a report titled "Driving U.S. Innovation in Artificial Intelligence: A Roadmap for Artificial Intelligence Policy in the United States Senate".
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted, sector-specific AI legislation rather than comprehensive omnibus bills. Emphasizes bipartisan approach focusing on existing law enforcement, emergency appropriations for AI research, and committee-driven policy development across eight priority areas including national security, workforce impacts, and high-risk AI uses.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Misinformation, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Cybersecurity, Power concentration

organizations.name: Juniper Ventures
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (also has Palo Alto office)
organizations.notes: Juniper Ventures is a venture capital firm that focuses on investing in startups that enhance the safety and security of artificial intelligence. The firm spun out of Climate Capital to invest in synthetic biology for the climate, with founding partners Michael Luciani and Jennifer Kan who had been investing in climate for years as part of Climate Capital. Their first fund is $10.6 million and is oversubscribed, with limited partners including family offices, foundations, and Allocator One serving as the anchor. The firm operates by writing checks between $100,000 and $500,000 to scientists who are working to commercialize their research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses exclusively on AI safety and assurance infrastructure investments, viewing AI safety as both necessary and commercially viable. Invests across the AI stack in areas like security, compliance, evals, interpretability, and governance. Takes a market-driven approach to safety rather than advocating for specific regulations.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination

organizations.name: Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative
organizations.notes: The Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative aims to understand and anticipate the lasting risks from AI through rigorous research into the technical and computational elements of AI development, combined with deep policy analysis. The Initiative is co-led by Professor Robert Trager, a social scientist specialising in international relations and frontier AI regulation, and Professor Michael Osborne, a specialist in machine learning. The Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative employs 30 people (+65.0% YoY, +13 people) and operates in 7 countries (including United Kingdom, United States, China, France, and Argentina). The Initiative is shaping global AI governance debates, with agenda-setting research informing policy and regulatory discussions across the UN, EU, UK and US.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for international AI governance frameworks including compute provider oversight, model evaluations, incident monitoring, and risk management protocols. Proposes jurisdictional certification approaches and non-proliferation regimes for advanced AI systems while emphasizing evidence-based policy development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Bias/discrimination, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: AI Standards Lab
organizations.location: Remote/distributed (team across US, Spain, Canada, Lithuania, Netherlands, UK, Germany)
organizations.notes: AI Standards Lab is a remote organization that aims to accelerate AI safety standards writing in organizations like ISO/IEC, CEN-CENELEC, the IEEE, and NIST. The organization employs 10 people and is structured as a virtual lab bringing together volunteers from around the world. AI Standards Lab has actively participated in the EU AI Act's General-Purpose AI Code of Practice drafting process, submitting detailed technical feedback across multiple drafts. The organization has published extensive reports analyzing EU AI Act amendments and providing recommendations on various regulatory proposals.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI safety standards and robust regulatory frameworks, particularly supporting EU AI Act implementation. Provides detailed technical recommendations on risk management measures, incident reporting requirements, and governance frameworks for general-purpose AI systems.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Privacy, Bias/discrimination, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: TruthfulAI
organizations.notes: TruthfulAI is a non-profit organization that researches situational awareness, deception, and hidden reasoning in language models. The team is led by Owain Evans and is based in Berkeley, California. The organization has published multiple papers including work on emergent misalignment published in Nature and the TruthfulQA benchmark. TruthfulAI works towards safe and aligned AI systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on creating standards and evaluation methods for AI truthfulness rather than broad regulation. Advocates for technical solutions to deception and alignment problems through research and dataset development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation

organizations.name: SaferAI
organizations.location: Paris, France (with staff across Belgium, UK, Spain, UAE)
organizations.notes: SaferAI is a French non-profit furthering risk management in policy and industry. SaferAI works across research and policy to make AI safer by advancing responsible AI development through modeling complex risks, improving company practices, and contributing to standards and policies. SaferAI has been selected by the European Commission's AI Office to provide technical assistance on assessing risks posed by GPAI models at EU level, winning Lot 1 of the 'Technical Assistance for AI Safety' tender. SaferAI released its first ratings evaluating the risk-management practices of top AI companies, with xAI receiving the lowest score of 0/5.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based regulatory frameworks with quantitative risk modeling, mandatory pre-deployment evaluations, and third-party audits for frontier AI systems. Supports EU AI Act implementation and develops technical standards for compliance. Promotes evidence-based capability thresholds and transparency requirements.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Existential risk

organizations.name: Alignment Ecosystem Development (AED)
organizations.location: United States (distributed team across US, France, New Zealand)
organizations.notes: Alignment Ecosystem Development (AED) is an AI safety field-building nonprofit focused on building and maintaining valuable digital infrastructure for the AI safety ecosystem. AED has built, taken ownership of, closely partnered with, and/or maintained approximately 15 different projects, including the AI Safety Map (aisafety.world), AI Safety Info (aisafety.info), and AI Safety Quest (aisafety.quest). The Long-Term Future Fund provided a $99,330 grant to AED through their fiscal sponsor, Ashgro Inc., covering a 1-year stipend for 1.25 full-time equivalents to build and maintain digital infrastructure. AED has received zero funding so far prior to this grant and has until now been entirely volunteer driven.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports infrastructure development for AI safety ecosystem including resources for alignment research, safety communities, and field-building. Focuses on enabling coordination and knowledge-sharing among safety researchers rather than direct regulatory advocacy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: AI Now Institute
organizations.notes: The AI Now Institute was founded in 2017 as a research institute studying the social implications of artificial intelligence and policy research that addresses the concentration of power in the tech industry. As of mid-2022, AI Now is operating as an independent organization and does not accept funding from corporate donors, including the tech industry we aim to hold accountable. The institute's co-executive directors are Amba Kak and Sarah Myers West. AI Now's leadership were invited to advise the US Federal Trade Commission on artificial intelligence in 2021.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'zero-trust' policy agenda with bright-line rules restricting harmful AI applications, independent oversight of AI development, and structural remedies including data restrictions and breaking up Big Tech's AI market dominance. Opposes industry self-regulation and supports strong federal and state enforcement.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Environmental

organizations.name: Workshop Labs
organizations.notes: Workshop Labs is a research lab focused on user-aligned models with a mission to make people irreplaceable. The company is building technology to train billions of models, each designed for their user, and describes itself as a public benefit corporation making AI that works for you, not AI that automates you for someone else. Luke Drago is identified as the co-founder and CEO of Workshop Labs. Rudolf Laine and Luke Drago are co-founding Workshop Labs according to a LinkedIn post from Rudolf Laine.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratizing AI through open-source development and decentralized infrastructure to prevent concentration of power. Emphasizes building AI systems that empower individual users while allowing them to retain control over their economically valuable data. Supports technical safety research to make AI systems 'tamper resistant' and alignment-focused.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Loss of control

organizations.name: The Verge
organizations.notes: The Verge is about technology and how it makes us feel, founded in 2011. The Verge was founded in 2011 in partnership with Vox Media, and covers the intersection of technology, science, art, and culture. The Verge is an ambitious multimedia effort to examine how technology will change life in the future for a massive mainstream audience. The site is powered by Vox Media's Chorus platform, a modern media stack built for web-native news in the 21st century.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage balances tech innovation enthusiasm with critical examination of corporate power and AI risks. Extensive reporting on AI company-Pentagon negotiations, data center environmental impacts, and regulatory battles suggests moderate oversight preference.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation, Environmental, National security, Labor displacement, Privacy

organizations.name: European Network for AI Safety (ENAIS)
organizations.location: Europe (distributed network), with key staff in Hungary, UK, Netherlands, Canada, France
organizations.notes: The European Network for AI Safety (ENAIS) is a dynamic community of researchers and policymakers from across Europe, united in their efforts to advance AI safety. ENAIS brings together experts from over 13 countries and counting, all working towards ensuring that artificial intelligence systems are safe, ethical, and positively impactful for humanity. ENAIS distinguishes itself from related organizations by its focus on existential risks from AI. ENAIS is funded by Lightspeed grants.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance and safety regulations, particularly focusing on existential risks from AI. Actively contributes to EU AI Act implementation and supports international coordination on AI safety standards. Promotes mandatory safety evaluations for advanced AI systems.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Power concentration

organizations.name: xAI
organizations.notes: xAI is an American AI company founded by Elon Musk on March 9, 2023, with a mission to "understand the true nature of the universe". SpaceX acquired xAI in February 2026 in an all-stock transaction that valued SpaceX at $1 trillion and xAI at $250 billion. The company's flagship product is Grok, an AI chatbot that is accessible on grok.com, iOS, and Android. xAI raised $20 billion in a Series E funding round in January 2026, with strategic investors including Nvidia and Cisco Investments.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for minimal AI regulation while acknowledging AI risks. Elon Musk has publicly opposed extensive AI regulation, favoring industry self-regulation through voluntary frameworks like their Risk Management Framework. Supports targeted transparency requirements but generally opposes broad regulatory constraints on AI development.
organizations.threat_models: Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: RAISEimpact
organizations.notes: Program of Successif launched in 2024-2025, fully funded by Open Philanthropy grant through May 2025. Led by Adam Tury (former Amazon tech leader, EA coach) and Patrick Gruban (board member of Effective Ventures UK). Currently serves 4 AI safety organizations as clients, eligibility requires organizations working on AI GCRs with 5+ paid staff. Offers 4-step process: discovery call, leadership workshop, recommendations review, and implementation support including conflict management, hiring consulting, and leadership coaching.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on strengthening organizational management and leadership practices within AI safety organizations rather than direct regulatory advocacy. Aims to amplify effectiveness of existing AI safety work through improved governance, hiring, and team dynamics.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Safe AI Forum
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA, USA + UK presence
organizations.notes: Safe AI Forum (SAIF) is a US 501(c)3 non-profit that aims to foster international collaboration to reduce AI risks. The organization was co-founded by Fynn Heide and Conor McGurk in late 2023. SAIF's flagship program is the International Dialogues on AI Safety (IDAIS), which brings together senior computer scientists and AI governance experts to build international collaboration on these risks. SAIF started as a fiscally sponsored project at FAR.AI in 2023 and has now successfully transitioned to operating as an independent non-profit as of May 2nd 2025.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety evaluations, third-party audits, and international cooperation on AI governance. Promotes minimum 33% spending commitment on AI safety R&D by developers and government agencies. Calls for clear red lines that mandate immediate termination of training if crossed, safety cases for frontier models, and strong international coordination mechanisms.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, National security

organizations.name: Trajectory Labs
organizations.notes: There are multiple organizations named Trajectory Labs with different focuses. One is a nonprofit based in downtown Toronto focused on AI safety, funded by Open Philanthropy and operational since January 1st, 2025. Another is described as a stealth AI security startup working with leading AI companies on red-teaming. A third appears to be a product studio that builds software, mobile apps, and web applications. There is also a company that builds safety evaluations and RL environments for frontier AI labs.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports targeted AI governance approaches including liability insurance mechanisms for catastrophic AI risk and measured regulatory frameworks for AI agents. Board member Kathrin Gardhouse researches practical policy solutions like insurance-based regulation and EU AI Act implementation for autonomous systems.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Catastrophic risk, Policy governance gaps

organizations.name: Safe Superintelligence Inc.
organizations.location: Palo Alto, CA + Tel Aviv, Israel
organizations.notes: Safe Superintelligence Inc. is an Israeli-American artificial intelligence company founded by Ilya Sutskever, the former chief scientist of OpenAI; Daniel Gross, former head of Apple's AI efforts; and Daniel Levy, an investor and AI researcher. The company was founded on June 19, 2024, with the goal to safely develop superintelligent AI. SSI raised $1 billion from venture capital firms including SV Angel, DST Global, Sequoia Capital, and Andreessen Horowitz in September 2024. In March 2025, SSI reached a $30 billion valuation in a funding round led by Greenoaks Capital.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Takes most precautionary approach among major AI labs, refusing to release any commercial products until safe superintelligence is achieved. Explicitly rejects commercial pressures and short-term deployment timelines in favor of pure safety-first research approach.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Timaeus
organizations.location: Remote-first with hubs in Berkeley, Melbourne, London
organizations.notes: Timaeus is an AI safety and alignment research nonprofit founded in October 2023, with the mission of making breakthrough scientific progress on AI safety using singular learning theory. The organization was co-founded by Jesse Hoogland (Executive Director) and Daniel Murfet, who recently left a tenured professorship in algebraic geometry to pursue this work full-time. Timaeus has grown from a team of four with an initial budget of $145k to a current team of eight (soon up to 12) with around $2.5M in funding for 2025. The organization focuses on applying singular learning theory to foundational problems within alignment, particularly through their "developmental interpretability" research agenda.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory positions found. Focus is on technical research rather than policy advocacy. Works on fundamental AI alignment research using singular learning theory and developmental interpretability.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Forethought (AI Safety Research Nonprofit)
organizations.location: Location not specified in search results
organizations.notes: Forethought is a nonprofit research organization focused on how to navigate the transition to a world with superintelligent AI systems. The organization was cofounded by Max Dalton, Will MacAskill, Tom Davidson, and Amrit Sidhu-Brar. They are a small research nonprofit that aims to tackle the most important questions they can find, unrestricted by the current Overton window. The organization was built out of the old Forethought Foundation for Global Priorities Research to help get the operations started.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive preparation for AI risks including preventing AI-enabled coups, establishing governance frameworks before superintelligence arrives, and ensuring democratic oversight keeps pace with AI development. Emphasizes need for safety measures and institutional preparedness rather than reactive responses.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, National security

organizations.name: Narrow Path (by ControlAI)
organizations.location: Unknown (ControlAI team members include US-based researchers)
organizations.notes: Narrow Path (by ControlAI) is a comprehensive plan to address extinction risks from artificial superintelligence, structured in three phases: Phase 0 (Safety), Phase 1 (Stability), and Phase 2 (Flourishing). The plan was published in October 2024 and is being actively pushed to lawmakers in the UK and US through ControlAI's Direct Institutional Plan (DIP). Andrea Miotti is identified as the Executive Director of Control AI and the lead author of A Narrow Path. The plan proposes preventing the development of artificial superintelligence for 20 years while building international oversight systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a comprehensive 3-phase plan to prevent artificial superintelligence development for 20 years through international coordination. Proposes strict licensing regimes, prohibitions on AI-improving-AI systems, and mandatory safety cases for powerful AI systems. Supports creating international institutions like MAGIC to oversee ASI development.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: FAR.AI
organizations.location: Berkeley, California (with global team across 10 countries)
organizations.notes: FAR.AI is a research & education non-profit founded in 2022 that focuses on ensuring advanced AI is safe and beneficial for everyone. The organization is led by CEO Adam Gleave and COO Karl Berzins. FAR.AI secured over $30 million in funding commitments throughout 2025 from supporters including Coefficient Giving (previously Open Philanthropy), Schmidt Sciences, Survival and Flourishing Fund, the Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET), and the AI Safety Fund (AISF) supported by the Frontier Model Forum (FMF). The organization was selected by the European Commission's AI Office to conduct technical safety research supporting the implementation of the EU's landmark Artificial Intelligence Act.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety evaluations and third-party audits for frontier AI systems before deployment. Supports the RAISE Act requiring safety plans for frontier AI companies and promotes technical governance mechanisms. Collaborates with EU AI Office on CBRN risk assessment and works with policymakers in UK, US, and EU on verification mechanisms for AI safety claims.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation

organizations.name: Intelligence Rising
organizations.location: London, UK (operates in 6 countries including US)
organizations.notes: Intelligence Rising is both a documentary film directed by Elena Andreicheva (2026) and a separate training workshop organization that provides strategic simulations of AI futures. The documentary features a geopolitical war game exploring AI-driven crises with participants including thinkers from the EU, China, and the United States. The training workshop organization is headquartered in London, United Kingdom and employs 10 people, offering educational roleplay games for decision-makers to experience tensions in AI development. There is also a separate Australian company called Intelligence Rising founded in 2018 by Neil Quarmby that provides online courses for intelligence professionals.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI governance through strategic simulation gaming to help decision-makers understand risks and coordination challenges in AI development. Emphasizes need for international cooperation, safety measures, and responsible development practices before transformative AI arrives.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Economic inequality, Existential risk

organizations.name: AI Safety Camp
organizations.notes: AI Safety Camp (AISC) is an online part time AI safety research program where participants join projects and work 10 hours/week with weekly meetings. The program runs as a 3-month long online program from January to April, where participants form teams to work on pre-selected AI Safety projects. AI Safety Camp has a seven-year track record of enabling participants to try their fit, find careers and start new organizations in AI Safety, hosting up-and-coming researchers outside the Bay Area and London hubs. The organization is headquartered in Diemen, North Holland, Netherlands and employs 58 people.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization has two distinct tracks: technical AI safety research projects and 'Stop/Pause AI' advocacy projects calling for moratoriums on AI development. Current edition includes projects ranging from technical alignment research to grassroots organizing for AI development bans.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality

organizations.name: AI Safety, Ethics and Society (Center for AI Safety)
organizations.notes: AI Safety, Ethics and Society is a course offered by the Center for AI Safety (CAIS) that offers 'a comprehensive introduction to how current AI systems work, their societal-scale risks, and how to manage them'. The course is described as 'Training 1000+ future AI safety leaders'. Dan Hendrycks authored a work titled 'AI Safety, Ethics, and Society' published by the Center for AI Safety in 2024. The course appears to be part of CAIS's broader mission to 'reduce societal-scale risks associated with AI by conducting safety research, building the field of AI safety researchers, and advocating for safety standards'.
