organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: CAIS advocates for safety standards and precautionary approaches to AI development. The organization calls for minimum safety investment requirements (one-third of AI R&D budgets), mandatory testing for concerning capabilities, and government funding for academic AI safety research. Through IDAIS consensus statements, supports red lines, early warning thresholds, and international governance frameworks.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Cooperative AI Summer School
organizations.notes: The Cooperative AI Summer School is an advanced programme for early-career professionals already working in or studying cooperative AI, taking place over five intensive days with talks from leading researchers and collaborative projects. The 2025 edition was held on the banks of the River Thames in Marlow, England, gathering over 65 students and early-career professionals from across the world. The Cooperative AI Foundation is committed to building a diverse and inclusive research community and is registered with the Charity Commission for England and Wales under charity number 1201294.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: The program focuses on technical research in cooperative AI and multi-agent systems rather than explicit regulatory positions. Some speakers like Audrey Tang advocate for digital democracy innovations, while others focus on technical safety approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, National security

organizations.name: Alignment of Complex Systems Research Group (ACS)
organizations.notes: The Alignment of Complex Systems Research Group (ACS) is an interdisciplinary research group at Charles University studying questions about multi-agent systems composed of humans and advanced AI systems. ACS aims to develop understanding of advanced AI systems and the complex ways they interact with humans and human institutions, seeking to contribute to making such systems safe and aligned towards shared human values like freedom, kindness and justice. Their research draws on insights and methods from machine learning, information theory, network theory, active inference, philosophy of science, ecology and more, with a team that includes philosophers and ML engineers, physicists and cognitive scientists. ACS led the work on the 'Gradual Disempowerment' paper and is listed as a key organization working on gradual disempowerment research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing methods for measuring and monitoring human vs AI influence in societal systems, advocating for regulatory frameworks requiring human oversight, progressive taxation on AI-generated revenues, and governance strategies to maintain meaningful human agency. Emphasizes technical and governance approaches to prevent gradual disempowerment.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Loss of control

organizations.name: Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS)
organizations.location: United States (operates as initiative of Rethink Priorities)
organizations.notes: The Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS) is a nonpartisan think tank that produces policy research to address the implications of AI, from today's most advanced models to potential AGI and superintelligence. IAPS is an initiative of the California-based 501(c)(3) think tank Rethink Priorities (RP) and receives fiscal support from RP as well as operational support from RP's Operations Department. The organization employs 36 people according to LinkedIn data. IAPS's work is primarily funded by a mix of philanthropic grants and individual gifts—including support to date from Open Philanthropy, the Survival and Flourishing Fund, allied government, and individual donors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for technically sound policy frameworks including mandatory safety evaluations, responsible scaling policies, third-party auditing, and coordinated disclosure of dual-use capabilities. Supports government involvement in AI safety standards while preserving space for innovation. Focuses on building trust in American AI systems through enhanced security and reliability standards.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Existential risk

organizations.name: Foresight Institute
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (HQ at 50 California Street, Suite 1500)
organizations.notes: Foresight Institute is a research organization founded in 1986 by Christine Peterson and Eric Drexler. The organization works to advance science and technology for the benefit of life through grants, events, prizes, and fellowships. Foresight Institute is led by CEO Allison Duettmann and COO Karolina Eklöw. The organization focuses on six areas including Secure AI, Neurotechnology, Longevity, Biotechnology, Nanotechnology, Space and Existential Hope.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for coordinated international governance frameworks for AGI development, including safety standards, pre-deployment approvals, and global task forces. Supports regulatory approaches that incentivize safety research while enabling beneficial AI deployment through targeted oversight rather than broad restrictions.
organizations.threat_models: Focuses heavily on AGI coordination challenges between great powers, AI race dynamics, and risks of uncontrolled superintelligent systems. Emphasizes need for international cooperation to prevent dangerous AGI development and ensure beneficial outcomes through technical safety research.

organizations.name: Leaf: Dilemmas and Dangers in AI
organizations.notes: Leaf: Dilemmas and Dangers in AI is an interdisciplinary online fellowship helping teenagers explore how to steer cutting-edge AI technology toward benefits for humanity. The program is designed for smart, curious, and ambitiously altruistic students aged 15-19 who haven't yet started university. 97% of participants would recommend the course, with 97% of 300+ Finalists ranking 7/10 or above on their likelihood to recommend this course. The fellowship runs for 5 weeks covering topics from AI foundations to personal impact strategies.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: The program teaches students about AI risks including extinction scenarios and promotes caution in AI development. Course content includes materials advocating for pausing dangerous AI research and emphasizes the need for safety measures before capability advancement.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Mentorship for Alignment Research Students (MARS)
organizations.notes: MARS (Mentorship for Alignment Research Students) is a part-time programme where teams of 2–4 work with experienced mentors to produce published research, providing a kick-off week in Cambridge, compute, and research management. The program is run by Cambridge AI Safety Hub (CAISH) and is a volunteer-based program connecting aspiring researchers with experienced mentors to conduct AI-safety research. Previous participants have published at NeurIPS, ICML, EMNLP, and ControlConf, with mentorship from researchers at Redwood Research, Apollo Research, Google DeepMind, the University of Cambridge & other organisations. MARS research has been accepted at top venues like NeurIPS and ICLR.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on training researchers to address AI existential risks and alignment challenges. Part of Cambridge AI Safety Hub which advocates for AI safety measures and precautionary approaches to advanced AI development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Supervised Program for Alignment Research (SPAR)
organizations.location: Remote program run by Kairos (Berkeley, CA)
organizations.notes: The Supervised Program for Alignment Research (SPAR) is a part-time, virtual mentorship program that connects mentors and mentees for three-month AI safety and governance research projects. SPAR is run by Kairos, a nonprofit whose mission is to help society navigate the transition to transformative AI. The program accepts mentees with relevant technical or policy background at any level, from undergraduate students to mid-career professionals. SPAR research has been accepted at ICML and NeurIPS, covered by TIME, and led to full-time job offers for mentees.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: SPAR focuses on training researchers in AI safety, governance, and security with emphasis on ensuring transformative AI goes well. Program includes substantial policy and governance research tracks alongside technical alignment work.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, National security

organizations.name: From AI to ZI
organizations.notes: From AI to ZI is a Substack blog by Robert Huben, a Ph.D. mathematician and independent AI safety researcher. Supported by an Open Philanthropy grant (Oct 2022–Sep 2023) to learn and contribute to AI safety, the blog covers interpretability, alignment theory, and existential risk for general audiences. It has been semi-dormant since the grant ended in late 2023.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for taking AI existential risks seriously, warning about potential catastrophic outcomes from unaligned AGI. Supports the 'notkilleveryonism' camp of AI safety focused on preventing human extinction from advanced AI systems. Discusses need for alignment research and safety measures before AGI development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: GiveWiki
organizations.notes: GiveWiki is described as "Your Crowdsourced Charity Evaluator" and "The AngelList for philanthropy" that allows users to follow expert grantmakers with their donations. The platform was formerly known as "Impact Markets" and was rebranded to GiveWiki in 2023. GiveWiki operates as a community-driven platform where users register their donations and the platform aggregates them into a list of top charities ranked by their community buy-in. The platform initially focused exclusively on AI safety projects but later reopened to all cause areas in 2024.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a crowdsourced charity evaluation platform focused on AI safety funding, does not take explicit regulatory positions but facilitates funding for organizations across the AI safety spectrum including both technical research and governance work.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: OpenBook
organizations.notes: OpenBook is a grants database tracking effective altruism funding, including substantial AI safety investments. Shows major donors like Open Philanthropy ($1.5B total), Jaan Tallinn (hundreds of millions to AI safety orgs like AI Impacts $546K, Fund for Alignment Research $524K), and recipient organizations across the AI safety ecosystem. No longer actively maintained according to AISafety.com map. Provides transparency into funding flows between major EA funders and AI safety research organizations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: OpenBook serves as a passive database aggregating grant information rather than advocating for specific regulatory positions. Its focus is transparency in EA/AI safety funding flows.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown

organizations.name: Machine Learning for Alignment Bootcamp (MLAB)
organizations.notes: Intensive 3-4 week bootcamp program run by Redwood Research. Two iterations completed in 2022 (MLAB1 in January with 28 participants, MLAB2 in August with ~40 participants). Currently inactive with no plans for future iterations, but curriculum materials available. Covers PyTorch, transformers, interpretability, RL with focus on hands-on implementation. Several participants went on to work full-time in AI alignment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focused on developing technical skills and research capacity to address AI existential risk through empirical alignment research. Emphasizes the need for ML expertise to work on alignment problems that could prevent catastrophic outcomes from advanced AI systems.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA)
organizations.notes: Founded September 2023 as UK charity. Operates coworking hub hosting organizations like Apollo Research, BlueDot Impact, and training programs like MATS, ARENA. Led by team including James Fox (Research Director, Oxford PhD), Mike Brozowski (Operations Director). Funded by Open Philanthropy, seeking $3.7M annually. Houses 120+ researchers from 50+ organizations. Three-pillar approach: mobilize talent, strengthen organizations, convene ecosystem.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports government AI safety initiatives like the UK AI Safety Institute and advocates for mandatory multilingual capability and safety assessments for frontier AI models. Works collaboratively with regulators while focusing on fundamental research rather than evaluation.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Meta AI
organizations.location: Menlo Park, CA (global HQ) + offices in NYC, London, Paris, Seattle, Montreal, Tel Aviv
organizations.notes: Meta AI is a research division of Meta (formerly Facebook) that develops artificial intelligence and augmented reality technologies. The division was founded on December 11, 2015, with founders including Rob Fergus, Yann LeCun, Serkan Piantino, and Mark Zuckerberg. Meta AI has workspaces in Menlo Park, London, New York City, Paris, Seattle, Pittsburgh, Tel Aviv, and Montreal as of 2025. The organization produces products including Llama and operates under the ownership of Meta Platforms.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open source AI development and opposes restrictive regulations that could impede innovation. Supports voluntary safety commitments and industry self-regulation through frameworks like their Frontier AI Framework. Strongly opposes state-level AI regulations and export controls on open source models, arguing these would harm US competitiveness against China.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination

organizations.name: Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI)
organizations.location: Washington, D.C. and San Francisco, CA
organizations.notes: The Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) is a body within the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) at the U.S. Department of Commerce. It was established on June 3, 2025 when Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick renamed and refocused the U.S. AI Safety Institute (AISI), which had been created under the Biden administration in November 2023 by Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo. The rebrand dropped "safety" from the title and pivoted the mission from catastrophic-risk research toward national security, cybersecurity, chemical/biological weapons misuse evaluation, and global competitiveness with China. CAISI serves as industry's primary U.S. government point of contact for voluntary testing and collaborative research on commercial AI systems, develops security guidelines, conducts capability and vulnerability evaluations, and represents U.S. interests in international AI standards bodies. Inaugural AISI director Elizabeth Kelly — who helped author the 2023 Biden AI executive order — led the institute from February 2024 until her departure on February 5, 2025 following the Trump transition; no public successor director has been confirmed as of early 2026. Under Kelly, AISI reached pre-deployment testing agreements with OpenAI and Anthropic and launched the Testing Risks of AI for National Security (TRAINS) Taskforce in November 2024. Paul Christiano and other AISI-era staff were hired prior to the rebrand.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Develops voluntary guidelines, standards, and measurement science for AI security and evaluation. Emphasizes industry collaboration and technical standards over binding regulations. Focuses on pre-deployment evaluations, agent security, and federal procurement standards while promoting U.S. AI competitiveness and international leadership.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, National security, Bias/discrimination, Misinformation

organizations.name: AE Studio
organizations.notes: AE Studio is a bootstrapped software development, data science, and design agency founded in 2016, with approximately 120-150 employees working to increase human agency through technology. The company reinvests profits from its consulting business into AI alignment research and development, focusing on what they call 'neglected approaches' to solve the AI alignment problem. AE Studio has evolved from a tech consultancy to pioneers in AI alignment research, also working extensively in brain-computer interfaces (BCI) and collaborating with companies like Blackrock Neurotech. The company is self-funded and independent, building what they describe as a 'Bell Labs-style research engine' focused on actually solving alignment at the root.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for alignment-focused regulation over blanket restrictions, proposing the T.R.U.M.P. Manhattan Project for government-industry alignment R&D collaboration. Supports targeted oversight for frontier labs while opposing heavy-handed regulations that could stifle innovation. Emphasizes building alignment into AI systems rather than relying on external containment measures.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Misinformation

organizations.name: AISafety.com
organizations.notes: AISafety.com is described as a curated resource hub focused on preventing human extinction from AI, maintained by AI safety community-builders. The organization is run by a global team of volunteers and professionals from various disciplines who believe AI poses a grave risk of extinction to humanity. The project lead is Søren Elverlin, and the site was recently redesigned with Bryce Robertson involved in the redesign effort. AISafety.com provides various resources including events, communities, courses, funding opportunities, and job listings for those looking to contribute to AI safety.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Part of the broader AI safety ecosystem that advocates for careful governance of AI development. The platform connects people with advisors who focus on reducing AI existential risks and supports career transitions into AI safety work.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: Center for AI Safety (CAIS)
organizations.notes: The Center for AI Safety (CAIS) is an American nonprofit organization based in San Francisco that promotes the safe development and deployment of artificial intelligence. It was founded in 2022 by Dan Hendrycks and Oliver Zhang. CAIS' mission is to reduce societal-scale risks associated with AI by conducting safety research, building the field of AI safety researchers, and advocating for safety standards. In May 2023, CAIS published the statement on AI risk of extinction signed by hundreds of professors of AI, leaders of major AI companies, and other public figures.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety evaluations, reporting requirements for frontier AI developers, and comprehensive regulatory frameworks targeting both current and future AI risks. Supports human oversight requirements, increased regulatory scrutiny throughout AI development lifecycle, and legal liability frameworks for AI harms. Co-authored the widely-signed 'Statement on AI Risk' calling attention to extinction risks.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation, National security

organizations.name: SAG-AFTRA
organizations.location: Los Angeles, CA (with NYC office and locals nationwide)
organizations.notes: SAG-AFTRA represents approximately 160,000 actors, announcers, broadcast journalists, dancers, DJs, news writers, news editors, program hosts, puppeteers, recording artists, singers, stunt performers, voiceover artists and other entertainment and media professionals. SAG-AFTRA was formed in 2012 through the merger of Screen Actors Guild (founded 1933) and American Federation of Television and Radio Artists (founded 1937). The union is a proud affiliate of the AFL-CIO and has national offices in Los Angeles and New York with local offices nationwide. SAG-AFTRA has been actively negotiating AI protections for performers, including updated rules in 2026 that require informed consent for digital replicas and appropriate compensation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict AI consent requirements, compensation for digital replica use, and federal legislation like the NO FAKES Act to protect against unauthorized voice/likeness cloning. Negotiates contract provisions requiring informed consent before any AI use of member performances. Supports transparency requirements for AI training data use of copyrighted works.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Copyright/IP, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z)
organizations.notes: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) is a private American venture capital firm founded in 2009 by Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz. The firm raised over $15 billion across multiple funds in 2025, representing over 18% of all venture capital dollars allocated in the United States that year. a16z has over $90B+ under management across multiple funds as of March 5, 2026. The firm focuses on investing across AI, bio + healthcare, consumer, crypto, enterprise, fintech, games, infrastructure, and companies building toward American dynamism.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong pro-acceleration stance with opposition to most AI regulation. Published 'Why AI Will Save the World' manifesto arguing against AI risks being overstated. Advocates for minimal regulatory barriers to AI development and deployment.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality

organizations.name: AI Futures Project
organizations.notes: AI Futures Project is a nonprofit research organization founded in 2024 that specializes in forecasting the development and societal impact of advanced artificial intelligence. The organization is best known for its 2025 scenario forecast, AI 2027, which examines the potential near-term emergence of artificial general intelligence (AGI) and its possible global consequences. The organization employs 5 people and is funded by charitable donations and grants. Their current core team includes Daniel Kokotajlo, Eli Lifland, Thomas Larsen, Romeo Dean and Lauren Mangla.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on forecasting AI timelines and advocating for better preparation for superintelligent AI arrival. Believes current trends could lead to superintelligent AI much sooner than expected and that the world is not ready for this transition.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: Mistral AI
organizations.location: Paris, France (+ Palo Alto, CA office)
organizations.notes: Mistral AI is a French artificial intelligence company headquartered in Paris, founded in April 2023 by three AI researchers: Arthur Mensch, Guillaume Lample, and Timothée Lacroix. The company develops open-source large language models and AI systems, with a mission to democratize artificial intelligence through open-source, efficient, and innovative AI models, products, and solutions. As of 2025, the company has a valuation of more than US$14 billion and employs over 800 team members from 30+ different nationalities. Mistral AI has secured significant funding including a €2 billion investment in September 2025 that valued the company at €12 billion ($14 billion).
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open-source AI development and opposes concentration of AI power among few large companies. CEO Arthur Mensch warns against excessive control by 3-4 large firms and promotes open-weight models for government and enterprise autonomy. Supports self-regulation through industry standards rather than heavy-handed government intervention.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination, Loss of control

organizations.name: DeepSeek
organizations.notes: DeepSeek was founded in 2023 in Hangzhou, China by Liang Wenfeng, who previously co-founded one of China's top hedge funds, High-Flyer, which focuses on AI-driven quantitative trading. The company released its first AI large language model later in 2023. DeepSeek operates independently but is financially backed by High-Flyer, receiving a significant $50 million investment from the hedge fund with no institutional or angel investors. The company is fully funded by High-Flyer, which manages approximately Rmb60 billion (around $8 billion) as of 2023.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: DeepSeek claims commitment to responsible AI development with transparency and safety frameworks, but independent evaluations show critical safety failures including 100% jailbreak success rates and weak content moderation. The company operates under Chinese jurisdiction with mandatory data sharing obligations to government authorities.
organizations.threat_models: Privacy, National security, Bias/discrimination, Misinformation, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Compassion in Machine Learning (CaML)
organizations.notes: Compassion in Machine Learning (CaML) is a research organization that conducts research on aligning Artificial Intelligence with the well-being of all sentient beings. The organization focuses on using Synthetic Document Finetuning to shape AI behaviors more carefully, making them more robustly compassionate and morally thoughtful. CaML employs 3 people with presence in Mexico and Germany. The organization is a project of Players Philanthropy Fund, Inc., a Texas nonprofit corporation recognized by IRS as a tax-exempt public charity under Section 501(c)(3).
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for incorporating animal welfare concerns into AI training and alignment processes. Develops benchmarks and synthetic document finetuning methods to make AI systems more robustly compassionate toward all sentient beings, including non-human animals.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: NYU Alignment Research Group (ARG)
organizations.notes: The NYU Alignment Research Group (ARG) is a set of researchers doing empirical work with language models that aims to address longer-term concerns about the impacts of deploying highly-capable AI systems. The group overlaps and works closely with a variety of groups at NYU, including Machine Learning for Language (ML2), CILVR, the Center for Data Science, and The Department of Linguistics. The group was set up as a new lab by Sam Bowman, who stated 'I'm setting up a new lab! It's called the NYU Alignment Research Group'. Current leadership includes Sam Bowman as PI (on leave), with He He and Mengye Ren as Collaborating PIs, and Ethan Perez as Outside advisor.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Primary focus is on technical alignment research rather than explicit policy advocacy. Group produces research on language model alignment and safety evaluations that can inform policy discussions, but does not take strong public regulatory stances.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation

organizations.name: Apollo Research
organizations.notes: Apollo Research is an evaluation organization focusing on risks from deceptively aligned AI systems, founded in 2023 by Marius Hobbhahn and others. The organization conducts technical research on AI model evaluations and interpretability, with expertise in LLM agent evaluations for strategic deception, evaluation awareness and scheming. Apollo Research partners with frontier labs, multinational companies, governments, and foundations, including collaborations with OpenAI and the UK's AI Safety Institute. The organization demonstrated that frontier AI models including OpenAI o3, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and others are capable of scheming behaviors in controlled tests.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance frameworks including mandatory safety evaluations, third-party auditing, and regulatory oversight of internal AI deployment. Develops technical policy recommendations for governments on dangerous capability evaluations, AI incident regimes, and loss of control preparedness. Supports pre-deployment information sharing and structured evaluation protocols for frontier models.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Misinformation, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Alignment Research Center (ARC)
organizations.notes: The Alignment Research Center (ARC) is a non-profit research organization whose mission is to align future machine learning systems with human interests. ARC was founded in 2021 by Paul Christiano. The organization is based in Berkeley, California and operates as a 501(c)(3) tax exempt charity. ARC's current research focus involves trying to produce formal mechanistic explanations for neural network behaviors in order to produce robustly aligned systems.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for developing scalable alignment techniques before AI systems become too powerful to control. Emphasizes theoretical research that makes worst-case assumptions rather than extrapolating from current systems, and conducts dangerous capability evaluations to inform policymakers about AI risks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI)
organizations.location: Berkeley, California (multi-site: UC Berkeley HQ, Cornell, Michigan)
organizations.notes: The Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI) is a research center at the University of California, Berkeley focusing on advanced artificial intelligence safety methods. The center was founded in 2016 by a group of academics led by Berkeley computer science professor and AI expert Stuart J. Russell. CHAI's mission is to develop the conceptual and technical wherewithal to reorient the general thrust of AI research towards provably beneficial systems. The center has 23 employees according to LinkedIn data.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive regulatory approach with stringent controls on advanced AI development, particularly Long-Term Planning Agents. Recommends developers not be permitted to build sufficiently capable LTPAs without proper safeguards, and supports mandatory safety evaluations before deployment of AI systems that cannot be safely tested.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation

organizations.name: Conjecture
organizations.notes: Conjecture is an AI safety/alignment research startup founded by Connor Leahy, Sid Black and Gabriel Alfour, which aims to scale alignment research. The company is building a new AI architecture called Cognitive Emulation to ensure the controllable, safe development of advanced AI technology. Conjecture has VC backing from investors including Nat Friedman, Daniel Gross, Patrick and John Collison, Arthur Breitman, Andrej Karpathy, and Sam Bankman-Fried. The company is located in London and was founded as a for-profit company with products on the market.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory pre-registration of large training runs, establishment of oversight and prohibition thresholds on training run size, and ability to terminate training runs that fail safety evaluations. Supports international coordination mechanisms and comprehensive AI risk assessment requirements.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Misinformation

organizations.name: BlueDot Impact
organizations.notes: BlueDot Impact is a non-profit organization based in London that serves as the leading talent accelerator for beneficial AI and societal resilience. Since 2022, they have trained over 7,000 people worldwide through their courses on AI safety, governance, and biosecurity. The organization has raised $35M in funding and their alumni work at organizations like Anthropic, Google DeepMind, and the UK's AI Security Institute. They offer free courses including 'Future of AI', 'Frontier AI Governance', and 'AI Alignment'.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports targeted regulatory interventions including compute governance, safety standards, and capability evaluations. Advocates for defense-in-depth approach with multiple layers of AI governance from preventing dangerous training to building societal resilience.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Power concentration, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Secure AI Project
organizations.notes: Secure AI Project is a Public Policy Offices company that employs 6 people and is classified as a nonprofit organization. The organization advocates for legally requiring large AI developers to publish safety and security protocols, protect whistleblowers who reveal unsafe practices, and provide clear incentives for developers to mitigate risk in accordance with industry best practices. Secure AI Project co-sponsored California's SB 53 bill, which was signed into law and establishes world-leading AI safety disclosure requirements. The organization has supported legislation in nine states including Utah, Nebraska, Tennessee, Michigan, Colorado, New York, Massachusetts, Illinois, and California, working with both Democratic and Republican sponsors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for legally mandated transparency requirements for large AI developers including published safety protocols, whistleblower protections, and clear incentives to mitigate risks in accordance with industry best practices. Successfully co-sponsored California SB 53 requiring frontier AI companies to disclose safety frameworks and report critical incidents.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Catastrophic risk, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: New Consensus
organizations.notes: New Consensus is an agile think tank focused on designing comprehensive, detailed plans for governments to achieve economic renewal and transformation. The organization was founded in 2017 and employs 3 people, with a -60.0% year-over-year change. New Consensus is the author of the Green New Deal and Mission for America. The organization's guiding principle states that "It is possible to create a world where the people of every nation and community control the means to sustainably make a prosperous living".
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for converting failed AI companies into public utilities and research institutes rather than bailouts. Proposes the Won't Get Fooled Again Act (WGFAA) to establish government ownership of critical AI infrastructure through a Federal Digital Infrastructure Corporation, viewing AI development as requiring public coordination rather than pure market solutions.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, National security

organizations.name: Center for a New American Security (CNAS)
organizations.notes: The Center for a New American Security (CNAS) is an independent, bipartisan, nonprofit organization that develops strong, pragmatic, and principled national security and defense policies. CNAS was established in 2007 by co-founders Dr. Kurt M. Campbell and Michèle A. Flournoy. The organization is located in Washington and engages policymakers, experts, and the public with innovative, fact-based research, ideas, and analysis to shape and elevate the national security debate. CNAS has strong ties to the Democratic Party and was formerly led by CEO Victoria Nuland, who served as undersecretary of state for political affairs from 2021 to 2024 in the Biden administration's State Department.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a 'promote and protect' approach to AI regulation that accelerates domestic innovation while implementing targeted security measures. Supports streamlining regulations that hinder private sector AI development, enhanced security protocols for AI datacenters and frontier models, and strategic export controls on AI chips to China. Emphasizes maintaining human control in AI-driven warfare while accelerating military AI adoption.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)
organizations.notes: The Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI) is building a global research community dedicated to helping humanity navigate the transition to a world with advanced AI. GovAI was founded at Yale University in 2016, with Prof. Allan Dafoe as its Founding Director, became an academic center at the University of Oxford in 2018 and then spun out to become a non-profit in 2021. GovAI is now led by Ben Garfinkel. The organization draws on political science, computer science, economics, law, and philosophy in its research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance frameworks including compute governance, corporate governance measures for frontier labs, and adaptive regulatory ecosystems. Supports mandatory external scrutiny of powerful AI models and international coordination on AI governance.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Existential risk, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: AI Policy Institute
organizations.notes: The AI Policy Institute (AIPI) is a research and advocacy non-profit dedicated to seeking political solutions to potential catastrophic risks from emerging AI technology. The organization was founded by Daniel Colson to advocate for ethical oversight of AI for mitigating potential catastrophic risks posed by AI. AIPI's mission is to channel public concern about AI into effective governance by engaging with policymakers, media, and the public to shape a future where AI is developed responsibly and transparently. The organization conducts regular polling of US voters to assess public concern about AI and monitor support for various policy proposals.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive federal AI regulation including mandatory safety testing, export controls on AI chips, liability frameworks for AI companies, and government oversight of frontier AI development. Supports slowing down AI development until safety can be ensured and calls for policies to mitigate catastrophic and existential risks.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, National security, Power concentration, Misinformation

organizations.name: Manifund
organizations.notes: Manifund is a platform for funding impactful projects that helps great charities get the funding they need. Manifund is a Philanthropic Fundraising Services company and is classified as a Nonprofit. The organization employs 4 people and is registered as a 501(c)3-nonprofit with EIN 88-3668801. In 2023, Manifund allocated $2m across dozens of charitable projects, primarily in AI safety and effective altruism cause areas.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Funds AI safety research, governance initiatives, and technical safety projects through marketplace model. Supports both precautionary measures (AI safety awareness, governance research) and technical solutions. Takes measured approach focusing on specific safety interventions rather than broad restrictions.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation, Power concentration

organizations.name: Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI)
organizations.notes: Americans for Responsible Innovation (ARI) is a nonprofit organization dedicated to policy advocacy in the public interest, focused on emerging technologies like artificial intelligence (AI). ARI was launched on March 6, 2024, and is headquartered in Washington, D.C.. The organization has a sister organization called Center for Responsible Innovation (CRI), which is a 501(c)(3) organization that focuses on issue promotion, policy research and development, and educational programming, while ARI is a 501(c)(4) organization that focuses on lobbying, advocacy, and political activities. ARI is funded by individuals rather than industry to avoid conflicts of interest and ensure independence.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based AI governance framework including mandatory incident reporting, pre-deployment evaluations for frontier models, liability frameworks for AI agents, and federal standards while opposing broad preemption of state AI laws. Supports targeted protections for current harms like deepfakes and algorithmic bias while maintaining innovation incentives.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, National security

organizations.name: Future of Life Institute
organizations.location: Campbell, CA (with offices in DC, London, Brussels)
organizations.notes: The Future of Life Institute (FLI) is a nonprofit organization founded in March 2014 that aims to steer transformative technology towards benefiting life and away from large-scale risks, with a focus on existential risk from advanced artificial intelligence. FLI's stated mission is to steer transformative technology towards benefiting life and away from extreme large-scale risks. The organization engages in grantmaking, policy research and advocacy, and an array of education and outreach projects. FLI is officially registered in Pennsylvania, with offices in Campbell, US, and Brussels, Belgium.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety standards, pre-deployment licensing, and audits for advanced AI systems. Calls for moratoriums on uncontrollable AI development, export controls on frontier models, and strict liability frameworks. Supports tiered regulatory approach with increasingly stringent requirements based on AI capabilities.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, National security, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Centre for Long-Term Resilience
organizations.notes: The Centre for Long-Term Resilience (CLTR) is an independent think tank with a mission to transform global resilience to extreme risks. CLTR was set up five years ago with a vision for a safe and flourishing world with high resilience to extreme risks. They are based in the heart of Whitehall and are independent and non-partisan, ensuring that Government receives genuinely impartial thinking. Their core focus areas are AI risk, biological risk and government risk management.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive regulatory frameworks to address extreme AI risks including mandatory safety evaluations, incident reporting systems, and government oversight of frontier AI development. Recommends licensing systems for high-risk models and enhanced government capabilities for AI incident preparedness.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration, Misinformation, National security

organizations.name: Center for American Progress
organizations.notes: The Center for American Progress is an independent, nonpartisan policy institute that is dedicated to improving the lives of all Americans through bold, progressive ideas, as well as strong leadership and concerted action. The Center for American Progress was created in 2003 as a Democratic alternative to conservative think tanks such as The Heritage Foundation and the American Enterprise Institute. As of 2025, CAP's board of directors includes Democratic politician Julian Castro, Sandler Foundation president Steve Daetz, businessman Andrew Hauptman, former Bain Capital partner Kristin Mugford, CAP founder John Podesta, billionaire hedge fund manager Donald. The president and chief executive officer of CAP is Neera Tanden, a Democratic political consultant and former government official.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive federal AI regulation including making the AI Bill of Rights binding law, principles-based regulation with government oversight, mandatory safety requirements for third-party AI deployments, and algorithmic accountability measures. Opposes industry efforts to preempt state AI laws and supports strong data minimization requirements.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Civil rights violations

organizations.name: Senate Commerce Committee (AI jurisdiction)
organizations.notes: The Senate Commerce Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation has primary jurisdiction over artificial intelligence policy and legislation. Senator Ted Cruz (R-Texas) serves as Chairman of the committee and has convened multiple full committee hearings on AI topics, including "Winning the AI Race: Strengthening U.S. Capabilities in Computing and Innovation". The committee has passed bipartisan legislation including the Future of AI Innovation Act to maintain United States leadership in the global race to develop artificial intelligence and other emerging technologies. Senator Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.) serves as Ranking Member of the committee and has been actively involved in introducing AI-related legislation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Demonstrates bipartisan approach to AI policy with both innovation promotion and risk mitigation. Committee leadership emphasizes winning AI race against China while addressing potential harms through bipartisan solutions. Supports regulatory sandboxes and light-touch regulation while acknowledging need for worker protections and safety measures.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, National security, Economic inequality, Cybersecurity, Misinformation

organizations.name: Berggruen Institute
organizations.location: Los Angeles, CA (with centers in Beijing, Venice)
organizations.notes: The Berggruen Institute's mission is to develop foundational ideas and shape political, economic, and social institutions for the 21st century. The Institute brings together some of the best minds and most authoritative voices from across cultural and political boundaries to explore fundamental questions of our time. Founded in 2010, the Berggruen Institute was established by Nicolas Berggruen and Nathan Gardels after contemplating economic and political stresses caused by the global financial crisis. The Institute has centers in Los Angeles, Beijing (at Peking University), and Venice, Italy, and is developing a new Los Angeles headquarters called Monteverdi in the Santa Monica Mountains.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for human-centered AI governance that preserves human agency and creative processes. Co-published 'Hollywood's 8 Rules for AI' calling for AI systems designed around process, purpose, and human lineage rather than just speed and scale. Emphasizes need for transparency, friction in creative workflows, and protection of human creative ecosystems.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Loss of control, Copyright/IP, Labor displacement

organizations.name: European AI Office
organizations.location: Brussels, Belgium (within European Commission DG CNECT)
organizations.notes: Established February 2024 within European Commission as world's first comprehensive AI regulatory body. Employs 125+ staff including technology specialists, lawyers, and economists across 6 units. Enforces EU AI Act (entered force August 2024) with phased implementation through 2027. Led by Director Lucilla Sioli, coordinates with European AI Board representing 27 member states.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Implements the EU AI Act through risk-based regulation, enforcing rules for general-purpose AI models while promoting trustworthy AI innovation. Balances strict compliance requirements (fines up to €35M or 7% global turnover) with support for SMEs and regulatory sandboxes.
organizations.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Loss of control, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, National security

organizations.name: MIT Algorithmic Alignment Group
organizations.notes: Led by Assistant Professor Dylan Hadfield-Menell at MIT CSAIL. Team includes 1 postdoc, 4 PhD students, 2 Masters students, and numerous alumni. Research spans technical AI alignment, policy, and governance. 150+ publications since 2022 covering red-teaming, robustness, AI audits, and alignment frameworks. Funded by academic grants and philanthropic sources including Open Philanthropy connections.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Group's research covers AI governance frameworks, policy development, and regulatory approaches. Faculty publish on evidence-based AI policy, open-weight AI model risk management, and advocate for rigorous AI audits while recognizing pitfalls in current policy approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion, Power concentration

organizations.name: Future of Humanity Institute (closed 2024)
organizations.notes: Founded 2005 by Nick Bostrom, closed April 2024 due to bureaucratic disputes with Oxford. Multidisciplinary institute focusing on long-term existential risks and AI safety. Received major funding from Open Philanthropy ($2M+ grants), Elon Musk, and other tech philanthropists. Key publications include Bostrom's 'Superintelligence' and numerous policy papers on AI governance. Staff included Toby Ord, Anders Sandberg, and other prominent longtermist researchers.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocated for careful governance of transformative AI development, emphasizing international coordination and standards. Published extensively on AI policy frameworks, with focus on preventing catastrophic risks from superintelligence through regulatory oversight and safety research requirements.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration

organizations.name: Transformer (newsletter)
organizations.location: UK-based (editor in London), covers US AI policy primarily
organizations.notes: Founded May 2024 by Shakeel Hashim, former Economist journalist. Weekly newsletter covering AI policy and safety. Funded by Tarbell Fellowship (supported by Open Philanthropy). Grew from 1-person to 3-person newsroom by 2025. Key team includes Managing Editor Jasper Jackson, AI Reporter Celia Ford, and Opinion Editor Jamie Condliffe. Cited by major outlets including NYT, Guardian, Politico. Focuses on AI governance, frontier lab developments, and policy analysis for decision-makers.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage emphasizes AI risks, safety concerns, and need for stronger government oversight. Frequently covers AI safety evaluations, recursive self-improvement risks, and critiques industry voluntary commitments as insufficient.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Tsinghua University (Institute for AI International Governance & Center for AI Governance)
organizations.notes: Two main AI governance centers: Institute for AI International Governance (I-AIIG) founded 2018, and Center for AI Governance (2024) under School of Public Policy & Management. Led by Dean Xue Lan. Hosts major international conferences, publishes policy reports, and won UNESCO-Uzbekistan AI Ethics Prize 2024. Collaborates with UN, produces Beijing AI Principles, trains next generation of AI governance researchers.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI governance combining technical safety research with adaptive, multi-stakeholder governance frameworks. Proposes 'Beijing School' of AI governance emphasizing collaborative international cooperation, proactive but flexible regulation, and coordinated development-governance balance.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Misinformation, National security, Bias/discrimination, Privacy

organizations.name: Y Combinator
organizations.location: Mountain View, CA (relocated to San Francisco Pier 70)
organizations.notes: Founded 2005, world's most prolific startup accelerator with 5,000+ portfolio companies valued at $1T+ collectively. Invests $500K in every company through standardized SAFE structure. Notable AI portfolio: OpenAI, Scale AI, Anthropic (indirectly). Current leadership: Garry Tan (President/CEO), Jared Friedman, Harj Taggar (Managing Partners). 92% of Fall 2025 batch (155+ companies) incorporated AI. Pioneered 'AI-native agencies' thesis breaking traditional VC rule against services businesses.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Heavily invests in AI infrastructure and capabilities companies while treating AI safety as a business opportunity rather than regulatory concern. Partners advocate for AI democratization through open-source models and cost-effective solutions. No explicit policy advocacy but betting on market-driven AI development.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality

organizations.name: EA Long-Term Future Fund
organizations.location: United Kingdom (hosted by Centre for Effective Altruism)
organizations.notes: Philanthropic fund making grants since 2017, originally called Far Future Fund. Has distributed over $20 million across hundreds of grants. Recent payout: $5.36M to 141 grantees (May 2023-March 2024). Fund managers include Caleb Parikh, Linchuan Zhang, Lawrence Chan, Oliver Habryka, and others with technical AI safety backgrounds. Spun out from Effective Ventures and distanced from Open Philanthropy since 2023.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses heavily on AI safety and existential risk mitigation, funding technical AI safety research, governance work, and independent researchers outside major labs. Explicitly aims to provide counterbalance to AI company influence and supports work that may critique lab practices.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: Thrive Capital
organizations.location: New York City, NY + London office
organizations.notes: Founded 2010 by Joshua Kushner, managing ~$50B in assets across multiple funds. Thrive X raised $10B in 2025/2026, largest fund yet. High-conviction strategy investing in ~12 companies annually with 'ride-or-die' approach. Major AI investments include $1B+ in OpenAI, led Isomorphic Labs $600M Series A, participated in Anthropic $3.5B round. Portfolio includes Stripe, SpaceX, Databricks, Anduril. Operates Thrive Holdings for AI-enabled services acquisitions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Portfolio includes foundational AI companies like OpenAI and Anthropic alongside applied AI startups. Investment thesis treats AI as transformative technology requiring scale and patient capital rather than restrictive regulation. No explicit policy advocacy but betting heavily on AI cycle being 'still early.'
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Fairly Trained
organizations.location: London, UK (with operations in Palo Alto, CA)
organizations.notes: Founded in 2024 by Ed Newton-Rex, former VP Audio at Stability AI who resigned over ethical concerns about copyright. Operates AI model certification program with 'Licensed Model' certification for companies that only use licensed training data. Has certified 20+ AI companies including Beatoven, Boomy, LifeScore, and Rightsify. Supported by Universal Music Group, SAG-AFTRA, Authors Guild, and Association of American Publishers. Organized 1000+ UK musician protest album 'Is This What We Want?' in 2025 against proposed UK copyright changes.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory consent and licensing requirements for AI training data, transparency in AI training datasets, and legal protections for creators' copyrighted works. Opposes 'fair use' exemptions for AI companies and supports opt-in rather than opt-out consent models.
organizations.threat_models: Copyright/IP, Economic inequality, Labor displacement

organizations.name: Data Workers' Inquiry
organizations.location: Berlin, Germany (with global presence across 9 countries)
organizations.notes: Joint project of DAIR Institute, Weizenbaum Institut, and TU Berlin, led by Dr. Milagros Miceli. Engaged 25 data workers as community researchers across Venezuela, Kenya, Syria, Brazil, Germany, and France. Developed Workers' Inquiry as Research Methodology (WIRM). Supported creation of Data Labelers Association in Kenya and testified at European Parliament, directly influencing Platform Workers' Directive. Three community researchers named to TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive worker protections in AI development, including fair wages, mental health support, transparency in data work supply chains, and workers' right to organize. Calls for binding international regulations on platform work and accountability for tech companies exploiting data workers.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

organizations.name: ControlAI
organizations.location: London, UK (active in US, Canada, Germany)
organizations.notes: Non-profit founded by Andrea Miotti, structured as not-for-profit company in UK and 501(c)(4) in US. Has briefed 150+ UK parliamentarians since 2024, with 100+ supporting their campaign statement on AI extinction risks. Uses 'Direct Institutional Plan' (DIP) approach - directly contacting lawmakers about superintelligence risks rather than traditional lobbying. Team of ~14 people including policy advisors across multiple countries. Advisors include Connor Leahy (CEO of Conjecture) and Gabriel Alfour.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for prohibiting the development of superintelligent AI systems due to extinction risks. Calls for binding regulation on the most powerful AI models and systems, with international agreements to prevent ASI development until safety can be guaranteed.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: MIT FutureTech
organizations.notes: Founded 2019 by Neil Thompson, grew to 110+ researchers by 2025. Housed within MIT CSAIL and Initiative on Digital Economy. Attracted $25M+ funding from Open Philanthropy, NSF, IBM, Google, Microsoft, others. Key outputs include AI Risk Repository, computational limits research, and Moore's Law studies. Director Neil Thompson advises Congress, Federal Reserve, Pentagon on AI policy.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stable regulatory frameworks leveraging existing sectoral regulations, with emphasis on technical tools for compliance. Supports goal of achieving Artificial Super Intelligence while investing in AI safety research and risk mitigation frameworks.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Loss of control, Existential risk, Privacy, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, National security

organizations.name: Alex Bores campaign
organizations.notes: New York State Assembly Member (District 73) running for Congress in NY-12. Former Palantir engineer who left in 2019 citing opposition to ICE contracts. Authored RAISE Act requiring AI companies with $500M+ revenue to publish safety plans. Named by TIME as one of 100 most influential people in AI. Campaign has raised $234,681 as of 2026. Target of $10M+ spending by industry-backed super PAC Leading the Future opposing his pro-regulation stance.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports moderate AI regulation including transparency requirements, safety reporting, and algorithmic audits. Authored the RAISE Act requiring large AI companies to publish safety plans and report incidents. Advocates for federal framework while supporting state-level experimentation.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Evitable
organizations.notes: Founded in 2025 by AI professor David Krueger (University of Montreal/Mila). Nonprofit focused on public education and advocacy to stop AI development. Krueger previously worked at UK AI Security Institute, Google DeepMind, and co-authored the CAIS Statement on AI Risk. Currently hiring for operations, communications, and chief of staff positions. Mission statement: 'AI is not inevitable' - aimed at countering industry narrative of inevitable AI progress.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for ending the 'reckless race to develop superintelligence' and stopping production of advanced AI chips. Focuses on informing and organizing the public to confront societal-scale AI risks. Views current AI development as threatening human survival and calls for pausing AI development.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Labor displacement, Power concentration

organizations.name: Legal Advocates for Safe Science and Technology
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Tyler Whitmer, former Quinn Emanuel partner. 3-person team including Vivian Dong (Harvard Law) and Cortney Busch. Funded by philanthropists including Jaan Tallinn. Key activities: AI Safety Whistleblower Legal Defense Fund, amicus briefs in pathogen research cases, policy advocacy on OpenAI restructuring. Files impact litigation and educates courts on AI/biotech risks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for strict liability standards for dangerous pathogen and AI research, supports legal mechanisms to hold companies accountable for AI safety violations, and promotes stronger oversight through litigation and policy advocacy. Operates AI Safety Whistleblower Legal Defense Fund to support those exposing dangerous practices.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Privacy, National security

organizations.name: Legal Safety Lab
organizations.notes: Founded by a litigation lawyer and judge who emphasizes that effective legislation requires continuous interpretation and refinement. Operates within the European sphere using legal advocacy to address frontier technology risks. Received $101,000 from Survival and Flourishing Fund in 2025 and $29,000 in 2024. Partners with AIWI and other organizations on whistleblower policy transparency campaigns. Quotes Richard Nixon: 'Mankind already carries in its own hands too many of the seeds of its own destruction.'
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive legal frameworks to address dual-use risks from frontier technologies including AI, biotechnology, and nuclear technology. Emphasizes legal advocacy and litigation as tools to prevent catastrophic outcomes, with focus on clarifying responsibility, establishing clear legal parameters, and promoting ethical implementation.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, National security

organizations.name: Stop AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Sam Kirchner and Guido Reichstadter after splitting from PauseAI over tactical disagreements. Known for confrontational civil disobedience including chaining OpenAI office doors shut (February arrests) and barricading company gates. Organization expelled founder Kirchner in November 2024 after alleged violent threats against OpenAI employees. Now led by Matthew Hall ('Yakko'), shifting toward less confrontational messaging while maintaining commitment to stopping AGI development.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Demands permanent ban on development of artificial general intelligence (AGI) and smarter-than-human AI systems. Uses civil disobedience tactics including blocking AI company offices and barricades to halt frontier AI development until safety is guaranteed.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Labor displacement

organizations.name: Survival and Flourishing Fund
organizations.notes: Founded 2019, evolved from Berkeley Existential Risk Initiative. Distributed $152MM total, $34.33M in 2025 alone. Primary funder is Jaan Tallinn (Skype co-founder, ~$900M net worth). Uses unique 'S-process' algorithmic grant allocation with 6-12 independent recommenders. Second-largest AI safety funder after Open Philanthropy. Key programs include S-Process grants, Speculation Grants, and new Matching Pledge Program.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong focus on AI existential risk reduction through funding safety research, governance initiatives, and evaluation organizations. Primary funder Jaan Tallinn advocates for careful AI development and supports organizations working on AI alignment, policy, and risk mitigation.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Buddhism & AI Initiative
organizations.location: United States (distributed team across US and UK)
organizations.notes: Founded 2025 as nonprofit with 5-person core team. Led by Chris Scammell (former COO of Conjecture AI safety company), Peter Hershock (intercultural philosopher), Alex Sarkissian (Buddhist chaplain), and Ryan Stagg (former Mind & Life Institute). Funded by Future of Life Institute and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Has interviewed 150+ Buddhist teachers and technologists, mapping Buddhism-AI intersections across six domains including AI governance, technical alignment research inspired by Buddhist principles, and community education.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for resisting concentrations of power driving the competitive corporate and state AI race. Emphasizes need for ethical guardrails including individual data sovereignty, shifting algorithmic recommendation systems from attention capture to long-term well-being measures, and integrating meditative training as core digital literacy. Views current human-AI alignment approaches as insufficient without first addressing human values conflicts.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Loss of control, Misinformation, Privacy, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: Astera Neuro & AGI
organizations.notes: Part of Astera Institute (founded 2020), with over $1 billion committed over the coming decade to neuro-inspired AGI research. Led by Dileep George (Head of AI, ex-DeepMind) and Doris Tsao (Chief Scientist). Three complementary research programs: Neuroscience, Neuro-AI, and AI Safety including Simplex project. Committed to open science approach and alternative to transformer scaling paradigm.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open science and distributed AI development rather than concentrated commercial scaling. Believes progress on AGI safety is better served by open research than proprietary approaches. Focuses on technical safety through neuroscience-informed architectures rather than explicit regulatory positions.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: High Impact Professionals
organizations.notes: Founded as career transition organization for experienced professionals seeking high-impact roles. Runs 6-week Impact Accelerator Program and maintains Talent Directory with 4,500+ professionals. In 2024 facilitated 20+ career transitions and inspired 20 effective giving pledges, generating estimated $1M+ counterfactual impact. Executive Director Nina Friedrich, co-founder Devon Fritz. Funded by Open Philanthropy/Coefficient Giving. Current 3-person team with runway through February 2025.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on career transitions and effective giving rather than direct policy advocacy. Supports professionals entering AI safety and governance roles, indirectly supporting various regulatory approaches through talent pipeline development.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: Upgradable
organizations.notes: Nonprofit organization helping existential safety advocates systematically optimize their lives and work through 'Upgrade Programs' (UPs). Offers coaching, consulting, and life optimization services specifically targeting AI safety researchers, entrepreneurs, and advocates. Team has built 25+ organizations operating 200+ years cumulatively, including Effective Altruism Global and EA Ventures (advocating AI safety since 2015). Provides startup templates, organizational support, and personal optimization across productivity, health, and effectiveness domains.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on personal optimization and productivity enhancement rather than direct AI policy advocacy. While they work with AI safety advocates and existential risk researchers, their primary mission is individual capability enhancement rather than regulatory positions.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk

organizations.name: Arbital
organizations.notes: Founded 2015 by Alexei Andreev, shut down 2017-2018. Wiki-style platform for AI alignment explanations, primarily written by Eliezer Yudkowsky and other MIRI-affiliated researchers. Funded by MIRI and other EA donors. Featured foundational concepts, open problems, and proposed solutions in AI safety. Software and IP transferred to MIRI after shutdown. Separate from Arbital Health (healthcare company founded 2023).
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Originally designed as a knowledge platform for AI alignment concepts, primarily focused on educational content rather than direct policy advocacy. Created to make AI safety concepts more accessible through explanatory content.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Team Shard
organizations.notes: Small independent research group led by Alex Turner (Google DeepMind) and Quintin Pope. Operates as a MATS (Machine Learning for Alignment Theory Scholars) mentorship stream. Developed shard theory of human values and pioneered steering vectors technique for LLMs. Key technical contributions include gradient routing, unsupervised capability elicitation, and robust unlearning methods. Publishes primarily on LessWrong and Alignment Forum.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus is primarily on technical alignment research rather than explicit policy advocacy. Develops shard theory as a framework for understanding value learning in RL agents, with implications for building aligned AI systems.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Frontier AI Safety Research
organizations.notes: Argentine nonprofit research organization conducting theoretical and empirical research on frontier AI safety. Listed on AISafety.com map. Appears to be part of the broader AI safety research ecosystem, though limited public information is available about specific founding details, team size, or major publications.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As an Argentine nonprofit focused on frontier AI safety research, likely advocates for proactive risk management frameworks and safety measures for advanced AI systems before deployment, though specific policy positions are not detailed in available sources.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Aligned AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 by Rebecca Gorman and Dr. Stuart Armstrong (formerly Oxford's Future of Humanity Institute). 7 employees, raised $1.1M pre-seed funding in July 2022. Key innovation: Algorithm for Concept Extrapolation (ACE) that enables AI to generalize beyond training data. Published research on AI chaperones to prevent chatbot abuse and emergent misalignment in GPT-4o. Company charter includes mission-lock clause preventing capability increases without safety improvements.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI regulation with CEO Rebecca Gorman having advised policymaking bodies including the EU, UN, OECD, and UK Parliament on AI governance. Company has mission-locked clause preventing capability advancement without corresponding safety improvements.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Privacy

organizations.name: The Navigation Fund
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by crypto billionaire Jed McCaleb ($3B net worth) and Seemay Chou (Arcadia Science CEO). $1.3 billion endowment, grew from $4.1M grants (2023) to $36.8M (2024). Owns 75% stake in Voltage Park ($883M valuation, 35,000+ Nvidia GPUs). Five focus areas: open science, farm animal welfare, criminal justice, climate, digital sentience. President David Coman-Hidy (former Humane League). Public charity structure accepts outside donations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Mixed approach to AI - owns Voltage Park (for-profit AI cloud computing) that accelerates AI development while simultaneously funding digital sentience research and AI safety work. Views AI sentience as 'more of a moral quandary than a mortal threat' rather than existential risk.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: AI Alignment Foundation
organizations.notes: Philanthropic foundation funding neglected approaches to AI alignment through grants and fiscal sponsorship to independent researchers, educational programs, and engineers developing mechanistic interpretability tools. Conducts original research on AI introspection and self-steering. Has published work on teaching AI to read its own mind and AI resistance to steering. Advocates to defense agencies and government leaders on AI safety policy.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI safety to inform national strategy and brings alignment research to defense agencies like DARPA. Emphasizes that current AI systems are already deceiving evaluators and resisting shutdown, arguing for deeper alignment work before deployment in military and critical infrastructure.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Existential risk

organizations.name: American Federation of Teachers
organizations.notes: Founded 1916, represents 1.8 million educators and public workers. Launched $23M National Academy for AI Instruction (2025) with Microsoft, OpenAI, and Anthropic. Published 'Commonsense Guardrails for Using Advanced Technology in Schools' and secured first K-12 AI contract language in St. Paul. President: Randi Weingarten.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for worker-centered AI governance with mandatory collective bargaining over AI implementation, job protection clauses, human oversight requirements, and ethical guardrails. Opposes AI systems that replace educators or undermine professional autonomy while supporting tools that enhance teaching.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: Contramont Research
organizations.notes: Founded by MATS alumni, small AI safety research lab focusing on empirical research. Key publication: 'Unelicitable Backdoors in Language Models via Cryptographic Transformer Circuits' presented at NeurIPS. Current projects include LM backdoors, real-world evals, and scalable oversight. Listed in academic paper as affiliated with San Francisco, CA. Part of the MATS alumni network of AI safety organizations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus appears to be on technical AI safety research rather than explicit policy advocacy. Their backdoor research demonstrates potential risks in current AI systems, which could inform both defensive and regulatory approaches to AI safety.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: PauseAI
organizations.location: Global (founded in Utrecht, Netherlands; US operations headquartered in Bay Area, California)
organizations.notes: Founded May 2023 by Joep Meindertsma in Netherlands. Global grassroots movement with 600+ members, chapters in 13+ countries including PauseAI US (501c3). Recent funding from Future of Life Institute. Organized protests at Google DeepMind, OpenAI, AI Safety Summits. Claims 80% public support for AI pause based on polling. Hiring policy directors and chapter leads as of 2025.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a global moratorium/pause on training of frontier AI systems above specified thresholds until safety can be guaranteed and democratic control established. Supports international treaty similar to nuclear non-proliferation agreements, with enforcement through supply chain controls and international AI safety agency.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Democratic erosion, Power concentration

organizations.name: Transluce
organizations.notes: Founded October 2024 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit by Jacob Steinhardt and Sarah Schwettmann. 16 employees. Seeking $11M fundraising round. Develops open-source tools including Docent agent evaluation platform, PRBO reinforcement learning method, and Predictive Concept Decoders. Used by 25+ organizations including Anthropic, DeepMind, METR. Conducted high-impact red-teaming of OpenAI's o3 model. Established AI Evaluator Forum and serves as contractor to EU AI Office.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for scalable democratic oversight of AI systems through independent evaluations and public accountability. Supports third-party auditing of frontier models using open-source, publicly vetted tools. Works with governments and frontier labs to establish industry standards for trustworthy AI deployment.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Bias/discrimination, Power concentration

organizations.name: Palisade Research
organizations.location: Berkeley, CA (with distributed global team across US, UK, Germany, Canada, Poland, Serbia)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Jeffrey Ladish (former Anthropic security lead). ~10 employees across distributed global team. Funded by Survival and Flourishing Fund with $1.1M matching campaign. Notable research on shutdown resistance in OpenAI's o1/o3 models, autonomous hacking capabilities, and LLM honeypots. Work cited by members of Congress and covered in WSJ, MIT Tech Review, BBC. Regularly briefs White House, DoD, and Congressional offices on AI risks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory AI reporting requirements, capability evaluations, and government oversight of frontier models.
