Submitted detailed recommendations to Department of Commerce for stronger dual-use foundation model reporting, including protected whistleblower mechanisms and regular government interviews with AI company employees. Briefs policymakers on loss-of-control risks and need for regulatory preparedness.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Existential risk, National security

organizations.name: EleutherAI
organizations.location: New York, NY (distributed globally with team members in US, UK, India, Australia, Netherlands, Germany)
organizations.notes: Founded 2020 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit. 27 employees globally. Known for open-source models including GPT-NeoX and Pythia series. Evolved from general open-source AI research to focused alignment work. Leadership transitioned to full-time alignment research at Conjecture and OpenAI. Operates primarily through Discord server for research coordination. Partners with national labs and government agencies on AI applications.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open-source AI development and opposes restrictive regulations on model weights. Co-signed letters against California's SB 1047, arguing for proportional risk management based on technical feasibility rather than blanket restrictions. Supports researcher access and 'safe harbor' protections for AI evaluation and red teaming.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Power concentration

organizations.name: Center for Long-Term Cybersecurity
organizations.notes: Founded 2015 at UC Berkeley School of Information. Houses the AI Security Initiative (AISI) which develops risk management standards and evaluation frameworks. Key outputs include AI Risk Management Standards Profile for General-Purpose AI, Agentic AI Risk Management Profile, and research on intolerable risk thresholds. Funded by Hewlett Foundation, Open Philanthropy, Future of Life Institute, and tech companies. Runs AI Policy Hub training program for graduate students. Partners with NIST on AI risk management frameworks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for structured AI risk management frameworks and standards, particularly for frontier AI models. Develops technical guidance for risk thresholds, evaluation protocols, and governance mechanisms. Supports regulatory requirements for AI systems in high-risk applications while enabling innovation.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation, National security

organizations.name: Harmony Intelligence
organizations.location: San Francisco, US (with Sydney, Australia office)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Alex Browne and Soroush J. Pour. Raised $3M seed funding in March 2025 led by Airtree Ventures with 30+ investors including Eric Ries. ~5 employees. Published peer-reviewed AI safety research including 'Scalable and Transferable Black-Box Jailbreaks for Language Models via Persona Modulation'. Conducts model evaluations and red-teaming for top AI labs and governments including Australian government AI safety evaluations. Building defensive cybersecurity products using AI agents.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stronger mandatory controls on AI technology to prevent misuse risks and catastrophic harms. Supports creation of AI Safety Institutes and regulatory frameworks similar to those for cars or aircraft, with ongoing review, audit and inspection of deployed AI systems.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, National security, Existential risk

organizations.name: TIME Magazine
organizations.notes: Founded 1923. Launched TIME AI Agent in November 2025 - an AI-powered platform built with Scale AI for interactive journalism. Key AI reporters include Andrew R. Chow, Billy Perrigo, Harry Booth, and Tharin Pillay. Named 'Architects of AI' as 2025 Person of the Year. Has licensing agreement with OpenAI for archive access. Major cover stories include 'The People vs. AI' (Feb 2026) and extensive Anthropic coverage.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage presents balanced perspectives on AI regulation, featuring both industry boosters and critics. Recent major cover story 'The People vs. AI' highlighted grassroots resistance to AI development while also covering industry perspectives. Coverage includes both AI risks and benefits without taking explicit regulatory positions.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Misinformation, Environmental, Loss of control, Privacy

organizations.name: Transformative Futures Institute
organizations.location: United States (works with policymakers in DC)
organizations.notes: 501(c)(3) nonprofit research organization. Executive Director Ross Gruetzemacher is Assistant Professor at Wichita State University and Research Affiliate at Cambridge's Centre for the Study of Existential Risk. Specializes in underutilized foresight techniques including wargaming (Intelligence Rising simulation game), horizon scanning, and agent-based modeling. Disseminates research directly to DC policymakers and through academic publishing. Co-signed congressional letter supporting NIST AI Safety Institute funding in 2024.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive risk assessment and crisis management planning for advanced AI systems. Focuses on developing governance frameworks that address catastrophic and existential risks from AI through foresight techniques and policy recommendations to government stakeholders.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality

organizations.name: AI Risk Mitigation Fund
organizations.notes: Launched December 2023 as spinoff from Long-Term Future Fund. Led by Thomas Larsen with team of AI safety researchers and experienced grantmakers. Previously made $20+ million in grants under LTFF. Focus areas include technical AI alignment research, AI policy, and building research capacity. Past grants include $530K from SFF in 2024. Grantmaking process uses -5 to +5 scoring system with funding threshold around 2.5-3.0.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for independent AI safety funding separate from industry labs due to concerns about incentive misalignment. Supports research on catastrophic risks and policies to mitigate existential threats from advanced AI. Emphasizes need for external oversight of AI development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Astralis Foundation
organizations.location: Stockholm, London, New York, Vienna (multi-city presence)
organizations.notes: European-based multi-donor foundation with $25M annual giving capacity. Raised >$20M from 15 donors, deployed to 14 organizations. Key initiative is $5.8M Shared Horizons Fund for international AI governance from 5 donors to 11 organizations. Leadership includes CEO Jona Glade and President Vilhelm Skoglund. Maintains strict donation acceptance policy excluding AI company leaders and government funding to preserve independence.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Explicitly focused on preventing catastrophic AI risks through global governance structures and international cooperation. Supports building bridges between West and Asia for AI safety governance, European AI safety leadership, and developing clear mandates and safeguards for trustworthy AI innovation.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: EA Infrastructure Fund
organizations.notes: Founded ~2017, part of EA Funds managed by Effective Ventures. Has distributed $18.9M+ since 2020 across 499 grants. Focuses on 'principles-first EA' supporting cause prioritization research, epistemic infrastructure, and EA community building. Fund managers include Harri Besceli, Jamie Harris, with advisors like Jonas Vollmer and Catherine Low. Recent grants include $168K to Rethink Priorities for cross-cause cost-effectiveness modeling.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a meta-level funder focused on 'principles-first EA', supports both AI safety research and broader cause prioritization work. Does not take explicit regulatory positions but funds organizations across the spectrum from AI capabilities to safety research.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Economic inequality, Power concentration

organizations.name: Giving What We Can
organizations.notes: Founded 2009 by Toby Ord and William MacAskill. Community of 10,000+ pledgers who commit to donate 10% of income to effective charities. Annual revenue $3.2M, 36 employees. Has facilitated over $500M in total donations across causes including AI safety through recommended funds like EA Funds Long-Term Future Fund and Longview's Emerging Challenges Fund. BHAG goal: 1M pledgers donating $3B annually by 2040.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Functions as philanthropic platform directing donations to various causes including AI safety organizations. Recommends AI safety funds like Long-Term Future Fund and Emerging Challenges Fund but takes no direct regulatory positions. Focused on effective giving rather than policy advocacy.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: Long-Term Future Fund
organizations.notes: Founded 2017 as part of EA Funds program. Has distributed over $50M across 1,500+ grants since inception. Recent funding: $5.36M distributed May 2023-March 2024 across 141 grants with 19.3% acceptance rate. Fund managers include Caleb Parikh (chair), Linchuan Zhang, Oliver Habryka, Lawrence Chan. Makes primarily small grants (<$100k) to individuals and early-stage projects in AI safety, biosecurity, and longtermist research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses heavily on AI existential risk mitigation and safety research. Funds technical AI safety work, governance research, and field-building activities aimed at reducing catastrophic risks from advanced AI. Supports both capability oversight and alignment research.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: Halcyon Futures
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Mike McCormick (former GPV partner). Dual structure: Halcyon Futures (nonprofit making grants and career transition support) and Halcyon Ventures (VC arm). Recent $4.2M pre-seed raise (Jan 2026). Portfolio includes Goodfire, AIUC, Asymmetric Security, Confident Security. Has helped portfolio companies raise $300M+ in follow-on funding. Publishes quarterly AIUC-1 AI safety standard.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on building 'secure and resilient future' for transformative AI through targeted investments in AI safety, security, and governance companies. Supports both defensive capabilities and responsible AI development while acknowledging rapid AI advancement. Featured in NYT discussing AI risk and robust testing standards.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, National security, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Collective Action for Existential Safety (CAES)
organizations.notes: Initiative of the Center for Existential Safety, a new nonprofit being formed in the US as of February 2025. Led by James Norris who has '26+ years' of preparation and holds 'short timelines and high p(doom)' views. Offers action lists for individuals/orgs/nations, hosts coordination calls for organization leaders and frontier AI developers. Currently fundraising and recruiting co-founders, C-suite executives, and volunteers. Maintains Discord community and runs existential safety pledge campaigns.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for collective action to mitigate existential risks from AI, nuclear weapons, and engineered pandemics. Promotes comprehensive action lists for individuals, organizations, and nations to improve existential safety, with particular focus on addressing 'reckless frontier AI development.'
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Science of Trustworthy AI (Schmidt Sciences)
organizations.notes: Major philanthropic program within Schmidt Sciences (founded 2024) by Eric and Wendy Schmidt. Offers grants up to $5M+ for 1-3 year projects across two tiers. Features advisory board including Percy Liang, Ajeya Cotra, and others. Provides compute resources, API credits, and engineering support. 2026 RFP deadline May 17, with emphasis on academic and nonprofit researchers addressing foundational AI safety science.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports technical research to understand, predict, and control risks from frontier AI systems while enabling trustworthy deployment. Focuses on foundational safety science rather than direct policy advocacy, viewing rigorous evaluation and oversight methods as essential for responsible AI development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, National security, Bias/discrimination

organizations.name: Explainable
organizations.notes: Media organization focused on translating AI developments into accessible stories for public understanding. Mission is 'empowering organizations, researchers, and creators to translate the future of AI into stories the world understands.' Very limited public information available about team size, funding, or specific outputs. Appears to be primarily a storytelling/communication platform rather than a technical research organization.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a media organization focused on translating AI developments into accessible stories, likely supports transparency and explainability in AI systems but does not appear to advocate for specific regulatory approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown

organizations.name: AI Governance & Safety Canada
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, nonpartisan nonprofit with 15 employees (+50% YoY growth). Executive Director Wyatt Tessari L'Allié has testified before Canadian parliamentary committees (ETHI, SRSR) on AI regulation. Key publication: 'Preparing for the AI Crisis: A Plan for Canada' (October 2025) white paper. Board member Evan R. Murphy also serves at UC Berkeley's AI Security Initiative. Produces policy recommendations, legislative submissions, and government briefings.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive AI governance to address existential risks from smarter-than-human AI. Recommends Canada pivot to treat AI development as a national security threat, spearhead global talks on AI prohibition, and implement protective measures including AI labeling requirements and banning unacceptable capabilities.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Misinformation, Labor displacement

organizations.name: Center for Law & AI Risk
organizations.location: University of Alabama (Arbel) + University of Houston (Salib)
organizations.notes: Academic research center co-directed by Yonathan Arbel (University of Alabama) and Peter Salib (University of Houston). Small organization with ~2 people. Publishes legal scholarship on AI safety law, including pioneering work on AI agent identification and liability ('How to Count AIs' paper). Hosts academic retreats and workshops. Focuses on building field of legal scholars working on catastrophic AI risk reduction through law.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for legal frameworks to address catastrophic AI risks through novel mechanisms like 'Algorithmic Corporations' (A-corps) for AI agent governance, strict liability for developers on critical infrastructure attacks and CBRN weapons, and comprehensive identification systems for AI agents to enable effective legal accountability.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, National security

organizations.name: Centre for Future Generations
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by tech entrepreneur Steven Schuurman and social entrepreneur Laurens de Groot. Staff of 27 people with 41.9% YoY growth. Entirely funded by philanthropy including Dreamery Foundation, Tanka Foundation, and Powoki Foundation. Key publications include 'Advanced AI: Possible Futures' scenario analysis, EU AI Act policy recommendations, and 'Strategic Innovation for European Security' project. Provides policy submissions to European Commission on AI governance and digital strategy.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based AI regulation with mandatory external evaluations and pre-development guardrails for frontier AI models. Supports the EU AI Act framework but argues for stronger enforcement, clearer standards, and additional safeguards for next-generation systems. Promotes hardware-enabled governance mechanisms for international AI oversight.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, National security, Cybersecurity, Labor displacement, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: Good Ancestors
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as Australian charity dedicated to long-term future of humanity. Small team led by CEO Greg Sadler (15 years public service experience). Funded by individual donors, US/European foundations, and government research contracts. Key outputs include AI Legislation Stress Test (2025) engaging 64 experts, Australia AI Policy White Paper 2025-2028, and parliamentary submissions. Coordinates Australians for AI Safety coalition. Testified before Senate Select Committee on AI.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI safety regulation including establishment of Australian AI Safety Institute, mandatory guardrails for high-risk AI systems through dedicated AI Act, and upstream regulation of general-purpose AI models. Calls for precautionary approach to prevent catastrophic AI risks before deployment.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Misinformation

organizations.name: Future Matters
organizations.notes: Founded 2020. German nonprofit (HRB 251407 B) with U.S. 501(c)(3) equivalency. Think tank and capacity-building hub working on climate protection, AI governance, and biosecurity. Coordinated messaging for 20 AI safety organizations at UK AI Safety Summit 2023. Provides strategy consulting, policy prioritization research, and training services. Client rating of 8.4/10 for AI governance consulting services.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Conducts policy prioritization research on AI governance to reduce risks and ensure ethical development of powerful systems. Focuses on strategic clarity for effective engagement rather than specific regulatory positions. Provides capacity-building and strategic guidance to organizations working on AI safety policy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Power concentration

organizations.name: The AI Policy Network
organizations.notes: Founded as 501(c)(4) advocacy organization led by Executive Director Daniel Colson (also founder of AI Policy Institute). Team includes former Pentagon AI Policy Director Mark Beall Jr., former congressional staff from Pelosi and Schumer offices. Submitted detailed policy recommendations to Trump administration's AI RFI in March 2025. Conducts polling research showing voter support for AI regulation in swing districts. Sister organization AI Policy Institute conducts survey research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for targeted federal AI policies focused on advanced AI systems approaching AGI, including mandatory safety measures, cybersecurity requirements, and establishing a National AGI Commission. Supports light-touch regulation for most AI applications while implementing specific safeguards for high-capability systems that could pose national security risks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration

organizations.name: Global AI Moratorium (GAIM)
organizations.notes: Advocacy organization calling for immediate global pause on frontier AI training above GPT-4 capabilities. Part of broader AI safety ecosystem alongside PauseAI and other moratorium advocates. References support from AI experts including Geoffrey Hinton, Demis Hassabis, and others. Promotes public awareness campaigns and policy advocacy for international AI restrictions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for a global moratorium on large AI training runs above current state-of-the-art (GPT-4 level) until AI alignment is solved. Calls for strict international restrictions on frontier AI development, compute governance, and mandatory safety verification before any dangerous AI experiments.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Humans in Control
organizations.notes: New advocacy organization listed on AISafety.com map as demanding transparency, accountability, and real safeguards against AI risks. Appears to be part of broader movement emphasizing human control over AI systems, similar to concepts being developed at institutions like TU Delft's Centre for Meaningful Human Control. Limited public information available about specific founding, team, or activities.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for transparency, accountability, and real safeguards to protect humanity from risks of unchecked AI. Demands that humans remain in control of AI systems rather than allowing autonomous operation without human oversight.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: Atlas Computing
organizations.notes: Founded Q4 2023 by Evan Miyazono (former Protocol Labs). Nonprofit with 3 employees, raised $935k targeting $2.5-3.5M. Operationalizes Safeguarded AI architecture, co-organizes workshops with Guaranteed Safe AI paper authors including Yoshua Bengio. Currently merging with Convergent Research while CEO remains as Metagov Research Director.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specification-driven AI governance where AI systems must prove compliance with formal specifications rather than relying on values-based alignment. Promotes democratic, verifiable AI governance through formal methods and structured oversight mechanisms.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: Catalyze Impact
organizations.notes: Catalyze Impact is a non-profit organization that incubates AI safety, security, and resilience organizations. The organization has incubated approximately 15 AI safety organizations to date, with the majority raising 6 or 7 figures in funding. Catalyze operates seed funding networks including a non-profit Seed Funding Circle for donors with $15k+ annual capacity and an Investor Network for VCs and angel investors with $20k+ annual capacity.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Does not take explicit regulatory positions but incubates organizations across the spectrum from technical alignment research to AI governance policy advocacy. Focus is on organizational building rather than policy advocacy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, National security

organizations.name: The Alliance for Secure AI Action
organizations.notes: Founded May 2025 as 501(c)(3) nonprofit. 8-person team led by CEO Brendan Steinhauser (former political strategist). Communications-focused organization conducting public education campaigns, hosting DC events, and media outreach to shape AI safety narrative among policymakers. Launched JobLoss.AI campaign to track AI-related unemployment and applauded Sen. Slotkin's AI Guardrails Act.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safeguards on advanced AI development, supports bipartisan federal legislation requiring safety evaluations before deployment, and pushes for transparency regulations like whistleblower protections. Opposes Big Tech's push for federal preemption of state AI laws and calls for meaningful oversight before AGI development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Labor displacement, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration

organizations.name: Lightcone Infrastructure
organizations.notes: Founded ~2017 as 501c3 nonprofit. Small team of ~7 core members led by Oliver Habryka. Annual budget ~$2.7M funded by individual donors and foundations. Operates LessWrong.com (key AI safety forum), AI Alignment Forum, and Lighthaven (35,000 sq ft event campus in Berkeley). Significant influence on AI safety discourse - described as upstream of most public AI x-risk thinking. Hosts conferences, retreats, and research programs including MLAB bootcamp.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Operates under assumption that AI poses existential risk requiring careful coordination and safety research. Builds infrastructure for AI alignment researchers and advocates for slowing AI development to allow safety work to catch up.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: Mox
organizations.notes: Opened February 2025 as SF's largest AI safety community space. 40,000 sq ft facility with 183 active members, 50-80 daily coworkers. Revenue ~$100k/month from memberships and offices, expenses ~$130k/month. Hosted 377 events in first year including conferences, fellowships, and policy discussions. Supported 5 major fellowships including PIBBSS, Seldon Lab accelerator batches, and self-developed Frame Fellowship. Currently fundraising $450k mainline ask, up to $1.2M stretch goal. Partners include MIRI, Redwood Research, GovAI, EPOCH. Grant funding from Craig Falls, EAIF, and Manifund totaling $800k.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a community space and incubator, Mox doesn't take explicit regulatory positions but hosts diverse viewpoints. Has hosted events with policymakers like CA Senator Scott Wiener on AI safety legislation, suggesting engagement with regulatory discussions rather than advocacy for specific positions.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: The Midas Project
organizations.notes: Founded early 2024 by Tyler Johnston as 501(c)(3) nonprofit. Small organization with mostly volunteer staff. Received $31,000 from Survival and Flourishing Fund and $35,000 Open Philanthropy grant. Key projects include The OpenAI Files investigation, Seoul Commitment Tracker monitoring AI safety promises, and Model Republic publication. Has been particularly critical of OpenAI, leading to subpoena from OpenAI alleging secret funding by Elon Musk (which Midas denies).
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory safety evaluations, transparency requirements, and accountability measures for AI companies. Supports regulatory oversight of frontier AI development and opposes industry self-regulation. Calls for democratic participation in AI governance decisions rather than leaving critical choices to corporate boardrooms.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Rethink Wellbeing
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as nonprofit mental health organization serving effective altruists and AI safety researchers. 12-person team led by Dr. Inga Grossmann. Funded by Open Philanthropy Mental Health Funding Circle and individual donors. Delivered programs to ~150 participants in 2024 including employees at AI Safety Institute, 80,000 Hours, CEA. Developing AI+ CBT Lab for Philippines deployment with $100k funding goal. Programs show 2 WELLBYs gained per participant and 6+ additional productive hours weekly.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus is on mental health support rather than AI policy. Developing AI-enhanced CBT programs while maintaining human oversight and clinical supervision. Emphasizes responsible AI integration with safety measures like risk flagging and human expert involvement for complex cases.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown

organizations.name: Singapore AI Safety Hub
organizations.notes: Founded February 2025 as a co-working, events and community space. Houses ~20 members including researchers from FAR.AI, Truthful AI, Apart Research, The Future Society, and Impact Academy. Partners with Singapore AISI on bootcamps and runs the Technical Alignment Research Accelerator (TARA) program. Published inaugural Frontier AI Trends Report evaluating 30+ frontier models. Organizes regular meetups, hackathons, and international cooperation events.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports Singapore's voluntary AI governance frameworks and collaborative approach to AI safety. Partners with government initiatives like AI Security Bootcamps and promotes international cooperation on AI safety research and evaluation standards.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity, Existential risk

organizations.name: Global Catastrophic Risk Institute
organizations.notes: Founded 2011 by Seth Baum and Tony Barrett. Currently has ~2 staff members (down from peak of 5 in 2021-2022). Cross-risk think tank working on multiple global catastrophic risks including AI, nuclear war, climate change, pandemics. Operates on 'shoestring budget', funded by individual donors like Gordon Irlam ($200k for AI work). Runs annual Advising and Collaboration Program and Fellowship Program for community building.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful governance of AI development including corporate governance measures, collective action between AI groups, and potential global coordination mechanisms like an 'Off Switch' for dangerous AI development. Emphasizes win-win solutions that address catastrophic risks while making progress on other AI issues.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Weapons proliferation, Environmental, Power concentration

organizations.name: Global Partnership on AI
organizations.location: Paris, France (OECD-hosted secretariat) + Montreal, Canada (Center of Expertise)
organizations.notes: Founded June 2020 by 15 countries (now 29 members including US, EU, Japan, India). Intergovernmental initiative hosted by OECD. Four working groups: Responsible AI, Data Governance, Future of Work, Innovation & Commercialization. Produces policy guidance, safety frameworks, and coordinates international AI governance. Key projects include AI safety risk mapping, Hiroshima AI Process reporting framework, and transformative AI policy recommendations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Promotes human-centric, trustworthy AI development through international cooperation. Advocates for responsible AI governance frameworks based on OECD AI Principles, emphasizing transparency, accountability, and human rights protections while supporting innovation.
organizations.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Privacy, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, Loss of control, Misinformation

organizations.name: Centre for the Study of Existential Risk (CSER)
organizations.notes: Founded 2012 by Lord Martin Rees, Jaan Tallinn, and Huw Price. Interdisciplinary research centre at University of Cambridge with ~38 staff. Part of Institute for Technology and Humanity. Funded by Jaan Tallinn, Matt Wage, Victoria Elenowitz, and other philanthropic sources. Key research areas include AI risks, biotechnology risks, environmental risks, and global governance. Notable publications on international AI governance and frontier model regulation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for proactive international governance of AI through jurisdictional certification approaches. Supports mandatory reporting requirements for foundation models and establishment of international oversight bodies. Emphasizes need for robust domestic regulation as foundation for global AI leadership.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation, Global systemic risk

organizations.name: AI Safety Quest
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as grassroots volunteer organization after AI Safety Support shut down. Provides 1:1 AI safety career advising calls, mentorship programs, and Quest Parties for skill development. Led by team of volunteers including Chris (IT/cybersecurity), offers navigation calls to help newcomers enter AI safety field. Organization operates entirely through volunteers and focuses on onboarding new talent to AI safety ecosystem.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on reducing catastrophic risk from AI through community building and career guidance. Operates under assumption that AI poses existential risks requiring urgent action and coordination.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: Rethink Priorities
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (team distributed across US, UK, and other countries)
organizations.notes: Founded 2018 by Marcus Davis and Peter Wildeford. Cross-cause research organization with ~46 employees covering AI safety, animal welfare, global health, and longtermist priorities. 2026 budget of $7.5M core/$9.3M expanded. Major funders include Open Philanthropy, Good Ventures Foundation, Silicon Valley Community Foundation. Special Projects program fiscally sponsors AI safety organizations like Apollo Research, Epoch, and Institute for AI Policy & Strategy. Launching dedicated AI Strategy team in 2026.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports AI safety bounties, international AI safety agreements, and systematic evaluation of AI risks. Conducts research on AI governance strategies and public opinion on AI regulation, finding majority support for AI regulation among Americans. Focuses on evidence-based policy recommendations rather than advocating specific regulatory approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation, National security, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Manifold Markets
organizations.notes: Founded 2021 by James Grugett and others. Raised $3M total funding including from Alameda Research. Play-money prediction market platform with 100,000+ markets covering politics, tech, AI timelines, and regulation. Uses 'mana' currency that can be donated to charity. Academic studies show play-money markets achieve accuracy within 3-5 percentage points of real-money platforms. Popular among EA/rationalist communities for forecasting practice.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Platform hosts prediction markets on AI regulation outcomes but doesn't advocate specific policy positions. Markets show community sentiment on regulatory timing and scope, with users betting on passage of various AI safety bills.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown

organizations.name: Odyssean Institute
organizations.location: United Kingdom (with global team across 4 continents)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 by Giuseppe Dal Pra. Team of 12 researchers across 4 continents. Develops the 'Odyssean Process' combining expert horizon scanning, decision-making under deep uncertainty (DMDU), and citizen assemblies. Key projects include Global Catastrophic Risk horizon scan (2024), GRAIN resilience network, and planned AI governance citizen assembly for 2026. Collaborates with ALLFED on resilience research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for democratic deliberation and citizen assemblies to shape AI governance rather than corporate-driven processes. Criticizes current 'democratic AI' efforts as insufficient and calls for enforceable institutional structures with genuine public authority over AI development and deployment decisions.
organizations.threat_models: Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Loss of control, Existential risk, Economic inequality

organizations.name: AI Safety Initiative at Georgia Tech
organizations.notes: Founded in 2022 as student-led initiative, now employs 19 people with faculty advisors from GT's computing and engineering schools. Runs educational fellowships, technical research programs, and policy analysis. Members collaborate with major AI safety orgs including Anthropic, Center for AI Safety, METR, and RAND. Published multiple papers in top venues (ACL, ICLR) and submitted formal policy recommendations to federal agencies. Supported by Open Philanthropy and Coefficient Giving.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for specific AI red lines and regulatory frameworks including frontier model evaluation requirements, NIST oversight of commercial AI, classified partnerships between AI labs and government agencies, and international agreements on catastrophic risk prevention. Submitted detailed policy recommendations to federal agencies including NSF and NITRD calling for mandatory safety audits and compute governance.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Misinformation

organizations.name: Probably Good
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA (team distributed across US, UK, Belgium, Israel, Norway)
organizations.notes: Founded as nonprofit career guidance organization, 7 employees with 57% YoY growth. Executive Director: Itamar Shatz (PhD Cambridge). Provides 1:1 career advising, job board with 1,300+ high-impact roles, fellowships, workshops. Strong focus on AI safety/governance careers, EA community connections. Revenue model through philanthropic donations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on AI safety and governance as high-impact career paths, advocating for technical safety research, governance frameworks, and policy development. Promotes careers in AI alignment, interpretability, and policy work to mitigate AI risks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration

organizations.name: Apart Research
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as remote-first AI safety organization. 25 employees globally. Runs 55+ research sprints with 6,000+ participants across 200+ locations. Programs include weekend hackathons, Studio (4-month), and Fellowship (6-month) leading to peer-reviewed publications. Notable outputs: ICLR 2025 Oral paper on AI safety evaluations, NeurIPS publications. Alumni placed at METR, Anthropic, and other AI safety organizations. Led by CEO Jaime Raldua and Research Director Jason Hoelscher-Obermaier.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI safety research, control mechanisms, and governance infrastructure. Organizes hackathons on AI control, policy frameworks, and technical governance solutions. Collaborates with organizations like ControlAI on policy development and red-teaming exercises.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Democratic erosion, Power concentration, Misinformation

organizations.name: Cambridge Boston Alignment Initiative (CBAI)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, nonprofit with 11 employees. Runs intensive 9-10 week summer and spring research fellowships with $8-10k stipends. Fiscally sponsors Harvard AISST and MIT MAIA student groups. Funded by Open Philanthropy ($475k+ grants). Director: Emre Yavuz. Mentors from Harvard, MIT, Northeastern, Anthropic, Redwood Research. Fellows have joined Goodfire, Constellation, published at NeurIPS/ICLR.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: CBAI supports research on restricting dangerous AI research through international treaties with classification systems, federal funding pauses for risky research, and coordinated trade sanctions for non-compliance. Their fellowship research explores policy frameworks for managing catastrophic AI risks.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security

organizations.name: Successif
organizations.notes: Founded by Claire Boine (formerly Pathfinder, rebranded April 2023). Career transition organization helping mid-career professionals move into AI safety and governance roles. Funded by Open Philanthropy ($778K+ in grants). Offers AI Policy Strategy Fellowship, career mentoring, and professional training. Previously supported 40+ EA and AI safety organizations. Team includes technical advisors and policy experts.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Organization focuses on mitigating catastrophic and existential risks from AI through career transitions and policy advocacy. Founder Claire Boine advocates for AI safety policy and has participated in expert panels on AI risks. Offers AI Policy Strategy Fellowship to translate AI safety expertise into policy change.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: Future Impact Group: Fellowship
organizations.location: Oxford, United Kingdom (remote-first, operates in 12 countries)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Suryansh Mehta and Callum Evans. 45 employees across research and operations. 12-week remote fellowship program matching postgraduate students with senior researchers. Three focus areas: AI Policy, Philosophy for Safe AI, and AI Sentience. Funded by Open Philanthropy and Longview Philanthropy. Project leads from Oxford, GovAI, DeepMind, and other leading institutions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: FIG produces research to inform government responses to AI developments, working on policy frameworks, governance institutions, and risk management approaches. Project leads from organizations like Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative work on regulatory interventions and safety standards.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Loss of control, Economic inequality, Existential risk

organizations.name: Global Challenges Project
organizations.location: Boston, MA, USA (with operations in Philippines)
organizations.notes: Founded 2021, runs intensive 3-day residential workshops for university students exploring AI safety and biosecurity. 4-person team led by Director Liam Epstein (MA from Leverhulme Centre for Future of Intelligence). Funded by Open Philanthropy. Operates workshops globally with 20-35 attendees each. Part of Rethink Priorities Special Projects program providing operational support.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on educating students about existential risks from AI and biotechnology, emphasizing the need for safety measures and governance frameworks to prevent catastrophic outcomes from advanced technologies.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Arcadia Impact
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as UK charity. Runs multiple AI governance and safety programs: AI Governance Taskforce (12-week policy research program), LASR Labs (technical AI safety research), AI Safety Engineering Taskforce, and Orion AI Governance Initiative. Primary funder is Coefficient Giving (£8M+ committed). Led by Joe Hardie and Erin Robertson. Partners with organizations like GovAI, UK AISI, SaferAI, and Future Society on research projects.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Runs AI Governance Taskforce producing policy research on frontier AI risk management, loss of control scenarios, and AI safety frameworks. Research outputs advocate for enhanced oversight mechanisms, safety evaluations, and governance infrastructure for advanced AI systems.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration

organizations.name: AI Impacts
organizations.notes: Founded by Katja Grace, operates as research organization under MIRI umbrella. Conducted largest AI expert survey to date (2,778 researchers in 2023). Received grants from Future of Life Institute ($49,310) and Future of Humanity Institute ($8,700) in 2015. Key outputs include expert surveys on AI timelines, AI capabilities database, and empirical research on technological progress patterns. Focuses on 'answering decision-relevant questions about the future of AI' through research, wiki, and expert surveys.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Primarily focused on empirical research and data collection rather than explicit policy advocacy. Conducts expert surveys and maintains databases to inform AI governance decisions rather than taking strong regulatory positions.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Economic inequality, Power concentration

organizations.name: London AI Safety Research Labs (LASR Labs)
organizations.notes: 13-week technical AI safety research program where participants work in teams of 3-4 supervised by experienced researchers to produce academic papers. Provides £11,000 stipend. Alumni have joined UK AISI, Apollo Research, Leap Labs, and OpenAI's dangerous capabilities team. All 5 papers from Summer 2024 cohort accepted to NeurIPS workshops. Operated by Arcadia Impact charity, funded by Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy). Supervisors from Google DeepMind, UK AISI, and top universities.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: LASR Labs focuses on technical AI safety research to reduce risks from loss of control to advanced AI systems. The program explicitly aims to train researchers for careers in AI safety organizations and emphasizes action-relevant research tackling concrete threat models related to advanced AI risks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Steve Byrnes's Brain-Like AGI Safety
organizations.notes: Individual researcher Steve Byrnes working at Astera Institute since 2021. Physics PhD turned AGI safety researcher. Published comprehensive 15-part 'Intro to Brain-Like AGI Safety' blog series. Research applies neuroscience insights to AI alignment, focusing on Learning and Steering Subsystems framework. Funded by grants and now Astera Institute. Estimates ~100-200 people globally work full-time on AGI safety.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for solving technical alignment problems before AGI development, warning that brain-like AGI could have 'radically nonhuman and dangerous motivations' without proper safety work. Emphasizes need for understanding reward functions and steering subsystems to prevent catastrophic accidents.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: ILINA Program
organizations.location: Nairobi, Kenya (with presence in Nigeria, Zimbabwe, Mauritius)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, African-led research program with 14 employees. Coordinates Cecil Abungu, runs Junior Research Fellowship program for talent development. Publishes research papers and policy briefs on AI governance from Global South perspectives. Recent work includes 'Assessing the Case for Africa-Centric AI Safety Evaluations' and analysis of AU Continental AI Strategy. Hosts policy engagement sessions and collaborates with international AI safety community.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI safety governance with particular focus on Global South perspectives. Publishes research arguing that Global South countries need to actively engage with highly capable AI risks and calls for substantial funding allocation (one-third of R&D budgets) toward AI safety research. Recommends AI liability frameworks and advocates for Africa-centric AI safety evaluations.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Economic inequality, National security

organizations.name: AI2050 (Schmidt Sciences)
organizations.notes: Launched in 2022 by Schmidt Sciences as a philanthropic initiative co-chaired by Eric Schmidt and James Manyika. Awards over $18M annually to researchers through fellowship program. Now has 99 fellows across 8 countries and 42 institutions in fourth cohort. Uses closed nomination process rather than open applications. Fellows have published 100+ papers in leading journals and created datasets like 'Humanity's Last Exam' AI benchmark.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on ensuring AI benefits society by 2050 through research funding rather than regulatory advocacy. Co-founded by Eric Schmidt who has advocated for maintaining US AI competitiveness and avoiding restrictive regulations that could hamper innovation. Emphasizes technological solutions over policy interventions.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, National security, Environmental

organizations.name: Formation Research
organizations.notes: Research organization focused on reducing lock-in risks by studying fundamental lock-in dynamics and implementing high-impact interventions. Appears to be a specialized research entity rather than a broad policy organization. Limited public information available about founding, staff size, or funding sources. Website indicates newsletter signup and mentions being 'supported by' unspecified entities.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses specifically on lock-in risks in AI development, researching fundamental dynamics around how certain technological or governance paths could become difficult to reverse. Does not appear to take broad regulatory positions beyond this specialized research area.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Loss of control

organizations.name: AI Alignment Forum
organizations.location: Virtual (managed by LessWrong team in Berkeley, CA)
organizations.notes: Launched 2018 by LessWrong team as centralized hub for AI alignment research discussion. ~100 vetted members including researchers from MIRI, Anthropic, DeepMind, OpenAI, and academia. Serves as archive and library for the field with integrated karma system. Replaced MIRI's Intelligent Agent Foundations Forum. Key introductory sequences on Value Learning, Iterated Amplification, and Embedded Agency.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Forum discussions strongly favor careful evaluation and safety measures before deploying powerful AI systems. Content emphasizes responsible scaling policies, third-party testing, and international coordination on AI governance. Many posts advocate for pausing or slowing development until alignment problems are solved.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Deceptive alignment, Misalignment

organizations.name: Donations List Website
organizations.notes: Created 2017 by mathematician Vipul Naik with contributions from Issa Rice. Tracks $407M+ in AI safety donations (2007-2023) from major funders like Open Philanthropy ($276M), MIRI ($44M), OpenAI. Open-source database covers 875 donations across 191 organizations. Data considered preliminary until July 2026 completion target. Serves EA/rationalist communities primarily.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Does not advocate for specific AI policies but provides transparency infrastructure that enables others to track AI safety funding patterns. Serves as neutral documentation platform for philanthropic giving across cause areas.
organizations.threat_models: Unknown

organizations.name: Iliad
organizations.notes: Applied mathematics research nonprofit dedicated to advancing foundational alignment research. Runs research residencies (6-week programs with $6,000-$15,000 stipends), fellowships (3-month mentored research programs), and intensive courses. Partners with PIBBSS and operates at LISA in London. Funded by AISTOF. Organizes ILIAD conferences and provides research mentorship with established mathematicians and AI alignment researchers.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focus is primarily on technical research rather than explicit policy positions. Develops mathematical approaches to AI alignment through research residencies and fellowships, suggesting support for foundational safety research.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Lightspeed Grants
organizations.notes: Fast-funding initiative run by Lightcone Infrastructure, primarily funded by Jaan Tallinn. Distributed $9.6M in 2023 round with grants ranging $5K-$5M. Applications processed in 14-60 days. Funded projects like Palisade Research ($401K), Center for AI Safety ($16K), and various individual researchers. Last active funding round was 2023.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a grantmaker, does not take explicit regulatory positions but funds projects across the spectrum from AI safety research to policy advocacy, including both technical alignment work and governance initiatives.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Preamble Windfall Foundation
organizations.notes: 501(c)(3) founded around 2022, now defunct for unknown reasons. Founded by Jonathan Rodriguez Cefalu (Preamble Inc. founder, Stanford CS, ex-Snap). Funded research including SIMC4 wargame simulator for nuclear scenarios and AI red-teaming research. Filed Form 990-N indicating annual revenue under $50,000. Connected to broader Preamble ecosystem including commercial AI safety company Preamble Inc.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on technical AI safety research and evaluation methods rather than broad regulatory advocacy. Supports development of AI safety guardrails and evaluation frameworks through funded research projects.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Superlinear Prizes
organizations.notes: Decentralized bounty platform for existential risk reduction and effective altruist cause areas. Offers prize money for delivering on specific AI safety projects. Listed as no longer accepting applications as of 2026. Part of the broader effective altruism ecosystem funding AI safety research through competition-based incentives.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Platform focuses on funding AI safety and existential risk reduction projects through prize competitions, suggesting concern about AI risks and need for safety research.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI)
organizations.notes: Founded 2000 by Eliezer Yudkowsky, 24-year history, ~20 staff. Major shift in 2023-2024 from technical research to communications and policy work. $16M reserves, historically funded by Open Philanthropy ($15.9M), Vitalik Buterin ($5.4M), Jaan Tallinn ($1.6M). Published influential works including 'AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities.' Launched Technical Governance Team in 2024 focusing on international AI agreements and verification mechanisms. CEO Malo Bourgon took leadership in 2024.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for international agreements to halt progress toward smarter-than-human AI until alignment problems are solved. Believes current technical understanding is vastly insufficient and calls for indefinite worldwide moratorium on large training runs, enforced by international agreement. Has shifted from technical research to policy advocacy believing technical solutions won't arrive in time.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Public AI Network
organizations.location: Global network, coordination hub appears US-based
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as global movement and network promoting AI as public infrastructure. Maintains extensive publication database with 150+ papers, policy briefs, and research from multiple organizations. Key publications include Public AI Whitepaper (2024), policy briefs on European digital sovereignty, and proposals for public AI utilities. Affiliated researchers from institutions like Harvard, Vanderbilt, Mozilla, and various European policy organizations. Operates inference utility providing access to open-source AI models via HuggingFace.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for public AI infrastructure developed under transparent governance with democratic oversight, public accountability, and equitable access. Supports risk-based regulatory frameworks that focus on outputs rather than training inputs, legal clarity for using publicly available data, and safe harbors for responsible AI development while opposing restrictive data regulations that could shift AI development to other jurisdictions.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality, National security

organizations.name: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP)
organizations.notes: Led by Director Michael Kratsios under Trump administration. Coordinates AI policy across federal agencies through America's AI Action Plan (July 2025). Key initiatives include removing regulatory barriers, establishing AI procurement standards, creating federal AI framework to preempt state laws, and promoting AI adoption in government. Staff of approximately 22 people with $325M proposed budget cuts to NIST creating tension with innovation goals.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Promotes American AI dominance through minimal regulation and removal of regulatory barriers. Focuses on accelerating AI innovation, preventing 'woke AI' bias, and preempting restrictive state laws while maintaining basic safety standards for government AI use.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Bias/discrimination, Cybersecurity, Misinformation

organizations.name: Dovetail Research
organizations.notes: Research organization working on foundational mathematics and theory for AI safety, particularly agent foundations. Produces technical content on topics like formalizing value fragility, corrigible architectures, and how theory can contribute to AI safety through direct calculations, pointing at possibilities, providing disproofs of practicality, and qualitative behavioral descriptions. Appears to be a small, specialized theory-focused group distinct from the customer insights platform company of the same name.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on foundational theoretical research in agent foundations and mathematical frameworks for AI safety. Does not appear to take explicit policy positions but contributes to technical understanding that could inform future governance approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS)
organizations.notes: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) is a bipartisan Washington, D.C.-based think tank founded in 1962. One of the leading U.S. foreign-policy and national-security research institutions. On AI, CSIS houses the Wadhwani AI Center (launched 2024, directed by Gregory C. Allen) which publishes research on AI and national security, chip export controls, and U.S.-China technology competition, and produces the biweekly CSIS AI Policy Podcast.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage takes a balanced approach to AI policy, examining both innovation benefits and regulatory needs. Discussion topics include export controls, national security implications, safety measures, and strategic competition with China, reflecting mainstream policy discourse.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Economic inequality, Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Association for Long Term Existence and Resilience (ALTER)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, Israeli nonprofit with 5 staff. Funded by Open Philanthropy and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Led by David Manheim. Institutional member of US AI Safety Consortium (NIST). Key work includes AI oversight standards, biosecurity policy, and salt iodization advocacy. Publishes research on AI governance gaps and incident preparedness frameworks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for mandatory synthetic nucleic acid screening, establishment of governance frameworks for biological AI models, and incident preparedness approaches to AI regulation. Supports comprehensive regulatory frameworks with pre-deployment safety testing and strong governmental powers to respond to AI security incidents.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: 80,000 Hours
organizations.location: Oxford, UK + Berkeley, CA + NYC offices
organizations.notes: Founded 2011 by William MacAskill and Ben Todd. Career advisory nonprofit with 46 employees focusing on directing talent toward high-impact careers, especially AI safety/governance. Published extensive problem profiles on AI risks, operates job board, provides 1-on-1 career advice. Over 3,000 people report changing careers due to their guidance. Primary funder: Coefficient Giving. Part of effective altruism community.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful development of AI systems with strong safety measures. Promotes working on AI governance, technical safety research, and policy approaches to reduce catastrophic risks. Estimates ~10% existential risk from AI within 100 years and calls it 'the world's most pressing problem'.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Heron AI Security Initiative
organizations.notes: Founded December 2024 as a non-profit AI security initiative. Team includes Ezra Hausdorff (Founder/CEO), Shanni Gurkevitch (Co-Founder), Nitzan Shulman (Head of Cyber), and advisory board with AI security experts. Funded by Coeffecient Giving. Runs events, research programs, and career guidance connecting cybersecurity professionals to high-leverage AI security opportunities. Collaborates with frontier AI companies, startups, think tanks, and policymakers to advance AI infrastructure security.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stronger cybersecurity measures for frontier AI models, emphasizing the need for secure infrastructure, model weight protection, and authorization solutions for AI misuse. Promotes policy solutions like SL5-level security standards and regulatory frameworks for AI lab security.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, National security, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Computational Rational Agents Laboratory (CORAL)
organizations.notes: Led by Vanessa Kosoy, fiscally sponsored by Ashgro (formerly Affiliate Learning Theoretic Employment and Resources). Works on learning theoretic research agenda for AI alignment. Received $140,000 from SFF-2025 round funded by Jaan Tallinn. Categorized as doing foundational mathematics and agent foundations work in the AI safety ecosystem.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on foundational alignment research to develop mathematical tools for aligning AI objectives with human values. Works on agent foundations and learning theory to provide rigorous frameworks for AI alignment solutions.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Computational and Biological Learning Lab (University of Cambridge)
organizations.notes: Part of University of Cambridge Department of Engineering, established research lab focused on computational neuroscience and machine learning. Contains multiple research groups including the Krueger AI Safety Lab (KASL) founded 2022. Key members include David Krueger (AI alignment researcher who worked with UK Frontier AI Taskforce), Carl Rasmussen, Adrian Weller, and other ML researchers. Conducts both biological learning research and AI safety work including alignment, interpretability, and policy engagement.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As an academic research lab, CBL does not take explicit regulatory positions. However, lab members like David Krueger have worked with UK government (Frontier AI Taskforce) and contributed to policy discussions around AI safety and compute governance, suggesting engagement with regulatory development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination

organizations.name: Krueger AI Safety Lab (KASL)
organizations.location: Cambridge, UK (University of Cambridge) + Montreal, Canada (Mila)
organizations.notes: Founded ~2022, led by David Krueger (Assistant Professor at Cambridge). Dual affiliation with University of Cambridge and Mila (Quebec AI Institute). Team includes ~15 PhD students, postdocs, and research assistants. Key publications include 'Goal Misgeneralization in Deep Reinforcement Learning' (ICML 2022), 'Foundational Challenges in Assuring Alignment and Safety of LLMs' (2024). Runs paid AI safety internship programs. Previously based at University of Cambridge, now also affiliated with Mila.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful development of AI systems with strong safety evaluations. Co-authored major paper identifying 18 foundational challenges in LLM alignment and safety with 35+ researchers. Research focuses on technical solutions to prevent misaligned AI behavior before deployment.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Model Evaluation & Threat Research (METR)
organizations.location: Berkeley, California (operates in 5 countries including Denmark, Netherlands, UK, Germany)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as ARC Evals, rebranded to METR. 36 employees including CEO Beth Barnes (named to TIME's 100 most influential people in AI 2024). Funded by The Audacious Project, Open Philanthropy, Schmidt Sciences, and other philanthropic sources - notably does not accept funding from AI companies. Partners with OpenAI, Anthropic, and other labs on pre-deployment evaluations. Key research includes 'Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks' showing exponential capability growth. Part of NIST AI Safety Institute Consortium.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for rigorous pre-deployment evaluations of frontier AI models, external third-party auditing rather than self-evaluation by labs, and mandatory safety assessments before release. Developed the Responsible Scaling Policy framework adopted by nine leading AI developers and provides technical assistance to regulators including the EU AI Office.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, National security

organizations.name: Quantified Uncertainty Research Institute (QURI)
organizations.notes: Founded 2019 as nonprofit research institute by Ozzie Gooen. Small team of ~5 full-time staff. Receives funding from Survival and Flourishing Fund ($62K in 2024), operates under Rethink Priorities fiscal sponsorship. Key projects include Squiggle probabilistic programming language, SquiggleAI, and research on AI for forecasting question resolution. Board includes AI policy experts like Abigail Olvera (Golden Gate Institute for AI).
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing epistemic tools and forecasting systems to improve decision-making rather than direct regulatory advocacy. Research on AI for question resolution and evaluation systems could inform governance frameworks, but no explicit policy positions identified.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Saving Humanity from Homo Sapiens (SHfHS)
organizations.notes: Small philanthropic foundation with long history in X-risk funding. Does not conduct direct research but identifies and financially supports individuals and organizations working on existential risk reduction. Part of broader AI safety funding ecosystem alongside organizations like Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) and Survival and Flourishing Fund. Currently not accepting applications according to multiple sources.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Dedicated to preventing human-created existential risks through funding researchers and organizations working on X-risk reduction. Operates as a philanthropic foundation that identifies and financially supports people doing the best work to prevent human extinction from technologies like AI, nanotechnology, and other emerging threats.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Beijing Institute of AI Safety and Governance (Beijing-AISI)
organizations.notes: Founded September 2024 with Beijing municipal government support. Led by Yi Zeng, CAS professor and UN High-Level Advisory Body on AI member. Core contributor to China AI Safety & Development Association (CnAISDA). Published AGILE Index 2025 evaluating AI governance across 40 countries. Co-organized Beijing AI Safety International Consensus with signatures from Geoffrey Hinton, Yoshua Bengio, Stuart Russell.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance frameworks balancing development and safety through international cooperation. Supports tiered regulatory approaches, mandatory safety evaluations for frontier models, and establishment of global AI governance institutions with enforcement mechanisms.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity, Power concentration, Misinformation

organizations.name: China AI Safety & Development Association (CnAISDA)
organizations.location: China (multi-institutional network based primarily in Beijing)
organizations.notes: Launched February 2025 at Paris AI Action Summit. Network of China's leading AI institutions including Tsinghua University, Beijing Academy of AI, Shanghai AI Lab, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and China Academy of Information and Communications Technology. Claims Chinese government backing but operates as collaborative platform rather than regulatory body. Primary function is international representation and dialogue coordination rather than domestic oversight or testing.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI development and safety governance through international cooperation. Positions itself as China's counterpart to international AI safety institutes, emphasizing collaborative governance frameworks, voluntary industry safety commitments, and multilateral dialogue while avoiding restrictive regulatory approaches that could hinder development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Cybersecurity, CBRN risks, National security, Misinformation

organizations.name: The AI Whistleblower Initiative (AIWI)
organizations.location: Berlin, Germany (with presence in UK and Kenya)
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 as nonprofit hosted by Whistleblower Netzwerk e.V. Staff of 7 with 200% growth. Offers 'Third Opinion' anonymous consultation service connecting AI insiders with independent experts. Launched 'Publish Your Policies' campaign with 35+ signatories demanding AI companies publish whistleblowing policies. Documented 22 whistleblower cases (6 named, 16 anonymous) in comprehensive resource. Provides legal support connections and policy advocacy in EU and US.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for stronger legal protections for AI whistleblowers, supports principle-based regulation that covers emerging risks not yet captured by existing law, and promotes transparency requirements for AI companies including mandatory publication of internal whistleblowing policies.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Loss of control, National security, Democratic erosion, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Meridian Cambridge
organizations.notes: Coworking space and field-building nonprofit based in Cambridge, UK. Houses projects like CAISH and the ERA:AI Fellowship. Runs 3-12 month Visiting Researcher Programme for AI safety and governance researchers. Partners with Cambridge AI Safety Hub. Provides workspace, community, and research support including assistance with grant applications to Open Philanthropy and other funders. Contact: visitingresearchers@meridiancambridge.org
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI safety and governance research through visiting researcher programs. Houses projects like CAISH and ERA:AI Fellowship. Supports research on technical alignment, governance, interpretability, forecasting, and other research ensuring AI benefits humanity.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Power concentration

organizations.name: Constellation Institute
organizations.notes: Founded as nonprofit research center in Berkeley with 35 employees (+266% growth). Four core strategies: mobilization (placing talent), incubation (supporting new orgs), growth (helping orgs scale), and convening (facilitating coordination). Runs Astra Fellowship (3-6 month AI safety research program), incubator for AI safety startups, and visiting fellows program. Over 80% of first Astra cohort now work full-time in AI safety at organizations like Redwood Research, METR, Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing AI safety talent and supporting key organizations rather than direct policy advocacy. Emphasizes coordination across the AI safety field and facilitating collaboration between researchers, policymakers, and industry.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Existential risk

organizations.name: Center for AI Risk Management & Alignment (CARMA)
organizations.notes: Founded as fiscally-sponsored project of Social & Environmental Entrepreneurs (501c3). 10 employees across US, UK, Finland, Kenya, Italy. Led by Richard Mallah (also Principal AI Safety Strategist at Future of Life Institute). Conducts interdisciplinary research on risk management, policy, and technical safety. Co-authored 'Intolerable Risk Threshold Recommendations for Artificial Intelligence' and 'Open Problems in Frontier AI Risk Management' papers. Mission to lower risks to humanity and biosphere from transformative AI.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive risk management frameworks and intolerable risk thresholds for frontier AI systems. Co-authored research on 'Open Problems in Frontier AI Risk Management' recommending mandatory safety evaluations, risk thresholds, and multi-stakeholder governance approaches before deployment of transformative AI.
organizations.threat_models: Research focuses on CBRN weapons risks, cyber attack capabilities, model autonomy, persuasion and manipulation, deception, and systemic risks from increasingly powerful AI systems. Develops risk assessment frameworks using probabilistic risk assessment techniques adapted from other industries.

organizations.name: Alignment Research Engineer Accelerator (ARENA)
organizations.location: London, UK (hosted at London Initiative for Safe AI)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, runs 4-5 week intensive ML bootcamps 2-3 times per year. Each cohort accepts ~30 participants. Funded by Coefficient Giving and Open Philanthropy ($18,800 grant). Alumni outcomes include roles at Anthropic, OpenAI, METR, UK AISI, and MATS scholarships. Based at LISA in Shoreditch, covers all participant costs including travel, accommodation, and meals.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: ARENA trains technical AI safety researchers who typically advocate for cautious AI development. The program emphasizes AI alignment research and technical safety measures, with alumni going to organizations like Anthropic, METR, and UK AISI that promote careful AI governance.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: ML4Good Laboratory - New York University
organizations.notes: Academic research lab at NYU's Center for Urban Science and Progress, directed by Prof. Daniel B. Neill. Faculty includes experts in biostatistics, epidemiology, and applied statistics. Research applications include disease surveillance, opioid crisis response, criminal justice fairness, and urban analytics. Funded by NSF (including $1M NSF-Amazon Fairness in AI grant), collaborates with city agencies and public health organizations. Over 100 published papers on machine learning for social good applications.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Research focuses on algorithmic fairness, AI bias detection and correction, and developing responsible AI methods for public sector applications. Lab develops technical methods to audit and mitigate systematic biases in AI systems used for urban policy decisions.
organizations.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Privacy

organizations.name: Talos Fellowship (Talos Network)
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as EU Tech Policy Fellowship, renamed to Talos Network. Flagship 7-month program trains ~50 fellows annually in European AI governance through 8-week online curriculum, Brussels summit, and 4-6 month paid placements. 70% alumni transition rate to AI policy roles across EU AI Office, UK AISI, OECD, think tanks. Funded primarily by Coefficient Giving/Good Ventures, seeking €200k diversification funding in 2026. Managing Director: David Conrad.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on 'safe and responsible deployment of advanced AI' through comprehensive European AI governance training. Curriculum covers regulatory approaches from principles-based to rules-based frameworks, emphasizing EU AI Act implementation and international coordination on AI risks.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, National security, Economic inequality, Democratic erosion, Power concentration

organizations.name: Arkose Labs
organizations.location: San Mateo, CA (with offices in Brisbane, London, Costa Rica)
organizations.notes: Founded 2013 by Kevin Gosschalk. 250+ employees. Raised $114M+ from SoftBank Vision Fund, Microsoft M12, PayPal, Sony Innovation Fund. Commercial cybersecurity company developing fraud prevention platform 'Arkose Titan' with specialized focus on AI agent detection and classification. This appears to be a different organization from the AI safety nonprofit 'Arkose' mentioned in the original notes that closed due to lack of funding.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focused on commercial fraud prevention and cybersecurity rather than AI safety regulation. Advocates for enterprise preparedness against AI-powered attacks and supports 'Know Your Agent' frameworks for distinguishing legitimate from malicious AI agents.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Economic inequality, Power concentration

organizations.name: Global Priorities Institute (University of Oxford)
organizations.notes: Founded 2018, closed in 2025. Was an interdisciplinary research center at Oxford conducting foundational research on global priorities and longtermism. Received $3.3M from Open Philanthropy in 2020. Notable publications include 'AI alignment vs AI ethical treatment: Ten challenges' (2024) and research on longtermism paradigm. Staff included Leopold Aschenbrenner (former OpenAI researcher who later founded $1.5B hedge fund) and Bradford Saad who co-authored key AI ethics research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for careful consideration of AI development risks, including potential global moratoriums on advanced AI systems until adequate safety and ethical frameworks are established. Emphasizes the need for strategic funding of AI safety research and potential tax policies to discourage mistreatment of AI systems with moral status.
