organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Misinformation

organizations.name: Cavendish Labs
organizations.notes: Founded 2023, 501(c)(3) nonprofit research organization in rural Vermont. Offers fellowships for early-career researchers with $1500/month grants plus lodging. Received $88,000 from Survival and Flourishing Fund in 2023. Research community focused on AI safety and pandemic prevention, with researchers living and working together. Key publications include work on unelicitable backdoors in language models via cryptographic transformer circuits.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing provable guarantees and safety techniques for AI systems, particularly in RL frameworks. Conducts research on unelicitable backdoors and alignment methods to ensure AI systems behave safely and as intended.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Coordinal Research
organizations.location: Remote/distributed (founded through MATS program with Berkeley/London connections)
organizations.notes: Founded by MATS alumni Jacques Thibodeau and Ronak Mehta (merged with Vectis AI). Nonprofit developing research automation tools and AI systems to accelerate alignment work. Secured $110k+ in seed funding through Catalyze Impact's funding circle. Currently in alpha phase with research workflow tool called 'Seed'. Part of broader trend of AI safety organizations adopting automation to compete with capabilities research pace.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory positions found. Focus is on technical research automation and accelerating alignment research rather than policy advocacy. Appears to prioritize building tools and systems over regulatory interventions.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: EquiStamp
organizations.location: Lewes, Delaware, US (remote workforce across 11 countries)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as Public Benefit Corporation. 18 employees across 11 countries with task-based contributor network. Revenue-generating through client contracts with METR, UK AISI, Redwood Research, and EU Commission. Specializes in evaluation implementation using frameworks like Inspect. Leadership includes CEO Chris Canal, CTO Daniel O'Connell, and AI Advisor Rob Miles. Raised $400k operating capital via Manifund SAFE agreement in 2025.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports technical implementation of AI safety evaluations and compliance infrastructure. Through AgentStamp subsidiary, develops tools for EU AI Act compliance including transparency disclosures, audit trails, and regulatory reporting for AI agents.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: LawZero
organizations.notes: Founded June 2025 by Turing Award winner Yoshua Bengio. Nonprofit AI-safety lab launched with ~$30M in philanthropic funding from Jaan Tallinn, Eric Schmidt, Open Philanthropy, the Future of Life Institute, and others; ~15–30 employees at launch. Key research focus on 'Scientist AI' — non-agentic, Bayesian-inference-based systems intended to be safe-by-design and to provide trustworthy oversight of more powerful AI. Subsequently announced major Canadian government investment of over $100M.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI safety-first approaches through technical solutions rather than purely regulatory measures. Focuses on developing non-agentic 'Scientist AI' systems that can serve as guardrails for more dangerous agentic AI systems. Supports government regulation but emphasizes the need for technical safeguards built into AI systems by design.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: Luthien
organizations.notes: Founded 2025 by Jai Dhyani, senior FAANG ML engineer with decade of experience deploying AI at billion+ user scale. Non-profit organization building on Redwood Research's AI Control research. Received seed grant from AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund. Part of Catalyze's Winter 2024/25 London incubation cohort. Currently seeking $500K to grow Seattle team with SDEs and PM for coordination with research labs and frontier AI companies.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing production-ready AI Control solutions for immediate deployment, emphasizing practical risk mitigation through oversight and management systems that prevent misaligned AI from achieving harmful goals.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Meaning Alignment Institute
organizations.location: Berlin, Germany + San Francisco, United States
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Joe Edelman. 30+ researcher network with 3 core team members. Funded by ARIA, OpenAI, SFF, and others. Key research areas include democratic fine-tuning (won OpenAI Democratic Inputs grant), moral graph elicitation, and 'thick models of value.' Released WiseLLaMa-8B model trained on values-based conversations. Collaborates with major AI labs on alignment research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for 'full-stack alignment' approaches that align not just AI systems but also markets and democratic institutions with human values. Focuses on technical solutions like democratic fine-tuning and thick models of value rather than restrictive regulation.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: Ought
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 as nonprofit research lab. ~10 person team led by Andreas Stuhlmüller and Jungwon Byun. Raised $3.8M in 2020 from Open Philanthropy ($2.6M), Paul Christiano ($900K), and others. Built Elicit AI research assistant (spun off 2022 with $9M funding). Developed Interactive Composition Explorer (ICE) for factored cognition research. Key technical focus on supervising AI reasoning processes rather than outcomes.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on developing process-based supervision and factored cognition approaches for AI alignment rather than advocating for specific regulations. Emphasizes building safer AI systems through technical research on interpretability-by-construction and supervising reasoning processes.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Simplex
organizations.location: San Francisco Bay Area + London office
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Adam Shai (neuroscientist) and Paul Riechers (theoretical physicist). Small research team funded by Astera Institute. Key research: NeurIPS 2024 paper on transformer belief-state geometry, ICML 2025 work on attention mechanisms. Develops theoretical frameworks connecting next-token prediction to geometric structures in neural networks. Mentors fellows at MATS and PIBBSS programs. Hiring research scientists in Bay Area and London with $120k-200k salaries.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for establishing scientific foundations for understanding AI systems' internal structures to enable better monitoring and control. Emphasizes need for interpretability research to address alignment challenges and promote evidence-based policy discussions rather than polarized debates between 'doomers and optimists.'
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation

organizations.name: Metaculus
organizations.notes: Founded 2015 as public benefit corporation. Staff of 30. Operates forecasting platform with pro forecasters and AI policy workshops. Partnership with Future of Life Institute on AI Pathways project supporting U.S. policymakers. Open Philanthropy commissioned AI progress tournament. Board member Peter Wildeford co-founded Institute for AI Policy and Strategy.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Through AI Pathways workshops with Future of Life Institute, advocates for specific interventions including API restrictions to prevent recursive self-improvement, compute capacity restrictions, and required reporting of large training runs. Supports establishing government capability to evaluate AI systems for national security risks.
organizations.threat_models: Focuses on catastrophic AI risks including potential for AI-caused global catastrophe. Research emphasizes recursive self-improvement risks, loss of human control over advanced AI systems, and national security implications of AI capabilities races between nations.

organizations.name: AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund (AISTOF)
organizations.notes: Pooled multi-donor charitable fund managed by JueYan Zhang, former BlackRock portfolio manager and Founders Pledge investment committee chair. Raised over $10M since 2023 and committed 70+ grants. Structured for rapid deployment to capture emerging opportunities in AI safety landscape. Notable grantees include Luthien Research and other technical safety organizations. Praised by Open Philanthropy as filling important gaps in AI safety funding before major funders scaled up.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Funds AI safety research, governance, and evaluation projects to reduce catastrophic risks from advanced AI systems. Supports both technical alignment work and policy initiatives aimed at ensuring AI development proceeds safely. Views current AI progress as potentially dangerous without proper safeguards.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Cybersecurity, Weapons proliferation

organizations.name: An Overview of the AI Safety Funding Situation
organizations.notes: Comprehensive analysis published on EA Forum mapping AI safety funding ecosystem from 2022-2025. Tracks $3.9B in total funding with Safe Superintelligence raising $3B across two rounds. Documents shift from philanthropic to venture funding in AI safety space. Referenced extensively by other funders and researchers as authoritative overview of funding landscape.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Analysis advocates for increased AI safety funding and coordination among philanthropic funders. Emphasizes need for governance infrastructure and responsible development practices. Aligned with effective altruism's longtermist approach to existential risk mitigation.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: Longview Philanthropy
organizations.notes: Founded 2018 by Natalie Cargill. 30 employees, directed over $133M toward AI risk reduction as of March 2026. Manages Frontier AI Fund ($20M raised, $11.1M disbursed to 18 orgs), Digital Sentience Fund ($2.5M), and Emerging Challenges Fund. CEO Simran Dhaliwal, serves major donors giving >$100K with bespoke philanthropy strategies.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strongly advocates for AI safety regulations and governance frameworks. Manages multiple AI safety funds totaling over $22.5M, funding research, policy advocacy, and field-building to shape frontier AI development and regulation. Supports organizations working on AI governance in US and Europe.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration, Weapons proliferation, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Simon Institute for Longterm Governance
organizations.notes: Founded 2021, nonprofit think tank with 5 staff based in Geneva. Funded by Open Philanthropy and other EA-aligned donors. Specializes in international AI governance and multilateral diplomacy. Key work includes designing frameworks for UN's Independent International Scientific Panel on AI, organizing briefings for diplomats, and publishing policy recommendations for international AI summits. Named after Herbert Simon, focusing on future-oriented policymaking tools.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based multilateral AI governance through international institutions. Develops policy frameworks for AI safety assessments, promotes technical safeguards for frontier AI systems, and supports structured international cooperation mechanisms like the UN's Independent International Scientific Panel on AI.

organizations.name: UChicago Existential Risk Laboratory (XLab)

organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Laboratory focuses on understanding and mitigating existential risks including AI safety, advocating for increased attention to catastrophic risks that could result in hundreds of millions of deaths or permanently curtail civilization's potential. organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance frameworks including binding regulations, external pre-deployment assessments for high-risk AI systems, AI red lines for unacceptable capabilities (CBRN uplift, mass surveillance), and robust enforcement mechanisms. Supports both horizontal and sector-specific regulation rather than self-governance approaches.

Sponsors projects including Orthogonal (formal alignment research), Timaeus, and various individual researchers. Enables projects to access funding and reduces administrative burden.

organizations.notes: Living document published October 2024 by Connor Leahy, Gabriel Alfour, Chris Scammell, Andrea Miotti, and Adam Shimi. Comprehensive worldview document arguing AI progress is rapidly converging on AGI driven by corporate race backed by Big Tech and nations. Estimates solving alignment requires Nobel-prize-level breakthroughs, billions/trillions in investment, and decades of work. Connected to ControlAI organization for public awareness and policy advocacy.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for aggressive slowing and regulation of AI progress, implementing mechanisms to pause/stop AI development, and preventing the creation of AGI altogether. Supports international coordination through treaties and regulations to deter nation-states from racing to superintelligence. Endorses 'A Narrow Path' strategy to prevent superintelligence development for 20+ years.

organizations.notes: Founded 2017 by David Kristoffersson and Justin Shovelain, formalized as institute in 2024. Non-profit with 9-person interdisciplinary team spanning AI alignment, ethics, governance, and policy. Funded by Future of Life Institute, Open Philanthropy, and individual donors. Key programs: AI Clarity (scenario research), Governance Recommendations, and AI Awareness. Published major reports including '2024 State of AI Regulatory Landscape' and 'Analysis of Global AI Governance Strategies'. Hosted Threshold 2030 conference on AI economics. Research has influenced US BIS dual-use AI reporting requirements and EU AI Act implementation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive AI governance through scenario planning and strategic policy recommendations. Supports risk-based regulatory frameworks, AI model registries, incident reporting requirements, and international cooperation mechanisms. Emphasizes the need for proactive governance to address existential risks from transformative AI.

Funded by Long-Term Future Fund and other EA sources including $50K+ grants for distillation fellowship program.

organizations.notes: Founded 2023 as Australian nonprofit, now merged with Existential Risk Observatory. Small team of 2 employees with focus on public advocacy and policy submissions.

Argues that existing alignment theory, designed for agent-like AI, may not apply well to self-supervised generative models. Emphasizes need for new conceptual frameworks to understand simulator vs simulacra dynamics.

Listed as 'no longer active' on AISafety.com map but website remains operational with recent content updates.

Conducts workshops nationwide at libraries, universities, and community centers on AI basics, safety evaluations, policy analysis, and forecasting. Partners with Seattle Public Library and other institutions for public education programs. Featured in AISafety.com field map as advocacy organization.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for public awareness and education about AI risks and benefits through workshops and community engagement. Focuses on empowering citizens to have a voice in shaping AI development rather than specific regulatory prescriptions.

organizations.name: Advanced Research + Invention Agency (ARIA)
organizations.notes: UK government R&D agency established 2023 by Act of Parliament, modeled after DARPA. £800M budget over 5 years (£184M allocated 2025-26). CEO Ilan Gur, 95 employees. Eight program directors with £50M+ budgets each. Major programs: Safeguarded AI (£69M), Scaling Compute (£50M), Precision Neurotechnologies. Portfolio includes AI hardware startups like Fractile (£5M) and Callosum (partnership). Operates independently from government intervention.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Government R&D agency funding high-risk, high-reward research including AI safety programs. Safeguarded AI program seeks to build 'gatekeeper' systems with quantitative safety guarantees for frontier AI deployment. Complementary to UK AI Safety Institute's policy work, focused on fundamental research for 3-10 year horizons.

organizations.name: Institute for Law & AI (LawAI)
organizations.location: Boston, MA (with offices in UK, Germany, Denmark, Canada, Australia)
organizations.notes: Founded as independent think tank, formerly Legal Priorities Project. Staff of 40+ researchers across 12 countries, led by Founding Director Christoph Winter. Publishes working paper series via SSRN, produces policy reports and research articles on AI governance. Notable recent work includes 'Legal Alignment for Safe and Ethical AI' and 'Automated Compliance and the Regulation of AI'. Hosts workshops on law-following AI and maintains research collaborations with academic institutions.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for precise, legally-grounded AI regulation that addresses specific harms rather than broad technology bans. Proposes innovative regulatory mechanisms like 'automatability triggers' that activate regulations only when AI can automate compliance tasks, and emphasizes liability frameworks for AI companies causing harm.

organizations.name: International AI Governance Alliance (IAIGA)
organizations.notes: Founded 2025 as initiative of Center for Existential Safety. Being incorporated as nonprofit in Geneva. Launched petition calling for supranational AI governance organization, aiming for 1M signatures. Led by James Norris who has 26+ years preparing for Singularity. Expects AI 'warning shot' or catastrophe soon. Seeks to develop vetted treaty quickly as 'Plan B' to other multilateral efforts.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for immediate pause on all large-scale frontier AI development until provably safe systems can be developed. Calls for creation of supranational organization to develop and enforce legally-binding Global AI Safety Treaty with stringent safety standards and mandatory fair distribution of AI-derived economic benefits.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Economic inequality

organizations.name: International Association for Safe & Ethical AI (IASEAI)
organizations.location: Geneva, Switzerland (headquarters) with regional chapters in 40+ countries, operational offices in San Diego, CA and Paris, France
organizations.notes: Founded in 2017 as international nonprofit coalition, significantly expanded in 2024 with 23 employees (+350% YoY growth). Received $1.85M grant from Future of Life Institute in 2025. Hosts major annual conferences (IASEAI'25 had 700+ attendees from 65 countries, IASEAI'26 had 1300+ from 99+ countries). Advises UN, OECD, EU on AI governance and helped shape EU AI Act. Develops practical tools including AI scorecards, ethical certification programs, and Safe AI Deployment Toolkit (SAIDT). Key leaders include Stuart Russell (President) and Mark Nitzberg (Interim Executive Director).
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for binding international safety standards, mandatory third-party audits for frontier AI systems, and establishment of AI red lines that trigger system termination. Promotes global coordination on AI governance while supporting flexible implementation that respects local cultural values.
organizations.threat_models: Bias/discrimination, Misinformation, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality, Privacy, National security, Power concentration

organizations.name: Centre pour la Sécurité de l'IA (CeSIA)
organizations.notes: Founded March 2024 by Charbel-Raphaël Ségerie and team. ~20 employees plus volunteers. Funded by Survival and Flourishing Fund, Longview Philanthropy, Swedish foundation, Effektiv Spenden. No AI company funding to avoid conflicts. Provides Europe's first university AI safety courses at ENS Ulm/Paris-Saclay. Initiated Global Call for AI Red Lines with 300+ signatories including Nobel laureates. Co-organized symposiums with Yoshua Bengio and Stuart Russell.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for robust AI governance frameworks including mandatory safety evaluations, third-party audits, and international coordination mechanisms. Contributed to EU AI Act Code of Practice implementation and supports establishing 'red lines' for unacceptable AI capabilities and uses.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, Misinformation, Weapons proliferation, Power concentration, Bias/discrimination, National security

organizations.name: 5050 AI (by Fifty Years)
organizations.location: San Francisco, CA + London, UK (partnership with ARIA)
organizations.notes: Free 13-week startup accelerator program run by Fifty Years VC, helping scientists and engineers build AI safety/alignment companies. 445 participants to date, launched 105 companies with 96% fundraising success rate. Mentors include OpenAI co-founder Wojciech Zaremba, former OpenAI interim CEO Emmett Shear. UK program launched in partnership with ARIA in 2025. No equity taken, entirely free program focused on commercializing AI safety research.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on building AI safety infrastructure through market-driven solutions. Supports startups developing safety evaluation tools, interpretability platforms, alignment infrastructure, and governance systems rather than direct regulatory advocacy.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Bias/discrimination

organizations.name: Centre for Enabling EA Learning & Research (CEEALAR)
organizations.notes: Founded 2018 by Greg Colbourn, registered charity in England & Wales since 2020. Provides subsidized accommodation and workspace in Blackpool for EA researchers and entrepreneurs at £750/month. Supports ~20 residents at a time with 4-6 month average stays. 66 grantees in 2025 (+36% YoY). Notable alumni include Chris Leong (Principal Organiser for AI Safety New Zealand) and Hoagy Cunningham (Stanford Existential Risks Initiative). Formerly known as 'EA Hotel', rebranded in 2020. Currently facing £330k funding gap and needs £30k for urgent roof repairs.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Primarily focused on talent development and research support rather than direct policy advocacy. Supports researchers working on global catastrophic risks including AI safety, but does not appear to take explicit regulatory positions.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control

organizations.name: AI Governance and Safety Institute (AIGSI)
organizations.notes: The AI Governance and Safety Institute (AIGSI) is a nonpartisan 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization on a mission to ensure artificial intelligence and other technologies benefit humanity and are developed safely, securely, and in alignment with human values. The organization aims to improve institutional response to the risk that future AI might cause human extinction and ensure the enormous potential benefits of AI are realized. They conduct research, engage in outreach, and educate stakeholders and the general public. The organization focuses on key technical concepts including machine learning interpretability, outer alignment, inner alignment, convergent instrumental subgoals, and advanced agents.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for preventing development of superintelligent AI until safety can be guaranteed. Emphasizes institutional response to existential risk through research, outreach, and education. Supports mandatory safety evaluations and oversight mechanisms.
organizations.threat_models: Existential risk, Loss of control, Power concentration

organizations.name: AI Safety Asia (AISA)
organizations.location: Hong Kong (global operations across 16 Asian countries)
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by Supheakmungkol Sarin (former Google AI lead and World Economic Forum Head of Data & AI) and Edward Tsoi. Team of 13 employees across 7 countries. Has engaged 2,000+ AI governance professionals across 16 Asian countries. Co-hosted sessions at India AI Impact Summit 2026 on crisis diplomacy and contributed to International AI Safety Report 2026. Focuses on building durable governance infrastructure through regional research, structured peer learning, and implementation-oriented engagement.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for evidence-based AI governance frameworks tailored to Asian contexts. Co-hosted AI crisis diplomacy sessions focusing on cross-border incident coordination and joint safety testing between countries. Contributed to International AI Safety Report 2026 emphasizing need for demonstrable safety evidence and credible liability frameworks.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, National security, Power concentration, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Athena Mentorship Program for Women (AI Alignment Research)
organizations.location: Remote program with rotating in-person retreats (previously Oxford UK, location varies)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023 by Claire Short. 10-week remote mentorship program with 1-week in-person retreat. Targets women transitioning into or early in AI alignment research careers. Funded through Manifund ($20k goal reached), Lightspeed grants. Requires 20+ hours/week commitment. Provides mentorship from established AI safety researchers, weekly research talks, networking opportunities, and potential placement in research groups post-program. Has run multiple cohorts with positive participant feedback.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on technical AI alignment research capacity building rather than explicit policy positions. Aims to increase diversity in AI safety research field through mentorship and training.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS) AI Policy Fellowship
organizations.notes: Nonpartisan think tank producing policy research on AI implications. Fellowship program runs 3 months with $15,000-$22,000 stipends, training professionals from varied backgrounds in AI policy. Alumni have secured positions at RAND, Institute for Progress, Center for Health Security, and government agencies. Recent research includes strategic visions in AI governance and policy recommendations for the AI Action Plan. Features mentorship from leading experts and produces concrete policy outputs like briefings to government officials and international conferences.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: IAPS focuses on practical policy solutions to address AI risks from today's advanced models to potential AGI and superintelligence, with emphasis on national security implications, government procurement, export controls, and international governance frameworks. Their research covers strategic visions for AI governance including government involvement and centralized development approaches.
organizations.threat_models: National security, Power concentration, Loss of control, Weapons proliferation, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Orion AI Governance Initiative (Arcadia Impact)
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 as part of Arcadia Impact. UK-based talent development scheme with 3 employees. Offers 9-week AI Policy Accelerator program, mentorship scheme, and internship placements. Has trained 57 participants across programs and placed 2 people into AI policy roles at think tanks. Applications for 2026 program closed January 6, 2026. Project manager is Callum Hinchcliffe, who previously worked on AI governance research and policy.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program focuses on developing talent for AI governance careers with emphasis on managing risks from advanced AI systems. Curriculum covers policy levers including compute governance, model evaluations, and international AI governance frameworks.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, National security, Loss of control, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Pathfinder Fellowship (Kairos)
organizations.notes: Launched 2025 by Kairos, replacing Open Philanthropy's University Organizer Fellowship and Kairos's prior Fieldbuilder Support Program. Supports ~70 AI safety university groups worldwide with funding, mentorship, and resources. 12 staff across 7 countries. Funded by Open Philanthropy grant, fiscally sponsored through BERI. Provides $500+ startup funding plus ongoing support and graduate student stipends.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program supports AI safety and policy university groups globally, training students to work on AI alignment, governance, and existential risk reduction. Mentors have experience at organizations like METR, RAND TASP, and US AISI, indicating focus on safety-first approaches to AI development.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security

organizations.name: Technical Alignment Research Accelerator (TARA)
organizations.location: Australia & New Zealand (Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane) + Asia-Pacific expansion
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by AI Safety Australia & New Zealand (AIS ANZ), led by Yanni Kyriacos. 14-week part-time program based on ARENA curriculum with weekly in-person Saturday sessions. Inaugural cohort targeted 25 participants across Australian cities. Funded by $14,901 grant from Ryan Kidd via Manifund. Expanding to Singapore, Manila, Tokyo, and Taipei in 2026. Program aims to develop technical talent for AI Safety Institutes and demonstrate community credibility to Australian government.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Program explicitly aims to staff Australia's AI Safety Institute with safety-conscious technical talent and leverage Australia's strategic alliance with the US to promote robust global AI safety governance. Focus on preventing harmful impacts of AI through technical alignment research.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk

organizations.name: Center for AI Policy (CAIP)
organizations.notes: Founded 2023, shut down in September 2025 due to lack of funding. Was a 501(c)(4) advocacy organization that conducted over 406 meetings with Congressional staffers, hosted 8 congressional briefings reaching 150+ staffers, and developed the 'Responsible AI Act' model legislation. Funded primarily by Survival and Flourishing Fund. Executive Director Jason Green-Lowe continues to offer legislative review services and seeks funding to revive the organization.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocated for comprehensive regulation of frontier AI systems through their 'Responsible AI Act' model legislation, requiring independent third-party audits, deployment permits, hardware security standards, and government oversight before powerful AI systems can be deployed. Focused on preventing catastrophic risks through mandatory safety evaluations.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Existential risk, National security, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Stanford HAI
organizations.notes: Stanford HAI (Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence) is an interdisciplinary research institute at Stanford University, founded in 2019 by Fei-Fei Li and John Etchemendy, along with James Landay and Chris Manning. The institute advances AI research, education, policy, and practice with the goal of ensuring AI benefits humanity. HAI publishes the annual AI Index Report — the most widely cited benchmark of global AI progress, tracking technical capability, industry investment, policy activity, and public opinion. It has invested over $40 million across 300+ Stanford researchers spanning computer science, social sciences, law, and medicine. In the U.S. AI policy ecosystem, HAI occupies an influential centrist position: its co-directors, fellows, and researchers regularly advise Congress, federal agencies, and international governance bodies. The institute advocates for AI that augments rather than replaces humans, and for targeted federal oversight rather than either blanket restriction or unchecked acceleration.
organizations.threat_models: Democracy, Economic inequality, Power concentration

organizations.name: Time (AI coverage)
organizations.notes: Time magazine has extensively covered AI developments, with correspondents Andrew R. Chow and Billy Perrigo leading AI coverage through their newsletter 'In the Loop'. The publication has launched TIME AI initiatives including the TIME AI Agent, developed in collaboration with Scale AI, which provides personalized, interactive journalism experiences. Time has also partnered with OpenAI to advance global AI literacy and created AI-powered features like daily audio briefs and translation services. The magazine named 'The Architects of AI' as the 2025 Person of the Year, reflecting AI's significant impact.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

organizations.name: Leverhulme Centre for the Future of Intelligence
organizations.notes: From AISafety.com map. Interdisciplinary research center at the University of Cambridge exploring the nature, ethics, and impact of AI.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: UK AI Security Institute
organizations.notes: The UK AI Security Institute (AISI) is a government research organisation within the UK's Department for Science, Innovation and Technology, established in 2023 as the AI Safety Institute and renamed in February 2025. AISI's mission is to equip governments with scientific understanding of advanced AI risks — particularly catastrophic risks such as AI-assisted development of chemical and biological weapons, cyberattacks, and AI-enabled crime. Under Chair Ian Hogarth and Interim Director Adam Beaumont (formerly GCHQ's Chief AI Officer), the institute employs 100+ technical staff including alumni from OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Oxford. Key programs include the £15m Alignment Project, £8m Systemic Safety Grants, and £5m Challenge Fund. AISI holds formal research MOUs with Anthropic, Google DeepMind, OpenAI, and Cohere, and co-led the International AI Safety Report (2024). It plays a central role in the international AI Safety Summit process and directly informs UK government AI policy.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Power concentration, Democratic erosion

organizations.name: Public AI
organizations.notes: Public AI is a nonprofit initiative advocating for public-interest AI infrastructure as an alternative to purely commercial frontier AI development. Positions public AI as critical digital infrastructure (analogous to public broadcasting or libraries) and promotes government and civil-society funded AI projects, open models, and democratic oversight of large-scale AI systems.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Environmental

organizations.name: Stanford RegLab
organizations.notes: Stanford RegLab (Regulation, Evaluation, and Governance Lab) partners with government agencies to design and evaluate programs, policies, and technologies that modernize government. The organization uses machine learning and data science to improve compliance, mass adjudication, policy making, and government services. RegLab has an extensive track record working with local and national government agencies including the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Internal Revenue Service (IRS), Santa Clara County Public Health Department, and others. Daniel E. Ho is the Faculty Director of RegLab and William Benjamin Scott and Luna M. Scott Professor of Law at Stanford University.

organizations.name: Leading the Future
organizations.funding_model: Super PAC with over $100 million in initial funding from AI industry figures including Andreessen Horowitz, Greg Brockman, Anna Brockman, Ron Conway, Joe Lonsdale, and Perplexity. Raised $125 million in late 2025 with $70 million cash on hand entering 2026.
organizations.notes:
Leading the Future (FEC ID: C00916114) is a bipartisan super PAC launched by a coalition of AI companies and industry leaders with the mission to ensure U.S. leadership in AI innovation and development. It engages in independent expenditures to support candidates aligned with pro-AI agendas, legislative advocacy, and grassroots efforts across federal and state levels, with operations beginning in 2025 and plans to expand nationwide in 2026.

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00916114). Classified as a pro-innovation AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports pro-innovation policies that foster AI development and economic growth while resisting measures that excessively constrain AI development. Opposes restrictive legislation and policies that hinder AI progress.

organizations.name: Think Big
organizations.funding_model: Political/PAC (sub-PAC of Leading the Future; funded by a16z/Brockman/Lonsdale/Conway)
organizations.notes: Democratic-aligned super PAC within the Leading the Future network (pro-AI industry). Sister PAC to Republican-focused American Mission. Funded primarily by Leading the Future ($10M) plus Ron Conway ($500K) and Joe Lonsdale ($250K); raised ~$10.5M total by March 2026. Opposes candidates who support AI regulation; spent ~$2.42M against Alex Bores in NY-12.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Think Big advocates for pro-AI innovation policies and a balanced approach to AI regulation that fosters innovation and economic growth. They support the position that being pro-AI does not equate to being anti-regulation.

organizations.name: American Mission
organizations.funding_model: Super PAC funded primarily by Leading the Future ($10 million), Lonsdale Enterprises Inc. ($250,000), Greg Brockman/OpenAI ($25 million), and Andreessen Horowitz ($25 million). Raised approximately $10.25 million total as of early 2026.
organizations.notes:
Supported candidates in TX and NC races. Backed Chris Gober, Tom Sell, Jessica Steinmann, Laurie Buckhout, Clay Fuller, Jace Yarbrough.

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00916692). Classified as a pro-innovation AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports minimal regulation of AI and advocates for policies favoring industry growth and innovation. Focuses on fostering AI innovation and competitiveness rather than restrictive policies.

organizations.name: Defending Our Values PAC
organizations.funding_model: Political/PAC (funded through Public First Action; Anthropic primary donor)
organizations.notes: Republican-aligned super PAC affiliated with Public First Action (backed by Anthropic's $20M donation). Supports GOP candidates who favor AI safety regulation and state-level AI oversight. FEC ID C00928390. Backed Alexandra Mealer, Carlos De La Cruz, and Pete Ricketts. Counterpart to Democrats-focused Jobs and Democracy PAC.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on AI security, transparency, and regulation with particular emphasis on controls over advanced AI chip exports to adversaries. Supports candidates advocating for AI safeguards while ensuring AI development benefits the public.

organizations.name: Jobs and Democracy PAC
organizations.funding_model: Political/PAC (funded through Public First Action; Anthropic primary donor)
organizations.notes: Democratic-aligned super PAC affiliated with Public First Action, funded by Anthropic's $20M donation. Backs Democratic candidates who support AI safety regulation and state-level AI oversight. FEC ID C00928374. Major spends for Valerie Foushee (~$1.61M), Alex Bores (~$468K), and Colin Allred (~$150K).
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Democratic super PAC advocating for policies that regulate Big Tech and AI in ways that serve the public interest, supporting candidates who prioritize voters over Big Tech interests.

organizations.name: Dream NYC
organizations.funding_model: Super PAC funded by Daniel Ziegler of Anthropic PBC ($50,000 raised as of Dec 31, 2025)
organizations.notes:
Supported Alex Bores ($1,332).

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00928069). Classified as a pro-safety AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Dream NYC supports strong AI regulation, corporate accountability, and safety protocols. The PAC backs candidates like Alex Bores who advocate for the RAISE Act to ensure AI safety and transparency requirements.

organizations.name: Cloudflare
organizations.notes: Founded 2009, provides web infrastructure services including CDN, security, and AI tools. Offers Workers AI platform with 80+ models from various providers but doesn't train LLMs itself. Launched AI Security Suite and application confidence scoring system. Created marketplace allowing websites to charge AI bots for content access. CEO Matthew Prince actively lobbying regulators on AI crawler separation.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for responsible AI bot principles including public disclosure, self-identification, and respecting website preferences. Supports targeted regulation like separating Google's search and AI crawlers while maintaining open ecosystems and innovation-friendly policies.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Copyright/IP, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Nvidia
organizations.notes: Founded 1993, became dominant AI chip provider with 92% discrete GPU market share in 2025. Market cap reached $4.6 trillion. Invested in 67+ AI startups in 2025, including OpenAI ($100M), Anthropic ($10B), and xAI ($6B). CEO Jensen Huang lobbied Trump administration to approve H200 chip sales to China, facing bipartisan congressional opposition over national security concerns.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for minimal export controls on AI chips, pushing for approval to sell advanced H200 chips to China. Supports voluntary safety commitments and industry-led frameworks rather than restrictive government regulation.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Environmental

organizations.name: Amazon
organizations.notes: Amazon has developed frontier AI models through its Nova family and operates Amazon Bedrock AI service. Published comprehensive Frontier Model Safety Framework in 2024 with critical capability thresholds for CBRN weapons, cybersecurity, and automated AI R&D. AWS CEO Andy Jassy projects AI could help AWS reach $600 billion in annual sales by 2036, double previous estimates. Major cloud infrastructure provider enabling AI development across the industry.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Amazon advocates for voluntary safety commitments and industry self-governance rather than prescriptive federal regulation. Supports the Korea Frontier AI Safety Commitments and has developed its own Frontier Model Safety Framework with critical capability thresholds and safety measures, but emphasizes market-driven solutions over regulatory mandates.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons

organizations.name: Open Machine
organizations.notes: Founded by CEO Allie K. Miller, focuses on CPU-first AI deployment without GPU requirements. Develops edge-native AI solutions with unified multimodal runtime. Emphasizes data sovereignty and offline capability. Claims sub-60ms recall performance with <2.4GB memory footprint on commodity hardware.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Emphasizes data sovereignty and privacy-first AI deployment with edge-native solutions that avoid cloud dependencies and vendor lock-in, suggesting preference for minimal regulatory interference while maintaining user control.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Cassava Technologies
organizations.notes: Founded by African entrepreneur Strive Masiyiwa, headquartered in London with operations across 94 countries. Built 110,000km of fiber infrastructure and 9 operational data centers. Launched Africa's first AI factory in 2024/2025 powered by NVIDIA, with partnerships including Microsoft, Google, AWS, and Anthropic. 1,848 employees as of 2025.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates against 'too-hasty or too-heavy regulation of AI' that may 'hobble its compelling potential for African development.' Emphasizes that inappropriate regulation could widen the digital divide for Africa and frustrate AI applications for common good.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality

organizations.name: Qualcomm
organizations.notes: Founded 1985 by UC San Diego professors, semiconductor company with 30,000+ employees (20,000+ engineers). Investing in AI R&D for 15+ years, first to run Stable Diffusion on smartphone (2023). Holds 130,000+ patents, partners with IBM and Truepic on AI initiatives. Focuses on edge AI and on-device inference capabilities.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for open AI ecosystems and voluntary responsible AI principles while opposing restrictive regulations that could create closed ecosystems or limit access to data for AI development. Supports collaborative, voluntary approaches over regulatory mandates.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Power concentration, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Scale AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 by Alexandr Wang at age 19, focuses on AI data infrastructure and labeling. Over 900 employees, valued at $14B after $1B Series F in May 2024. Major DoD contracts including multi-million dollar 'Thunderforge' AI agents deal. Wang stepped down as CEO in June 2025 to lead Meta's 'Superintelligence' unit as part of $14.3B Meta investment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based approach through existing regulatory agencies with centralized coordination rather than new comprehensive legislation. Emphasizes industry standards like red teaming and testing/evaluation over heavy-handed regulation.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Misinformation, Weapons

organizations.name: AI Grant
organizations.notes: Founded 2017 as non-profit distributed AI research lab. Provides $2.5K-$50K non-dilutive grants specifically for AI safety, interpretability, and alignment research. Made 104 investments with 10 exits including Common Sense Machines (acquired by Google). Operates as accelerator/incubator focused exclusively on AI startups.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on funding AI safety research through non-dilutive grants, supporting alignment and interpretability research without advocating for restrictive regulations.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control

organizations.name: TSMC
organizations.notes: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is the world's largest contract chip manufacturer, fabricating over 90% of advanced semiconductors as of 2023. Founded 1987, manufactures chips for Nvidia, Apple, AMD and other AI companies. Revenue $122.4B in 2025, planning $56B capex in 2026. Critical supplier for AI infrastructure but not an AI research lab.

organizations.name: Midjourney
organizations.notes: Founded in 2022 by David Holz (Leap Motion co-founder). Operates with only 11 full-time employees, generating ~$300M revenue in 2024 without venture capital. Faces major lawsuits from Disney/NBCUniversal over alleged copyright infringement in AI training data. Released V7 model in April 2025 after nearly year-long gap.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Received regulatory scrutiny from Senator Warner on AI security measures and faces multiple copyright lawsuits from Disney and NBCUniversal over training data usage. Company's stance on broader AI regulation not clearly articulated publicly.
organizations.threat_models: Copyright/IP, Misinformation

organizations.name: Huawei
organizations.notes: Chinese tech giant developing AI chips (Ascend series) and systems to compete with Nvidia. Launched CloudMatrix 384 AI system with 384 Ascend 910C chips in 2025. Under US export restrictions but continues advancing AI capabilities through system-level design innovations. Operates Noah's Ark Laboratory for AI research since establishment.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI security frameworks and responsible development with multi-layer defense systems, while operating under Chinese regulatory requirements. Emphasizes technical security measures and compliance with national laws.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, Weapons

organizations.name: Reddit
organizations.notes: Reddit has lucrative AI data licensing deals with Google ($60M annually) and OpenAI ($70M annually), representing ~10% of $1.3B revenue. The platform launched Reddit Answers AI search tool powered by Google Gemini in late 2024, growing from 1M to 15M weekly users by Q4 2025. CEO Steve Huffman aims to make Reddit a 'go-to search engine' while implementing policies to label AI-generated content and preserve human authenticity. The platform blocked most automated crawlers in 2024 and sued Anthropic for unauthorized scraping.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Reddit has positioned itself as supporting content licensing standards (backing Really Simple Licensing initiative) while maintaining control over its data through selective AI partnerships and blocking unauthorized crawlers. The platform emphasizes human-first content policies while monetizing AI training data deals.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Copyright/IP

organizations.name: SoftBank
organizations.notes: Founded 1981, manages $140+ billion through Vision Funds. Major AI investments include $64.6 billion in OpenAI (13% ownership), $1.4 billion in Skild AI, and leadership in the $500 billion Stargate AI infrastructure project. Portfolio includes 300+ AI and tech companies across foundation models, robotics, and infrastructure.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: SoftBank takes an aggressive pro-AI stance, investing massively in AI infrastructure and capabilities. CEO Masayoshi Son has declared the company's mission to realize Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) for humanity's advancement, with minimal emphasis on regulatory constraints.

organizations.name: Salesforce
organizations.notes: Founded 1999, publicly traded CRM company with 70,000+ employees. Launched Einstein AI platform in 2016, Agentforce AI agents in 2024. Chief Ethical and Humane Use Officer Paula Goldman sits on U.S. National AI Advisory Committee. Has comprehensive AI governance framework with cross-functional AI Council and established Trusted AI principles in 2018.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for comprehensive federal AI regulation including mandatory impact assessments for high-risk AI systems, support for EU AI Act framework, and specific prohibitions on harmful practices like facial recognition. Supports risk-based regulatory approach with strong civil rights protections.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Democratic erosion, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Arm
organizations.notes: Semiconductor IP company founded 1990, designs processor architectures used globally. Powers AI infrastructure from cloud datacenters (50% of hyperscaler compute in 2025) to edge devices. Exceeded $1 billion revenue for three consecutive quarters in 2025. Supports 22 million developers worldwide building on Arm compute platform.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for balanced AI regulation that mitigates harm while enabling innovation. Supports sector-specific approaches over comprehensive restrictions, favoring frameworks that address underlying compute infrastructure rather than controlling specific AI applications.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Unitree Robotics
organizations.notes: Founded 2016 by Wang Xingxing, ~500 employees. Ships over 5,500 humanoid robots annually (2025), leading global market. Products include G1 ($16k) and H1 humanoid robots, plus quadruped models. Subject of US Congressional hearings over cybersecurity vulnerabilities and alleged military ties. Planning IPO with Chinese government 'national champion' status.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Company appears caught between commercial interests and Chinese government support, with reported cybersecurity vulnerabilities and military ties raising regulatory concerns in the US while benefiting from 'little giant' enterprise designation and government subsidies in China.
organizations.threat_models: Cybersecurity, Loss of control, Weapons

organizations.name: Mobileye
organizations.notes: Founded 1999, focusing on autonomous driving and ADAS technologies. Develops EyeQ system-on-chip processors and safety frameworks like Responsibility-Sensitive Safety (RSS). Over 230 million vehicles built with Mobileye technology through 2025. Listed independently from Intel in 2022, generated $1.894B revenue in 2025. Agreed to acquire Mentee Robotics in 2026 to expand into humanoid robotics.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for national AV legislative framework with safety baselines and modernized FMVSS standards, but emphasizes industry self-regulation through safety methodologies like RSS. Supports transparency through safety cases rather than restrictive pre-deployment requirements.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control

organizations.name: Asteria Film Co.
organizations.notes: Asteria Film Co. is actually an AI-powered film production studio, not a traditional media/journalism organization. Founded by Bryn Mooser and Natasha Lyonne, it focuses on creating 'ethical AI' models for film production while developing the Continuum Suite operating system for streamlined filmmaking workflows. Backed by CAA and Comcast Ventures.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Asteria advocates for 'clean' and 'ethical' AI models that avoid copyright violations while supporting industry innovation, suggesting a self-regulatory approach rather than heavy government intervention.
organizations.threat_models: Copyright/IP, Labor displacement

organizations.name: Tools for Humanity
organizations.location: San Francisco, California and Munich, Germany
organizations.notes: Founded 2019 by Alex Blania (CEO) and Sam Altman (Chairman). Develops iris-scanning 'Orb' devices for World ID verification system. Over 500 employees, $2.5B valuation, raised $240M+ from investors including Andreessen Horowitz. 17.5 million verified users as of 2024, targeting 1 billion users.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Company operates globally with biometric data collection despite regulatory pushback, suggesting preference for minimal restrictions. However, faces bans and investigations in multiple countries for data protection violations.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation

organizations.name: Samaya AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2022 as AI platform for financial services, not AI safety/alignment. Raised $43.5M Series A led by NEA in 2025. Team includes 30+ members from Google Brain, Meta AI, AWS, Stanford. Develops specialized AI agents for investment research and financial analysis workflows, deployed at Morgan Stanley and major banks with 10,000+ professional users.

organizations.name: ElevenLabs
organizations.notes: Founded 2022, raised $281M total including $180M Series C (Jan 2025) at $3.3B valuation. 120 employees across London, New York, Warsaw. Specializes in AI voice generation, cloning, and conversational agents. First company to secure AI agent insurance through AIUC-1 certification. Partners with governments including Ukraine, Czech Republic, and UK on public services.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Actively collaborates with governments on AI safety research and regulation, including partnering with UK AI Security Institute. Implements comprehensive safety frameworks with disclosure requirements, content moderation, and user accountability. Supports certification standards like AIUC-1 for enterprise AI deployment.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Cybersecurity, Loss of control

organizations.name: Agility Robotics
organizations.notes: Founded 2015 as Oregon State University spinout, grew to 300+ employees. Launched Digit humanoid robot in 2020, first commercially deployed humanoid. Raised $400M in 2025 at $1.75B valuation. Manufacturing facility 'RoboFab' in Salem with 100% US assembly.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for industry-led safety standards and regulatory sandbox approaches for robotics deployment. Supports responsible development with focus on controlled environments first, but emphasizes need for competitive positioning against China.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement

organizations.name: Pony.ai
organizations.notes: Founded 2016, specializes in autonomous driving technology for robotaxis and robotrucks. Raised $800M+ via Hong Kong IPO in 2025, operates 1,446 vehicle fleet as of March 2026. Achieved unit economics breakeven in Guangzhou and Shenzhen. Targets 3,000+ robotaxis across 20+ global cities by end of 2026.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for federal AV framework with safety cases and industry self-regulation rather than prescriptive rules. Supports voluntary safety commitments and opposes restrictive state regulations that could hinder innovation.

organizations.name: Humain
organizations.notes: HUMAIN is a Saudi artificial intelligence company established under the Public Investment Fund and launched on May 12, 2025, by Mohammed bin Salman, Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia. The company operates across the entire AI value chain, providing comprehensive AI services including next-generation data centers, AI infrastructure and cloud capabilities, and advanced AI models and solutions. HUMAIN aims to position Saudi Arabia as a globally competitive AI hub and is chaired by HRH the Crown Prince. The company's CEO is Tareq Amin.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Operates within Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 framework emphasizing rapid AI development and deployment. CEO praises government 'enablement' rather than restrictive regulation, with focus on strategic partnerships and infrastructure scaling.

organizations.name: KoBold Metals
organizations.notes: Founded 2018, uses AI for mineral exploration targeting copper, lithium, nickel, and cobalt. Raised $1.2B total including $537M Series C (Jan 2025), valued at $2.96B. Discovered major copper deposit in Zambia (Mingomba project) expected to produce 300,000+ tons annually by 2030s. Backed by Bill Gates, Jeff Bezos, Andreessen Horowitz.

organizations.name: FutureHouse
organizations.notes: Founded November 2023 as nonprofit by Sam Rodriques and Andrew White. Backed by Eric Schmidt. Mission to build 'AI Scientists' for biology research over 10 years. Launched platform with specialized AI agents (Crow, Falcon, Owl, Phoenix) in May 2025. Spun out commercial entity Edison Scientific in November 2025 raising $70M. Operates wet lab for human-AI collaboration.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a nonprofit focused on building AI scientists, emphasizes responsible AI use and safety without sacrificing scientific progress, but doesn't advocate for specific regulatory approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control

organizations.name: Adobe
organizations.notes: Founded 1982, major creative software company that has integrated AI into products for over a decade. Developed Firefly generative AI model trained exclusively on licensed content, offers enterprise indemnification. Key AI products include Firefly, GenStudio, and AI assistants across Creative Cloud. Founded Content Authenticity Initiative in 2019 with 5,000+ members to combat misinformation through Content Credentials technology.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for risk-based regulation that accommodates different AI use cases, with lighter oversight for low-risk applications and appropriate guardrails for higher-risk uses. Supports targeted disclosure requirements and industry standards while opposing broad restrictions that could stifle innovation.
organizations.threat_models: Copyright/IP, Misinformation, Labor displacement

organizations.name: Credo AI
organizations.notes: Enterprise AI governance platform company founded by Navrina Singh. Provides software and advisory services for AI risk management and regulatory compliance. Named leader in Forrester Wave AI Governance Solutions 2025 and recognized in Gartner Market Guide. Has partnerships with Microsoft, McKinsey, and Databricks.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for enterprise AI governance through compliance with existing and emerging regulations like EU AI Act, NIST RMF, and NYC Local Law 144. Focuses on practical implementation of AI governance frameworks rather than broad regulatory restrictions.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Cybersecurity, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Sakana AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2023, raised $302M total funding. Develops nature-inspired foundation models and automated scientific discovery systems. Created 'AI Scientist' tool that generates complete research papers for ~$15 each. First AI system to pass peer review at academic conference (ICLR 2025). Partnered with Google in 2026 for enterprise AI solutions targeting finance and government sectors.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Emphasizes responsible AI development with transparency about AI-generated research and ethical considerations. Advocates for sandboxing and safety measures while pursuing automated scientific discovery.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation

organizations.name: Surge AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2020 by Edwin Chen (ex-Google/Facebook/Twitter). Bootstrapped to $1.2B revenue in 2024 serving OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, Meta with high-quality human data labeling. 250 employees managing 50K+ expert contractors globally. Seeking first external funding of $1B at ~$24B valuation in 2025.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: As a data labeling platform serving frontier AI labs, Surge operates under existing data protection frameworks without advocating for restrictive AI-specific regulations that could hamper model development.

organizations.name: Tryolabs
organizations.notes: Founded 2009 as AI/ML consulting company, 15+ years before mainstream AI adoption. Grew from 4 people to 100+ employees serving enterprise clients globally. CEO Alan Descoins named in TIME's '100 Most Influential People in AI' 2025. Focuses on custom AI solutions, MLOps, and 'AI for Good' projects including conservation and sustainability initiatives.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for pragmatic AI governance focused on practical business deployment challenges rather than restrictive regulation. Emphasizes building trust through responsible practices and addressing real-world implementation barriers over hypothetical risks.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Splice
organizations.notes: Founded 2013, music creation platform with 600K+ paying subscribers generating $100M+ annual revenue. Acquired Spitfire Audio for ~$50M in 2025. Hit 350M sample downloads in 2024, focuses on AI-powered music creation tools that amplify rather than replace human creativity.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for ethical AI development with human-made sounds as foundation, artist compensation, and human oversight. Supports targeted regulations that protect creators while enabling innovation in music production tools.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Copyright/IP

organizations.name: Shield AI
organizations.notes: Founded 2015 by brothers Brandon and Ryan Tseng (both former Navy SEALs) and engineer Andrew Reiter; mission to protect service members with autonomous systems. Raised $240M Series F at $5.3B valuation (March 2025). In March 2026 closed a $2B round — $1.5B Series G equity plus $500M preferred from Blackstone — at a $12.7B post-money valuation, a 140% jump in twelve months following a U.S. Air Force deal. Projects $540M+ revenue in 2026 (over half international). First AI pilot deployed in combat (2018); selected for Air Force Collaborative Combat Aircraft program. Products include Hivemind autonomy software and V-BAT drones.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on defense applications with emphasis on ethical AI use and safety controls. Prohibits weapons/surveillance facilitation in commercial AI products but builds autonomous weapons systems for military use.
organizations.threat_models: Weapons, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Wild Dolphin Project
organizations.notes: Founded in 1985 by Dr. Denise Herzing, conducting the world's longest-running underwater dolphin research project studying Atlantic spotted dolphins in the Bahamas. Collaborates with Georgia Tech and Google on AI-powered dolphin communication research, including the DolphinGemma model and CHAT (Cetacean Hearing Augmentation Telemetry) system. Has 40 years of underwater video and audio data from wild dolphin communities, funded by grants including Templeton World Charity Foundation.

organizations.name: IIT Madras
organizations.notes: Houses multiple AI research centers including RBCDSAI (founded 2017), CeRAI for Responsible AI, and AI4Bharat for Indian language AI. Prof. B. Ravindran chairs government AI governance committee and serves on UN AI panel. Wadhwani School of Data Science and AI leads national AI policy discussions with 'whole-of-government' approach recommendations.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: IIT Madras advocates for agile, sector-specific AI governance using existing laws rather than new standalone AI legislation. Prof. Ravindran led committee recommending flexible frameworks with 'Do No Harm' principle and emphasis on innovation sandboxes.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Power concentration, Economic inequality

organizations.name: NoHarm
organizations.notes: Search results show NOHARM as a medical AI safety evaluation framework developed by academic researchers, not a traditional ethics/rights organization. Also found #NoHarmware campaign by Tāhono Trust (New Zealand) focused on online safety regulation. No evidence of a US-based Ethics/Rights Organization specifically named 'NoHarm' found in search results.

organizations.name: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center
organizations.notes: Founded 1975, Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center is a nonprofit research institution with 3,676 employees and $600M annual revenue. They coordinate the Cancer AI Alliance (CAIA) launched in 2024 with Dana-Farber, Memorial Sloan Kettering, and Johns Hopkins, backed by $40M+ from AWS, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Deloitte. The center actively deploys AI tools including GI Genius for colonoscopy analysis and DataVoyager for clinical research, while maintaining strict data protection protocols for patient health information.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Fred Hutch emphasizes responsible AI deployment with privacy safeguards and ethical guidelines, particularly through their Cancer AI Alliance (CAIA) which uses federated learning to protect patient data while enabling AI research collaboration.
organizations.threat_models: Copyright/IP, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: World Labs
organizations.notes: Founded 2024 by AI pioneer Fei-Fei Li and team, developing 'spatial intelligence' and Large World Models for 3D environments. Raised $1.23B total funding including $1B round in Feb 2026 from AMD, Nvidia, Autodesk. Released Marble product in Nov 2025 for generating 3D virtual worlds. 39+ employees, valued at ~$5B.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Co-founder Fei-Fei Li was cited as having undisclosed industry affiliations in a paper advocating for 'evidence-based AI policy' that some critics argue delays meaningful regulation. However, no explicit regulatory positions are stated by the company itself.

organizations.name: Khosla Ventures
organizations.notes: Founded 2004 by Sun Microsystems co-founder Vinod Khosla. Manages $16B AUM across 527+ portfolio companies. Early OpenAI investor (valued at $150B+). Investment focus includes AI, climate tech, healthcare with 'black swan' philosophy targeting contrarian opportunities. Recent major AI investments include Cognition Labs (Devin AI), Waabi ($750M Series C), and Emergent ($70M Series B).
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Vinod Khosla advocates for minimal AI regulation, arguing that overregulation could stifle innovation. He has publicly criticized California's SB 1047 AI safety bill and emphasizes techno-optimism over precautionary approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Sequoia Capital
organizations.notes: Founded 1972, manages ~$85B in assets. Major AI investments include OpenAI, Anthropic, Reflection AI, and Ricursive Intelligence. Published influential AI market analyses including 'Generative AI's Act Two' and '2026: This is AGI' defining AGI as 'the ability to figure things out.' Portfolio companies account for 25% of NASDAQ's total value.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Supports market-driven AI development with emphasis on innovation and competition. Partners advocate for allowing AI capabilities to develop through market forces while maintaining basic safety standards.
organizations.threat_models: Labor displacement, Economic inequality, Power concentration

organizations.name: Greylock
organizations.notes: Founded 1965, one of Silicon Valley's most storied VC firms. Partners include Reid Hoffman (LinkedIn founder). Over 80% of investments are first checks (pre-seed to Series A). Explicit 'AI-first' strategy with major investments in Abnormal Security, Baseten, Adept AI, Cogent Security, and Resolve AI. Check sizes $300K-$10M across early stages.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Portfolio heavily weighted toward AI capabilities companies with limited evidence of explicit regulatory positions. Focuses on 'AI-first' investing across infrastructure, applications, and foundation models without clear stance on AI governance.

organizations.name: Lux Capital
organizations.notes: Founded 2000, $7B AUM across 9 funds. Latest Fund IX raised $1.5B in January 2026. 200+ portfolio companies including major AI investments: Anduril ($30.5B valuation), Applied Intuition ($15B), Hugging Face, Runway AI, Cognition ($10.2B), Physical Intelligence. Strong focus on defense tech, robotics, and frontier science applications.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong focus on defense/national security AI applications suggests support for strategic regulation, while backing frontier AI companies indicates preference for innovation-friendly policies. Investment in both AI capabilities and dual-use defense technologies reflects complex regulatory position.

organizations.name: 8VC
organizations.notes: Founded 2015, approximately $7B AUM across six flagship funds. 363+ portfolio companies including 35 unicorns. Major AI investments include Palantir (co-founded by partner Joe Lonsdale), AI21 Labs, Atomic AI, Cognition AI. Strong focus on 'Smart Enterprise' thesis applying AI to traditional industries like healthcare, logistics, and government.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for AI-friendly regulatory approach, particularly in healthcare. Published detailed vision calling current healthcare AI regulations overly restrictive and proposing specific policy reforms to enable AI deployment while maintaining safety.

organizations.name: Slow Ventures
organizations.notes: Founded 2011, generalist early-stage VC firm with ~$1B deployed across 500+ companies. Led by Sam Lessin and Kevin Colleran (Facebook alums). Notable exits include Slack, Robinhood, Airtable, Solana. Recently raised $275M across Fund VI and Opportunity Fund III (2024). Portfolio spans fintech, SaaS, consumer, healthcare, crypto with $250K-$3M check sizes primarily at seed stage.

organizations.name: Entrepreneur First
organizations.notes: Founded 2011, operates globally with offices in London, Paris, Singapore, New York, and San Francisco. Recently raised $200M at unicorn valuation with portfolio worth over $16B. Notable AI investments include Gensyn, Tractable (unicorn), Photoroom, and numerous other AI startups. Runs specialized AI Startup School program and moves all funded companies to Bay Area for scaling.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Strong focus on AI startups and scaling them globally, particularly moving companies to US markets for faster growth. Portfolio heavily weighted toward AI companies with emphasis on building 'globally significant companies' in the AI space.

organizations.name: Plural
organizations.notes: Founded 2021, employs 22 people. Led by partners including Taavet Hinrikus, Khaled Helioui, and Ian Hogarth. Recent AI investments include $10.25M in Callosum (AI compute orchestration), investments in Helsing (defense AI), Hypersonica (hypersonics), and Upside Robotics (agricultural AI). Fund II has $30.3M total value with 50.19% gross IRR.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Invests in AI compute infrastructure companies like Callosum that challenge NVIDIA's dominance, and defense AI companies like Helsing, suggesting support for competitive markets and European sovereignty in AI.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration

organizations.name: Schmidt Futures
organizations.notes: Founded 2017 by Eric and Wendy Schmidt, transitioned into Schmidt Sciences in 2024. 164 employees across 36 countries. Major AI investments include backing Anthropic, Cloudline ($6M), and Gradium ($70M). Operates AI2050 fellowship program and Science of Trustworthy AI initiative. Spun out Ex/ante investment fund in 2023 with $33M inaugural fund.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Focuses on 'trustworthy AI' research and safety evaluation frameworks. Major funding for AI safety, governance, and responsible deployment initiatives including $18M for AI safety research and support for regulatory compliance tools.
organizations.threat_models: Loss of control, Misinformation, Economic inequality

organizations.name: Humane Intelligence
organizations.notes: 501(c)(3) nonprofit founded by Dr. Rumman Chowdhury and Jutta Williams, with Mala Kumar as Interim Executive Director as of August 2025. Specializes in AI red teaming, bias bounties, and contextual evaluations. Has conducted 30+ evaluations with 5,250+ participants across 55+ countries. Plans to release red teaming app as open source in 2026.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for rigorous AI evaluation frameworks, red teaming requirements, and evidence-based regulation. Supports collaborative governance models involving industry, government, and civil society rather than restrictive top-down approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Economic inequality, Power concentration, Misinformation

organizations.name: Stanford CRFM
organizations.notes: Stanford Center for Research on Foundation Models (CRFM), founded 2021 as part of Stanford HAI and led by Percy Liang. Coined the term ''foundation models'' and publishes influential work on model evaluation (HELM), transparency (Foundation Model Transparency Index), and governance. One of the most cited academic sources in U.S. AI policy debates.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: CRFM advocates for transparency requirements and coordinated flaw reporting infrastructure while engaging with policymakers on AI governance frameworks. Their work on the Foundation Model Transparency Index has been incorporated into the EU AI Act and US congressional proposals.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Loss of control, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Exponential View
organizations.notes: Founded and written by Azeem Azhar, prominent AI policy commentator and author of 'Exponential'. Weekly newsletter with 200,000+ subscribers analyzing AI's economic and societal impacts. Co-authored research with Epoch AI on AI economics and regularly interviews tech leaders and policymakers.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Advocates for thoughtful AI governance while emphasizing innovation benefits. Supports targeted regulation focusing on AI chip controls and infrastructure oversight rather than broad restrictions.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Economic inequality, Cybersecurity

organizations.name: Wired
organizations.notes: Major technology publication with dedicated AI coverage team including senior writers Will Knight, Maxwell Zeff, and Zeyi Yang. Academic research shows WIRED's AI coverage from 2018-2023 had predominantly positive sentiment with increasing polarization over time. Reaches over 30 million people monthly and is considered one of the most influential technology publications globally.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Coverage shows nuanced approach to AI regulation, reporting on both benefits and risks of AI development while covering geopolitical tensions and policy debates without taking strong editorial positions on specific regulatory approaches.
organizations.threat_models: Power concentration, Misinformation

organizations.name: MIT Technology Review
organizations.notes: Leading technology publication with dedicated AI reporting team including Will Douglas Heaven (senior AI editor), Michelle Kim, and others. Coverage spans technical developments, policy analysis, and societal impacts. Known for annual '10 Breakthrough Technologies' list and deep investigative pieces on AI industry practices.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Editorial coverage shows balanced approach to AI regulation, reporting critically on both regulatory gaps and overreach. Coverage emphasizes need for targeted oversight while acknowledging innovation concerns.
organizations.threat_models: Misinformation, Power concentration, Loss of control

organizations.name: Bloomberg
organizations.notes: Bloomberg has extensive AI coverage across multiple verticals including technology, markets, and legal analysis. Operates dedicated AI section with regular reporting on policy developments, market trends, and corporate adoption. Bloomberg Law provides specialized AI legal analysis. The company has also developed its own AI products including ASKB for the Bloomberg Terminal, built on proprietary AI capabilities dating back to 2009.
organizations.regulatory_stance_detail: Bloomberg's coverage presents multiple perspectives on AI regulation, from reporting on industry resistance to state-level AI laws to critical analysis of federal AI frameworks that favor tech companies.
