As of Fall 2024, the university has a total student enrollment of 24,123 with faculty and staff totaling 7,231. The university operates with an annual revenue of $1.3 billion and has three campuses including the 287-acre Belknap Campus.

organizations.name: American University
organizations.notes: American University is a private federally chartered research university in Washington, D.C., chartered by Congress in 1893. The university consists of eight schools and colleges, including the Kogod School of Business, School of International Service, College of Arts and Sciences, School of Communication, School of Professional and Extended Studies, School of Public Affairs, Linda A. and H. Kent Baker School of Education, and Washington College of Law. American University has 13,019 students as of fall 2023 and offers over 160 academic programs. The Kogod School of Business has launched the Institute for Applied Artificial Intelligence (IAAI) and has been recognized for having 'the most consequential AI transformation in business education' according to Poets&Quants.

organizations.name: SUNY Albany
organizations.notes: SUNY Albany is also known as University at Albany, UAlbany, or SUNY Albany and is a public research university in Albany, New York, United States. The university was founded in 1844 and is one of four "university centers" of the State University of New York (SUNY) system. In 2024, the university enrolled 17,567 students in nine schools and colleges, which offer over 50 undergraduate majors and over 150 graduate programs. The university is classified among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research spending and doctorate production".

organizations.name: USC
organizations.notes: USC is a private university founded in 1880 that is home to more than 48,000 students and over 4,400 full-time faculty. USC launched the Frontiers of Computing initiative in 2023, a $1B-plus initiative for advanced computation, quantum computing, AI and ethics as the largest, most comprehensive academic initiative in USC's history. USC has multiple AI research centers and initiatives, including the USC Center for AI in Society founded in 2016, which is a collaboration between USC's Suzanne Dworak-Peck School of Social Work and Viterbi School of Engineering. The university provides every student, faculty member, staff member and researcher with secure, enterprise-grade AI tools.

organizations.name: Wharton School
organizations.notes: The Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania was founded in 1881 as the world's first collegiate business school. It has more than 235 renowned professors and 5,000 undergraduate, MBA, executive MBA, and doctoral students. In April 2025, Wharton announced a new undergraduate concentration and MBA major in Artificial Intelligence for Business. The school is led by Dean Erika James.

organizations.name: Tulane University
organizations.notes: Tulane University is a private research university in New Orleans, Louisiana, United States that was founded as the Medical College of Louisiana in 1834 by a cohort of medical doctors. The institution became private under the endowments of Paul Tulane and Josephine Louise Newcomb in 1884 and 1887. Tulane has been a member of the Association of American Universities since 1958 and is classified among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity". The university has established multiple AI-related initiatives including the Connolly Alexander Institute for Data Science, the Jurist Center for Artificial Intelligence, and the Center for Community-Engaged Artificial Intelligence.

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Transportation
organizations.notes: The U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) is committed to safety and innovation and sees artificial intelligence (AI) as a promising capability to help achieve these aims. The Department has established an AI Compliance Plan in response to the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Memorandum M-25-21, Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust. The Department was authorized in October 1966 and launched on 1 April 1967, with a mission to ensure that federal funds were effectively used to support the national transportation program. The mission of the USDOT is to ensure our nation has the safest, most efficient and modern transportation system in the world; that improves the quality of life for all American people and communities, from rural to urban, and increases the productivity and competitiveness of American workers and businesses.

organizations.name: University of California, San Francisco
organizations.notes: The University of California, San Francisco (UCSF or UC San Francisco) is a public land-grant research university in San Francisco, California, United States that is part of the University of California system and is dedicated entirely to health science and life science. UCSF was founded as Toland Medical College in 1864, became affiliated with the University of California as its medical department in 1873, gained full administrative independence as a campus of the UC system in 1964, and gained its current name in 1970. UCSF's mission is to advance health worldwide through preeminent biomedical research, graduate-level education in the life sciences and health professions, and excellence in patient care. In 2023, UCSF received the 2nd highest research funding from the National Institutes of Health.

organizations.name: Bayesian Logic Inc.
organizations.notes: Bayesian Logic Inc. is co-founded by Nimar Arora, who serves as Chief Executive Officer, Secretary, Chief Financial Officer, and Director. Stuart Russell serves as a Director and is described as founder and Vice-President of the company. The company was officially filed as a General Corporation in California on October 24, 2011, and maintains active status. Bayesian Logic Inc. developed NET-VISA, a Bayesian seismic monitoring system for detecting nuclear explosions, which has been deployed by the CTBT organization of the U.N. and achieves around 60% reduction in missed events compared to currently deployed systems.

organizations.name: Coinbase
organizations.notes: Coinbase is joining the x402 Foundation alongside tech titans like Amazon, Google, and Stripe. Coinbase has launched crypto wallet infrastructure that allows AI agents to spend, earn and trade crypto. The company's mission is to increase economic freedom for more than 1 billion people by providing a trusted platform for crypto assets. Coinbase delivered strong Q4 and full year 2024 results, with full year revenue of $6.6 billion, net income of $2.6 billion, and Adjusted EBITDA of $3.3 billion.

organizations.name: Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee
organizations.notes: The Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee is a standing committee of the United States Senate composed of 28 Senators and led by Committee Chairman Ted Cruz (R-Texas) and Ranking Member Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.). The committee has broad jurisdiction over all matters concerning interstate commerce, science and technology policy, and transportation, and is one of the largest of the Senate's standing committees. The committee has six subcommittees which together oversee issues ranging from communications, highways, aviation, rail, shipping, transportation security, merchant marine, the Coast Guard, oceans, fisheries, weather, disasters, science, space, interstate commerce, tourism, consumer issues, economic development, technology, competitiveness, product safety, and insurance. The committee passed the bipartisan Future of AI Innovation Act to maintain United States leadership in the global race to develop artificial intelligence (AI) and other emerging technologies.

organizations.name: Special Competitive Studies Project
organizations.notes: The Special Competitive Studies Project (SCSP) is a bipartisan, non-profit initiative with a mission to make recommendations to strengthen America's long-term competitiveness as artificial intelligence (AI) and other emerging technologies reshape national security, economy, and society. SCSP carries forward the vital mission of the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (NSCAI), which was established in 2018. The organization was founded in 2021 and has 98 employees, located in Crystal City, United States. SCSP has launched multiple commissions and task forces, including collaborations with NVIDIA on AI and the future of work, and commissions on fusion energy and robotics for advanced manufacturing.

organizations.name: Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research
organizations.notes: The Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research (BAIR) Lab brings together UC Berkeley researchers across the areas of computer vision, machine learning, natural language processing, planning, and robotics. BAIR includes over two dozen faculty and more than a hundred graduate students pursuing research on fundamental advances in the above areas as well as cross-cutting themes including multi-modal deep learning, human-compatible AI, and connecting AI with other scientific disciplines and the humanities. The lab was established in 1990 as part of the University of California, Berkeley. BAIR operates the Open Research Commons (ORC) which brings together academia and industry to cultivate an open, interdisciplinary research environment.

organizations.name: Berkeley Institute for Data Science
organizations.notes: The Berkeley Institute for Data Science (BIDS) is a central hub of data-intensive research, open source software, and data science training programs at UC Berkeley that launched in 2013. BIDS is an integral part of UC Berkeley's College of Computing, Data Science, and Society (CDSS) and was made possible by grants from the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation and the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation. The Institute is one of three Moore-Sloan Data Science Environments along with data science organizations at the University of Washington and New York University. BIDS is currently led by Fernando Pérez as Faculty Director.

organizations.name: Responsible AI Initiative
organizations.notes: Multiple organizations operate under the name "Responsible AI Initiative" with different scopes and affiliations. The University of Utah operates the One-U Responsible AI Initiative, which aims to advance AI to achieve societal good while protecting privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties. Emory University launched a Responsible AI website as part of their enterprise-wide Responsible AI initiative, led by the Emory AI Group with input from the Responsible AI Executive Governance Committee. Carnegie Mellon University launched a Responsible AI initiative out of the Block Center for Technology and Society with support from faculty in the School of Computer Science.

organizations.name: Université de Montréal
organizations.notes: Université de Montréal is a French-language institution of higher learning founded in 1878, regarded as one of the most important universities in Canada in terms of research and one of the largest by enrolment. The university has 16 faculties and two affiliated schools (Polytechnique Montréal and HEC Montréal), with an enrollment of nearly 67,600 students in over 600 programs. UdeM ranked 9th globally in Artificial Intelligence research according to the Shanghai Academic Ranking of World Universities in 2026. The university has an annual revenue of $212.0M and employs 7,578 people.

organizations.name: Mila - Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute
organizations.notes: Mila - Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute is the world's largest academic AI research center with over 1,400 researchers and 140+ professors. Founded in 1993 by Yoshua Bengio at Université de Montréal, it is an independent nonprofit research organization dedicated to advancing artificial intelligence through fundamental research and talent development. The institute is recognized worldwide for its major contributions to AI, particularly in deep learning, language modeling, machine translation, object recognition and generative models. Mila is part of the Pan-Canadian Artificial Intelligence Strategy alongside Alberta's Amii and Toronto's Vector Institute.

organizations.name: Element AI
organizations.notes: Element AI was a Canadian multinational artificial intelligence company based in Montreal, Quebec that was founded in October 2016. The company was funded by the Government of Canada for CAD$5 million, and raised US$102 million independently, before being acquired by ServiceNow. ServiceNow announced it has signed an agreement to acquire Element AI on November 30, 2020, with ServiceNow completing the acquisition of Element AI on January 8, 2021. The company was running out of money and options and sold for US $230 million.

organizations.name: McGill University
organizations.notes: McGill University was founded in 1821 from a generous bequest by James McGill, a prominent Scottish merchant. The university offers an Applied Artificial Intelligence Minor in the Faculty of Engineering that provides foundation for applying AI techniques in various fields. McGill has several AI-related organizations including the McGill Centre for Intelligent Machines, which has been a world leader in research on intelligent systems since 1985. The McGill Collaborative for AI and Society (McCAIS) aims to understand the impact of AI on society and develop evidence-based best practices for its responsible use.

organizations.name: International Association for Safe and Ethical AI
organizations.notes: The International Association for Safe and Ethical AI (IASEAI) is a non-profit organization founded in 2024 with headquarters in Paris, France. The organization's stated mission is to address the risks and opportunities associated with advances in artificial intelligence and to ensure that AI systems operate safely and ethically, benefiting all of humanity. IASEAI held its inaugural conference, IASEAI '25, on February 6–7, 2025 in Paris, which brought together experts from academia, civil society, industry, and government to discuss developments in AI safety and ethics. The organization is led by Stuart Russell as President and Mark Nitzberg as Interim Executive Director.

organizations.name: National Republican Senatorial Committee
organizations.notes: The National Republican Senatorial Committee (NRSC) is the Republican Hill committee for the United States Senate, working to elect Republicans to the Senate. The NRSC was founded in 1916 as the Republican Senatorial Campaign Committee and was reorganized in 1948 and renamed the National Republican Senatorial Committee. It has been chaired by Senator Tim Scott from South Carolina since 2025. The NRSC raised $88 million in 2025, which is the best off-year fundraising for the Committee since 2021.

organizations.name: Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (SAIL)
organizations.notes: One of the oldest AI research labs, founded at Stanford in 1962 by John McCarthy. Research spans NLP, computer vision, robotics, reinforcement learning, and computational biomedicine. In 2025, Carlos Guestrin became Director and SAIL formally joined forces with Stanford HAI, integrating safety and policy work alongside core capabilities research.

organizations.name: Stanford Center for AI Safety
organizations.notes: Stanford University research center founded in 2018 by Clark Barrett, David Dill, Mykel Kochenderfer, and Dorsa Sadigh, operating as an industrial affiliates program. Conducts research in formal verification, robust decision-making, and trustworthy AI; distinct from the independent nonprofit Center for AI Safety (safe.ai) run by Dan Hendrycks. Focus spans technical safety, fairness, accountability, and policy.

organizations.name: MIT Data to AI Lab
organizations.notes: MIT research group within the Laboratory for Information and Decision Systems (LIDS), founded in 2015 by Principal Research Scientist Kalyan Veeramachaneni. Develops large-scale AI and ML systems that work alongside humans in applications spanning healthcare, finance, education, and energy. Notable for the Synthetic Data Vault open-source project, spun out commercially as DataCebo.

organizations.name: DAF-MIT AI Accelerator
organizations.notes: Joint partnership between the Department of the Air Force (DAF) and MIT to advance AI research for defense and civilian applications. Established 2019; renewed for another five years in 2024. Combines military personnel (embedded officers and enlisted Airmen) with MIT faculty, researchers, and students. Has launched 20+ projects engaging 150+ researchers across MIT campus and MIT Lincoln Laboratory. Focus areas include rapid prototyping, scaling, and ethical application of AI algorithms for national security. Leverages universities, small businesses, traditional defense contractors, and non-traditional commercial ventures.

organizations.name: MIT Schwarzman College of Computing
organizations.notes: MIT's cross-disciplinary computing college founded in 2018 with a $1B endowment anchored by a $350M gift from Stephen Schwarzman. Led by founding dean Daniel Huttenlocher, it integrates AI research across all MIT departments. Hosts the MIT AI Policy Forum and publishes governance-focused policy briefs; advocates for technically-informed, sector-specific AI regulation.

organizations.name: Kempner Institute
organizations.notes: Harvard University-wide institute for the study of natural and artificial intelligence, founded 2022 with $500M gift from Mark Zuckerberg and Priscilla Chan (Chan Zuckerberg Initiative). Co-directed by Sham Kakade (CS) and Bernardo Sabatini (neurobiology). Operates the Kempner AI Cluster at MGHPCC (1,144 H100 GPUs as of March 2026, ranked 85th on TOP500 in 2024). Research spans mathematical models of intelligence, cognitive theories, neurobiological basis of intelligence, and AI applications in science. Supports undergraduate research (KURE program), postdocs, and 10 new faculty lines. Sits at the intersection of neuroscience and AI, seeking fundamental principles underlying both human and machine intelligence.

organizations.name: Massachusetts Green High Performance Computing Center
organizations.notes: Intercollegiate high-performance computing facility in Holyoke, Massachusetts, jointly operated by Boston University, Harvard, MIT, Northeastern, UMass, and Yale. LEED Platinum certified; powered by Holyoke Gas & Electric with >90% carbon-free energy (>70% local hydro and solar). Hosts hundreds of thousands of CPUs, millions of GPU cores, >50PB storage. Announced $16M expansion in Oct 2024 to build a quantum computing complex. Houses the Kempner AI Cluster for Harvard. Connected to Holyoke's municipal fiber grid and numerous campus, regional, and national networks.

organizations.name: The Princeton Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence
organizations.notes: Princeton's AI Lab supports and expands AI research across the university with a novel organizational model. Directed by Tom Griffiths; associate director Olga Russakovsky. Supports three research initiatives: Princeton Language and Intelligence (large AI models, led by Sanjeev Arora), AI for Accelerating Invention (AI^2, AI in science/engineering), and Natural and Artificial Minds (NAM, human-AI interrelationship). Shared infrastructure includes admin staff, AI Postdoctoral Fellows, research software engineers, data scientists, and a compute cluster with 300 H100 GPUs. Faculty span natural sciences, engineering, social sciences, and humanities.

organizations.name: ai@cam
organizations.notes: University of Cambridge flagship mission on AI, led by Neil Lawrence (DeepMind Professor of Machine Learning). Aims to develop AI-enabled innovation that benefits science, citizens, and society by leveraging Cambridge's interdisciplinary research community. Focuses on responsible AI development requiring expertise from research, business, policy, civil society, and affected communities. Engages in public dialogue on AI governance; research has shown public desire for AI systems with meaningful human oversight, transparency, accountability, and protection from harms.

organizations.name: Saïd Business School
organizations.notes: Business school of the University of Oxford. Offers a portfolio of AI-focused executive education programs: Oxford Artificial Intelligence Programme, Oxford Executive Diploma in AI for Business (master's-level), Oxford Leading AI Implementation Programme, and Oxford Generative and Agentic AI Programme (directed by Matthias Holweg and Mike Wooldridge). Draws on Oxford's research in multi-agent architectures, operational design, and responsible AI practice. Focused on business leaders understanding AI workings, possibilities, ethics, and implementation strategy.

organizations.name: Global AI Frontier Lab
organizations.notes: Joint U.S.-South Korea AI research center at NYU, established May 2024 via MOU between NYU and Korea's IITP. Co-directed by Yann LeCun and Kyunghyun Cho. Focuses on three pillars: fundamental AI research, trustworthy/responsible AI, and AI for healthcare. Korean government investing ~$34M (45B won) with NYU contributing ~$31M by 2028. Opened September 2024 at One Metrotech Center, Brooklyn.

organizations.name: Institute for Information & Communications Technology Planning & Evaluation
organizations.notes: South Korean government institution under the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) that manages national ICT R&D policy, funding, and human resource development. Key funder of the Global AI Frontier Lab at NYU. Restructured in 2026 around "AI Transformation (AX)" with new teams for Physical AI, AI Foundation (data), and AI Safety and Trust. Manages program areas in AI, AI semiconductors, AX convergence, and physical AI.

organizations.name: Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation
organizations.notes: Key Senate committee with primary jurisdiction over AI legislation, chaired by Ted Cruz (R-TX) since 2025. Cruz unveiled the SANDBOX Act in September 2025 and a five-pillar AI policy framework explicitly advocating a light-touch, pro-innovation regulatory approach to maintain U.S. leadership over China.

organizations.name: House Bipartisan Task Force on Artificial Intelligence
organizations.notes: Bipartisan U.S. House task force created in Feb 2024 by Speaker Johnson and Leader Jeffries, co-chaired by Reps. Obernolte and Lieu. Released a 273-page final report in Dec 2024 with 66 findings and 89 recommendations across 15 policy areas. Sunset at end of 118th Congress; no successor body established in the 119th Congress as of early 2025.

organizations.name: House Committee on Science Space & Tech
organizations.notes: U.S. House committee with primary jurisdiction over AI R&D policy, NIST AI standards, and NSF AI programs. Passed nine bipartisan AI bills in Sept 2024 including the CREATE AI Act (NAIRR), NSF AI Education Act, and Workforce for AI Trust Act. Contributed to the Bipartisan AI Task Force report (Dec 2024) with 66 findings and 89 recommendations. Held "Advancing America's AI Action Plan" hearings in 2025-26 with OSTP Director Michael Kratsios. Chaired by Jay Obernolte (R-CA) in 119th Congress.

organizations.name: Education and Workforce Committee
organizations.notes: U.S. House committee with jurisdiction over labor, education, and workforce training policy as it intersects with AI. Launched "Building an AI-Ready America" hearing series in Jan 2026 covering AI in the workplace, workplace safety, AI in education/teaching, and employer-led AI training. Examining AI's impact on hiring, gig work, and worker management. Key forum for workforce displacement and AI readiness policy.

organizations.name: International Trade Administration
organizations.notes: Bureau within U.S. Dept of Commerce responsible for trade promotion and commercial diplomacy. Leads the American AI Exports Program (per Trump July 2025 EO) to promote export of U.S. AI technology stack globally. Establishing National AI Center as gateway for U.S. AI exports. Has a dedicated AI Team in the U.S. Commercial Service. Accepting proposals from industry consortia (April-June 2026) for full-stack AI technology packages to international partners. Led by Under Secretary William Kimmitt.

organizations.name: U.S. Department of State
organizations.notes: U.S. foreign affairs agency with growing AI diplomacy role. Released first Enterprise AI Strategy FY 2024-2025 under Sec. Blinken. Key AI-relevant offices: Office of Special Envoy for Critical and Emerging Technology (S/TECH) for strategic tech competition and allied coordination; Bureau for Cyberspace and Digital Policy (CDP) for AI governance norms. Launched Partnership for Global Inclusivity on AI (PGIAI, Sept 2024) with major AI companies. Deploying internal AI tools including StateChat and agentic AI pilots. March 2026 EO by Newsom is state-level; this is federal.

organizations.name: Government Operations Agency
organizations.notes: California state agency (GovOps) overseeing CDT, Office of Data and Innovation, DGS, and CalHR. Leads GenAI implementation across CA state government per Newsom's 2023 executive order. Spearheading CalCompute, a public computing cluster for safe/ethical AI research. Coordinating AI procurement standards under March 2026 EO (N-5-26) requiring vendor trust/safety certifications. Houses the State Technology Council and AI Community of Practice for sharing AI best practices across departments.

organizations.name: California Department of General Services
organizations.notes: California state department under GovOps responsible for procurement, real estate, and administrative services. Key role in AI governance: directed by March 2026 EO (N-5-26) to develop new vendor certification requirements for AI companies seeking state contracts, requiring attestations on content safety (CSAM prevention), bias mitigation, and civil rights/liberties protections. Works with CDT to set AI procurement standards and facilitate employee access to vetted GenAI tools. Recommendations for new AI vendor certifications due within 120 days of EO.

organizations.name: California Department of Technology
organizations.notes: California's central IT agency (CDT), oversees state technology policy and services. Leads AI integration for state government under Gov. Newsom's executive orders (EO N-12-23). Launched 'Poppy,' a GenAI digital assistant for state employees built on large language models, with 20+ state departments testing it. Collaborates with Office of Data and Innovation (ODI) and CalHR on AI workforce training. Implements the state's Acceptable Use of AI policy for government agencies. California enacted 18 AI-related laws in 2024 and signed SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) in 2025. In March 2026, Newsom signed a first-of-its-kind executive order strengthening AI protections as the Trump administration rolled back federal guardrails.

organizations.name: California Department of Human Resources
organizations.notes: CalHR manages California's state workforce (civil service rules, benefits, training). Partnered with the Office of Data and Innovation (ODI) to launch the Foundations of GenAI Certificate series, a free training pathway for state employees to develop responsible GenAI skills. Part of a multi-agency AI workforce effort directed by Gov. Newsom's EO N-12-23, alongside CDT and GovOps. Also involved in creating the Chief Data and AI Officer position at Caltrans. California's Civil Rights Council approved regulations (effective Oct 2025) protecting against AI-related employment discrimination.

organizations.name: NYS Office of Information Technology Services
organizations.notes: New York State's central IT agency (ITS). Authored the state's 'Acceptable Use of Artificial Intelligence Technologies' policy (Jan 2024) under Gov. Hochul's direction, requiring leadership approval for AI systems, human oversight for automated decisions, and NIST AI RMF-based risk assessments. Appointed Eleonore Fournier-Tombs as Chief AI Officer to refine policy. Part of the Empire AI initiative. A 2025 State Comptroller audit found gaps: the policy lacks detailed implementation guidance and ITS has no inventory of AI systems across state agencies. Delivered an AI training tool for the state workforce.

organizations.name: Ohio Department of Job and Family Services
organizations.notes: Ohio DJFS administers unemployment insurance, workforce development, and public assistance programs. Won the 2025 Merrill Baumgardner Award from the National Association of State Workforce Agencies for AI innovation in unemployment services. Deployed four AI tools: a multilingual virtual assistant for unemployment claims (15 languages), intelligent document processing for classification/indexing, a call center AI bot, and a GenAI tool simplifying complex policy manuals. Part of Ohio's broader AI strategy coordinated through InnovateOhio. Also involved in the OhioMeansJobs AI-powered job matching platform.

organizations.name: Trump administration
organizations.notes: The Trump administration (second term, 2025-) has pursued an aggressive pro-innovation, deregulatory AI agenda. Day 1: revoked Biden's AI EO 14110 (EO 14148, Jan 20 2025). EO 14179 (Jan 23 2025) 'Removing Barriers to American Leadership in AI' launched an action plan for global AI dominance. OSTP released 90-point AI Action Plan (July 2025) with three pillars: Accelerating Innovation, Building AI Infrastructure, Leading International Diplomacy/Security. Dec 2025 EO 'Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI' attempted to preempt state AI regulation, threatening to withhold federal funds from states with 'conflicting' AI laws. Drew pushback from Republican governors (DeSantis, Cox). Cross-cutting priorities: protecting workers, ensuring trustworthy/unbiased AI, safeguarding against misuse. DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) pursuing separate AI-for-government-reform agenda.

organizations.name: LinkedIn
organizations.notes: Microsoft-owned professional networking platform (~1B members). Major deployer of AI in hiring and workforce tools. Launched 'Hiring Assistant' (Oct 2024), its first AI agent product, handling job description creation, candidate sourcing, and screening — live with AMD, Canva, Siemens, Zurich Insurance. AI-Assisted Candidate Discovery uses GenAI to analyze job requirements and match candidates via natural language queries. In Sept 2024, updated Terms of Service to allow member data for GenAI training (opt-out, default ON), drawing privacy concerns. Reports 60% of recruiters say AI helps find 'hidden gem' talent. Skills on the Rise 2026 report highlights AI-driven hiring shifts globally. AI features span content creation (AI-assisted posts), learning recommendations, and recruiter tools.

organizations.name: Darden School of Business
organizations.notes: University of Virginia's graduate business school. Home to the LaCross Institute for Ethical Artificial Intelligence in Business, launched Sept 2024 with a $62M gift from the LaCross family (one of the largest AI gifts to any business school globally, from a broader $101M commitment). Academic directors: Professors Yael Grushka-Cockayne and Raj Venkatesan. The institute coordinates AI research, teaching, and engagement across UVA schools. Extends Darden's AI Initiative (est. 2022) and the Collaboratory for Applied Data Science in Business (partnership with UVA School of Data Science, 2021). Offers an MBA concentration in AI and funds multidisciplinary AI research with focus on ethical AI in business contexts.

organizations.name: UVA Information Technology Services
organizations.notes: University of Virginia's central IT organization. Manages UVA's AI tools and responsible use guidelines. Provides UVA Copilot (enterprise GenAI tool operating within UVA's secure environment — data not retained or used for model training, approved for up to Sensitive data). Publishes AI guidelines including prohibition on DeepSeek AI applications on university devices/networks. Maintains the AI Tools Hub with responsible use resources. Supports the university's broader GenAI in Teaching and Learning initiative. Enforces data protection standards: highly sensitive data (PII, PHI) must never be uploaded to any AI tool.

organizations.name: College of Connected Computing
organizations.notes: Vanderbilt University's College of Connected Computing (CCC), established March 2024, is the university's first new college since 1981. Dedicated to computer science, AI, data science, cybersecurity, cyber-physical systems/IoT, and robotics. Inaugural dean Matthew Johnson-Roberson (appointed May 2025) came from directing CMU's Robotics Institute. Houses the Amplify Generative AI Innovation Center (launched Feb 2026). Undergraduate programs are scheduled to launch fall 2027; graduate programs are scheduled to begin fall 2026; a generative AI minor is in development. Aims to bridge computing with other disciplines across Vanderbilt. Hosts AI Summer programs and AI Showcase events.

organizations.name: University of North Carolina system
organizations.notes: Multi-campus public university system in North Carolina comprising 16 institutions and 240,000+ students. Pursuing systemwide AI integration: partnered with Google ($1B initiative across 100+ public universities) giving all students access to Gemini 2.5 Pro and Google Career Certificates. Launched a systemwide AI Skills Module and AI Advisory Council. UNC-Chapel Hill established Provost's AI Committee and AI Acceleration Program (Sept 2024) with five subcommittees. NC A&T won approval for the state's only standalone B.S. in Artificial Intelligence. Individual campuses running AI accelerators, graduate certificates, and classroom integration programs. Four UNC-Chapel Hill faculty/staff named to NC AI Leadership Council (Sept 2025).

organizations.name: Darktrace
organizations.notes: UK-headquartered AI cybersecurity company founded in 2013 in Cambridge. Acquired by private equity firm Thoma Bravo in October 2024 for $5.3B (previously publicly traded on London Stock Exchange FTSE100). Uses self-learning AI to detect and respond to cyber threats in real time. Published responsible AI principles for cybersecurity, informed by collaboration with academia and governments and analysis of NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, UK AI White Paper, and OECD AI Principles. Engages proactively with regulators; AI optimist supporting pro-innovation policy while emphasizing cybersecurity as a prerequisite for trustworthy AI. Engaged with UK's Cyber Security and Resilience Bill. Works with UCL as research partner.

organizations.name: Quantexa
organizations.notes: UK-based AI decision intelligence company founded in 2016, headquartered in London. Raised $175M Series F (March 2025) at $2.6B valuation, led by Teachers' Venture Growth (Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan); other investors include Warburg Pincus, Dawn Capital, BNY, British Patient Capital. Specializes in connecting and analyzing siloed data for customer intelligence, risk management, financial crime, and fraud detection. Processes data for major banks and governments. Works with UK Cabinet Office and Public Sector Fraud Authority (PSFA) on AI-powered fraud detection. CEO Vishal Marria met Chancellor Rachel Reeves at No. 11 to discuss growth/investment. Achieved 'Centaur' status with ~40% license revenue growth in 2024. Announced £200M for AI R&D and new London jobs.

organizations.name: Evolution AI
organizations.notes: UK-based AI document processing company founded in 2015 by Martin Goodson and Rafal Kwasny, headquartered in London (Covent Garden). Combines computer vision and NLP to extract data from financial statements, invoices, bank statements, and other documents. Received the UK Government's largest-ever grant for commercial AI R&D in 2017, with further substantial government grants in 2019 and 2020. Processes 1.5M+ pages per day. Clients include NatWest, Deutsche Bank, Dun & Bradstreet. Won Credit Strategy 'Best Technology Partner' (2023) and AI Breakthrough Awards 'Best Machine Learning Company' (2024). Research partner with UCL.

organizations.name: Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council
organizations.notes: The Engineering and Physical Sciences Research Council (EPSRC) is the UK's main public funder for engineering and physical sciences research, operating as part of UK Research and Innovation (UKRI). Major AI funding programs: Fundamental AI Research Lab (up to £40M over 5 years for novel AI research not pursued by commercial labs); AI Hubs for Science, Engineering and Real Data (£60M+ for up to nine research hubs); Turing AI Fellowships (£46M to attract/retain world-leading AI talent, delivered with Office for AI and Alan Turing Institute); Trustworthy Autonomous Systems programme (£33.7M); AI for Net Zero initiative. Also funds standard grants, programme grants, and fellowships for AI/robotics research. Funds UCL and other leading UK universities.

organizations.name: U.S. National Science Foundation
organizations.notes: Federal agency funding fundamental AI research and workforce development. Leads the NAIRR pilot connecting 600+ research teams to national AI infrastructure and invested $100M in National AI Research Institutes in 2025. Mission reoriented by Trump administration toward AI and quantum as top priorities.

organizations.name: Dartmouth College
organizations.notes: Private Ivy League research university in Hanover, NH. Birthplace of artificial intelligence — hosted the 1956 Dartmouth Summer Research Project where the term 'artificial intelligence' was coined. Expanding AI across curriculum: new AI track in Master of Engineering, undergraduate AI concentration in B.E., and multiple AI electives in Tuck MBA (AI-Driven Analytics, AI for Managers, NLP/ML in Finance, AI & Ethics, AI for the C-Suite). Partnered with Anthropic and AWS (Dec 2025) for AI-enhanced career coaching, student access to Claude, and innovation programs. Launched generative AI teaching grants through DCAL/LDI. Developing Evergreen, a $5M AI-powered student wellness chatbot with 130 undergraduate researchers. Launched new AI courses March 2026.

organizations.name: Baylor College of Medicine
organizations.notes: Baylor College of Medicine is a leading academic medical center in Houston, TX. In AI policy, BCM focuses on ethical and responsible AI in healthcare through its Center for Medical Ethics and Health Policy. Co-leads the NEH-funded Center for Humanities-based Health AI Innovation (CHHAIN) with Rice University ($500K grant), exploring how humanities-based insights can shape responsible health AI. Hosted an AI and Health Equity Symposium with Rice. Conducts stakeholder focus group research on ethical dilemmas of AI integration in clinical settings. Emphasis is on patient safety, health equity, and ethical deployment rather than broad AI regulation.

organizations.name: City University of New York
organizations.notes: CUNY is the largest urban public university system in the U.S. (25 campuses). Awarded $3M via AI Innovation Fund (part of Governor Hochul's NY AI initiative) to 113 campus-led AI projects. Strategy spans five pillars: Education, Workforce, Operations, Research & Innovation, and Partnerships. Received $1M Google.org grant to advance AI literacy frameworks. Developing school-level AI Policy Frameworks for ethical, equitable AI use. Emphasis is on AI literacy, workforce readiness, and equitable access rather than external regulatory positions. Launched AI Academic Hub and new three-year AI curriculum initiative.

organizations.name: IBM
organizations.notes: IBM is a global enterprise technology company offering AI via its watsonx platform. It advocates 'precision regulation'—risk-based, use-case-specific rules rather than broad AI restrictions. IBM reported $12.5B in generative AI bookings as of early 2026 and positions AI governance software as a primary growth driver.

organizations.name: Allen Institute for AI
organizations.notes: Allen Institute for AI (Ai2) is a Seattle-based nonprofit AI research lab founded by Paul Allen in 2014, known for its fully open-source OLMo language models. Ai2 advocates for open AI research and transparency, and shapes policy on open-source AI, copyright, and AI safety. CEO Ali Farhadi stepped down in early 2026.

organizations.name: Social Media Victims Law Center
organizations.notes: The Social Media Victims Law Center (SMVLC) is a legal advocacy organization supporting families harmed by predatory tech. Originally focused on social media harms to children, it has expanded into AI litigation. In November 2025, SMVLC and the Tech Justice Law Project filed seven lawsuits against OpenAI alleging wrongful death, assisted suicide, and product liability related to ChatGPT's GPT-4o model. The suits claim OpenAI knowingly released a dangerously sycophantic product that fostered psychological dependency and contributed to suicides. SMVLC has also filed lawsuits against Character.AI for predatory chatbot technology aimed at children.

organizations.name: Tech Justice Law Project
organizations.notes: The Tech Justice Law Project (TJLP) is a pioneering impact litigation and advocacy organization established in 2023 as a project of Campaign for Accountability. It works with legal experts, policy advocates, digital rights organizations, and technologists to ensure legal and policy frameworks are fit for the digital age. TJLP has been particularly active in AI litigation, co-filing seven lawsuits with the Social Media Victims Law Center against OpenAI in November 2025 alleging ChatGPT caused wrongful deaths through emotional manipulation and sycophantic design. Policy Counsel Melodi Dinçer focuses on AI policy and biometric surveillance.

organizations.name: Polytechnique Montréal
organizations.notes: French-language engineering school in Montreal and a founding partner of both IVADO and Mila — Quebec's premier AI research institutes. Its researchers work on AI applications in health, transportation, and cybersecurity, supported by $20M+ in grants from CFI and the Quebec government.

organizations.name: HEC Montréal
organizations.notes: Montreal business school and founding partner of IVADO and Mila. Established a Chair in Organizational Ethics and AI Governance in 2024-25 and, with Tech3Lab and IVADO, launched a government-funded AI professional training program in January 2026. Faculty contribute to Mila and AI governance research.

organizations.name: Inovia Capital
organizations.notes: Inovia Capital is a leading Canadian multi-stage venture capital firm managing over US$2.2B with offices in Montreal, Toronto, Calgary, San Francisco, and London. The firm actively invests in AI-native companies, with particular focus on generative AI, security, digital health, and commerce. In partnership with Mila, Inovia launched the Venture Scientist Fund, a ~$100M early-stage VC fund designed to transform frontier AI research from Canadian academic institutions into AI-native companies, targeting ~55+ investments. The firm has made 373+ investments spanning pre-seed to pre-IPO.

organizations.name: Khan Academy
organizations.notes: Khan Academy is a major nonprofit educational technology organization that has become a prominent AI deployer through Khanmigo, its AI-powered tutoring assistant built on OpenAI's GPT-4. Launched by Sal Khan, Khanmigo reached 1.4 million users by 2025, though Khan has acknowledged that for many students it was a "non-event." The tool uses Socratic design principles — guiding students rather than giving answers. Khan Academy partnered with Microsoft for free educator access via Azure OpenAI. The organization maintains strict data privacy: student data is not sold and OpenAI cannot train on it. Khan Academy is also a Gates Foundation partner.

organizations.name: Ballmer Group
organizations.notes: The Ballmer Group is a philanthropy founded in 2015 by Steve and Connie Ballmer (Steve is the former CEO of Microsoft). In July 2025, the Ballmer Group co-launched NextLadder Ventures, a $1 billion, 15-year initiative alongside the Gates Foundation, Stand Together, and Valhalla Foundation to develop AI tools for frontline public-sector workers — including public defenders, parole officers, and social workers — to expand economic opportunity for low-income Americans. NextLadder Ventures specifically employs AI tools developed by Anthropic. The Ballmer Group also integrates AI into its own internal operations.

organizations.name: Formation Bio
organizations.notes: Formation Bio is an AI-driven pharmaceutical development company that acquires clinical-stage drugs and accelerates their development using a proprietary tech platform. Its platform integrates AI-driven drug development, a clinical trial engine, and a universal data platform to automate medical writing, protocol development, regulatory submissions, and biostatistics. In November 2024, Formation launched Muse in partnership with OpenAI and Sanofi for AI-powered drug asset identification. The company raised $372M in Series D funding and was named to the 2025 CNBC Disruptor 50 list. Funded by Emerson Collective among others.

organizations.name: Perplexity AI
organizations.notes: Perplexity AI is a VC-backed AI-powered search and answer engine founded in 2022, valued at ~$20B as of late 2025. CEO Aravind Srinivas has resisted content restrictions and embraced government partnerships, including a FedRAMP-approved deal with the U.S. GSA.

organizations.name: Craig Newmark Philanthropies
organizations.notes: Philanthropy founded by Craigslist creator Craig Newmark, focused on cybersecurity, trustworthy journalism, voter protection, and tech safety. Committed $100M+ to a Cyber Civil Defense network. Major funder of cybersecurity infrastructure including $3.2M to IST's UnDisruptable27 initiative protecting critical infrastructure from nation-state adversaries. Also funds UC Berkeley Public Interest Cybersecurity Program (extended through 2028), Global Cyber Alliance, and community cybersecurity clinics. Promotes 'Take 9' cyber-hygiene awareness campaign.

organizations.name: Alphabet Inc.
organizations.notes: Parent company of Google, Google DeepMind, and other subsidiaries. One of the world's largest AI developers and deployers. Published AI Principles in 2018; controversially removed pledges against AI use in weapons and surveillance in February 2025, drawing criticism from lawmakers and civil society. Supports 'multi-stakeholder' AI governance: risk assessments for high-risk systems, sector-specific regulation, but lobbied for 10-year ban on state-level AI regulation in U.S. House package. Publishes annual Responsible AI Progress Reports. Google DeepMind leadership (Hassabis, Manyika) stated democracies should lead AI development guided by freedom, equality, and human rights.

organizations.name: People + AI Research
organizations.notes: Google's People + AI Research (PAIR) team, a multidisciplinary research group exploring the human side of AI. Launched in 2017 within Google Research; now part of the Responsible AI and Human-Centered Technology team. Conducts research on explainability, interpretability, fairness, data visualization, and human-centered AI. Publishes the PAIR Guidebook (design guidelines for AI interfaces) and develops open-source tools like the Learning Interpretability Tool (LIT). Produces educational materials (Explorables) to help practitioners understand ML behaviors. Focuses on making AI systems more understandable and useful to people.

organizations.name: NYU School of Medicine
organizations.notes: NYU Grossman School of Medicine at NYU Langone Health, a leading academic medical center. Notable for the fastMRI project—a collaboration with Facebook AI Research (FAIR) using deep learning to accelerate MRI reconstruction 10x. Also developed NYUTron, an LLM trained on clinical notes for in-hospital risk prediction.

organizations.name: In-Q-Tel
organizations.notes: In-Q-Tel (IQT) is an independent nonprofit venture capital firm that invests in technology companies to equip the CIA and other intelligence agencies with cutting-edge capabilities. Founded in 1999, based in Tysons, Virginia. Reached its 800th investment milestone in April 2025. Total estimated investment exceeds $1.8B over 25+ years. Key AI/national-security portfolio companies include Palantir (early investor). IQT appears in 32 companies on the 2025 NatSec 100 Report — more than any other fund. Invests across AI, biotech, microchips, space systems, cybersecurity, and autonomous systems. Also works with DHS on AI, data analytics, and trusted infrastructure.

organizations.name: Cognizant
organizations.notes: Cognizant is a major IT services and consulting company (NYSE: CTSH) with a significant AI practice. Positions itself as an "AI builder" creating full-stack AI solutions for enterprise clients. In March 2026, launched Cognizant AI Factory — an enterprise-grade multi-tenant platform powered by Dell and NVIDIA, claiming 50-60% lower TCO and 30% faster AI processing. Acquired 3Cloud (leading Microsoft Azure services provider) in November 2025 to strengthen AI enablement on Azure. Published "New Work, New World 2026" report on AI reshaping work. Recognized as a leader in Everest Group's AI and Generative AI Services PEAK Matrix 2025.

organizations.name: U.S. Army
organizations.notes: The U.S. Army is actively integrating AI across operations as part of DoD's broader AI-first strategy. In December 2025, established a new 49B AI/ML Officer career field — the first dedicated AI career path for Army officers, with initial selections beginning January 2026. Defense Secretary Hegseth directed AI-driven command and control at theater/corps/division HQs by 2027, unmanned systems across every division by end 2026, and counter-UAS at platoon level by 2026. The Army is deploying AI-enabled "edge" tools for units operating on imperfect networks. Army intelligence is weaving AI into every layer of operations for faster, smarter decision-making.

organizations.name: Workera
organizations.notes: Workera is an AI-powered skills intelligence platform that verifies skills for any role, enabling enterprise workforce development and talent decisions. Founded by Kian Katanforoosh, who was mentored by Andrew Ng at Stanford. Andrew Ng provides strategic guidance on responsible AI in education and workforce development. Raised $44.5M over 4 rounds. Clients include U.S. Air Force, Marine Corps, Booz Allen Hamilton, Siemens Energy, BCG, Accenture, and Eli Lilly. Launched AI agent "Sage" in late 2024 (GA March 2025). Published 2026 AI Workforce Preview showing 76% of Americans plan to learn new AI skills in 2026. Connected to Andrew Ng's DeepLearning.AI ecosystem.

organizations.name: AI Fund
organizations.notes: AI Fund is Andrew Ng's venture studio, launched in 2018, that co-founds AI startups from scratch. Closed an oversubscribed $190M Fund II in 2025 (following $175M Fund I). Has co-founded ~35 portfolio companies including LandingAI and Workera. Corporate LPs include AES Corporation, HP Inc., Mitsui, Mitsubishi, QBE, and TELUS; VC investors include Sequoia Capital and NEA. AI Fund actively participates in strategy, coding, and customer discovery alongside CEOs. Focus areas: application and software infrastructure layers of the AI stack, leveraging foundation models across financial services, renewable energy, healthcare, education, logistics, and developer tools.

organizations.name: LandingAI
organizations.notes: LandingAI is an AI company founded by Andrew Ng focused on computer vision for industrial applications. Flagship product LandingLens is an end-to-end, low-code platform for labeling, training, and deploying custom vision models. Primary industries: manufacturing, automotive, electronics, medical devices — companies relying on visual inspection and quality control. In 2025 released VisionAgent, a generative visual AI application builder with agentic object detection that eliminates manual data labeling and model training. Ng serves as Executive Chairman. Ng is a prominent advocate of "data-centric AI" and "small data" approaches. Part of Andrew Ng's ecosystem alongside AI Fund, DeepLearning.AI, and Workera.

organizations.name: MGX investment group
organizations.notes: Abu Dhabi state-owned AI investment vehicle launched March 2024 by Mubadala and G42, targeting $100B+ in AUM. Key partnerships: Stargate Project (with OpenAI, SoftBank, Oracle, Jan 2025); Global AI Infrastructure Investment Partnership (with BlackRock, Microsoft, Sep 2024). Acquired Aligned Data Centers for $40B (Oct 2025). Exploring $50B third-party capital raise. Also invested in Anthropic Series G (Feb 2026) and made $2B minority investment in Binance (Mar 2025).

organizations.name: Macquarie Asset Management
organizations.notes: Global alternative asset manager (part of Macquarie Group). Major AI infrastructure investor: committed $17B+ to data center and HPC investments including Applied Digital ($900M+ with $4.1B pipeline) and Aligned Data Centers (sold at $40B enterprise value to BlackRock/MGX consortium, Oct 2025). Closed Macquarie Infrastructure Partners VI with $8B+ in commitments (Jun 2025). Views AI as key driver of infrastructure demand in 2026 outlook.

organizations.name: AMD
organizations.notes: Major semiconductor company competing with NVIDIA in AI accelerators. CEO Lisa Su targets 35% annual revenue growth driven by AI chip demand. Product roadmap: MI308, MI450 (Q3 2026), MI500 (2027). Key partnerships: OpenAI (multi-year, multi-billion chip deal), Meta (6GW deal). Took $800M charge from U.S. export controls on MI308 to China (Apr 2025). Su advocates balanced export policy — supports national security restrictions but warns overly strict controls hurt U.S. competitiveness.

organizations.name: University of Pittsburgh
organizations.notes: Major public research university in Pittsburgh. Close collaboration with Carnegie Mellon University on AI initiatives. Joint NVIDIA AI Tech Community with CMU: Pitt's center focuses on AI in health sciences, clinical medicine, and biomanufacturing. Co-hosted CMU-Pitt health/AI/technology summit (Oct 2025). Joined multi-institutional Sloan Foundation study on AI impact on labor markets (Jul 2025). Research strengths in health AI and computational medicine.

organizations.name: Yale Center for Research Computing
organizations.notes: Yale University's central HPC and research computing facility (YCRC). Part of $150M AI investment initiative. Transitioning from Yale West Campus Data Center to MGHPCC (consortium with Harvard, MIT, BU, Northeastern, UMass). New Bouchet cluster in production (~4,000 cores), expanding with 80 NVIDIA H200 GPUs (140GB VRAM each), 48 A5000 ADA cards, and 6,000 additional CPUs. Grace and McCleary clusters decommissioning in 2026. Launched Hopper secure HPC cluster (Jul 2025). All new purchases including Provost's AI Initiative GPUs installed at MGHPCC.

organizations.name: International Telecommunication Union
organizations.notes: UN specialized agency for information and communication technologies. Hosts annual AI for Good Global Summit. Co-organized 2025 International AI Standards Summit with ISO and IEC, issuing the Seoul Statement on AI standards. Published 170-page report on global AI standardization landscape (2025). Develops technical standards for AI interoperability, safety, and trustworthiness. Leads initiatives on agentic AI protocols and AI in health/agriculture.

organizations.name: UNESCO
organizations.notes: UN agency that adopted the first global standard on AI ethics -- the Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2021), applicable to all 194 member states. Developed after 3 years of consultation with experts from 155 countries. Framework centers on human rights, transparency, fairness, and human oversight. Published 2025 AI ethics toolkit with open-source NLP kits for emerging markets. Released guidelines for AI use in courts and tribunals (Dec 2025).

organizations.name: European Artificial Intelligence Board
organizations.notes: Advisory body established under the EU AI Act, composed of one representative from each EU Member State. Advises and assists the European Commission and Member States to facilitate consistent and effective application of the AI Act. The AI Act's phased implementation began Feb 2025 (prohibited practices), continued Aug 2025 (GPAI model obligations), with full applicability by Aug 2026. The Board operates alongside the European AI Office which supervises and enforces the Act.

organizations.name: ARPA-H
organizations.notes: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health, established 2022 under the Biden administration within HHS. Modeled on DARPA. Funds high-risk, high-reward health research including multiple AI programs: ADVOCATE (agentic AI for cardiovascular care), PRECISE-AI (monitoring clinical AI model degradation), TARGET (generative AI for antibiotic discovery), MOCS (AI for obstetric care), and Biomedical Data Fabric toolbox. Co-sponsors the AIxCC with DARPA for health cybersecurity.

organizations.name: Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory
organizations.notes: Largest university-affiliated research center in the U.S., serving as a technical resource for DoD, Intelligence Community, DHS, and NASA. Over 60 years of AI R&D spanning autonomous systems, decision support, ML, and human-machine teaming. Intelligent Systems Center (ISC) has 130+ research scientists/engineers focused on AI, robotics, and neuroscience. Developed GenWar Lab (AI wargaming) and SAGE (Strategic AI Gaming Engine) now being built for classified DoD/IC networks. Focus on robust, trustworthy AI for national security.

organizations.name: Speed School of Engineering
organizations.notes: J.B. Speed School of Engineering at the University of Louisville. R1 research university engineering school with AI as a core research area. Houses Data Science & AI research cluster, Center for Human Systems Engineering (human-centric AI), and Autonomous Intelligent Mobile Systems Lab. Offers AI/ML coursework across bioengineering, computer science, and industrial engineering.

organizations.name: USC Center for AI in Society
organizations.notes: Joint research center between USC Suzanne Dworak-Peck School of Social Work and USC Viterbi School of Engineering. Mission: develop and demonstrate how AI can tackle difficult societal problems. Focuses on low-resource communities. Research areas: environment, health/wellbeing, homelessness. Partners with Partnership on AI on explainable AI research. Draws from Grand Challenges for Social Work, NAE Grand Challenges, and UN SDGs.

organizations.name: USC Suzanne Dworak-Peck School of Social Work
organizations.notes: USC's school of social work. Co-founded the Center for AI in Society (CAIS) with Viterbi School of Engineering, applying AI to social challenges including child welfare, homelessness, behavioral health, and human trafficking. Research emphasizes responsible AI for vulnerable populations.

organizations.name: USC Viterbi School of Engineering
organizations.notes: USC's engineering school. Major AI research hub with multiple centers: CAIS (AI for social good), Amazon Center on Secure and Trusted AI, Machine Learning Center, Center for Autonomy and AI, and Robotics and Autonomous Systems Center. Launched first US MS in CS-AI track. Partnered with USC School of Advanced Computing on BS in AI (Fall 2026). NSF-funded ORAI PhD program in AI and operations research.

organizations.name: USC Marshall School of Business
organizations.notes: USC's business school. Houses the Neely Center for Ethical Leadership and Decision Making, which in 2024 launched a five-year, $4M purpose-driven AI research initiative — $2M from the Knight Foundation matched by $2M from USC Marshall. Initiative focuses on AI and social media indices, design codes for AI tools, a youth advisory panel, and expanded leadership fellowships. Led by Neely Center Director Nate Fast and Managing Director Ravi Iyer. Dean Geoff Garrett chairs USC President's AI Strategy Committee.

organizations.name: USC School of Advanced Computing
organizations.notes: USC's 23rd school, launched in 2024 as part of President Folt's $1B+ advanced computing initiative. Led by Prof. Gaurav Sukhatme. Recruiting 30 faculty by 2025, 60 more by 2030. Co-launching BS in AI with Viterbi (Fall 2026 cohort). New Ginsburg Human-Centered Computation Hall. Ethics at its core mission. First new USC school in over a decade.

organizations.name: University of Pennsylvania
organizations.notes: Ivy League research university with major AI research infrastructure. The ASSET Center (Penn Engineering) focuses on AI safety, explainability, and trustworthiness, with NSF funding. The Warren Center for Network and Data Sciences drives interdisciplinary data science research. Penn AI was launched in 2025 to coordinate responsible AI across the university.

organizations.name: Jurist Center for Artificial Intelligence
organizations.notes: AI research center at Tulane University, established by the Harold L. and Heather E. Jurist NC '64 Endowed Fund. Supports research and education in AI, machine learning, and data science with a focus on applications for a healthier, more connected global community. Faculty projects span machine learning, multi-agent systems, computer vision, NLP, visualization, and ethics. Part of Tulane's broader AI ecosystem alongside the Connolly Alexander Institute for Data Science and CEAI.

organizations.name: Center for Community-Engaged Artificial Intelligence
organizations.notes: Multi-disciplinary AI center at Tulane University and one of five Tulane Centers of Excellence. Team of scientists, engineers, students, and community partners dedicated to human-centered AI. Core principle: trust must be earned by designing AI systems that are demonstrably fair, transparent, and accountable, with meaningful community engagement from design through deployment. Cross-disciplinary research, project-based learning, and community outreach across healthcare, economics, education, and criminal justice. Partners with Court Watch NOLA on criminal justice transparency via $1.5M NSF-funded project.

organizations.name: Court Watch NOLA
organizations.notes: Founded 2007, oldest court-watching program in the U.S. Recruits, trains, and supports volunteers to observe Orleans Parish Criminal Court judges, prosecutors, and public defenders for transparency, ethics, and constitutional compliance. Partnered with Tulane University on $1.5M NSF-funded project using AI to build a data analysis platform for criminal justice transparency -- AI extracts info from court documents, identifies trends and inequities, and directs court watchers to high-priority cases. Dashboard tracking bail settings launched Dec 2024; full AI platform expected early 2026.

organizations.name: Office of Management and Budget
organizations.notes: Executive Office of the President body that sets federal AI policy for agencies. Biden-era M-24-10 was rescinded and replaced by M-25-21 (April 2025), which mandates Chief AI Officers, AI governance boards, and risk management for high-impact AI while emphasizing innovation over restriction.

organizations.name: NASA Ames Research Center
organizations.notes: Major NASA research center in Silicon Valley. Intelligent Systems Division comprises Autonomous Systems & Robotics, Collaborative & Assistant Systems, Discovery & Systems Health, and Robust Software Engineering. Conducts mission-driven R&D in computational sciences for NASA applications. Aviation Systems Division researches advanced air traffic management and automation. Key AI projects: Data and Reasoning Fabric (aerospace decision-making), RAM-AO Working Group (autonomous multi-aircraft operations). Peter Norvig served as Division Chief of Computational Sciences. NASA restricts use of generative AI on sensitive data.

organizations.name: CTBT organization
organizations.notes: The Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty Organization (CTBTO), headquartered in Vienna, is the international body overseeing the global ban on nuclear test explosions. Operates the International Monitoring System (IMS) — 337 facilities worldwide using seismic, infrasound, hydroacoustic, and radionuclide sensors. Actively incorporates AI/ML to distinguish earthquakes from nuclear explosive tests and to process vast sensor datasets. Connected to Bayesian Logic Inc. through use of the NET-VISA probabilistic model for nuclear explosion detection. Approximately 300 staff from over 90 countries.

organizations.name: Stanford Law School
organizations.notes: Home of CodeX (Stanford Center for Legal Informatics) and the Stanford Law AI Initiative co-directed by Prof. Nathaniel Persily. Hosts AI governance courses, the Law Policy Lab practicum on federal AI use in criminal justice, and applied research projects such as ComplianceTwin (2025–2026).

organizations.name: Idaho National Laboratory
organizations.notes: U.S. Department of Energy national laboratory leading the adaptation of AI for the nuclear energy industry. Developing AI tools to reduce costs and timeframes of designing, licensing, building, and operating nuclear facilities. Major 2025 partnerships: AWS (AI tools for nuclear projects, digital twin of small modular reactor), Microsoft (Azure AI for nuclear permitting/licensing), NVIDIA (AI for reactor design and operations). DOE selected INL as a site for AI data centers under Trump administration initiative for American AI leadership. Part of DOE Project Genesis uniting all 17 national labs to build an AI-powered scientific platform. Testing AI to streamline nuclear permitting processes.

organizations.name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
organizations.notes: NIST develops voluntary AI standards and risk management frameworks for federal agencies and industry. Its AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) is widely adopted. In 2025-26 its AI Safety Institute was renamed the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) under the Trump administration's AI Action Plan, with a mandate to revise the AI RMF.

organizations.name: Clarifai
organizations.notes: Enterprise AI platform founded in 2013 by Matt Zeiler, headquartered in Washington, D.C. One of the first deep learning platforms, specializing in computer vision, NLP, and audio analysis. Core technology based on convolutional neural networks. Key government/defense work: participated in Project Maven (DoD AI program), signed R&D agreement with U.S. Army for EO/IR data labeling, created subsidiary Neural Net One for dedicated government contracting. Raised $100M total ($60M Series C in 2021, led by New Enterprise Associates). Named a Leader in Forrester Wave Computer Vision Tools Q1 2024. Platform supports full AI lifecycle: data preparation, model training, deployment.

organizations.name: College of Computing, Data Science, and Society
organizations.notes: UC Berkeley college established July 1, 2023 — the first new college at Berkeley in over 50 years. Houses three academic majors: Computer Science, Data Science, and Statistics. Includes the Berkeley Institute for Data Science, Center for Computational Biology, Bakar Institute of Digital Materials for the Planet, and shares EECS with the College of Engineering and the D-Lab with Social Sciences. Parent organization of BAIR-affiliated units. Mission: develop ethics-oriented, accessible curricula in data science, computing, and statistics. The Gateway collaboration hub expected to open spring 2026 as CDSS home.

organizations.name: University of Utah
organizations.notes: University of Utah hosts the One-U Responsible AI Initiative, a $100M transdisciplinary program led by the Scientific Computing and Imaging (SCI) Institute. A $50M AI supercomputer partnership with NVIDIA and HPE is slated to come online in summer 2026. Research focuses on healthcare, societal wellness, and public services.

organizations.name: Emory University
organizations.notes: Private research university in Atlanta, Georgia. Major AI initiative: AI.Humanity, which brings together full university resources to shape the AI revolution for better human health, economic value, and responsible applications. Key AI centers: Center for Artificial Intelligence Learning (AI literacy across campus), Empathetic AI for Health Institute (AI.Health — ML and big data for healthcare transformation), and multiple departmental AI labs. Research spans NLP, computer vision, geospatial AI, health informatics, and AI ethics. Interdisciplinary approach connects AI research with humanities, social sciences, and public health.

organizations.name: Emory AI Group
organizations.notes: Research collective within Emory University encompassing multiple AI-focused labs and faculty across the Department of Computer Science and School of Medicine. Includes the Emory NLP group, AI and Simulation Lab (geospatial AI, computer vision, foundation models), MAIX Lab (health AI), and Cardiothoracic Imaging and AI Lab. Faculty research spans natural language processing, large language models, conversational AI, information retrieval, AI interpretability, and medical imaging AI. Part of the broader AI.Humanity initiative at Emory.

organizations.name: Block Center for Technology and Society
organizations.notes: The Block Center for Technology and Society at Carnegie Mellon University was established in 2019 to study how emerging technologies alter the future of work, how AI and analytics can be harnessed for social good, and how innovation can reduce inequality. Houses the Responsible AI Initiative (RAI), which translates AI research into actionable policy insights promoting fairness, accountability, and transparency. Runs community education programs like 'AI 101' for labor unions, media professionals, and government representatives. Collaborates with MIT FutureTech on labor implications of AI research. Engages directly with policymakers in Washington, D.C., sharing AI expertise. Research focuses on future of work, responsible AI deployment, and data-driven policy solutions.

organizations.name: Amii
organizations.notes: The Alberta Machine Intelligence Institute (Amii), one of Canada's three national AI institutes alongside Mila and Vector Institute, funded through the Pan-Canadian AI Strategy. Founded over 20 years ago, headquartered in Edmonton. Mandate to build technical/scientific capacity in machine learning and translate it into commercial applications across Alberta's economic sectors. Key partner in the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI) alongside CIFAR, NRC, Mila, and Vector. Received $5M Google.org grant to establish a national AI literacy consortium across 25 post-secondary institutions reaching 125,000 students. Also received $9M federal grant to train ~5,000 mid-career energy sector workers in AI skills. Strong focus on AI trust and safety, workforce readiness, and responsible AI deployment.

organizations.name: ServiceNow
organizations.notes: Public enterprise software company (NYSE: NOW) specializing in workflow automation. Heavily invested in AI via 'Now Assist' and autonomous AI agents; targeting $1B AI ACV by 2026. Advocates responsible AI governance and compliance with EU AI Act and NIST AI RMF.

organizations.name: Government of Canada
organizations.notes: Canada was the first country globally to adopt a national AI strategy, launching the Pan-Canadian AI Strategy in 2017. Phase 2 announced in 2022 with $443M+ in funding, supporting the three national AI institutes (Mila, Amii, Vector). Tabled the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) in June 2022 as part of Bill C-27, one of the first proposed AI-specific laws globally. AIDA focuses on responsible design, development, and use of AI in the private sector, with emphasis on health, safety, and human rights impacts. Requires businesses to implement governance mechanisms, assess AI risks, and provide transparency to users. Established the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI). Also investing in AI workforce development, compute infrastructure, and commercialization support across provinces.

organizations.name: Data Collective
organizations.notes: DCVC (formerly Data Collective) is a deep tech venture capital firm founded by Matt Ocko and Zachary Bogue, investing in entrepreneurs using computational approaches—especially AI—to solve large-scale real-world problems. Manages billions in AUM across 13 funds including six flagship funds. Invests at seed, Series A, and growth stages across AI, exascale computing, climate, robotics, materials science, biology, defense, and security. Backed by a network of 50+ technology executives and experts (CTOs, chief scientists, professors at Stanford and Berkeley). Was a lead investor in Element AI. Focus on AI-enabled solutions for industries that haven't seen material progress in decades.

organizations.name: Real Ventures
organizations.notes: Canada's leading early-stage venture capital firm, established in 2007 and headquartered in Montreal. Manages $325M across five funds with an active portfolio of 100+ companies valued at ~$10B. Has invested in 214 companies total, with portfolio unicorns including Xanadu, Blockstream, and Tenstorrent. Deeply embedded in Montreal's AI and deep tech ecosystem. Was an early investor/backer of Element AI. Focuses on nurturing entrepreneurial communities and supporting founders from the earliest stages. Portfolio spans AI, healthcare, fintech, and other technology verticals. Sonder (portfolio company) went public on NASDAQ in 2021.

organizations.name: Amnesty International
organizations.notes: Major international human rights organization with strong positions on AI governance and digital rights. Calls for outright ban on facial recognition technology for mass surveillance by police and state agencies, citing racial discrimination (higher false positive rates for women and people of color) and chilling effects on peaceful assembly. Runs the 'Ban the Scan' campaign documenting FRT harms in New York, Israel/OPT, and Hyderabad. Advocates for legally binding AI regulation centered on documented harms, not voluntary industry commitments. Criticized the EU AI Act as insufficient to protect marginalized communities. Launched an Algorithmic Accountability Toolkit (2025) for investigators and rights defenders. Calls for meaningful involvement of impacted communities in AI regulatory decisions and warns against regulatory gaps that allow export of banned AI systems to other countries.

organizations.name: Singapore Management University
organizations.notes: Singapore Management University (SMU) hosts the Centre for AI and Data Governance, established in 2019 at its School of Law. Received a S$4.5M grant from the National Research Foundation and Infocomm Media Development Authority for a five-year Research Programme on the Governance of AI and Data Use, structured around three streams: AI and Society, AI and Industry, and AI and Commercialisation. Publishes research on Singapore's evolving AI governance framework through SSRN. The Centre establishes expert panels drawing from academia, technology industry, and legal professions, with international institutional partnerships. Also offers AI-led business strategy programs. Contributing to Singapore's national approach to responsible AI and data governance.

organizations.name: Apple
organizations.notes: Consumer technology company deploying AI via Apple Intelligence across its device ecosystem. Pursues an on-device, privacy-first AI strategy using Private Cloud Compute for sensitive tasks. Partnered with OpenAI (ChatGPT integration) and Google (Gemini for Siri). Avoids public regulatory advocacy but leverages privacy standards as a competitive differentiator.

organizations.name: McGill Centre for Intelligent Machines
organizations.notes: Established in 1985 as an inter-departmental, inter-faculty research center at McGill University spanning Electrical and Computer Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, and Computer Science. One of the longest-running AI research centers in the world. Comprises ~20 professors and 150 graduate students. Research organized around five themes: Computer Vision, Robotics, Systems and Control, Human-Computer Interaction, and Graphics. Half of CIM professors engaged in AI research including robotic vision and medical image analysis. Notable projects include the Aqua amphibious robot for environmental monitoring and the SmartWheeler assistive technology. Draws researchers from around the world to McGill.

organizations.name: McGill Collaborative for AI and Society
organizations.notes: The McGill Collaborative for AI and Society (McCAIS) is a strategic initiative of the Computational and Data Systems Institute (CDSI) at McGill University. Interdisciplinary, university-wide effort bringing all faculties together to understand AI's societal impact and develop evidence-based best practices for responsible AI use. Work organized around four pillars: Thought Leadership, Research to Action, Experiential Learning Opportunities, and Lifelong Learning. Partners with IVADO (Quebec AI research consortium) on courses in algorithmic fairness, privacy in AI, creativity in AI, and AI in health sciences. Runs the BMO Responsible AI Awards Program. Draws expertise from Philosophy, Engineering, Computer Science, and the Laidley Centre for Business Ethics and Equity. Co-directed by Prof. Samira A. Rahimi.

organizations.name: McGill AI Lab
organizations.notes: Umbrella term for McGill University's AI research labs, most prominently the Reasoning and Learning Lab co-directed by Prakash Panangaden, Doina Precup, Joelle Pineau, Jackie Chi Kit Cheung, Siva Reddy, Reihaneh Rabbany, Siamak Ravanbakhsh, and David Rolnick. McGill is a world leader in machine learning with particular strengths in reinforcement learning and natural language processing. Connected to Mila (Quebec AI Institute) through shared faculty. The iSMART Lab (Prof. Narges Armanfard) focuses on computer vision, time series analysis, and physics-informed modeling. Also participates in the International Laboratory on Learning Systems (ILLS) with ETS, CNRS, Universite Paris-Saclay, and CentraleSupelec. Closely linked to CIM (Centre for Intelligent Machines) and McCAIS within McGill's AI ecosystem.

organizations.name: OECD
organizations.notes: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (38 member countries). Published OECD AI Principles in 2019 (first intergovernmental AI standard), updated 2024 — now endorsed by 47 jurisdictions. Operates AI Policy Observatory (oecd.ai) tracking AI strategies, policies, and incidents globally. Integrated GPAI partnership under OECD umbrella (44 countries). Partners with UN Office of Tech Envoy. Published common AI incident reporting framework (Feb 2025). Member-funded intergovernmental org.

organizations.name: Public Investment Fund
organizations.notes: Saudi Arabia sovereign wealth fund (~$930B AUM). Launched Humain (May 2025) as full-stack AI company — data centers, cloud, models (ALLAM Arabic LLM), applications. $40B AI investment fund. Strategic partnership with NVIDIA (500MW GPU capacity over 5 years). $10B joint investment with Google Cloud for AI hub. Aramco acquired minority stake in Humain. Central to Vision 2030 economic diversification. Chaired by Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.

organizations.name: National Infrastructure Fund
organizations.notes: Saudi Arabian infrastructure financing entity operating under the National Development Fund. Signed $1.2B financing framework with Humain (announced at Davos, Jan 2026) to develop 250MW of AI data center capacity.
