Exploring creation of AI data center investment platform to attract global/local institutional investors for Humain AI strategy. Supports Vision 2030 tech infrastructure goals.

organizations.name: Arizona Secretary of State
organizations.notes: Office held by Adrian Fontes (D). Formed AI & Election Security Advisory Committee (June 2024), co-chaired by Chris Cummiskey and Gowri Ramachandran. Released final report (Feb 2025) proposing AI Elections Lab for developing guidelines and pilot programs. Deployed AI tools in 2024 election: chatbot handled 655K+ interactions, SMS system reached 281K voters. Advocates both beneficial AI use in elections and safeguards against AI-driven threats. Pushes for state and federal AI election policy.

organizations.name: Colorado AI Policy Work Group
organizations.notes: Convened by Governor Jared Polis (Oct 2025) to revise Colorado AI Act (SB 24-205), the nation's first comprehensive AI anti-discrimination law. Diverse membership: consumer groups, hospitals, school districts, tech companies large and small. Delivered unanimous support for revised policy framework (March 2026) emphasizing transparency over bias audits: up-front notice when AI is used in consequential decisions, access to information on adverse decisions, right to correction, human review option. Recommendations to be codified in legislation.

organizations.name: Helion Energy
organizations.notes: Private nuclear fusion company building the Orion plant in Chelan County, WA, targeting power delivery to Microsoft data centers by 2028 under the world's first fusion power purchase agreement. Backed by Sam Altman ($350M+) and SoftBank; raised $425M Series F in Jan 2025 at a $5.4B valuation. Directly tied to AI compute energy demands.

organizations.name: Eleos AI
organizations.notes: Nonprofit research org focused on AI welfare, consciousness, and moral patienthood. Co-founded by Kyle Fish, Robert Long, Kathleen Finlinson. Published landmark report "Taking AI Welfare Seriously." Commended Anthropic's model welfare efforts. Five research priorities: welfare interventions, human-AI cooperation, leveraging AI for welfare research, standardized evaluations, credible communication.

organizations.name: Niskanen Center
organizations.notes: Centrist/center-right think tank founded 2015 by Jerry Taylor (ex-Cato). Named "Most Interesting Think Tank" by TIME (2023). AI policy stance: regulatory restraint, intervene only when harm is realistic. Has 501(c)(3) research arm and 501(c)(4) lobbying arm. Major funders include Hewlett Foundation, Open Philanthropy, Carnegie Corp.

organizations.name: Federation of American Scientists
organizations.notes: DC-based science and technology policy nonprofit founded in 1945. FAS runs the Day One Project to translate scientific expertise into federal policy; active in AI governance, advocating for responsible AI innovation, NIST AISI funding, and federal AI adoption. Submitted AI Action Plan recommendations to the White House in 2025.

organizations.name: U.S. Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute
organizations.notes: Housed within NIST. Develops science-based metrics, tools, and guidelines for AI safety evaluation. Established by Biden EO 14110 (Oct 2023). Paul Christiano served as Head of AI Safety. Runs AISIC consortium (290+ members). Renamed to Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) in 2025. Budget ~$10M (2024). NOTE: Related entities — 205 (U.S. AI Safety Institute at NIST, likely same entity), 1629 (AISIC consortium, its membership body), 1309 (NIST, parent agency).

organizations.name: Model Evaluation and Threat Research
organizations.notes: Nonprofit AI safety eval org (formerly ARC Evals, spun off Dec 2023). Founded by Beth Barnes (ex-OpenAI). Evaluates frontier model capabilities for catastrophic risk. Worked with OpenAI (o3, GPT-4.5) and Anthropic (Claude). Does NOT accept lab funding to avoid conflicts of interest. Budget ~$13-15M/year. Funded via Audacious Project (TED) and donations. Paul Christiano was originally involved but stepped back for AISI role.

organizations.name: UK government's Frontier AI Taskforce
organizations.notes: UK government body established April 2023 with £100m mandate to evaluate frontier AI safety risks ahead of the Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit. Chaired by Ian Hogarth, it transitioned in November 2023 into the AI Safety Institute (later renamed AI Security Institute in 2025), which is a separate entity in this database.

organizations.name: MIT Jameel Clinic
organizations.notes: Abdul Latif Jameel Clinic for Machine Learning in Health, founded 2018 as a joint MIT–Community Jameel initiative. Conducts translational AI research across clinical AI, drug discovery, and epidemiology. Developed clinical AI tools MIRAI and SYBIL, and discovered antibiotics halicin and abaucin using AI-driven methods.

organizations.name: MIT Computer Science & Artificial Intelligence Laboratory
organizations.notes: MIT's largest AI research lab, directed by Daniela Rus, covering robotics, systems, and machine learning. In 2025 submitted AI Action Plan recommendations to the White House advocating federal investment in basic research, AI hardware co-design, and ASI-level capabilities. Published the 2025 AI Agent Index assessing transparency of deployed AI systems.

organizations.name: Cornell University
organizations.notes: Major research university with broad AI activities spanning Cornell Tech (NYC), the AI Initiative, and the MacArthur-funded AI, Policy, and Practice (AIPP) initiative focused on AI's societal impacts. In 2025 NSF awarded Cornell a $20M AI Materials Institute, and a new Vice Provost for AI Strategy was named in January 2026.

organizations.name: Black in AI
organizations.notes: Nonprofit founded 2017 by Timnit Gebru and Rediet Abebe. Increases presence and inclusion of Black people in AI. Four pillars: education pathways, financial barriers, visibility, and community support. Received MacArthur Foundation funding. Hosts annual workshop at NeurIPS. Aims to broaden voices in AI development, deployment, and regulation.

organizations.name: Club for Growth Action
organizations.notes: Conservative super PAC affiliated with Club for Growth. Supports limited-government, free-market candidates. Major donors include Peter Thiel and Richard Uihlein. Focuses on tax cuts, deregulation, free trade, school choice. No specific AI policy position — general deregulatory stance would extend to opposing AI regulation. Founded 2010 as independent expenditure arm.

organizations.name: University of Notre Dame
organizations.notes: Private Catholic research university in Indiana. Major AI initiatives: AI@ND hub, Lucy Family Institute for Data & Society, Scientific AI Initiative. Received $50.8M Lilly Endowment grant for DELTA Network expanding faith-based AI ethics framework. Won $1M Bloomberg Mayors Challenge Award for human-centered AI in public service with City of South Bend. Pete Buttigieg is an alumnus.

organizations.name: Munich Security Conference
organizations.notes: Premier annual international security conference held in Munich, Germany (since 1963). Increasingly prominent on AI governance. Convened 'Tech Accord to Combat Deceptive Use of AI in 2024 Elections' signed by 27 tech companies. Hosts Frontier AI Safety Commitments discussions. Technology program frames AI as 'decisive factor' in systemic competition. 2025 conference marked shift from AI safety to AI security framing.

organizations.name: SpaceX
organizations.notes: Aerospace and space launch company that merged with xAI in February 2026 to create a $1.25T 'Orbital Intelligence' entity. Plans space-based AI data centers using Starlink satellite infrastructure. Founder Elon Musk favors light-touch AI regulation while pursuing aggressive AI deployment through xAI's Grok models.

organizations.name: X
organizations.notes: X (formerly Twitter), owned by Elon Musk, integrates the Grok AI chatbot developed by xAI. In March 2025, xAI formally acquired X in an all-stock deal. X has faced EU and UK regulatory investigations over Grok-generated deepfake images and has deployed content restrictions in response.

organizations.name: Neuralink
organizations.notes: Brain-computer interface company co-founded by Elon Musk (2016). Developing implantable BCI (N1 chip) for paralysis patients. FDA IDE approval May 2023; first human implant Jan 2024 (PRIME trial). FDA Breakthrough Device Designation for speech restoration tech (May 2025). Raised over $700M. Regulatory gap: neural data privacy laws lag behind technology. Competitor Precision Neuroscience also received FDA clearance.

organizations.name: High-Flyer AI
organizations.notes: High-Flyer (Zhejiang High-Flyer Asset Management) is a Chinese quantitative hedge fund founded by Liang Wenfeng. In 2023 it incubated DeepSeek as a spin-off AI lab, funding it internally. High-Flyer posted 56.6% average returns in 2025 and manages over 70 billion yuan (~$10B USD).

organizations.name: Stanford Vision and Learning Lab
organizations.notes: Stanford Vision and Learning Lab (SVL), co-directed by Fei-Fei Li, focuses on computer vision, embodied AI, and robotics. Key projects include BEHAVIOR benchmark for household AI agents and the PAIR group for generalizable robot perception. Li has called for science-based AI governance.

organizations.name: AI4ALL
organizations.notes: Nonprofit co-founded in 2017 by Fei-Fei Li, Olga Russakovsky, and Rick Sommer to increase diversity in AI. Grew from 2015 Stanford summer program for high school girls. 2022 strategic pivot to College Pathways — accelerating underrepresented college students into AI internships and research. Partnerships with 16+ universities (Texas A&M, UIUC, UMBC). Early funders include Melinda French Gates/Pivotal Ventures and Jensen Huang.

organizations.name: Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences
organizations.notes: The primary academic division of Harvard University housing most arts, humanities, and sciences departments. Home to the Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence (launched 2022), a university-wide initiative with strong FAS ties bridging neuroscience and AI. FAS Research Computing supports the Kempner AI cluster.

organizations.name: Ford Foundation
organizations.notes: Major US philanthropy (est. 1936) with significant AI accountability and governance funding. Part of $200M+ coalition (with MacArthur, Omidyar, Mozilla, Open Society Foundations) for public-interest AI. Co-launched Humanity AI, a $500M five-year initiative (Oct 2025) with ten major foundations. Funds AI Now Institute. Supports efforts on AI racial/social bias accountability, labor rights in AI deployment, and global AI fairness standards through Current AI collaboration. Focus areas: transparency, algorithmic accountability, worker protections.

organizations.name: Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur
organizations.notes: Premier Indian technical university (est. 1959). Established Wadhwani School of Advanced AI and Intelligent Systems with Department of Intelligent Systems (2025), admissions starting 2026. Hosts Sustainable Cities Centre of Excellence in AI under Ministry of Education initiative. Research partnerships with TCS (urban planning), IBM (air quality monitoring via AIRAWAT Foundation). Active in AI for sustainable cities, robotics, cybersecurity. Growing AI policy and outreach center.

organizations.name: Trust::Data Alliance
organizations.notes: MIT-based consortium (Trust::Data Consortium) founded by Alex 'Sandy' Pentland. Builds open-source tools for secure, privacy-preserving data sharing using blockchain and AI. Mission: create trusted internet-based network for safe data exchange. Focus areas: data cooperatives, privacy-preserving identity systems, safe distributed computation. Advocates collective data governance model over individualized data control. Part of MIT Connection Science ecosystem.

organizations.name: University of Tuebingen
organizations.notes: German research university and major European AI hub. Home to Tübingen AI Center (co-directed by Philipp Hennig and Matthias Bethge since July 2025) and Cluster of Excellence 'Machine Learning: New Perspectives for Science' (renewed 2025). Partners with Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems, ELLIS Institute Tübingen, and Cyber Valley ecosystem. ML Cloud serves 430+ active researchers. Focus areas: machine learning, computer vision, robotics, probabilistic methods.

organizations.name: Jukedeck
organizations.notes: British AI music startup (founded 2012 by Ed Newton-Rex) that built a platform for generating royalty-free music using AI. Raised $2.5M from Cambridge Innovation Capital, Parkwalk Advisors, Backed VC, Playfair Capital. Acquired by ByteDance/TikTok in July 2019; site went offline post-acquisition. Technology integrated into ByteDance's AI music capabilities. Newton-Rex became director of ByteDance AI Lab, then VP Audio at Stability AI (later resigned over copyright stance).

organizations.name: ByteDance
organizations.notes: Chinese tech giant (est. 2012), parent company of TikTok. Major AI capabilities in recommendation algorithms, content moderation, generative AI. Spent $5.4M on US lobbying in H1 2025 (41 lobbyists). Acquired AI music startup Jukedeck (2019). Subject to PAFACA law requiring divestiture of US TikTok operations; deal closed Jan 2026 creating TikTok USDS (with Oracle, Silver Lake, MGX). Developing large language models and AI tools internally. One of the most heavily scrutinized tech companies in US-China AI competition.

organizations.name: Stability AI
organizations.notes: Open-source generative AI company (est. 2019). Creator of Stable Diffusion. Founded by Emad Mostaque, who stepped down as CEO in March 2024 citing desire to 'fix concentration of power in AI.' Current CEO: Prem Akkaraju (since June 2024). Raised $80M from Eric Schmidt, Coatue, Lightspeed. Strong open-source advocate: released model weights under permissive licenses. Wrote to US Senate urging open-source AI preservation. B2B2C model: open-source distribution + commercial API. Expanding into video, audio, 3D. Growing at triple-digit rates as of late 2024.

organizations.name: TikTok
organizations.notes: Short-form video platform owned by ByteDance, central to US-China tech tensions. Subject to PAFACA ban law (signed April 2024, effective Jan 2025). After multiple Trump executive order delays, US operations divested to TikTok USDS (Jan 2026) consortium including Oracle, Silver Lake, MGX. Uses AI extensively: recommendation algorithm, content moderation (85%+ AI removal rate), creator tools. Requires AI-generated content labeling. Revised Community Guidelines (Sept 2025). ~150M US users. Acquired Jukedeck (2019) for AI music tech.

organizations.name: Thrive Holdings
organizations.notes: AI-focused holding company launched April 2025 by Joshua Kushner's Thrive Capital. Buys, owns, and runs companies positioned to benefit from AI — starting with accounting (Crete Professionals Alliance) and IT services (Shield Technology Partners). OpenAI took ownership stake in Thrive Holdings (Dec 2025) and embeds engineering/product teams within portfolio companies. Parent firm Thrive Capital manages $25B+, raised $10B in 2026. Named TIME100 AI 2025 (Kushner). Early OpenAI investor ($130M in 2022, $1B at $285B valuation). Also invested in Databricks, Anduril, Cursor/Anysphere.

organizations.name: Weizenbaum Institute
organizations.notes: German research institute for interdisciplinary digitalization research, formally 'Weizenbaum Institute for the Networked Society.' Founded 2017, funded by German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (~€50M over 5 years). Consortium led by Berlin Social Science Center (WZB), includes TU Berlin, FU Berlin, HU Berlin, UdK Berlin, Uni Potsdam, Fraunhofer FOKUS. Studies social impact of digitalization — AI ethics, data governance, platform economies. Named after Joseph Weizenbaum, German-American computer scientist who critiqued uncritical tech adoption.

organizations.name: Technische Universität Berlin
organizations.notes: Major German technical university (TU9 member). ~50 academic chairs in AI and computer science. Hosts BIFOLD (Berlin Institute for Foundations of Learning and Data), one of Germany's six national AI competence centers with permanent federal funding. Co-founded Einstein Center Digital Future (ECDF) for digitalization research. Strong socio-technical AI focus — ethics, fairness, explainability. Key partner in Weizenbaum Institute consortium.

organizations.name: Universidad de Buenos Aires
organizations.notes: Argentina's largest public university, accounts for ~40% of national research output. Has AI-focused labs: IALAB (Law Faculty — AI governance, judicial AI guidelines) and LIAA (Applied AI Lab in Computer Science). IALAB published guide on responsible use of generative AI for judiciary, modeled on MIT Task Force approach. Key institution in Latin American AI policy discourse.

organizations.name: Senate AI Caucus
organizations.notes: Bipartisan U.S. Senate caucus on artificial intelligence, launched March 2019 by Sens. Martin Heinrich (D-NM) and Rob Portman (R-OH). Original members included Schatz (D-HI), Gardner (R-CO), Peters (D-MI), Ernst (R-IA). After Portman's retirement (2023), Mike Rounds (R-SD) became co-chair. Heinrich named one of TIME's 100 Most Influential People in AI. The caucus works toward responsible AI policy balancing innovation with ethical standards.

organizations.name: Wall Street Journal
organizations.notes: Major U.S. business newspaper owned by News Corp (Rupert Murdoch). Covers AI extensively through its tech and business desks and has employed prominent AI journalists including Karen Hao (China tech, 2022–2023). Has adopted AI-generated tools internally (Narrativa for market summaries, AI article summaries) and its editor-in-chief Emma Tucker has been openly pro-AI in newsrooms. Editorial coverage is business-focused and generally frames AI as an economic and competitive opportunity.

organizations.name: The American Scholar
organizations.notes: Quarterly magazine of public affairs, literature, science, history, and culture published by Phi Beta Kappa Society since 1932. Named after Ralph Waldo Emerson's 1837 oration. Multiple National Magazine Award winner. Publishes essays, criticism, poetry, and (since 2006) fiction. Also maintains daily website content and bimonthly e-newsletter. Relevant to AI discourse through long-form cultural and intellectual essays on technology and society.

organizations.name: Department of Energy
organizations.notes: Federal agency overseeing U.S. energy systems and nuclear infrastructure. Plays a central AI role through its 17 National Laboratories, its membership in the TRAINS Taskforce for national security AI testing, and its initiative to co-locate AI data centers with federal energy infrastructure across 16 identified sites.

organizations.name: Small Business Administration
organizations.notes: U.S. federal agency (est. 1953) supporting small businesses and entrepreneurs. Increasingly relevant to AI policy as small business AI adoption surges (58% using generative AI per 2025 Chamber survey, up from 40%). Provides AI guidance for small businesses (sba.gov/ai). Subject of the bipartisan Small Business AI Training Act (2026, Cantwell/Moran) directing Commerce Dept + SBA to create AI training resources. Under Senate oversight scrutiny regarding AI practices (Sen. Ernst). Trump admin's 2026 AI policy framework includes 'AI infrastructure and small business support' pillar.

organizations.name: Department of Defense
organizations.notes: The Department of Defense is the largest U.S. federal agency and a major driver of AI policy and procurement. In January 2026, Secretary Hegseth released an "AI-first" strategy directing the department to adopt AI at wartime speed, including deploying frontier models to warfighters within 30 days of public release. The Chief Digital and AI Office (CDAO) was realigned under the Under Secretary for R&E/CTO Emil Michael with a Barrier Removal Board to accelerate AI delivery. DoD AI work spans autonomous systems, logistics, intelligence, and enterprise operations across seven pace-setting projects.

organizations.name: Joint Economic Committee
organizations.notes: The Joint Economic Committee (JEC) is a bicameral congressional committee that studies economic policy. In the 119th Congress (2025–2026), it is chaired by Rep. David Schweikert (R-AZ) with Sen. Maggie Hassan (D-NH) as Ranking Member. The JEC held a hearing in June 2024 on "Artificial Intelligence and Its Potential to Fuel Economic Growth and Improve Governance," examining AI's productivity impacts and regulatory approaches. The committee's AI work focuses primarily on economic competitiveness and workforce implications.

organizations.name: Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs
organizations.notes: Senate's primary oversight committee for government operations and DHS. Active AI oversight role: held hearings on 'AI Risks and Opportunities' and 'AI in Government.' Key legislation includes AI LEAD Act (Chief AI Officer at every federal agency), No Adversarial AI Act (S.2177, protecting government from foreign adversary AI), and AI procurement standards bill (Peters-Tillis). Focus on federal AI use management, security, and procurement guardrails.

organizations.name: U.S. Senate Commerce Committee
organizations.notes: U.S. Senate committee with primary jurisdiction over AI, technology, and telecommunications. Chaired by Ted Cruz (R-TX) since 2025, who introduced the SANDBOX Act and a five-pillar light-touch AI framework. Oversees the Senate AI Working Group and has held hearings on U.S. AI competitiveness vs. China.

organizations.name: Encode AI
organizations.notes: Youth-led DC-based nonprofit founded by Sneha Revanur advocating for responsible AI governance. Sponsored California SB 53 and New York's RAISE Act; challenged OpenAI's for-profit conversion. Funded by FLI, Heising-Simons, Survival and Flourishing Fund, and others; explicitly rejects corporate and AI-executive funding.

organizations.name: Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs
organizations.notes: Full legal name: Advanced Machine Intelligence (AMI Labs). AI startup co-founded by Yann LeCun (exec chairman) and Alex LeBrun (CEO) after LeCun left Meta (Nov 2025). Raised $1.03B seed at $3.5B pre-money valuation (Mar 2026) — one of largest seed rounds ever. Building 'world models' using JEPA architecture as alternative to LLMs. Backed by Nvidia, Samsung, Bezos Expeditions, Eric Schmidt. Key team: Saining Xie (CSO), Pascale Fung (CRIO), Michael Rabbat (VP World Models), Laurent Solly (COO). Target: industrial control, robotics, healthcare, wearables. NOTE: Entity 1433 (AMI Labs) is the SAME organization — short name variant.

organizations.name: AT&T Bell Laboratories
organizations.notes: Legendary corporate research lab (est. 1925). Pioneering AI/ML contributions: Claude Shannon's learning machines (1952), neural network research (1986-1996) including handwritten digit recognition with USPS data, early deep learning work by Yann LeCun on convolutional neural networks. 11 Nobel Prizes, 5 Turing Awards for work done there. In the 1990s, AT&T Labs' AI/ML group was arguably the strongest in the world. LeCun worked as researcher there before moving to NYU and later Facebook/Meta. Now AT&T holds 6th most AI patents among U.S. firms (2025).

organizations.name: Collège de France
organizations.notes: Prestigious French academic institution (est. 1530) in Paris. AI relevance: Yann LeCun held the Annual Chair for Computer Science and Digital Technologies (Chaire Annuelle Informatique et Sciences Numériques) in 2016, delivering inaugural lecture on deep learning. The institution hosts visiting professorships connecting top researchers to French academia. Not an AI-focused institution per se, but its rotating chairs have featured leading AI scientists.

organizations.name: National Artificial Intelligence Advisory Commission
organizations.notes: Federal advisory committee created by National AI Initiative Act of 2020. Advises the President and National AI Initiative Office (within OSTP). Housed at NIST. Members drawn from academia, nonprofits, civil society, private sector. Covers AI competitiveness, workforce, R&D, standards, ethics, governance. Finalized transition recommendations for Trump admin (Mar 2025) with 10 priority recommendations. NOTE: Formally called NAIAC (National AI Advisory Committee) — 'Commission' in entity name may be a slight misnomer.

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Labor
organizations.notes: Federal cabinet department overseeing labor standards, workforce, and employment. Key AI role: published national AI Literacy Framework (Feb 2026) with 5 content areas and 7 delivery principles. Partnered with NSF on AI-ready workforce initiative (MOU for AI literacy, training pathways, labor market research). Hosts AI page at dol.gov/ai. Focus on workforce preparation for AI disruption through guidance and skills development rather than heavy regulation. Daniel Ho serves as Senior Advisor on Responsible AI.

organizations.name: Public First Action
organizations.notes: Bipartisan 501(c)(4) nonprofit focused on AI policy advocacy, co-founded in 2025 by former Congressmen Brad Carson (D) and Chris Stewart (R). Received $20M from Anthropic in Feb 2026 — its largest single donation — to counter 'Leading the Future,' a $125M super PAC backed by tech billionaires opposing AI regulation. Policy priorities: AI model transparency safeguards, federal AI governance framework, smart export controls on AI chips, targeted regulation of near-term high risks (bioweapons, cyberattacks). Plans to back 30-50 candidates from both parties in 2026 state and federal races.

organizations.name: Columbia Law School
organizations.notes: Ivy League law school at Columbia University, New York. Active in AI governance research and education. Faculty-led task force (professors Gillis, Liebman, Talley, Wexler) developing AI curriculum. Key AI faculty: Tim Wu (Julius Silver Professor — competition and tech policy, former Biden White House advisor), Rebecca Wexler (AI and criminal justice/due process). Offers seminar on 'Emerging Law of AI' comparing regulatory approaches across US, EU, and China. Hosted 2023 symposium on new legal frameworks for generative AI revolution. Adopted formal generative AI policy for academic integrity (Aug 2025).

organizations.name: Stony Brook University
organizations.notes: SUNY flagship research university on Long Island, New York. Home to the AI Innovation Institute (AI3) directed by Lav Varshney. Participates in Empire AI — SUNY's multi-campus partnership for AI research and public-good applications. Three faculty received SUNY-IBM AI Research Alliance grants (Zadok, Balasubramanian, Ferdman) for cloud computing, sustainable energy, and supercomputer optimization. Computer science department has active AI/ML, NLP, and computer vision research groups. Governor Hochul proposed Empire AI Beta (2026 State of the State) to scale to world's most advanced academic supercomputer. Yejin Choi was previously an assistant professor of CS here.

organizations.name: Seoul National University
organizations.notes: South Korea's top research university, located in Seoul. Major AI research hub with 100+ AI professors, 200+ AI application labs, and 2,000+ AI researchers. Houses the Artificial Intelligence Institute (AIIS), selected as core part of Korea's National Strategy AI Development Project. Established Interdisciplinary Program in Artificial Intelligence (IPAI). Key labs: Biointelligence Lab (Prof. Byoung-Tak Zhang — biological/artificial intelligence), Data Science & AI Lab, AI and Biomedical Informatics Lab. Yejin Choi is affiliated with SNU (edge 1609). Strong in NLP, computer vision, and biomedical AI.

organizations.name: Trail of Bits
organizations.notes: Leading cybersecurity research, engineering, and consulting firm founded in 2012. Pioneered AI security assurance practice combining safety and security methodologies for ML/AI risk assessment — Engineering Director of AI Assurance team is Heidy Khlaaf. Won $3M second-place prize in DARPA's AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC) with their 'Buttercup' system. Clients include Facebook/Meta, DARPA, and major cryptocurrency protocols. Strong open-source ethos — builds and maintains public security tools across static/dynamic analysis, AI/ML security, and fuzzing. Member of OpenSSF. As of 2026, has 94 plugins, 201 skills, 84 specialized agents for AI-augmented security auditing.

organizations.name: UNSG's AI Advisory Body
organizations.notes: UN Secretary-General's High-Level Advisory Body on Artificial Intelligence, established October 2023. Comprised 39 preeminent AI leaders from 33 countries — geographically diverse, gender-balanced group from government, private sector, civil society, and academia. Published final report 'Governing AI for Humanity' in September 2024, proposing seven recommendations for global AI governance cooperation including foundations for the first globally inclusive and distributed architecture for AI governance. Process included 18 deep-dive discussions, 50+ consultation sessions, 50+ written submissions from 150+ organizations. Recommendations inspired the Global Digital Compact adopted at the Summit of the Future (September 2024). Heidy Khlaaf served as Network of Experts member.

organizations.name: British Standards Institute
organizations.notes: The British Standards Institution (BSI) is the UK's national standards body, playing a central role in shaping international AI governance standards. First certification body accredited by UKAS to certify ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System standard, published end of 2023) — also accredited by RvA (Netherlands) and ANAB (US). Actively involved in developing ISO/IEC 42001 and published ISO 42006 (July 2025) setting requirements for bodies auditing AI management systems. ISO/IEC 42001 addresses AI-specific aspects: unwanted bias, fairness, safety, security, privacy, accountability, explainability, and transparency. BSI performs AI management system audits globally. Heidy Khlaaf is an ISO SC 42 Committee Member through BSI.

organizations.name: Adaptive Security
organizations.notes: AI-native cybersecurity startup founded by serial entrepreneurs Brian Long (CEO) and Andrew Jones. Builds platform simulating AI-powered attacks — deepfake voice/video phishing, smishing, and GenAI social engineering — to train corporate defenders. Raised $136M+ total: $43M round co-led by OpenAI and Andreessen Horowitz (OpenAI's first cybersecurity investment), plus $55M Series A (April 2025) led by a16z and OpenAI Startup Fund. Platform includes real-time threat triage, personalized training, and AI-driven risk scoring. Also partnered with Elliston Berry and Pathos Consulting Group to create free deepfake safety training course for US schools. Brian Long previously co-founded Attentive ($500M+ ARR, 8000+ customers) and TapCommerce (acquired by Twitter).

organizations.name: Pathos Consulting Group
organizations.notes: AI ethics consulting firm focused on K-12 education, founded by Evan J. Harris (President). Specializes in helping independent schools integrate AI tools responsibly — staff department automation, student AI empowerment, and ethical professional development. Harris holds master's in private school leadership from Teachers College and studied tech ethics at Stanford's Human-Centered AI Institute (HAI). National expert on emerging AI risks in schools, particularly deepfake abuse and digital safety. Employs holistic systems approach addressing data privacy, bias, authentic communication. Partnered with Elliston Berry and Adaptive Security to create free deepfake safety training course for US schools (17-minute online course for students, teachers, parents).

organizations.name: Quartz
organizations.notes: Digitally native business news outlet founded in 2012 by Atlantic Media, now owned by Redbrick (Canadian software firm). Organized around 'obsessions' rather than traditional beats. Notable for AI integration controversy: in January 2025, drew significant attention for quietly publishing AI-generated news articles — described as 'purely experimental' AI newsroom intended to free editorial staff for deeper reporting. Publishes 'Quartz AI & Tech' newsletter covering business of AI and technology. Uses in-house Chartbuilder tool and Atlas data visualization platform. Previously employed Karen Hao as tech reporter and data scientist. Covers global business, economics, and technology with focus on the 'new global economy.'

organizations.name: Pulitzer Center
organizations.notes: Nonprofit journalism organization (Pulitzer Center on Crisis Reporting) supporting independent journalists on underreported global issues. Major AI accountability journalism hub: runs AI Accountability Network with dedicated fellowships, reporting grants, ML reporting grants, and AI Spotlight Series (2,000+ journalists trained since 2024). AI Accountability Fellowship launched 2022, supporting 27 journalists from five continents. Awards $2M+ annually in direct grants. Stories by grantees have been cited by courts, sparked official investigations. Funded by MacArthur Foundation, Omidyar Network, Howard Hughes Medical Institute, Humanity United, and Facebook Journalism Project endowment gift. Karen Hao is a collaborator/co-creator. Focus areas include AI, environment, health, conflict, and human rights.

organizations.name: Craig Newmark Graduate School of Journalism
organizations.notes: Public graduate journalism school at CUNY (est. 2006), the only public graduate journalism school in the northeastern U.S. Named after Craig Newmark (craigslist founder) who donated $20M (2018) and an additional $10M endowment for ethics/security center. AI relevance: runs AI Journalism Labs (5th and 6th cohorts in 2026 with Microsoft support and Nordic AI Journalism partnership) training experienced journalists on generative AI tools. Community Media Training program helps niche outlets report on how AI systems affect immigrants, communities of color, and vulnerable populations. Working toward tuition-free model with $60M fundraising goal. Key funders include Ford Foundation, MacArthur Foundation, Knight Foundation.

organizations.name: House Democratic Commission on AI and the Innovation Economy
organizations.notes: House Democratic caucus body launched Dec 2025 by Leader Hakeem Jeffries. Co-chaired by Rep. Ted Lieu (CA-36), Rep. Josh Gottheimer (NJ-05), and Rep. Valerie Foushee (NC-04). Ex officio co-chairs: Rep. Zoe Lofgren (Ranking Member, Science, Space & Tech Committee) and Rep. Frank Pallone Jr. (Ranking Member, Energy & Commerce Committee). Will convene throughout 2026 to develop AI policy expertise on guardrails, economic impacts, safety, and health. Successor to Democrats' participation in the now-expired Bipartisan AI Task Force. All House Democratic Caucus members invited to participate.

organizations.name: Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law
organizations.notes: Nonpartisan civil rights legal organization founded in 1963 at the request of President Kennedy. Champions the AI Civil Rights Act (co-endorsed with Rep. Pressley, Sen. Markey, Rep. Clarke) requiring transparency, accountability, and anti-discrimination safeguards in AI systems used in housing, lending, healthcare, employment, education, and law enforcement. Applauded Biden's AI Executive Order and White House AI Bill of Rights. Opposed Trump-era executive order seeking to bar states from addressing AI harms. Leverages the nation's largest pro bono legal network (62,000+ hours/year). Headquarters: Washington, D.C.

organizations.name: The Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights
organizations.notes: America's oldest and largest civil rights coalition (240+ member organizations including ACLU, NAACP LDF, Sierra Club). Launched the Center for Civil Rights and Technology in 2023 focused on AI policy impacts on civil and human rights. Published an Innovation Framework guiding responsible AI investment, creation, and use. Led a 50+ organization letter to the Senate opposing a 10-year ban on state/local AI laws. Submitted comments to NSF on national AI R&D strategy and to NIST on AI standards, emphasizing civil rights must be embedded at every stage of the AI lifecycle. Headquarters: Washington, D.C.

organizations.name: George Mason University
organizations.notes: Virginia public research university (est. 1972) with significant AI research infrastructure. Key AI centers: Mason Autonomy and Robotics Center (MARC) for autonomous systems and responsible AI; Center for Mathematics and Artificial Intelligence (CMAI) for applied math/AI research funded by NSF, DOE, DTRA, and DoD. Offers MS in AI. Home to the Mercatus Center, a libertarian-leaning policy think tank with an active AI & Progress Project (led by Dean Ball) advocating light-touch, innovation-first AI governance. GMU's AI policy output tends toward deregulatory/innovation perspectives.

organizations.name: Congressional Artificial Intelligence Caucus
organizations.notes: Bipartisan House caucus promoting AI education and policy dialogue. Originally co-chaired by Reps. Anna Eshoo (D-CA) and Michael McCaul (R-TX); Don Beyer (D-VA) was an active co-chair figure. Convenes members to engage on AI legislation, coordinates with the House AI Task Force, and maintains a nonpartisan pro-innovation stance.

organizations.name: University of California, Los Angeles
organizations.notes: Major public research university and UC system flagship. Launched the AI Innovation Initiative — a campus-wide effort to build AI fluency and drive responsible AI adoption. First California university to partner with OpenAI (Sept 2024), also partnered with Google. Supported 68 AI pilot projects across 27 departments. Maintains multi-layered AI governance structure for responsible, ethical adoption. UCLA Health established the Health AI Council (HAIC) for clinical AI oversight. UCLA Law offers Strategic AI for Legal Professionals executive education. Strong interdisciplinary AI presence across engineering, medicine, law, and social sciences.

organizations.name: University of Tennessee
organizations.notes: Public flagship university in Knoxville, Tennessee. Home to the AI Tennessee Initiative — the first statewide AI initiative of its kind in the U.S., founded and directed by Lynne Parker (former White House Deputy CTO and founding NAIIO director). Launched major AI cluster hiring in Foundational AI and Science-Informed AI. CECS houses Tennessee's first applied AI degree program. AI focus areas: smart manufacturing, climate-smart agriculture, precision health, future mobility, and AI for science. Partners with Oak Ridge National Laboratory on computing/AI research.

organizations.name: AI Tennessee Initiative
organizations.notes: First statewide AI initiative in the U.S., launched by the University of Tennessee, Knoxville. Founded and directed by Lynne Parker, who served as White House Deputy CTO and founding director of the National AI Initiative Office (2018-2022) before returning to UT. Coordinates AI research, education, and workforce development across disciplines and institutions statewide. Focus areas: smart manufacturing, climate-smart agriculture and forestry, precision health, future mobility, and AI for science. Offers AI education courses (AI 101, AI 401) and is developing an applied AI degree. Engages academic, industry, and community partners across Tennessee.

organizations.name: National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Office
organizations.notes: Federal coordinating office established by the National AI Initiative Act of 2020, housed within the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP). Serves as central coordination hub for AI R&D across federal agencies, industry, academia, nonprofits, and state/tribal governments. Led the National AI R&D Strategic Plan updates. Four statutory mandates: ensure continued U.S. AI R&D leadership, develop trustworthy AI systems, prepare the workforce for AI integration, and coordinate federal AI activities across civilian agencies, DoD, and intelligence community. First director was Lynne Parker (2021-2022).

organizations.name: AI Research Institute on Interaction for AI assistants (ARIA)
organizations.notes: NSF-funded ($20M) AI Research Institute on Interaction for AI Assistants, headquartered at Brown University. Focuses on developing trustworthy, context-aware AI assistants with specific application to mental and behavioral health. Research themes include AI interpretability, adaptability, and participatory design. Co-directed by Ellie Pavlick and Suresh Venkatasubramanian. One of five national AI institutes funded as part of a $100M NSF initiative (with Capital One and Intel). Brings together researchers in computer science, neuroscience, cognitive science, philosophy, law, and education alongside mental health practitioners and civil society groups.

organizations.name: ACM US Technology Policy Committee
organizations.notes: ACM's US Technology Policy Committee (USTPC), comprising 170+ members, serves as the focal point for ACM's interaction with all branches of the US government on technology policy. Has issued influential policy statements including 'Principles for Responsible Algorithmic Systems' (nine principles: legitimacy, harm minimization, transparency, accountability, etc.) and 'Principles for the Development, Deployment, and Use of Generative AI Technologies.' Called for immediate suspension of facial recognition in prejudicial circumstances. Recent outputs: Response to RFI on AI Regulatory Landscape (Oct 2025), Statement on Inappropriate Use of AI Chatbots (Sep 2025). Chaired by Sorelle Friedler (Aug 2025); AI & Algorithms subcommittee co-chaired by Suresh Venkatasubramanian and Serena Booth.

organizations.name: Empire AI
organizations.notes: New York State AI consortium launched January 2024 by Governor Kathy Hochul. Backed by $400M+ in public and private funds ($275M state investment, expanded to $500M+ total). Building state-of-the-art AI computing center on the University at Buffalo campus. Consortium of 10 member institutions: Columbia, Cornell, NYU, RPI, SUNY, CUNY, Simons Foundation, University of Rochester, RIT, and Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai. Focused on responsible AI research for the public good — workforce development, healthcare, climate science. Independent analysis projects billions in economic benefits for New York.

organizations.name: University at Buffalo
organizations.notes: Major SUNY research university with extensive AI research dating back decades (early handwriting recognition system saved USPS hundreds of millions). Home to the Institute for Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (IAD, est. 2021) with 200+ faculty on AI. Hosts Empire AI supercomputing center on North Campus. Home to NSF-funded National AI Institute for Exceptional Education (children with speech-language challenges). Launched seven AI-focused bachelor's degrees and two AI minors (AI ethics; AI, crime and society). Applied AI focus: brain aneurysms, diabetes, deepfakes, manufacturing, disaster response.

organizations.name: SUNY
organizations.notes: Largest integrated public university system in the United States. Central player in New York's AI strategy as founding member of Empire AI. Key initiatives: Center for AI Responsibility and Research at SUNY Binghamton (nation's first independent AI research center at a public university, $30M gift + $25M SUNY investment); 'AI for the Public Good' research program; revised General Education Framework includes AI literacy (effective Fall 2026). Hosts Empire AI SUNY Campus Partnerships expanding AI access across system. Strategic plan (SUNY STRIVE) includes AI as priority research area: responsible development, workforce preparation, public interest.

organizations.name: William and Flora Hewlett Foundation
organizations.notes: Major U.S. philanthropic foundation ($13.9B assets as of Dec 2024, $631M in grants in 2024). Significant funder of AI governance, cyber policy, and responsible technology through its Cyber Initiative ($150M over 10 years) and Special Projects program. Funded CSET at Georgetown ($3M+), Hoover Institution Technology Policy Accelerator ($2.5M), Vanderbilt AI information warfare research ($2.5M), and multiple HBCU/MSI cyber policy programs ($20M+). Co-convener of PIT-UN alongside Ford Foundation and New America. One of the largest philanthropic funders of AI governance infrastructure in the U.S. Headquarters: Menlo Park, CA.

organizations.name: Public Interest Technology University Network
organizations.notes: University consortium created in 2019 by New America's Public Interest Technology program, co-convened with the Ford Foundation and Hewlett Foundation. 64 member institutions committed to training civic-minded technologists. Deployed $14M through Network Challenges and RFPs for experiential learning, faculty development, career pathways, and inclusive tech governance. Develops curricula, research agendas, and experiential learning programs. Focuses on ensuring technology design and governance includes communities traditionally excluded from tech. Note: category 'VC/Capital/Philanthropy' reflects its grantmaking role but PIT-UN is primarily an academic consortium.

organizations.name: Bipartisan Policy Center
organizations.notes: Washington, D.C. think tank founded in 2007 by former Senate majority leaders Howard Baker, Tom Daschle, Bob Dole, and George Mitchell. 501(c)(3) nonprofit ($31.2M revenue FY2023). Runs an active AI policy program including an AI 101 education initiative for congressional staff, the AI and Energy Task Force, and policy research on governance frameworks. Advocates a use-case-specific, risk-based approach to AI accountability that balances innovation with civil rights, safety, and national security. Published 'Eight Considerations to Shape the Future of AI Governance.' CEO: Margaret Spellings (since 2023, former U.S. Secretary of Education).

organizations.name: Association for Computing Machinery
organizations.notes: World's largest educational and scientific computing society, founded 1947. Over 100,000 members across 170+ countries. Operates the US Technology Policy Committee (USTPC, 170+ members) providing nonpartisan, technology-neutral AI policy recommendations to lawmakers. Published TechBriefs on 'Trusted AI' and 'Automated Vehicles'; issued formal comments on the US AI Action Plan (2025), OMB AI governance frameworks, and EU AI Act. USTPC advocates for clear documentation, rigorous evaluation standards, transparency, and effective oversight of AI systems. Also warned about manipulative potential of chatbots with minors and generative misinformation risks. Co-hosts AAAI/ACM Conference on AI, Ethics, and Society (AIES). Partner of Partnership on AI.

organizations.name: Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (AAAI)
organizations.notes: World's premier AI scientific society, founded 1979. Promotes responsible research in and use of AI. Maintains an AI Public Policy Committee with dedicated Global AI Initiatives and Policy Chair and US Initiatives and Policy Chair positions. Organizes the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence (flagship AI conference) and co-hosts the AAAI/ACM Conference on AI, Ethics, and Society (AIES). Sponsors 14 AI journals and numerous symposia. Governed by an elected Executive Council. Advocates for responsible AI development while advancing the field. Partner of Partnership on AI.

organizations.name: Human Rights Data Analysis Group
organizations.notes: Nonprofit, non-partisan organization (est. 1991, founded by Patrick Ball) applying rigorous statistical science to human rights violations worldwide. Pioneered research demonstrating how predictive policing algorithms amplify racial bias — showed PredPol and similar tools learn from biased police recording patterns rather than actual crime patterns, reinforcing discriminatory enforcement. Fiscal sponsorship through Community Partners since 2013. Funded by MacArthur Foundation ($2M+ since 2015), Open Society Foundations, Oak Foundation, Sigrid Rausing Trust, Ethics & Governance of AI Initiative, and Humanity United. Active at the intersection of statistical methodology, human rights accountability, and algorithmic fairness.

organizations.name: The Hastings Center
organizations.notes: Independent, nonpartisan bioethics research institute (est. 1969) based in Garrison, NY. Conducts major research program on Ethics of AI in Health and Biomedical Research. Received $800K five-year Donaghue Foundation award for research on trustworthy AI integration in healthcare. Launched 'Hastings on the Hill' initiative (Greenwall Foundation-funded) translating bioethics research on AI in health for policymakers. Created 'A Patient's Journey with Medical AI' interactive tool for policymakers, patients, and providers navigating medical AI ethics. President Vardit Ravitsky is PI on two NIH Bridge2AI projects. Publishes the Hastings Center Report (premier bioethics journal). Partner of Partnership on AI.

organizations.name: MacArthur Foundation
organizations.notes: Major U.S. philanthropy (est. 1978, ~$7B endowment). Part of Humanity AI, a $500M five-year initiative ensuring AI is shaped by and for people; committed $10M in aligned AI grantmaking. Technology in the Public Interest program funds AI governance, auditing, and accountability research. Granted $2M each to AI Now Institute and Brookings AI Initiative. Overarching goal: strengthen democratic oversight and innovation in AI governance through evaluation, auditing, and accountability mechanisms centering public interest. Three-pronged strategy: (1) strengthen research base for AI evaluations; (2) advance AI laws/policies centering public interest; (3) support networks ensuring AI in high-stakes sectors prioritizes people. Joined 9 other foundations in AI governance collaboration. Funder of Partnership on AI and HRDAG.

organizations.name: Alliance of Motion Picture and Television Producers
organizations.other_categories: NOTE: Represents studio management in collective bargaining — not a labor/worker org. 'Labor/Civil Society' kept pending schema addition (e.g. Trade Association).
organizations.notes: Trade association representing major Hollywood studios and streaming platforms (Disney, Netflix, Warner Bros. Discovery, NBCUniversal, Paramount, Sony, Amazon/MGM, Apple) in collective bargaining with entertainment unions. Central player in 2023 SAG-AFTRA and WGA strikes where AI was a key issue. Agreed to: require advance written consent from performers to create and use digital replicas (AI-generated likenesses); prohibit requiring writers to use AI; disclose AI involvement in scriptwriting. IATSE contract ratified in 2024 despite union concerns about insufficient AI protections. Committed to ongoing AI discussions through next negotiation cycle. Note: AMPTP represents management/studio interests, not labor — current category 'Labor/Civil Society' may warrant review.

organizations.name: Yale Law School
organizations.notes: Yale Law School houses the Information Society Project (ISP), founded in 1997 by Jack Balkin, one of the most influential academic centers studying law and technology policy. ISP fellows research AI governance, algorithmic decision-making, content moderation, privacy, and digital rights. Balkin's "information fiduciary" framework (proposed 2014) is widely cited in AI governance debates. The school also hosts the AI Policy Group at Yale, fostering interdisciplinary policy research. Executive director Chinmayi Arun co-teaches AI Law and Policy with Balkin.

organizations.name: B Capital
organizations.notes: Global venture capital firm co-founded in 2015 by Eduardo Saverin (Facebook co-founder) and Raj Ganguly. Over $9B in AUM across 9 offices and 100+ employees. Invests seed through late-stage in enterprise software, AI/ML, fintech, healthcare tech, and security. Led Goodfire's $150M Series B at $1.25B valuation. Saverin views AI as "a foundational capability that amplifies everything we do." Active across US and Southeast Asia markets.

organizations.name: Menlo Ventures
organizations.notes: Major Silicon Valley venture capital firm investing across AI, consumer, enterprise, and healthcare. Raised $1.35B for AI-era investments. Portfolio includes 466 companies, 25 unicorns, 20 IPOs (including Uber, Roku). Key AI investments: Anthropic, Pinecone, Goodfire. Led Goodfire's $50M Series A. Created the $100M Anthology Fund with Anthropic to fund AI companies. Actively invests across the AI stack from infrastructure/models to applications.

organizations.name: Mayo Clinic
organizations.notes: World-renowned academic medical center with 200+ AI projects across clinical, research, and operational applications. Unified EHR system supports AI at scale; operates an AI factory on Google Cloud Vertex AI for 250+ projects. AI applications include early detection of cardiovascular disease, breast cancer, pancreatic cancer, neuromuscular disease, and mental health conditions. Runs Mayo Clinic Platform for data-driven innovation and responsible AI in healthcare. Partnership with Goodfire for AI interpretability in genomic medicine. Harper Family Foundation AI Education in Medicine Program trains staff and students on ethical AI use.

organizations.name: Radical AI
organizations.notes: NYC-based autonomous materials science discovery company founded in 2024. Uses AI to screen billions of material compositions, predict structures and properties, then synthesize and characterize candidates in a closed-loop self-driving lab. Claims 370x acceleration over traditional 15-25 year materials discovery timelines. Lab produces 25+ alloys per day. Raised $55M Series Seed+ led by RTX Ventures. Signed MOU with U.S. Department of Energy for Genesis Mission. Establishing fully autonomous labs at Brooklyn Navy Yard (115 new jobs). Partners with Goodfire for AI interpretability in materials discovery, improving viable candidates by ~30%.

organizations.name: Canadian Journalism Foundation
organizations.notes: Canadian non-profit organization founded in 1990 that promotes, celebrates, and facilitates excellence in journalism. Runs annual journalism awards and fosters education, training, and research opportunities. Partner of the AI Safety Foundation (AISF). Launched the CJF-Hinton Award for Excellence in AI Safety Reporting in October 2025, named for Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton, recognizing journalism that critically examines AI safety implications. Conducts media literacy research on AI-generated misinformation — a 2023 study found 58%% of Canadians believed they had encountered AI-generated misinformation while 48%% lacked confidence to identify it.

organizations.name: LTFF
organizations.notes: Long-Term Future Fund (LTFF) — one of four funds managed by Effective Ventures under EA Funds. Supports projects working to positively influence the long-term trajectory of civilization, with AI safety/alignment research as its largest funding category (~2/3 of grants). Granted $6.67M total in 2023 (~$4.3M on AI safety). Median AI safety grant is $25k (vs. Open Phil's $257k median), making it well-suited for individuals, upskilling, and career transitions. Distinctive for funding unconventional projects and acting as a funder of last resort. Applications reviewed by committee of grantmakers with expertise in x-risk, AI safety, and EA.

organizations.name: SERI-MATS
organizations.notes: ML Alignment & Theory Scholars (MATS, formerly SERI MATS) — independent research and educational seminar program connecting talented researchers with top AI alignment mentors. Originally ran under the Stanford Existential Risks Initiative (SERI), now independent. Three-phase structure: remote Training Phase, in-person Research Phase in Berkeley, and Extension Phase in London/Berkeley. Provides funding, housing, training, office space, and research assistance. Alumni have gone on to Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and founded research groups like Apollo Research, Timaeus, and Cadenza Labs. Widely regarded as a key pipeline for AI safety talent.

organizations.name: RAND Center on AI, Security, and Technology
organizations.funding_model: Donor-supported independent research, directed government grants and contracts (DoD, DHS), philanthropic funding
organizations.notes: RAND Center on AI, Security, and Technology (RAND CAST) is a research center within the RAND Corporation focused on transformative technologies and global security. Conducts policy research at the intersection of AI, cybersecurity, biotechnology, and national security. Research areas include AI and information security (securing frontier AI systems, assessing cyber capabilities), AI and biotechnology (preventing misuse, promoting responsible innovation), and building AI analysts for policy research at machine speed. Formed by merging the Technology and Security Policy Center and Meselson Center. Runs a competitive fellows program developing policy analysts at the tech-security intersection. Research frequently informs U.S. defense and intelligence community policy.

organizations.name: Good Ventures
organizations.notes: Philanthropic foundation co-founded by Dustin Moskovitz and Cari Tuna; primary funder of Open Philanthropy (rebranded Coefficient Giving, Nov 2025). Accounts for >50% of philanthropic AI safety funding globally; has distributed $5B+ in grants including $5.5M to launch CHAI at UC Berkeley.

organizations.name: Princeton AI Lab
organizations.notes: Princeton Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence, launched fall 2024 under inaugural director Tom Griffiths and associate director Olga Russakovsky. Supports interdisciplinary AI research across campus including safety, ethics, natural and artificial minds, and AI for scientific discovery. Distinct from Princeton CITP.

organizations.name: Forward Global
organizations.notes: International commercial and geopolitical risk advisory and public affairs firm headquartered in Paris, with ~470 staff and ~€100M in revenue. Offices in Paris, Brussels, London, Washington DC, New York, Los Angeles, and Miami, plus Africa. Specializes in public affairs, strategic communication, due diligence, and cybersecurity. In 2025, formed strategic partnership with Ballard Partners (leading US lobbying firm) for integrated lobbying and public affairs solutions worldwide. Partner of the Effective Institutions Project. While not an AI-focused think tank, its public affairs and regulatory advisory work intersects with tech policy and AI governance through its EU and US presence.

organizations.name: Founders Pledge
organizations.notes: EA-aligned nonprofit where entrepreneurs pledge to donate a share of exit proceeds. Publishes cause-area research including advanced AI, recommending funders prioritize catastrophic-risk reduction and international coordination. Has moved over $323M in grants; funds organizations like FAR AI, CEIP, and the Effective Institutions Project.

organizations.name: Piramidal
organizations.notes: AI neurotech startup building a foundation model for understanding brainwaves (EEG data). Founded in 2024 by Dimitris Fotis Sakellariou and Kris Pahuja. Y Combinator W24 batch. Raised $6.5M led by YC with participation from Adverb Ventures, Lionheart Ventures, and angel investors. Initial focus on neuro ICU applications for real-time seizure and brain dysfunction detection. Piloting in hospitals in England (King's College and Saint Thomas). Headquartered in San Francisco.

organizations.name: Metaplanet
organizations.notes: Venture capital and philanthropy vehicle founded in 2011 by Skype co-founding engineer Jaan Tallinn, based in Tallinn, Estonia. Operates as a hybrid venture-philanthropy investing on decades-to-centuries timelines in deep tech addressing existential risks, especially advanced AI. Has invested in 184+ companies, primarily at Seed and Series A stages, with 11 unicorns in portfolio. Tallinn was a Series A investor and former board member at DeepMind, led the Series A for Anthropic (now board observer), and co-founded the Centre for the Study of Existential Risk (CSER) and the Future of Life Institute (FLI).

organizations.name: Delphi Ventures
organizations.notes: Crypto and Web3-focused venture capital firm launched in 2020. Thesis-driven, high-conviction fund investing in blockchain, DeFi, and crypto-AI intersection. Notable portfolio companies include Axie Infinity, Uniswap, dYdX, Celestia, Solana, Lido, and zkSync. Has invested in 98+ companies. Co-launched an AI accelerator with the NEAR Foundation. Invested in Mythos Ventures (an AI venture capital fund). Note: distinct from the older Delphi Ventures life sciences VC (founded 1988, San Mateo).

organizations.name: The Operating Group
organizations.notes: Small venture capital fund based in Road Town, British Virgin Islands, investing primarily in Seed-stage blockchain and crypto startups. Has invested in 5 companies including Mythos Ventures. Team of 2 partners. Limited public information available.

organizations.name: Elicit
organizations.notes: AI research assistant that uses language models to automate parts of researchers' workflows, including finding papers, extracting data, and synthesizing findings. Originally incubated at Ought (a nonprofit ML research lab), spun off as an independent public benefit corporation in September 2023. Raised $9M seed led by Fifty Years (with Basis Set, Mythos Ventures, and angels including GitHub co-founder Tom Preston-Werner and Dropbox co-founder Arash Ferdowsi), then $22M Series A at $100M valuation led by Spark Capital and Footwork. Searches over 126M academic papers. Well-known in EA/research community for evidence-based reasoning.

organizations.name: Oxford Martin School
organizations.notes: Interdisciplinary research institute at the University of Oxford, founded 2005 via £70M+ endowment from James Martin. Houses the Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative, which bridges technical AI research and global policy through research, fellowships, and engagement with the UN, EU, and US.

organizations.name: SecureBio
organizations.notes: Nonprofit biosecurity organization founded in 2022 by MIT professor Kevin Esvelt. Develops AI biosecurity benchmarks and evaluations cited by all major frontier labs (Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta, OpenAI, xAI). Funded partly by effective altruism philanthropy.

organizations.name: US AI Safety Institute Consortium
organizations.notes: NIST-led consortium of 290+ companies, universities, and civil society organizations launched in February 2024 to advance AI safety research and standards. Renamed to Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) in June 2025 under the Trump administration, shifting focus from safety to innovation and national security.

organizations.name: AI Safety Dublin
organizations.notes: Grassroots AI safety community in Dublin, Ireland, working to reduce risks from advanced AI through awareness, upskilling, and community building. Part of the European Network for AI Safety (ENAIS). Runs 8-week reading/discussion groups for newcomers to AI safety, an Intro to AI Safety Governance program, and an AI Safety Alignment technical program. Hosts 48-hour Alignment Jam Sessions for collaborative research. Welcomes AI enthusiasts, researchers, and those concerned about AI futures.

organizations.name: Dutch Network for AI Safety
organizations.notes: Field-building organization launched in collaboration with ENAIS to establish a national AI safety community in the Netherlands. Builds connections and pathways to reduce catastrophic AI risk. Focuses on lowering barriers to entry, linking participants to courses, mentoring, and career opportunities in AI safety research, policy, and governance. Plans to expand into policy engagement with European partners, research exchanges, and development of a dedicated AI safety hub in the Netherlands. Growth is iterative, based on evidence and community needs.

organizations.name: Cisco Investments
organizations.notes: Corporate venture investment arm of Cisco Systems, founded 1993, based in San Jose, CA. Launched a $1B AI investment fund in June 2024. Has invested in Cohere, Mistral AI, Scale AI, and World Labs among others. Over 30 years of investing in hundreds of companies across Cisco's core markets and strategic areas. Over 20 AI-focused acquisitions and investments in recent years. Uses build-buy-partner-invest-co-develop strategy. Has 8 funds including dedicated Cisco AI Investment Fund. Strategic investor in xAI.

organizations.name: DST Global
organizations.notes: Global venture capital and private equity firm founded in 2009 by Yuri Milner, spun off from Russian company Digital Sky Technologies (now Mail.ru/VK). Primarily invests in late-stage internet and technology companies. Major portfolio includes Facebook, Twitter, WhatsApp, Snapchat, Spotify, Alibaba, ByteDance. Invested in SSI $1B Series A (Sept 2024) alongside Sequoia, a16z, SV Angel. Has invested in 80+ companies across multiple funds. Milner renounced Russian citizenship in 2022; DST Global funds have no Russia-based investments. Also connected to Breakthrough Foundation (science philanthropy).

organizations.name: SV Angel
organizations.notes: Iconic Silicon Valley seed-stage venture capital firm founded in 2009 by Ron Conway, known as the "Godfather of Silicon Valley." Made early bets on Google, Facebook, Twitter, Airbnb. Raised six funds through 2018, then briefly returned to angel investing before raising a $269M growth equity fund in 2022. Currently managed by Ron Conway and sons Topher and Ronny Conway. Invested in SSI $1B Series A (Sept 2024) alongside Sequoia, a16z, DST Global. Portfolio of nearly 300 companies through 2011. Focus on early-stage technology companies that cannot secure traditional VC funding.

organizations.name: Greenoaks Capital
organizations.notes: San Francisco-based growth equity investment firm founded in 2012. Known for extremely concentrated bets (~15 companies per fund, $25-75M initial checks). Closed $2.5B Fund VI (beating $2.25B target). Led SSI $2B funding round (2025, $32B valuation) alongside a16z, Lightspeed, and DST Global. Greenoaks reportedly invested $500M in that round. Focus areas include AI, cybersecurity, fintech, and vertical SaaS. Emphasizes long-term, high-conviction investing with active governance. Also invested in Scale AI and Anthropic.

organizations.name: University of Melbourne
organizations.notes: Major Australian research university. Home to the Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Digital Ethics (CAIDE), which builds cross-disciplinary research, teaching, policy and regulatory expertise in AI. Established Generative AI Taskforce (GAIT) in 2023 to oversee institutional response to GenAI risks and opportunities. School of Mathematics and Statistics housed Daniel Murfet (Timaeus co-founder, SLT researcher) before he left academia ~2025. School of Computing and Information Systems runs active AI research programs. Collaborates with Timaeus on singular learning theory research for AI safety.

organizations.name: Wichita State University
organizations.notes: Public research university in Wichita, Kansas. Founding member of the NSF AI Institute for Foundations of Machine Learning (IFML), receiving a renewed 20 million USD grant (WSU share: 1.27 million USD) for AI research and workforce development. School of Computing conducts research in AI, statistical learning theory, distributed AI/multi-agent systems, machine learning, and robotics intelligence. Co-designed the Intelligence Rising AI war game exercise alongside University of Cambridge and University of Oxford, which was featured in Elena Andreicheva documentary premiering at CPH:DOX 2026. Also home to the Applied AI in Education (A2E) Lab focused on generative AI in education.

organizations.name: Charles University
organizations.notes: Largest and most renowned university in the Czech Republic, founded in 1348 (oldest university in Central Europe). Ranked in top 2% of universities worldwide. Hosts the Alignment of Complex Systems (ACS) Research Group led by Jan Kulveit, focused on AI alignment and complex systems. Faculty of Mathematics and Physics offers AI-focused programs in the School of Computer Science with research strengths in theoretical CS, machine learning, language technologies, and computer graphics. Participates in the EU Humane AI Net Centre of Excellence. Relevant to the mapping as the institutional home of ACS, which works on AI safety, LLM psychology, and gradual disempowerment research.

organizations.name: Protocol Labs
organizations.notes: Innovation network founded by Juan Benet (2014) connecting 600+ tech startups, funds, accelerators, and foundations. Created IPFS and Filecoin. Raised $204M over 5 rounds; made 86+ investments. Funds Foresight Institute's AI for Science & Safety program. Co-hosts LabWeek Field Building and Funding The Commons events with Foresight Institute. Focuses on decentralized infrastructure, open-source protocols, and Web3. Expanding into AI, AR/VR, BCI, and hardware via its network model. Headquartered in Palo Alto.

organizations.name: Gigafund
organizations.notes: Venture capital firm founded in 2017 by Luke Nosek (PayPal co-founder, Founders Fund) and Stephen Oskoui in Austin, TX. Runs an extremely concentrated, multi-decade strategy focused on capital-intensive physical-world technology companies (SpaceX, Neuralink, Boring Company). Funder of Foresight Institute's AI for Science & Safety Nodes program, supporting AI-driven nanotechnology and molecular assembly research. Portfolio of ~18 companies spanning space, biotech, neurotech, and AI.

organizations.name: 100 Plus Capital
organizations.notes: Longevity-focused venture capital firm founded in 2007 by Sonia Arrison in Menlo Park, CA. Invests in companies positively impacting human longevity, both directly (anti-aging) and broadly (clean food, water). Portfolio of ~15 companies concentrated in HealthTech and Life Sciences. Arrison is also a Foresight Institute board member and chairs the Alliance for Longevity Initiatives. Funder of Foresight Institute's AI for Science & Safety Nodes alongside Gigafund.

organizations.name: Cambridge AI Safety Hub
organizations.notes: AI safety community hub at the University of Cambridge, co-directed by Gaurav Yadav and Justin Dollman. Runs MARS (Mentorship for Alignment Research Students), an AI Safety Fast-Track program, a writing programme, and regular paper reading groups with external researchers.

organizations.name: Berkeley AI Safety Initiative for Students (BASIS)
organizations.notes: Student-run AI safety organization at UC Berkeley. Runs a DeCal course on AI safety, a research program, AI Policy Reading Group, and tech education workshops. Affiliated with SPAR (Supervised Program for Alignment Research) and the broader EA-adjacent safety community.

organizations.name: General Services Administration
organizations.notes: The General Services Administration (GSA) is a federal agency that manages government buildings, procurement, and technology services. GSA plays a central role in federal AI adoption: it launched USAi in August 2025, the government's first shared platform for AI experimentation providing chat-based AI, code generation, and document summarization tools. GSA also oversees FedRAMP (including the 2025 FedRAMP 20x initiative for streamlined cloud authorization) and proposed contract clause GSAR 552.239-7001 in March 2026 requiring AI safeguards from government contractors. The agency's AI strategy emphasizes removing procurement blockers, enabling cross-agency AI collaboration, and ensuring responsible use.

organizations.name: Department of Homeland Security
organizations.notes: Federal cabinet department responsible for border security, cybersecurity, immigration, and disaster response. Published a three-year AI strategy focused on responsible adoption across mission areas: threat detection, border surveillance (CBP edge AI), cybersecurity defense, and emergency response. In Jan 2025 issued DHS Directive 139-08 setting prohibited AI uses and acquisition governance. CIO serves as Chief AI Officer; AI Governance Board convened July 2025. Created AI Safety and Security Board advising critical infrastructure operators. Member of NIST TRAINS Taskforce (AI safety testing). Budget: ~$60B/yr (federal appropriations).

organizations.name: National Institutes of Health
organizations.notes: Primary federal agency for biomedical and public health research, part of HHS. Developing its first institute-wide AI Strategic Plan (RFI closed July 2025) covering AI in biomedical discovery, clinical decision support, and public health. AI/ML research funding grew 233% (inflation-adjusted) from FY2019–2023, outpacing overall NIH budget growth. In Aug 2025 issued policy restricting AI-generated content in grant applications (substantially AI-generated proposals disqualified; post-award AI misuse triggers misconduct investigation). Facing FY2026 budget cuts that may constrain future AI funding. Member of NIST TRAINS Taskforce. Budget: ~$47B/yr (federal appropriations).

organizations.name: Center for AI Safety Action Fund
organizations.notes: 501(c)(4) advocacy arm of the Center for AI Safety (CAIS/safe.ai). Nonpartisan advocacy org focused on AI policy to maintain U.S. leadership and protect against AI-related national security threats. Policy built on three pillars from "Superintelligence Strategy" (Hendrycks, Schmidt, Wang): Nonproliferation (chip export controls, location verification), Deterrence (mandatory reporting for frontier models, intelligence monitoring), Competitiveness (domestic chip production, AI talent immigration). Led by Varun Krovi (Exec Dir) and Brian McGrail (Policy Lead). Member of ASAP Coalition. Distinct from Stanford Center for AI Safety.

organizations.name: EliseAI
organizations.notes: NYC-based AI company (founded 2017) building conversational AI agents for housing and healthcare. Co-founded by CEO Minna Song (MIT CS) and Tony Stoyanov. Platform automates leasing, maintenance, and resident communications for property managers. Powers ~10% of U.S. apartment market, 600+ operators including 75% of NMHC Top 50. Raised $250M Series E (led by a16z, Sep 2025) at $100M+ ARR, 300+ employees. Previously raised $75M Series D (Aug 2024) at $1B valuation. NOT a VC firm — should be reclassified as Deployers & Platforms.

organizations.name: ASML
organizations.notes: Dutch semiconductor equipment manufacturer holding a global monopoly on Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines, essential for manufacturing advanced AI chips at nodes below 7nm. Founded 1984, headquartered in Veldhoven, Netherlands. EUV machines cost up to $400M each. Central to U.S.-China chip export control dynamics — the Dutch government has blocked EUV exports to China since 2019 under U.S. pressure. Current CEO Christophe Fouquet (since 2024) and predecessor Peter Wennink have both opposed further export restrictions, arguing they are economically motivated and could push China to develop its own chipmaking capabilities. ASML's monopoly position makes it a potential EU leverage point for AI safety and compute governance policy. NOTE: Miscategorized as VC/Capital/Philanthropy — should be Infrastructure & Compute.

organizations.name: Players Philanthropy Fund, Inc.
organizations.notes: Nonprofit fiscal sponsor (501(c)(3)) providing tax-exempt status, back-office services, and operational support to 500+ organizations and family funds. Founded in 2010 by retired NFL player Matt Stover and nonprofit professional Seth McDonnell. Widely used within the effective altruism community as a fiscal sponsor for EA-aligned organizations, including Charity Entrepreneurship incubatees and CEARCH. Funded by Centre for Effective Altruism; FWD.us Education Fund gave $200K in 2022. Serves as fiscal sponsor for CaML (Compassion in Machine Learning).

organizations.name: Survival & Flourishing Fund
organizations.notes: Major EA/longtermist grantmaker focused on long-term survival and flourishing of sentient life. Founded 2019, primarily funded by Jaan Tallinn (Skype co-founder). Distributed ~$152M since inception, $34.9M in 2025 alone — 86% to AI safety. Second-largest AI safety funder after Open Philanthropy. Uses S-Process algorithm for grant recommendations, plus Speculation Grants for expedited funding (~35 grantors). Also funds biosecurity and institutional resilience. Key funder of CaML and many other AI safety orgs.

organizations.name: Electric Sheep
organizations.notes: Nonprofit (501(c)(3) fiscally sponsored) dedicated to harnessing AI for animal welfare, environmental protection, and social good. Runs Futurekind Fellowship (12-week AI for Animal Protection program) and Code for Compassion Campus (in-person accelerator in Bengaluru). Founded by Ash, former Chief Economist at Royal Society of Arts who co-founded Forum for Ethical AI with DeepMind. Partner of CaML (Compassion in Machine Learning). Focus areas: safe AI/AGI, alignment for environmental/social/animal protection.

organizations.name: Machine Learning for Language (ML2)
organizations.notes: NLP research group at NYU Center for Data Science, affiliated with the CILVR lab. Focuses on developing and studying state-of-the-art machine learning methods for natural language processing, especially neural network models. Faculty include He He (Asst Prof, CS & Data Science), Tal Linzen (Assoc Prof, Linguistics & Data Science), and João Sedoc. Sam Bowman was a core member before leaving for Anthropic. NYU Alignment Research Group (ARG) is affiliated with ML2.

organizations.name: Center for Data Science
organizations.notes: NYU's Center for Data Science (CDS), established in 2013, is the university's focal point for data science research and training. Houses ~30+ interdisciplinary faculty, ~650 undergrads, 330+ MS, and ~60 PhD students. Home to the Center for Responsible AI (led by Julia Stoyanovich) and closely connected to AI@NYU initiatives. Sam Bowman held a joint appointment here. Connected to NYU Alignment Research Group (ARG) via edge. CDS faculty work spans high-dimensional statistical inference, NLP, computer vision, and AI governance/policy.

organizations.name: The Department of Linguistics
organizations.notes: NYU's Department of Linguistics, part of the Faculty of Arts & Science. Sam Bowman held a joint appointment here as Associate Professor alongside his CDS role. The department has growing ties to computational linguistics and NLP research, with faculty contributing to AI/ML work on language understanding, benchmarking, and evaluation. Connected to NYU Alignment Research Group (ARG) via edge.

organizations.name: Economic Security Action California
organizations.notes: State-level advocacy arm of Economic Security Project focused on California AI policy. Directed by Teri Olle. Co-sponsored SB 1047 (AI safety bill vetoed by Gov. Newsom) and SB 53 (signed into law, creating CalCompute public cloud infrastructure and establishing AI transparency/safety standards). Advocates for mandatory safety guardrails on frontier AI companies, whistleblower protections, and public computing access to democratize AI development. Part of the California Antimonopoly Table coalition. Takes a strong stance against tech industry self-regulation, arguing companies will not put public interest above profits without mandatory requirements.

organizations.name: Carnegie Corporation of New York
